> **Distinction:** Single-factor productivity isolates the efficiency of *one* resource, while multifactor productivity captures the *combined* efficiency of a *bundle* of resources, giving a more balanced view.

---

**Q2. What is a productivity index? Why is it useful in benchmarking?**

**Model Answer:**

A *productivity index* is a ratio (expressed as a percentage) of productivity in a current period to the productivity in a base period.

$$ \text{PI} = \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100 $$

It is useful in benchmarking because it:
1. Quantifies productivity change over time.
2. Allows comparison across departments, plants, or industries.
3. Provides a baseline (PI = 100) to track improvements (PI > 100) or declines (PI < 100).

> A **PI greater than 100** indicates productivity improvement; a **PI less than 100** indicates decline.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, ESE Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Multifactor Productivity Numerical

**(a)** A manufacturing firm produced **₹8,00,000** worth of products in a quarter. The inputs were:
- Labor: 2000 hours at ₹60/hr
- Material: 1500 kg at ₹150/kg
- Capital: 500 machine-hours at ₹120/machine-hour

Compute the **Multifactor Productivity (MFP)** and the **Productivity Index (PI)** if last quarter's MFP was 2.5. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Total Productivity**. A factory has the following data for a year:
- Output: 20,000 units
- Labor cost: ₹3,00,000
- Material cost: ₹4,00,000
- Energy cost: ₹1,00,000
- Capital depreciation: ₹50,000
- Overheads: ₹50,000

Calculate the total productivity. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Identify the output.
$$ O = ₹8,00,000 $$

**Step 2:** Convert each input to ₹.
- Labor: $2000 \times 60 = ₹1,20,000$
- Material: $1500 \times 150 = ₹2,25,000$
- Capital: $500 \times 120 = ₹60,000$

**Step 3:** Sum the inputs.
$$ I_{\text{total}} = 1,20,000 + 2,25,000 + 60,000 = ₹4,05,000 $$

**Step 4:** Compute MFP.
$$ P_{\text{MFP}} = \frac{8,00,000}{4,05,000} = 1.975 \text{ ₹ output per ₹ input} $$

**Step 5:** Compute Productivity Index.
$$ \text{PI} = \frac{1.975}{2.5} \times 100 = 79.0 $$

**Conclusion:** The MFP is **1.975** and the PI is **79**, indicating a **21% decline** in productivity from the base period — the firm needs intervention.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Define total productivity.
> *Total productivity is the ratio of total output to the sum of all input costs (labor, material, capital, energy, overheads) in a work system.* It is the most comprehensive productivity measure.

$$ P_T = \frac{\text{Output (units)}}{\text{Total Input Cost (₹)}} $$

**Step 2:** Sum all input costs.
$$ I_{\text{total}} = 3,00,000 + 4,00,000 + 1,00,000 + 50,000 + 50,000 = ₹9,00,000 $$

**Step 3:** Apply formula.
$$ P_T = \frac{20,000}{9,00,000} = 0.02222 \text{ units per ₹} $$

Equivalently, cost per unit:
$$ \text{Cost per unit} = \frac{1}{P_T} = ₹45 \text{ per unit} $$

**Conclusion (Comment):** The factory incurs **₹45** in combined input cost to produce one unit. To improve total productivity, the firm must either **increase output (better methods, automation)** or **reduce input cost (less waste, cheaper materials, energy efficiency)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Fractional & Multifactor Comparison

**(a)** A factory employs 10 workers for an 8-hour shift and produces 800 units per day. The material consumed is 200 kg/day. Compute the **labor productivity** and **material productivity**. If the wage rate is ₹40/hour and material cost is ₹100/kg, compute the **Multifactor Productivity (Labor + Material)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with an example why **high fractional productivity does not always imply high multifactor productivity**. Support your answer with calculations. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Labor-hours calculation.
$$ L = 10 \text{ workers} \times 8 \text{ hours} = 80 \text{ labor-hours} $$

**Step 2:** Labor productivity.
$$ P_L = \frac{800}{80} = 10 \text{ units per labor-hour} $$

**Step 3:** Material productivity.
$$ P_{\text{mat}} = \frac{800}{200} = 4 \text{ units per kg} $$

**Step 4:** Convert inputs to ₹.
- Labor cost: $80 \times 40 = ₹3,200$
- Material cost: $200 \times 100 = ₹20,000$

**Step 5:** Total input cost.
$$ I_{\text{total}} = 3,200 + 20,000 = ₹23,200 $$

**Step 6:** Output in ₹ (assume selling price = ₹50/unit).
$$ O = 800 \times 50 = ₹40,000 $$

**Step 7:** MFP.
$$ P_{\text{MFP}} = \frac{40,000}{23,200} = 1.724 \text{ ₹ output per ₹ input} $$

**Conclusion:** The firm produces 10 units per labor-hour and 4 units per kg. The MFP is 1.724, meaning every ₹1 spent yields ₹1.724.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** State the concept.
> Fractional productivity measures only ONE input. A high value in that single dimension may mask inefficiencies in OTHER inputs.

**Step 2:** Construct a numerical example.

Consider two factories producing the same 1000 units/month:

| Parameter | Factory A | Factory B |
|---|---|---|
| Labor-hours | 500 | 500 |
| Material used (kg) | 200 | 800 |

**Step 3:** Compute labor productivity (same for both).
$$ P_L^A = P_L^B = \frac{1000}{500} = 2 \text{ units per labor-hour} $$

**Step 4:** Compute material productivity.
$$ P_{\text{mat}}^A = \frac{1000}{200} = 5 \text{ units/kg}, \quad P_{\text{mat}}^B = \frac{1000}{800} = 1.25 \text{ units/kg} $$

**Step 5:** Conclusion.
> Both factories have **identical labor productivity (2 units/labor-hour)**, but **Factory B wastes 4× more material**. If we only look at *labor productivity* (fractional), we wrongly conclude they are equally efficient. The multifactor view (labor + material) would clearly rank Factory A as superior.

> This is the **productivity paradox** that KTU Module 1 emphasizes: **always look at multifactor productivity for a true picture.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing Units in MFP** — Adding labor-hours and kg of material *without converting to ₹*. KTU evaluators deduct **2–3 marks** for this. **Always convert all inputs to a common monetary unit (₹) before summing.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Single-Factor with Multifactor** — Many students write "$P = O / (L + M)$" but call it *labor productivity*. It is **MFP**, not labor productivity. Use precise terminology.
>
> 3. **Productivity Index vs. Growth Rate** — Confusing $\text{PI} = (P_n / P_o) \times 100$ with $g = [(P_n - P_o) / P_o] \times 100$. PI is a ratio; growth rate is a *percentage change*.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Multiply Quantities by Unit Costs** — In MFP problems, $I_1 + I_2 + \dots$ is **NOT** the sum of quantities, but the sum of **costs**. Use $I_i = Q_i \times c_i$.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Units in Final Answer** — Always state units (e.g., "₹ output / ₹ input" or "units / labor-hour"). KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Fractional & Multifactor Productivity**

- **Productivity = Output / Input.** Always.
- **Fractional (Single-Factor) Productivity** uses *one* input. Most common: **Labor Productivity** ($O$ / labor-hours).
- **Multifactor Productivity (MFP)** uses *two or more* inputs, *converted to a common unit (₹)*.
- **Total Productivity** uses *all* inputs (labor + material + capital + energy + overheads).
- **Productivity Index (PI)** = $(P_{\text{new}} / P_{\text{base}}) \times 100$. Baseline = 100.
- **Growth Rate** = $[(P_n - P_o) / P_o] \times 100$ (percentage).
- **High fractional productivity ≠ high MFP** — always cross-check with MFP.
- **Convert all inputs to ₹** before summing in MFP. Mixing units is the #1 error.
- **Standard KTU forms:** $P_L = O / L$, $P_M = O / M_h$, $P_{\text{MFP}} = O / (L\text{₹} + M\text{₹} + K\text{₹})$.
- **Real-world domains:** Manufacturing → machine productivity; Agriculture → land productivity; BPO → labor productivity; Power → energy productivity.
- **Three-Tier Hierarchy:** Fractional (base) → Multifactor (middle) → Total (apex).
- **Service vs. Manufacturing:** Service sector uses *labor* productivity predominantly; manufacturing uses *machine* + *material* productivity.
- **Productivity paradox:** Optimizing one input may worsen another — always take a **multifactor view** for strategic decisions.
- **KTU Board Numerical Mantra:** Read problem → identify $O$ → identify all $I_i$ → convert to ₹ → compute $P$ → state units.

> **Final Mantra:** *"Productivity is not about working harder; it is about converting inputs into outputs more intelligently. The multifactor view is the intelligent view."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Systems and Productivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Work Systems and Productivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Work System — Formal Academic Definition

A **Work System** is a systematic, integrated arrangement of **people, processes, machines, materials, information, and the environment** working in coordinated fashion to produce a defined output (product or service) of value to a customer or end-user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A work system is defined as the combination of human resources, plant and equipment, materials, procedures, information, and the working environment operating in an interrelated manner to accomplish a common objective or goal.

The term *"work system"* replaces the older vocabulary of "method study," "work design," or "industrial engineering" because it embraces a **holistic view** of how value is created in an organization — from strategic planning down to the smallest manual motion on a shop floor.

## 1.2 Productivity — Formal Academic Definition

**Productivity** is the quantitative relationship between the **output produced** (goods or services) and the **input(s) consumed** to produce that output. It is the most fundamental and universally accepted measure of how effectively an organization converts its resources into useful results.

Mathematically (concept-level):

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric of productivity is a **dimensionless ratio** when both output and input are expressed in identical units (e.g., pieces per man-hour), but it carries units (e.g., kg/labour-hour) when measured against a specific resource.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Café Counter" ☕

Imagine a small coffee shop. The **work system** is everything you can see:
- The barista (people)
- The espresso machine (equipment)
- The coffee beans and milk (materials)
- The recipe card (procedure)
- The till and order app (information)
- The lighting, music, and queue layout (environment)

The **productivity** of this café is the number of coffees produced per hour per barista, or the total revenue generated per rupee of input cost.

> If the barista makes **20 coffees/hour** and uses 1.5 kg of beans → productivity = 20/1.5 = **13.33 cups/kg of bean**. If the same barista, with a better espresso machine and a clearer recipe, makes 30 coffees/hour with the same beans, productivity jumped from 13.33 to **20 cups/kg** — a **50% productivity gain** *without any extra material*.

This intuition — *getting more useful output from the same or fewer inputs* — is the heart of work system design.

## 1.4 Why Productivity Matters (KTU Industry Connect)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Productivity is the **single most important determinant** of:
> 1. A nation's standard of living
> 2. A firm's profitability and competitiveness
> 3. An employee's real wage growth
> 4. A country's GDP per capita

Standard benchmarks KTU examiners expect you to recall:
- **Partial Productivity** = Output / Single Input
- **Total Productivity** = Total Output / Total Input
- **Multifactor Productivity** = Output / (Sum of two or more inputs)

A productivity **index** of 110 means a 10% improvement over the base year (base year = 100).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Work System (Detailed)

A complete work system has **six interacting components**. Each can be designed or modified to improve performance.

| # | Component | Description | Example in Manufacturing |
|---|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **People (Human Resources)** | Workers, supervisors, managers — the labour force | Assembly line operators |
| 2 | **Plant & Equipment** | Machines, tools, jigs, fixtures | CNC lathe, robotic arm |
| 3 | **Materials** | Raw materials, WIP, consumables | Steel sheets, cutting oil |
| 4 | **Procedures/Methods** | Standard operating procedures (SOPs) | Routing sheets, work instructions |
| 5 | **Information** | Data, drawings, feedback, schedules | CAD drawings, ERP orders |
| 6 | **Environment** | Lighting, ventilation, noise, ergonomics | Factory layout, air-conditioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When answering theory questions, always mention *all six* components. Listing only 3 or 4 will cost you marks because the question setter expects the full framework.

## 2.2 Classification of Productivity

Productivity is **not a single number**. It is a *family* of measures, each with a specific purpose.

### A. Partial Productivity (Single-Input Measure)

$$\text{Partial Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{One specific input}}$$

Common forms:
- **Labour Productivity** = Output units / Direct labour hours
- **Machine Productivity** = Output units / Machine hours
- **Material Productivity** = Output / Material consumed
- **Capital Productivity** = Output / Capital invested
- **Energy Productivity** = Output / Energy consumed (kWh)

### B. Multifactor Productivity (MFP)

$$\text{MFP} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Group of inputs (e.g., Labour + Capital + Materials)}}$$

Used when a manager wants to know how efficiently a *combination* of resources is being used.

### C. Total Productivity (Total-Factor Productivity / TFP)

$$\text{Total Productivity} = \frac{\text{Total Output (all products, services)}}{\text{Total Input (all resources consumed)}}$$

This is the most comprehensive measure but is also the hardest to compute because converting all outputs and all inputs into a *common unit* (e.g., monetary value: ₹ or $) is often required.

## 2.3 Productivity Index (Comparing Across Time)

To compare productivity across years or across plants, we use an **index**:

$$\text{Productivity Index} = \frac{\text{Current Period Productivity}}{\text{Base Period Productivity}} \times 100$$

If index = 112, it means productivity is **12% higher** than the base period.

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Productivity

KTU examiners consistently test the *categorization* of these factors. Memorize in two groups:

### 2.4.1 Internal Factors (controllable by the firm)

1. **Product Design** — simpler designs → faster production
2. **Process Design** — selection of optimal process
3. **Workforce Skills & Training** — skilled workers are more productive
4. **Work Methods** — better methods → less waste
5. **Tooling and Equipment** — modern CNC vs. manual lathe
6. **Layout & Material Handling** — reduces movement and waiting
7. **Management Practices** — supervision, incentives, quality culture
8. **Working Conditions** — ergonomics, lighting, safety

### 2.4.2 External Factors (beyond firm's control)

1. Government regulations and labour laws
2. Infrastructure (power, transport, telecom)
3. Market conditions and customer demands
4. Technology changes (Industry 4.0, AI, IoT)
5. Socio-cultural factors (work ethics, education)
6. Natural factors (climate, geography)
7. Availability of capital and credit

## 2.5 The Productivity Equation (Extended Form)

For exam purposes, productivity can also be expressed as a product of three sub-efficiencies:

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Work Content}} \times \frac{\text{Work Content}}{\text{Work Time}} \times \frac{\text{Work Time}}{\text{Input}}$$

The three ratios correspond to:
- **Quality Efficiency** (Output / Work Content — accounts for defects and rework)
- **Method Efficiency** (Work Content / Work Time — accounts for method improvements)
- **Utilization Efficiency** (Work Time / Input — accounts for idle time and downtime)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question:** "Productivity improvement comes only from worker effort" — this is **FALSE**. Productivity can rise from better machines (method), fewer defects (quality), or reduced downtime (utilization), independent of worker effort.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{O}{I}$ | Generic productivity | Output / Input |
| 2 | $P_L = \dfrac{O}{L_h}$ | Labour productivity | pieces/man-hour |
| 3 | $P_M = \dfrac{O}{M_h}$ | Machine productivity | pieces/machine-hour |
| 4 | $\text{MFP} = \dfrac{O}{I_1 + I_2 + \dots}$ | Multifactor productivity | ₹ / ₹ |
| 5 | $P_T = \dfrac{\sum O}{\sum I}$ | Total productivity | ₹ / ₹ |
| 6 | $PI = \dfrac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100$ | Productivity index | Pure number (%) |
| 7 | $\text{\% Change} = \dfrac{P_{\text{new}} - P_{\text{old}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Productivity change | % |
| 8 | $P = E_Q \times E_M \times E_U$ | Productivity = Quality × Method × Utilization | Dimensionless |

> **Boundary condition:** All productivity ratios are **positive**. A "negative productivity" is meaningless in engineering economics; it indicates a loss, not a productivity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Productivity Identity

Let:
- $O$ = total output produced in a period
- $I$ = total input consumed in the same period
- $P = O/I$ = productivity

We wish to show that $P$ can be decomposed into three independent efficiency factors.

**Step 1 — Introduce Work Content ($W_c$):**
Work content is the *ideal* time (or resource) needed to produce one unit of output if there were no waste, no defects, and no inefficiency.

By definition:
$$W_c = \frac{I_{\text{ideal}}}{O_{\text{ideal}}}$$

**Step 2 — Define Quality Efficiency ($E_Q$):**
Quality efficiency is the ratio of *acceptable* output to *total attempted* output. It accounts for defects, rework, and rejections.

$$E_Q = \frac{O_{\text{good}}}{O_{\text{attempted}}}$$

Equivalently, $O_{\text{good}} = E_Q \times O_{\text{attempted}}$.

**Step 3 — Define Method Efficiency ($E_M$):**
Method efficiency is the ratio of *ideal* work content to the *actual* work time used. It captures the gains from method study, motion economy, and process improvement.

$$E_M = \frac{W_c}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

So $T_{\text{actual}} = W_c / E_M$.

**Step 4 — Define Utilization Efficiency ($E_U$):**
Utilization is the fraction of *available* time that was actually worked (machine utilization, operator utilization).

$$E_U = \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{available}}}$$

So $T_{\text{actual}} = E_U \times T_{\text{available}}$.

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{O}{I} \\
  &= \frac{O_{\text{good}}}{I} \\
  &= \frac{O_{\text{good}}}{O_{\text{attempted}}} \times \frac{O_{\text{attempted}} \cdot W_c}{I}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $O_{\text{attempted}} \cdot W_c$ = ideal total work required, and actual input $I = T_{\text{actual}} \times \text{rate}$, the equation reduces to:

$$\boxed{P = E_Q \times E_M \times E_U}$$

**Interpretation:** To improve $P$, you can:
- Improve $E_Q$ by reducing defects (Quality circle, Six Sigma, Kaizen)
- Improve $E_M$ by method study, better tooling (Motion study, time study)
- Improve $E_U$ by reducing downtime (TPM, better scheduling)

Each factor acts **independently** on productivity. This is why KTU examiners love this identity.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Multifactor & Total Productivity

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2023 type]:**
> A manufacturing firm produced 5,000 units of product A and 3,000 units of product B last year.
> - Selling price of A = ₹200, of B = ₹350
> - Labour hours = 20,000 hrs at ₹50/hr
> - Material cost = ₹2,80,000
> - Capital employed = ₹10,00,000
> - Other overheads = ₹1,20,000
>
> **Compute:** (i) Total output in ₹, (ii) Multifactor productivity (Labour + Material), (iii) Total productivity, (iv) % change in total productivity if next year output rises by 20% but all inputs rise by only 8%.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total Output in monetary terms:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Output} &= (5{,}000 \times 200) + (3{,}000 \times 350) \\
              &= 10{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}50{,}000 \\
              &= \text{₹ } 20{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Labour cost:**

$$\text{Labour cost} = 20{,}000 \times 50 = \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Multifactor Productivity (Labour + Material):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MFP} &= \frac{20{,}50{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}000} \\
           &= \frac{20{,}50{,}000}{12{,}80{,}000} \\
           &= 1.6016
\end{aligned}$$

So MFP ≈ **1.60** (i.e., ₹1.60 of output for every ₹1 of labour + material input).

**Step 4 — Total Productivity (all inputs):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Input} &= \text{Labour} + \text{Material} + \text{Capital} + \text{Overheads} \\
                   &= 10{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \\
                   &= \text{₹ } 24{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_T &= \frac{20{,}50{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.8542
\end{aligned}$$

Total productivity ≈ **0.854** (₹0.854 of output per ₹1 of total input — the firm is currently operating at a loss because total input exceeds output value, signalling inefficiency or pricing issues).

**Step 5 — Productivity next year (output ↑20%, inputs ↑8%):**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_{\text{new}} &= 20{,}50{,}000 \times 1.20 = 24{,}60{,}000 \\
I_{\text{new}} &= 24{,}00{,}000 \times 1.08 = 25{,}92{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{T,\text{new}} &= \frac{24{,}60{,}000}{25{,}92{,}000} = 0.9491
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage change in total productivity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta P_T &= \frac{0.9491 - 0.8542}{0.8542} \times 100 \\
             &= \frac{0.0949}{0.8542} \times 100 \\
             &= 11.11\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Total productivity improves by **≈ 11.11%** even though inputs grew.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternate shortcut:** When output grows by $a\%$ and inputs by $b\%$, productivity index changes by:
> $$\% \Delta P = \left[\frac{1 + a/100}{1 + b/100} - 1\right] \times 100 = \left[\frac{1.20}{1.08} - 1\right] \times 100 = 11.11\%$$
> KTU examiners accept this shortcut and reward it with full marks.

## 3.3 Productivity Index Table — KTU Style

| Year | Output (₹) | Input (₹) | $P = O/I$ | Index (Base 2021) |
|------|------------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| 2021 | 10,00,000 | 8,00,000 | 1.2500 | 100.00 |
| 2022 | 12,00,000 | 9,00,000 | 1.3333 | 106.67 |
| 2023 | 14,00,000 | 10,00,000 | 1.4000 | 112.00 |
| 2024 | 15,50,000 | 10,50,000 | 1.4762 | 118.10 |

This style of table appears in KTU previous-year question papers. The base year is normally the earliest year listed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six Components of a Work System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[PEOPLE\nHuman Resources] --> F[WORK SYSTEM\nIntegrated Output]
    B[PLANT AND EQUIPMENT\nMachines Tools Jigs] --> F
    C[MATERIALS\nRaw WIP Consumables] --> F
    D[PROCEDURES\nSOPs Routing Sheets] --> F
    E[INFORMATION\nDrawings ERP Feedback] --> F
    G[ENVIRONMENT\nLighting Ergonomics Safety] --> F
    F --> H[VALUE CREATION\nProduct or Service]
    F --> I[PRODUCTIVITY METRIC\nO over I]
    H --> I
```

> The arrow from **F → I** emphasizes that productivity is the *measurement* of the integrated work system, not a separate component.

## 4.2 Productivity Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[PRODUCTIVITY O over I] --> A{Single Input?}
    P --> B{Two or More Inputs?}
    P --> C{All Inputs?}
    A -- Yes --> D[PARTIAL PRODUCTIVITY]
    D --> D1[Labour Productivity]
    D --> D2[Machine Productivity]
    D --> D3[Material Productivity]
    D --> D4[Capital Productivity]
    D --> D5[Energy Productivity]
    B -- Yes --> E[MULTIFACTOR PRODUCTIVITY MFP]
    C -- Yes --> F[TOTAL PRODUCTIVITY TP]
    E --> G[PRODUCTIVITY INDEX]
    F --> G
    D --> G
```

## 4.3 Productivity Decomposition Identity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PRODUCTIVITY] --> EQ[QUALITY EFFICIENCY EQ]
    P --> EM[METHOD EFFICIENCY EM]
    P --> EU[UTILIZATION EFFICIENCY EU]
    EQ --> Q1[Reduce Defects\nSix Sigma Kaizen]
    EQ --> Q2[Rework Elimination]
    EM --> M1[Method Study\nMotion Economy]
    EM --> M2[Better Tooling\nAutomation]
    EU --> U1[TPM Maintenance]
    EU --> U2[Better Scheduling]
```

## 4.4 Factors Affecting Productivity — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FP[FACTORS AFFECTING PRODUCTIVITY] --> INT[INTERNAL FACTORS]
    FP --> EXT[EXTERNAL FACTORS]
    INT --> I1[Product Design]
    INT --> I2[Process and Method]
    INT --> I3[Workforce and Training]
    INT --> I4[Tools and Equipment]
    INT --> I5[Layout and Material Handling]
    INT --> I6[Working Conditions]
    INT --> I7[Management Practices]
    EXT --> X1[Government Policy]
    EXT --> X2[Infrastructure]
    EXT --> X3[Market Conditions]
    EXT --> X4[Technology Change]
    EXT --> X5[Socio Cultural Factors]
    EXT --> X6[Capital Availability]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Work System Improvement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[STAGE 1 IDENTIFY Work System] --> S2[STAGE 2 MEASURE Current Productivity]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3 ANALYZE Internal and External Factors]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4 DESIGN Improvements Method Tooling Layout]
    S4 --> S5[STAGE 5 IMPLEMENT Kaizen Pilot Run]
    S5 --> S6[STAGE 6 CONTROL Monitor Index Feedback]
    S6 --> S2
    S6 --> S7[CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT PDCA Cycle]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define productivity. Differentiate between partial productivity and total productivity.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Productivity is the ratio of output produced to the input consumed in a production process.
$$P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

**Partial Productivity:**
- Considers **only one** input at a time
- Easier to compute and interpret
- Examples: labour productivity, machine productivity
- Useful for departmental benchmarking

**Total Productivity:**
- Considers **all inputs** combined
- Requires conversion of all outputs and inputs to a common monetary unit
- Gives a *holistic* measure of organizational efficiency
- Harder to compute but more accurate

**Key Difference:**

| Aspect | Partial Productivity | Total Productivity |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Inputs considered | Single input | All inputs |
| Unit | Output / specific input | ₹ / ₹ |
| Use | Departmental analysis | Organizational analysis |
| Limitation | May be misleading alone | Hard to compute |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List any six components of a work system and explain the role of *procedures* in it.**

**Model Answer:**

The six components of a work system are:
1. People
2. Plant and Equipment
3. Materials
4. Procedures (Methods)
5. Information
6. Environment

**Role of Procedures:**
Procedures are the standardized *methods* and *step-by-step instructions* that tell workers *how* a job is to be done. They ensure:
- Consistency in output quality
- Reduced dependency on individual skill
- Easier training of new workers
- Compliance with safety and quality standards
- A baseline for continuous improvement (Kaizen)

In the absence of procedures, productivity falls due to variability, rework, and accidents.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**A manufacturing unit produced 4,000 units last year. The following data is given:**
- **Labour:** 10,000 hours at ₹40/hour
- **Material cost:** ₹1,60,000
- **Capital cost (annualized):** ₹2,00,000
- **Overheads:** ₹40,000
- **Selling price per unit:** ₹150

**(a)** Calculate (i) labour productivity, (ii) material productivity, (iii) multifactor productivity (labour + material), and (iv) total productivity.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** The management plans a 25% increase in output and 10% rise in all inputs for the next year. Calculate the new total productivity and the **percentage change** in total productivity. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a)

**Given:**
- Output = 4,000 units
- Output value = 4,000 × 150 = ₹6,00,000
- Labour cost = 10,000 × 40 = ₹4,00,000
- Material cost = ₹1,60,000
- Capital cost = ₹2,00,000
- Overheads = ₹40,000
- Total Input = 4,00,000 + 1,60,000 + 2,00,000 + 40,000 = ₹8,00,000

**(i) Labour Productivity (in units and in ₹):**

$$P_L = \frac{4{,}000 \text{ units}}{10{,}000 \text{ hrs}} = 0.40 \text{ units/hr}$$

$$P_L = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 1.50$$

**(ii) Material Productivity:**

$$P_M = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}60{,}000} = 3.75$$

**(iii) Multifactor Productivity (Labour + Material):**

$$\text{MFP} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{5{,}60{,}000} = 1.0714$$

**(iv) Total Productivity:**

$$P_T = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.75$$

> Total productivity < 1 indicates the firm is operating at a loss; output value is less than total input cost.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b)

**New Output (25% increase):**

$$O_{\text{new}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 1.25 = ₹7{,}50{,}000$$

**New Input (10% increase):**

$$I_{\text{new}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 1.10 = ₹8{,}80{,}000$$

**New Total Productivity:**

$$P_{T,\text{new}} = \frac{7{,}50{,}000}{8{,}80{,}000} = 0.8523$$

**Percentage change in total productivity:**

$$\%\Delta P_T = \frac{0.8523 - 0.7500}{0.7500} \times 100 = 13.64\%$$

**Shortcut verification:**

$$\%\Delta P = \left[\frac{1.25}{1.10} - 1\right] \times 100 = 13.64\%$$

**Comment:**
Total productivity has improved by **13.64%**, even though the firm is still incurring a loss ($P < 1$). The improvement shows the firm is moving in the right direction, but the management must still:
- Reduce overheads and capital cost
- Improve product design to increase selling price
- Or scale up output further to cross the break-even point of $P = 1$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 type]

**(a)** Define a *work system*. With the help of a block diagram, explain the six components of a work system and their interrelationship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **internal and external factors** affecting productivity of a manufacturing organization. Mention at least **six factors under each category**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a)

**Definition:**
A work system is an integrated arrangement of people, equipment, materials, procedures, information, and the working environment, all operating in a coordinated manner to convert inputs into useful outputs (goods or services) of value to the customer.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
+----------------+   +-----------------------+   +---------------+
|     PEOPLE     |   |   PLANT & EQUIPMENT   |   |   MATERIALS   |
+-------+--------+   +-----------+-----------+   +-------+-------+
        |                        |                       |
        v                        v                       v
        +------------------------------------------------+
        |              WORK SYSTEM (INTEGRATED)          |
        |     +-----------+   +-----------+              |
        |     |PROCEDURES |   |INFORMATION|              |
        |     +-----+-----+   +-----+-----+              |
        |           |              |                     |
        |           v              v                     |
        |       +-----------------------+                |
        |       |     ENVIRONMENT       |                |
        |       +-----------+-----------+                |
        +-------------------+----------------------------+
                                |
                                v
                    +-----------+-----------+
                    |     VALUE CREATION    |
                    |  (Product / Service)  |
                    +-----------+-----------+
                                |
                                v
                    +-----------+-----------+
                    |    PRODUCTIVITY       |
                    |       = O / I         |
                    +-----------------------+
```

**Interrelationship:**
- **People** operate the **Plant and Equipment**
- **Plant and Equipment** transform **Materials**
- All of the above are governed by **Procedures** and fed by **Information**
- The **Environment** affects human comfort, safety, and machine performance
- The output of this integrated system is a product/service, and its performance is measured by the **productivity** ratio

---

#### Model Solution for B(b)

### Internal Factors (controllable by the firm):

1. **Product Design:** A well-designed product is easier to manufacture, assemble, and maintain, directly reducing time and cost per unit.
2. **Process Design:** Choice of process (manual vs. automated, casting vs. forging) determines throughput, scrap rate, and flexibility.
3. **Workforce Skill and Training:** Skilled, well-trained workers produce more output with fewer errors.
4. **Work Methods:** Standardized, scientifically designed work methods (from method study) reduce wasted motion and time.
5. **Tools and Equipment:** Modern, well-maintained machines run faster, with higher accuracy and less downtime.
6. **Plant Layout and Material Handling:** A good layout reduces movement, waiting, and congestion.
7. **Working Conditions:** Lighting, ventilation, ergonomics, and safety influence worker fatigue and output rate.
8. **Management Practices:** Supervision style, incentive schemes, and quality culture drive worker motivation.

### External Factors (beyond the firm's control):

1. **Government Policy:** Tax structure, labour laws, industrial regulations.
2. **Infrastructure:** Power supply, transport networks, port and telecom facilities.
3. **Market Conditions:** Demand-supply gap, customer expectations, competitor moves.
4. **Technology Change:** Disruptive technologies (AI, IoT, 3D printing) can suddenly raise or lower productivity.
5. **Capital Availability:** Interest rates, banking policy, foreign investment climate.
6. **Socio-cultural Factors:** Work ethics, education level, labour attitudes.
7. **Natural Factors:** Climate, geography, natural calamities.

> **Conclusion:** A firm can only optimize the internal factors directly; external factors must be *adapted to* through strategic planning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in productivity answers** — always write `units/man-hour` or `₹/₹`. Marks deducted if units are missing.
> 2. **Confusing partial with total productivity** — labour productivity is *partial*, not *total*. Many students write the wrong term.
> 3. **Not stating the base year** in productivity index problems — the index is meaningless without a stated base.
> 4. **Skipping the "comment" step** in numerical problems — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests *interpretation*, not just calculation. Always add 2 lines of comment.
> 5. **Listing only 3–4 factors** under internal/external classification — full 7 marks require at least 6 factors under each.
> 6. **Writing productivity = efficiency** — these are *related* but *not identical*. Productivity = O/I; Efficiency is one component of productivity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Work System = 6 components:** People, Plant/Equipment, Materials, Procedures, Information, Environment (mnemonic: **PPMPIE**)
- ✅ **Productivity** = Output / Input (most fundamental formula)
- ✅ **Three types of productivity:** Partial (1 input), Multifactor (≥2 inputs), Total (all inputs)
- ✅ **Productivity Index** = (Current / Base) × 100; always state the base year
- ✅ **Productivity Identity:** P = E_Q × E_M × E_U (Quality × Method × Utilization) — very high-yield for KTU
- ✅ **Two categories of factors:** Internal (controllable) and External (non-controllable); minimum 6 of each required for full marks
- ✅ **Quick-change formula:** $\%\Delta P = \left[\dfrac{1+a/100}{1+b/100} - 1\right] \times 100$ when output rises by $a\%$ and input by $b\%$
- ✅ **Productivity < 1** in ₹/₹ terms ⇒ firm is running at a loss; **> 1** ⇒ profitable operation
- ✅ **Units matter:** always carry units — `units/hr`, `kg/labour-hr`, or `₹/₹`
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** productivity ≠ efficiency; efficiency is a *component* of productivity
- ✅ **Six components of a work system are NOT optional** in theory answers; examiners expect all six named
- ✅ **KTU 2024 scheme focus:** NEP 2020 emphasizes *holistic* and *sustainable* productivity — mention sustainability angle for bonus marks
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 link:** Smart manufacturing, IoT sensors, and digital twins are the modern levers for raising $E_M$ and $E_U$

> **One-line takeaway for viva:** *"Productivity is the ratio of what we get out to what we put in — and a work system is the entire set of levers we have to improve that ratio."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Productivity Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Productivity Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Productivity? (The KTU Definition)

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework for **Work System Design (PCIET403)**, **productivity** is formally defined as the quantitative measure of the efficiency with which an industrial system converts input resources into useful output goods or services. It is the cornerstone metric upon which all industrial engineering decisions are benchmarked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Productivity (n.) – KTU Academic Definition:**
> The ratio of the *value of output produced* by a given system to the *value of input resources consumed* in producing that output, over a specified period.

### 1.2 Mathematical Expression of Productivity

The basic productivity ratio is universally expressed as:

$$P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Productivity
- Output = Quantity of goods/services produced
- Input = Quantity of resources (labor, material, capital, energy) consumed

### 1.3 Classification of Productivity

| Type of Productivity | Formula Logic | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Productivity** | $\frac{\text{Total Output}}{\text{Total Input}}$ | Whole-organization performance |
| **Partial Productivity** | $\frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Single Input}}$ (e.g., Labor) | Shop-floor benchmarking |
| **Multifactor Productivity** | $\frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Combined Inputs}}$ (Labor + Material) | Department-level analysis |
| **Labor Productivity** | $\frac{\text{Output Value}}{\text{Labor Cost}}$ | Worker efficiency rating |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Benchmark:** KTU valuation accepts the productivity index of a system as $P_{index} = \frac{\text{Standard Hours}}{\text{Actual Hours}} \times 100$. A value greater than **100%** indicates performance above standard.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – The "Lemonade Stand" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Lemonade Stand 🍋:**
> Imagine two children, Riya and Arjun, each running a lemonade stand using the same recipe, same lemons, same sugar, and the same number of hours (8 hours/day).
> - **Riya** uses a manual juicer, spills sugar, and wastes time looking for cups. She sells **40 glasses** using 100 lemons.
> - **Arjun** uses a pre-arranged juicer station with labeled cups and a visible menu. He sells **70 glasses** using 100 lemons.
> 
> Arjun's **productivity** is higher because his **input (lemons)** produces more **output (glasses)**. He achieved this by improving his **method (workflow layout)** and reducing **wasted motion**.
> 
> In industry, this is precisely what the various productivity improvement techniques accomplish — they minimize *waste* (Muda) and maximize *value-added output* from the *same* or *fewer* inputs.

### 1.5 The "Big Picture" – Why KTU Emphasizes This in Module 1

Productivity improvement is **not a single technique** — it is a **portfolio of engineered approaches** that industrial engineers deploy systematically. Module 1 of PCIET403 lays the foundation by introducing the **classical techniques** rooted in the work of **Frederick Winslow Taylor** (Scientific Management) and **Frank & Lillian Gilbreth** (Motion Study), as well as modern adaptations like **Lean, Kaizen, and Value Engineering**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Productivity Improvement Techniques (KTU Module 1 Map)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups productivity improvement techniques into the following structured families:

**(A) Work Study Family** (Taylor, Gilbreth lineage)
1. Method Study
2. Work Measurement (Time Study)
3. Motion Study & Therbligs
4. Process Charts (Operation, Flow, Man-Machine, Two-Hand)
5. Principles of Motion Economy

**(B) Modern Improvement Family** (Lean / Continuous Improvement lineage)
6. Value Engineering (VE) / Value Analysis (VA)
7. Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)
8. 5S Methodology
9. Just-in-Time (JIT)
10. Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
11. Lean Manufacturing principles

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Method Study

**Definition:** Method Study is the systematic recording, analysis, and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work, with the objective of developing **simpler, more economical, and more effective methods**.

**Six-Step Procedure (KTU High-Yield):**
1. **Select** the work to be studied.
2. **Record** all relevant facts (using charts).
3. **Examine** the facts critically (using questioning techniques).
4. **Develop** the most economical method.
5. **Install** the new method.
6. **Maintain** the new method (standardization).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List the six steps of Method Study."* Memorize this in the exact order above.

#### 2.2.2 Motion Study

Motion Study is the analysis of the **microscopic motions** made by an operator while performing a task. It was pioneered by Frank Gilbreth in the late 19th century.

**Therbligs (Gilbreth's 18 Fundamental Motions):**
A *therblig* is the basic unit of motion. Gilbreth identified **18 therbligs** (note: "therblig" is "Gilbreth" spelled backwards).

- **Effective Therbligs (Add Value):** Reach (R), Move (M), Grasp (G), Position (P), Assemble (A), Disassemble (DA), Use (U), Release (RL).
- **Ineffective Therbligs (Add No Value):** Search (SH), Find (F), Select (ST), Inspect (I), Pre-position (PP), Plan (PN), Unavoidable Delay (UD), Avoidable Delay (AD), Rest (R) — for fatigue allowance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Therbligs are classified by color in industrial engineering practice. *Effective* therbligs are shown in green/olive; *Ineffective* ones in red/black. KTU questions commonly ask students to **classify a given motion** as effective or ineffective.

#### 2.2.3 Principles of Motion Economy (Gilbreth's 10 Principles)

KTU Module 1 expects students to internalize these **10 core principles** (often asked for 3–7 marks):

1. Both hands should begin and end their motions **simultaneously**.
2. Both hands should **not be idle** simultaneously (except during rest).
3. Motions of the arms should be **symmetrical and opposite**.
4. Hand motions should be **confined to the lowest classification** (finger > wrist > arm > body).
5. **Momentum** should be used to assist the worker (never opposed).
6. **Smooth, continuous curved motions** are preferable to straight-line jerky motions.
7. **Ballistic motions** are faster, easier, and more accurate than controlled motions.
8. Work should be arranged to allow an **easy, natural rhythm**.
9. **Eyes should follow the hands less frequently** (work should be pre-positioned).
10. **Tools and materials should be pre-positioned** at the point of use.

#### 2.2.4 Process Charts (KTU Module 1 – High Yield)

| Chart Type | Symbol Set Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Operation Process Chart** | $\bigcirc$ Operation | Shows overall process sequence — inspections and operations only |
| **Flow Process Chart** | All 5 symbols | Shows **man/material flow** in detail (Op, Insp, Trans, Storage, Delay) |
| **Man-Machine Chart** | Operator vs Machine timing | Studies operator-machine idle time |
| **Two-Hand Process Chart** | Both hands | Studies **symmetry** and simultaneous motions |
| **String Diagram** | Physical layout | Tracks path of movement over a scale model |
| **Cycle Graph / Chronocycle Graph** | Timed path | Records motion with duration |

> [!NOTE]
> The **5 standard process chart symbols** are: $\bigcirc$ Operation, $\square$ Inspection, $\rightarrow$ Transportation, $\nabla$ Storage, D Delay.

#### 2.2.5 Value Engineering (VE) / Value Analysis (VA)

**Core Equation (VE Foundation):**

$$V = \frac{F}{C}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Value
- $F$ = Function (the specific work a product performs)
- $C$ = Cost (total cost to deliver that function)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction:**
> - **Value Engineering (VE)** is applied during the **design / pre-production** phase.
> - **Value Analysis (VA)** is applied during the **production / post-production** phase.
> 
> Both share the **Job Plan (5 phases)**: Information → Speculation (creative) → Analysis → Evaluation → Implementation.

#### 2.2.6 Kaizen, 5S, JIT, TPM (Modern Techniques – Brief)

- **Kaizen:** Japanese term = *Kai* (change) + *Zen* (good). Means **continuous, incremental improvement** involving *all employees*, from CEO to shop floor.
- **5S:** Sort (Seiri), Set in Order (Seiton), Shine (Seiso), Standardize (Seiketsu), Sustain (Shitsuke).
- **JIT (Just-in-Time):** Production philosophy where materials arrive *exactly when needed*, minimizing inventory and WIP.
- **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance):** Proactive and preventive maintenance by *all employees* to maximize equipment effectiveness (**OEE**).

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

Where $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality rate.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Productivity | $P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | Productivity Index | $PI = \frac{SH}{AH} \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| 3 | Productivity Improvement % | $\Delta P = \frac{P_{new} - P_{old}}{P_{old}} \times 100$ | Percentage change |
| 4 | Value Engineering | $V = \frac{F}{C}$ | Function over Cost |
| 5 | OEE (TPM) | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | A, P, Q as decimals |
| 6 | Standard Time | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | NT = Normal Time, A = Allowance (fraction) |
| 7 | Normal Time | $NT = OT \times R$ | OT = Observed Time, R = Rating factor |
| 8 | Total Float Time | $TFT = \text{Cycle Time} - \sum \text{Activity Times}$ | Used in line balancing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Formatting Note:** All absolute-value and division bars in formulas use LaTeX `\\vert` or `\\mid` — never the vertical pipe character `\\vert` literal — to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry (Toyota):** JIT + Kaizen transformed Toyota into the world's most productive automaker.
- **Software Industry:** Lean principles adapted into **Agile/Scrum** for productivity.
- **Manufacturing Plants:** TPM reduced unplanned downtime by 60–80% globally.
- **Healthcare:** Method Study reduced patient-waiting times in hospitals by 35%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Basic Productivity to Productivity Index

This derivation walks through the mathematical logic that connects the **fundamental productivity ratio** to the **productivity index** commonly used in KTU numerical problems.

#### Step 1: Define the Output in Standard Terms

In a measured job, the output is typically expressed in **Standard Hours (SH)** — i.e., the time that *should* be taken by a qualified worker at a normal pace.

Let $SH$ = Standard hours allowed for a given quantity of output.

#### Step 2: Define the Input in Actual Terms

The actual time consumed is recorded as **Actual Hours (AH)**.

Let $AH$ = Actual clock hours spent to produce the same quantity of output.

#### Step 3: Construct the Productivity Ratio

Following the base definition $P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$, and expressing "output" as standard effort (SH) and "input" as actual effort (AH):

$$P = \frac{SH}{AH}$$

#### Step 4: Convert to Percentage Form (Productivity Index)

$$PI = \frac{SH}{AH} \times 100 \quad \text{(percent)}$$

#### Step 5: Express the Productivity Change (Before vs After)

If $PI_1$ is before improvement and $PI_2$ is after improvement:

$$\Delta PI = PI_2 - PI_1 \quad \text{(percentage points)}$$

$$\% \text{Improvement} = \frac{PI_2 - PI_1}{PI_1} \times 100$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Style)

> **Problem:**
> A workshop employs 12 workers producing 240 units/day. After applying Method Study, the same workforce produces 300 units/day with the same standard time per unit. Calculate:
> (a) Original productivity index
> (b) New productivity index
> (c) % improvement in productivity

#### Given Data
- Workers = 12
- Original output = 240 units/day
- New output = 300 units/day
- Standard time per unit = constant

#### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute standard hours allowed per day (SH).

Since standard time per unit is constant, let's assume it equals 1 standard hour per unit (for illustration; the ratio is what matters).

$$SH_{original} = 240 \times 1 = 240 \text{ std-hr/day}$$

$$SH_{new} = 300 \times 1 = 300 \text{ std-hr/day}$$

**Step 2:** Compute actual hours available per day (AH).

Each worker works 8 hours → 12 workers → $12 \times 8 = 96$ actual hours/day.

$$AH = 96 \text{ hr/day}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate original productivity index.

$$PI_1 = \frac{SH_{original}}{AH} \times 100 = \frac{240}{96} \times 100 = 250\%$$

**Step 4:** Calculate new productivity index.

$$PI_2 = \frac{SH_{new}}{AH} \times 100 = \frac{300}{96} \times 100 = 312.5\%$$

**Step 5:** Calculate % improvement.

$$\% \text{Improvement} = \frac{PI_2 - PI_1}{PI_1} \times 100 = \frac{312.5 - 250}{250} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Improvement} = \frac{62.5}{250} \times 100 = 25\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** (a) $PI_1 = 250\%$ (b) $PI_2 = 312.5\%$ (c) **Productivity improvement = 25%**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Technique Selection Guide

| Problem Identified | Best Technique | Expected Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Excessive operator idle time | Man-Machine Chart | Balanced operator-machine utilization |
| Repetitive fatigue injuries | Motion Study + Ergonomics | Reduced cycle time, fewer injuries |
| Material handling delays | Flow Process Chart | Reduced transport, storage waste |
| High product cost | Value Engineering | Lower cost-to-function ratio |
| Equipment breakdowns | TPM | Increased OEE |
| High inventory | JIT | Reduced carrying cost |
| Disorganized workplace | 5S | Faster tool retrieval, fewer accidents |
| Small, daily inefficiency | Kaizen | Continuous incremental gains |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Six-Step Method Study Application

1. **Select:** Identify the bottleneck operation (e.g., manual drilling station).
2. **Record:** Draft an Operation Process Chart and a Two-Hand Process Chart for the existing method.
3. **Examine:** Apply the **questioning technique** — *Purpose? Where? When? Who? How? Means?* — to each step.
4. **Develop:** Combine motions, eliminate transport, redesign workplace layout.
5. **Install:** Train workers, update SOPs, remove old tools.
6. **Maintain:** Conduct periodic audits; standardize via written procedures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Productivity Improvement Engine

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how the various techniques interact within a Work System Design context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Identification] --> B[Technique Selection]
    B --> C{Classical IE Path}
    B --> D{Modern Lean Path}
    C --> E[Method Study]
    C --> F[Motion Study and Therbligs]
    C --> G[Work Measurement]
    C --> H[Process Charts]
    D --> I[Value Engineering]
    D --> J[Kaizen and 5S]
    D --> K[JIT and TPM]
    E --> L[Process Optimization]
    F --> L
    G --> M[Time Standardization]
    H --> L
    I --> N[Cost Reduction]
    J --> O[Continuous Improvement]
    K --> P[Resource Efficiency]
    L --> Q[Improved Productivity]
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Feedback Loop for Sustainability]
    R --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid: Six-Step Method Study Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Select Work] --> S2[Step 2 Record All Facts]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Examine Critically]

```

### 4.3 Mermaid: Classification of Therbligs

```mermaid
    T[Therbligs - 18 Fundamental Motions] --> EFF[Effective Therbligs]
    T --> INE[Ineffective Therbligs]

    EFF --> E3[Grasp]
    EFF --> E4[Position]
    EFF --> E5[Assemble]
    EFF --> E6[Disassemble]

    EFF --> E8[Release]

    INE --> I2[Find]
    INE --> I3[Select]
    INE --> I4[Inspect]
    INE --> I5[Pre-position]
    INE --> I6[Plan]

    INE --> I8[Avoidable Delay]
    INE --> I9[Rest for Fatigue]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid: 5S Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[1S Sort Seiri] --> F2[2S Set in Order Seiton]
    F2 --> F3[3S Shine Seiso]
    F3 --> F4[4S Standardize Seiketsu]
    F4 --> F5[5S Sustain Shitsuke]
    F5 --> F6[Disciplined Workplace Culture]
    F6 -.audit.-> F1
```

### 4.5 Functional Mapping: Value Engineering Job Plan

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VP[Value Engineering Job Plan] --> P1[Phase 1 Information Gathering]
    P1 --> P2[Phase 2 Creative Speculation]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Analytical Evaluation]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Decision and Selection]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Implementation and Follow-up]
    P5 --> OUT[Optimized Function to Cost Ratio V = F over C]
```

### 4.6 Block Diagram: Work Study Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WS[Work Study Framework] --> MS[Method Study Branch]
    WS --> WM[Work Measurement Branch]
    MS --> MS1[Process Charts]
    MS --> MS2[Motion Study and Therbligs]
    MS --> MS3[Motion Economy Principles]
    WM --> WM1[Time Study]
    WM --> WM2[Work Sampling]
    WM --> WM3[Predetermined Motion Time Systems PMTS]
    MS1 --> OUT1[Simplified and Economical Method]
    MS2 --> OUT1
    MS3 --> OUT1
    WM1 --> OUT2[Established Standard Time]
    WM2 --> OUT2
    WM3 --> OUT2
    OUT1 --> FINAL[Higher Productivity]
    OUT2 --> FINAL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define productivity. Explain the different types of productivity.**

**Model Answer:**

Productivity is defined as the ratio of output produced to the input consumed over a specified period: $P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$.

The types are:
1. **Total Productivity** – Output of the entire organization divided by total input of all resources.
2. **Partial Productivity** – Output divided by a single class of input (e.g., labor productivity).
3. **Multifactor Productivity** – Output divided by a combined set of inputs (e.g., labor + material).
4. **Labor Productivity** – Specifically measures output per worker-hour or per labor cost unit.

> **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **What is Value Engineering? State the value equation.**

**Model Answer:**

Value Engineering (VE) is a systematic method to improve the *"value"* of goods or products by using an examination of function. Value, as defined in VE, is the ratio of *function* to *cost*.

The value equation is:

$$V = \frac{F}{C}$$

Where $F$ = Function performed by the item, $C$ = Cost of producing that function. The objective of VE is to increase $V$ by either increasing function or reducing cost, or both.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure of Method Study in detail with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Define Therbligs. Classify them with examples. Why is Motion Study important in productivity improvement? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

Method Study is the systematic procedure for analyzing and improving methods of work. It follows **six sequential steps**:

1. **Select:** Identify the work or operation to be studied based on bottleneck, frequency, or strategic importance.
2. **Record:** Capture all facts about the current method using process charts (operation, flow, two-hand) and diagrams.
3. **Examine:** Subject every recorded detail to critical questioning using the *Questioning Technique* — *Purpose, Place, Sequence, Person, Means*.
4. **Develop:** Generate the most practical, economical, and safe improved method by combining, simplifying, or eliminating steps.
5. **Install:** Implement the new method — train workers, update layouts, write SOPs.
6. **Maintain:** Periodically audit to ensure the standard method is followed; take corrective action on deviations.

> *The block diagram for the 6-step method study has been provided in Section 4.2 of these notes.*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Therbligs** are the 18 fundamental, indivisible motions identified by Frank Gilbreth (the word "therblig" is "Gilbreth" reversed). They form the building blocks of all manual work.

**Classification:**
- **Effective Therbligs** (8) — add value to the product: *Reach, Move, Grasp, Position, Assemble, Disassemble, Use, Release*.
- **Ineffective Therbligs** (10) — do not add value: *Search, Find, Select, Inspect, Pre-position, Plan, Unavoidable Delay, Avoidable Delay, Rest, Hold*.

**Importance of Motion Study in Productivity Improvement:**
1. Identifies and eliminates wasted motion, reducing cycle time.
2. Establishes the most efficient method as a standard.
3. Reduces operator fatigue and improves ergonomic safety.
4. Provides the foundation for setting accurate work standards.
5. Enables training of new operators to the *one best way* of working.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the principles of Motion Economy with suitable examples. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A manufacturing unit employs 20 workers producing 400 units/day. After implementing 5S and Kaizen suggestions, the output rose to 500 units/day with the same workforce and standard time. Calculate the (i) Original Productivity Index, (ii) New Productivity Index, and (iii) Percentage Improvement. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

The **Principles of Motion Economy** (Gilbreth's 10 Principles) are guidelines for designing efficient hand-and-body motions:

1. **Two hands begin/finish simultaneously** — both hands should start and end at the same instant to avoid idle time.
2. **No simultaneous idle hands** — both hands should not be idle at once except during rest.
3. **Symmetric arm motions** — both arms should move in opposite, symmetric directions.
4. **Lowest motion class** — use fingers when possible; escalate only to wrist, arm, body.
5. **Momentum assistance** — design tasks so that natural momentum aids the worker.
6. **Continuous curved motions** — smooth arcs reduce fatigue versus sharp zig-zag movements.
7. **Ballistic motions** — fast, releasing motions are more accurate than tightly controlled ones.
8. **Natural rhythm** — work should flow with a comfortable rhythm.
9. **Pre-position tools/materials** — eliminates searching and reaching during the task.
10. **Reduce eye movements** — pre-locate items by feel or fixed position.

*Example:* In a screw assembly operation, providing a gravity-fed hopper of screws (pre-positioning) eliminates the *Reach* and *Search* therbligs, reducing cycle time by 15–20%.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- Workers = 20, Shift hours = 8 hr/day → $AH = 20 \times 8 = 160$ hr/day.
- Original output = 400 units/day; New output = 500 units/day.
- Standard time per unit is constant (assume 1 std-hr/unit for ratio).

**Step 1 — Original Productivity Index:**

$$PI_1 = \frac{SH_{original}}{AH} \times 100 = \frac{400 \times 1}{160} \times 100 = 250\%$$

**Step 2 — New Productivity Index:**

$$PI_2 = \frac{SH_{new}}{AH} \times 100 = \frac{500 \times 1}{160} \times 100 = 312.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Improvement:**

$$\% \text{Improvement} = \frac{PI_2 - PI_1}{PI_1} \times 100 = \frac{312.5 - 250}{250} \times 100 = 25\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SH and AH:** Many students invert the productivity index formula. *Output in standard hours (SH) goes in the numerator.* AH (actual labor hours available) goes in the denominator.
> 2. **Forgetting the ×100:** KTU valuation explicitly checks for the percentage form; omitting the multiplication leads to a 0.5 to 1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing up Therbligs:** Writing "Hold" as effective or "Release" as ineffective loses full marks. Memorize the exact 8/10 split.
> 4. **Method Study step order:** Out-of-order sequence (e.g., Develop before Examine) attracts a direct 1-mark penalty.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Summary:**

- **Productivity** = Output / Input. Index form: $PI = \frac{SH}{AH} \times 100$.
- **Method Study** has **6 steps** (Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Install → Maintain).
- **Work Measurement** has **3 main techniques**: Time Study, Work Sampling, PMTS.
- **Therbligs = 18** (8 effective + 10 ineffective). Term coined by Gilbreth.
- **Process Chart Symbols = 5**: Operation ($\bigcirc$), Inspection ($\square$), Transportation ($\rightarrow$), Storage ($\nabla$), Delay (D).
- **Motion Economy = 10 Principles** (Gilbreth). First principle = *both hands begin/end motions simultaneously*.
- **Value Equation** = $V = \frac{F}{C}$. VE = pre-production; VA = post-production.
- **5S = Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain** (Japanese: Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke).
- **OEE** = Availability $\times$ Performance $\times$ Quality (TPM metric).
- **Kaizen** = continuous improvement by *all employees* — small, frequent, incremental.
- **JIT** = produce/deliver *just in time* — minimizes inventory and WIP.
- **Taylor** = Father of Scientific Management; **Gilbreth** = Father of Motion Study.
- Productivity improvement is **not a one-time event** but a **continuous feedback loop** (see Section 4.1 diagram).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques in Work Study for Productivity Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Techniques in Work Study for Productivity Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Study** is a generic term used to denote a body of knowledge and a set of techniques concerned with the analysis of work systems. It is the systematic investigation of work methods to determine the most efficient and effective method of performing a task, followed by the establishment of standard times for that method.

According to the **British Standards Institution (BS 3138:1979)**, Work Study comprises two principal branches:
1. **Method Study** — the systematic recording, analysis, and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work.
2. **Work Measurement** — the application of techniques designed to establish the time for a qualified worker to carry out a specified task at a defined level of performance.

## 1.2 Productivity Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Productivity** is the ratio of output (goods or services produced) to the input (resources consumed). In a work system context, productivity improvement is achieved by either increasing output for the same input, or producing the same output with reduced input.

Mathematically, the fundamental productivity identity is:

$$
\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}
$$

## 1.3 The Productivity Equation (Composite Form)

A more detailed engineering formulation decomposes productivity improvement into three leverage factors:

$$
P = \frac{(A \times B)}{C}
$$

Where:
- **P** = Productivity index
- **A** = Output per unit time
- **B** = Effective working time ratio
- **C** = Standard hours per unit

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Kitchen Chef:** Imagine you are a chef in a small restaurant trying to cook a 3-course meal in 30 minutes. 
> - **Method Study** is like rearranging your kitchen: placing the cutting board closer to the stove, pre-chopping vegetables, and reorganising the workflow so the chef's hands never cross unnecessarily. 
> - **Work Measurement** is like using a stopwatch to time each recipe and then writing a *standard recipe card* so that any trained chef can replicate the same performance.
> 
> The result (productivity) is the same meal, cooked faster, with less effort, and at consistent quality.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Standard Working Day | **8 hours / 480 minutes** |
| Standard Working Week | **40 hours** |
| Normal Performance Rating | **100 B.S. (British Standard) points** |
| Standard Performance | Worker walking 3 mph without load in a straight line |
| Rest Allowance (BR) | **10 % – 20 %** of basic time (typical) |
| ILO / IS Standard | **BS 3138 / IS 9507** |

## 1.6 Objectives of Work Study

- **Optimise** the use of human, material, and machine resources.
- **Standardise** work methods, conditions, and tools.
- **Establish** fair and reasonable work standards (piece-rate / incentive basis).
- **Reduce** human effort, fatigue, and monotony.
- **Improve** quality, safety, and morale of workers.
- **Establish** the basis for cost control, planning, and incentive payment systems.

## 1.7 Techniques Covered in Module 1

The syllabus (KTU 2024 PCIET403) lists the following techniques under Module 1:

1. **Process Charts** — Operation, Inspection, Combined, Flow Process, Two-Handed
2. **String Diagram**
3. **Multiple Activity Chart (Man-Machine / Multi-man Chart)**
4. **Therbligs** (Fundamental motions)
5. **Principles of Motion Economy**
6. **Micro-motion Study** and **Memo-motion Study**
7. **Time Study** (with rating and allowances)
8. **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** — e.g., MTM, BasicMOST, Work-Factor
9. **Work Sampling**
10. **Analytical Estimating**
11. **Standard Data & Formula Construction**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Structure of Work Study Techniques

Work Study is structured hierarchically as shown below, ranging from macro-level process analysis down to micro-level motion measurement.

### 2.1.1 Method Study Hierarchy (Top-Down)

The Method Study phase proceeds from broad to fine analysis:

1. **Process Charts** (Operation / Flow / Two-Handed) — *Macro*
2. **String Diagram** — *Movement of worker over workspace*
3. **Multiple Activity Chart** — *Synchronisation of man/machine*
4. **Therbligs** (17 fundamental motions) — *Micro*
5. **Micro-motion / Memo-motion Study** — *Sub-second analysis*

### 2.1.2 Work Measurement Hierarchy

Work Measurement techniques are categorised by data source:

| Technique | Data Source | Accuracy | Time Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stopwatch Time Study** | Direct observation | High (±5 %) | Low |
| **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** | Published tables | High (±5 %) | Medium |
| **Work Sampling** | Statistical observations | Moderate (±5 %, 95 % CI) | High |
| **Analytical Estimating** | Synthesised from standard data | High | Low |
| **Standard Data Systems** | Previously computed data | High | Very Low |

## 2.2 Five-Step Procedure of Method Study

> [!NOTE]
> The universal five-step approach is the foundation of every Method Study exercise. Examiners frequently test the *sequencing* of these steps.

1. **Select** the work to be studied (define the problem, scope, objectives).
2. **Record** all relevant facts about the present method (using charts, diagrams).
3. **Examine** the recorded facts critically (using questioning techniques).
4. **Develop** the most practical, economic, and effective method.
5. **Install** the new method and **Maintain** it as a standard (define + implement + monitor).

## 2.3 The Five Questioning Techniques (for Critical Examination)

These are the analytical probes applied during Step 3:

- **Purpose** — What is done? Why is it done? What else could achieve the purpose?
- **Place** — Where is it done? Why there? Where else could it be done?
- **Sequence** — When is it done? Why then? When else could it be done?
- **Person** — Who does it? Why that person? Who else could do it?
- **Means** — How is it done? Why that way? How else could it be done?

## 2.4 Process Charts — Symbols (ASME Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect exact symbol recall. Memorise the five standard process chart symbols.

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **○ (Circle)** | Operation | A productive step; material changes or progresses |
| **□ (Square)** | Inspection | Checking quality, quantity, or attributes |
| **▷ (Arrow, D-shape)** | Transport | Moving material from one place to another |
| **▽ (Inverted triangle)** | Delay / Temporary Storage | Material waiting; no action |
| **D (D-shape in box)** | Permanent Storage | Material placed in storage, awaiting release |

### 2.4.1 Operation Process Chart
- Records only **Operations and Inspections**.
- Shows the *chronological sequence* of manufacturing or assembly.
- Answers: "What is done to the material?"

### 2.4.2 Flow Process Chart (Man / Material / Equipment)
- Records all **five events** (Operation, Inspection, Transport, Delay, Storage).
- Can be of three types: **Man-type** (worker's movements), **Material-type** (material flow), **Equipment-type** (machine usage).
- **Summary panel** at bottom records total counts and distances, enabling before/after comparison.

### 2.4.3 Two-Handed Process Chart (Right-Hand / Left-Hand Chart)
- Records the **simultaneous motions** of both hands of a single operator.
- Used for repetitive, short-cycle bench operations.
- Enables identification of idle time, simultaneous use, and balance between hands.

## 2.5 String Diagram

A **String Diagram** is a scale plan of the work area on which a thread is stretched to trace the path of a worker (or material) during the work cycle.

- **Purpose:** Identify backtracking, excessive travel, and inefficient layout.
- **Quantifies:** Total distance travelled, number of backtracks, time spent moving vs. working.
- **Typical uses:** Workshop layout, hospital nurse routes, warehouse order picking.

## 2.6 Multiple Activity Chart

Also called the **Man-Machine Chart** or **Multi-Manned Chart**.

- A time-scaled chart showing the **simultaneous activities** of one or more workers and one or more machines.
- Time is plotted on the X-axis; each worker/machine is a horizontal lane.
- **Three categories of activity**:
  - **Work** (effective productive use)
  - **Idle** (operator waiting for machine, or vice versa)
  - **Reset / Prepare** (setup, minor adjustments)

**Critical calculation — Operator cycle time:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_m + T_h
$$

Where $T_m$ is the machine time and $T_h$ is the operator's hand time (load/unload/setup) per cycle.

**Machine utilisation:**

$$
\eta_{\text{machine}} = \frac{T_m}{T_{\text{cycle}}}
$$

**Operator utilisation:**

$$
\eta_{\text{operator}} = \frac{T_h}{T_{\text{cycle}}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For maximum productivity, design the work such that the operator can service **multiple machines** in parallel. If the operator's hand time equals the machine cycle time, one operator can run **one machine**. If hand time is 1/2 of machine time, the operator can run **2 machines**, and so on.

$$
N_{\text{machines per operator}} = \frac{T_m}{T_h}
$$

## 2.7 Therbligs — The 17 Fundamental Motions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> "Therblig" is **Gilbreth** spelled backwards. These are the indivisible elements of human motion used in micro-motion study.

| # | Therblig | Symbol | Colour | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Search (SH) | ⌕ | Black | Eyes/locate an object |
| 2 | Find (F) | ⊙ | Black | Mental reaction at end of search |
| 3 | Select (ST) | → | Black | Choose between alternatives |
| 4 | Grasp (G) | ∩ | Red | Close fingers on object |
| 5 | Hold (H) | ⊃ | Gold | Retain object without effort |
| 6 | Transport Loaded (TL) | ⊃• | Green | Move object held |
| 7 | Transport Empty (TE) | ⊃° | Green | Move empty hand |
| 8 | Position (P) | ∩* | Blue | Orient object for use |
| 9 | Assemble (A) | ∩• | Blue | Join two parts |
| 10 | Disassemble (DA) | ∩° | Blue | Separate two parts |
| 11 | Use (U) | ∩/ | Violet | Manipulate tool/instrument |
| 12 | Pre-position (PP) | ∪ | Light Blue | Position for next operation |
| 13 | Release Load (RL) | ∩° | Red | Let go of object |
| 14 | Inspect (I) | ◯ | Yellow | Examine object |
| 15 | Plan (PN) | △ | Brown | Mental decision |
| 16 | Avoidable Delay (AD) | ⌒ | Yellow | Unnecessary wait |
| 17 | Unavoidable Delay (UD) | ∽ | Yellow | Necessary wait |

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective therbligs** (red, green, gold, blue, violet) advance the work. **Ineffective therbligs** (search, find, select, plan, avoidable delay) — counted with a separate column — represent waste and must be eliminated.

## 2.8 Principles of Motion Economy (Gilbreth's 22 Principles)

Grouped into three categories:

### 2.8.1 Principles Concerning the Use of the Human Body
1. Both hands should begin and end motions at the same time.
2. Both hands should not be idle simultaneously (except during rest).
3. Motions of both arms should be symmetric and simultaneous.
4. Hand motions should be confined to the lowest practical classification.
5. Momentum should be used to assist the worker; it should never become a hindrance.
6. Smooth, continuous curved motions are preferable to straight-line motions with sudden changes.
7. **Ballistic (projectile) motions** are faster, easier, and more accurate than controlled motions.
8. Rhythm is essential to smooth, automatic performance.
9. Work should be arranged so that eye movements follow a natural, easy sequence.

### 2.8.2 Principles Concerning the Arrangement of the Work Place
10. Fixed locations for tools and materials ("jigs and fixtures").
11. Tools, materials, and controls should be placed within the *normal work area* (the area swept by both hands with elbows at sides).
12. Materials and tools should be pre-positioned to avoid search and select.
13. Drop deliveries (chutes) wherever possible.
14. Provide adequate illumination with proper colour contrast.
15. Provide a chair of the right type and height; adjust height of work for sitting/standing.

### 2.8.3 Principles Concerning the Design of Tools and Equipment
16. Combine tools to eliminate tool-changing.
17. Use jigs, fixtures, and foot controls wherever possible to free both hands.
18. Locate controls and dials within easy reach.
19. Use the *best type of finger, hand, or arm* motion for each task.
20. Handles of tools should be designed for firm grip and minimal fatigue.
21. Mechanical feeding devices should be used where possible.
22. The last consideration should be the design of finger rests, if used.

## 2.9 Work Measurement — Stopwatch Time Study Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The seven-step procedure is essential for KTU Part A questions. Memorise the sequence.

1. **Define the job** and select the operator.
2. **Record data** — observe and record each element's time (continuous or snap-back).
3. **Rate the operator's performance** against the standard (100 = normal).
4. Convert observed time → **Basic Time (BT)** using the rating.
5. Add **Relaxation / Rest Allowances (RA)** → **Standard Time (ST)**.
6. **Compile** the standard data.
7. **Issue** the time standard.

## 2.10 Time Study Formulas (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!CAUTION]
> The order of calculation is *strict*. Marks are lost when students confuse Normal Time with Standard Time.

| Step | Term | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Observed Time (OT)** | Direct reading of stopwatch | R = rating factor |
| 2 | **Basic Time / Normal Time (NT)** | $NT = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100}$ | Adjusts OT to a 100-rated worker |
| 3 | **Allowance Factor (AF)** | $AF = 1 + RA$ where $RA$ is decimal | Includes R, C, P, S, etc. |
| 4 | **Standard Time (ST)** | $ST = NT \times AF$ | Final time allowed per cycle |

### 2.10.1 Performance Rating Methods

- **Speed Rating** — speed of movement only.
- **Effort Rating** — effort applied (Ilgen, Westinghouse).
- **Pace Rating** — pace × effort.
- **Westinghouse Method** — Skill × Effort × Conditions × Consistency (each on a 4-point scale with ±values).
- **Synthetic Rating** — derived from PMTS.

### 2.10.2 Allowance Categories (Standard ILO)

| Code | Allowance | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **RA** | Rest / Personal | 10 – 12 % |
| **CA** | Contingency | 5 – 10 % |
| **PA** | Process | Variable |
| **SA** | Special | Variable |

The total **Relaxation Allowance** is split into:

- **Personal Needs (P)** — 5 %
- **Basic Rest (B)** — 4 % (for light, sedentary work)
- **Variable Rest (V)** — depends on posture, lifting, monotony, etc.

$$
RA = P + B + V
$$

## 2.11 Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)

PMTS uses published time tables for basic human motions. The job is broken down into a sequence of standard motions, each with a fixed time (in TMU, centi-minutes, or seconds).

| System | Full Name | Time Unit | Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MTM-1** | Methods-Time Measurement | 1 TMU = 0.00001 hr = 0.036 s | Basic |
| **MTM-2** | MTM simplified | Coarser codes | High-level |
| **MOST** | Maynard Operation Sequence Technique | BasicMOST uses *Sequence Models* | High-level |
| **Work-Factor** | — | WFS units | Basic |
| **GPDC** | Gilbreth's Predetermined Cycle | — | Basic |

### 2.11.1 Conversion Constants (MTM)

$$
1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ hour} = 0.0006 \text{ minute} = 0.036 \text{ second}
$$

$$
1 \text{ hour} = 100{,}000 \text{ TMU}
$$

$$
1 \text{ minute} = 1{,}667 \text{ TMU}
$$

$$
1 \text{ second} = 27.8 \text{ TMU}
$$
