## 2.12 Work Sampling

A statistical technique for determining the proportion of time spent by workers, machines, etc., on various activities by taking a **large number of instantaneous, random observations**.

### 2.12.1 Sample Size Formula

$$
N = \frac{4 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{s^2}
$$

Where:
- **N** = Number of observations required
- **p** = Estimated proportion of activity (decimal)
- **s** = Required precision (absolute, decimal). At 95.45 % confidence, the constant is **4**.

> [!NOTE]
> For a wider CI (e.g., 90 %), the constant drops; for 99.7 %, it rises to **9**. **s** is typically chosen as **± 0.05** (5 %).

### 2.12.2 Confidence Interval (CI)

$$
CI = p \pm \frac{2\sqrt{p(1-p)/N}}{p} \times 100 \;\%
$$

Or expressed as absolute precision:

$$
\text{Precision} = 2\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}
$$

## 2.13 Standard Data & Formula Construction

**Standard Data** are times for elements of work that have been established by extensive time study, PMTS, or work sampling, and stored for reuse. They eliminate the need to re-study similar elements.

**Formula Construction** is the most refined form: the standard time is expressed as an algebraic function of one or more variables (e.g., diameter, weight, length).

General form:

$$
T = a + b \cdot x^n
$$

Where $T$ is standard time, $x$ is the variable (size/weight), and $a$, $b$, $n$ are constants derived from regression on prior data.

## 2.14 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive industry:** Assembly-line balancing using Two-Handed Charts and String Diagrams.
- **CNC machine shops:** Multiple Activity Charts for operator-to-machine ratio optimisation.
- **Warehousing:** String Diagrams for order-picker routing.
- **Hospitals:** Work Sampling to determine nurse-time distribution.
- **Garment industry:** MTM and BasicMOST for sewing operations.
- **Construction:** Standard Data for repetitive tasks (concrete pouring, bricklaying).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation — Number of Machines per Operator

**Objective:** Derive the optimal number of machines one operator can tend, given the cycle time.

Let:
- $T_m$ = Machine cycle time (time machine runs unattended per cycle)
- $T_h$ = Operator's hand time (load + unload + tool change + reposition) per cycle
- $N$ = Number of machines tended by one operator

**Step 1:** During one full machine cycle, the operator must complete all $N$ hand-time operations.

$$
\text{Time required for one operator} = N \times T_h
$$

**Step 2:** This time must fit within the machine cycle time:

$$
N \times T_h \leq T_m
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the maximum integer $N$:

$$
N_{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_m}{T_h} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4:** Machine utilisation:

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T_m}{N \times T_h} \quad \text{(if N chosen by formula above)}
$$

But the actual utilisation when operator services $N$ machines:

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T_m}{T_m + T_h \cdot \text{slack}}
$$

For exact balance ($N T_h = T_m$):

$$
\eta_m = 1.0 \quad \text{(100 \% machine utilisation)}
$$

**Worked Example 1:**

> A CNC lathe takes 18 minutes for one machining cycle ($T_m$). The operator requires 6 minutes to load, unload, and reset per machine ($T_h$). 
> (a) How many machines can one operator tend?
> (b) What is the machine utilisation?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the ratio:

$$
\frac{T_m}{T_h} = \frac{18}{6} = 3
$$

Step 2 — Operator can tend $N = 3$ machines exactly.

Step 3 — Machine utilisation:

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T_m}{N \cdot T_h} = \frac{18}{3 \times 6} = \frac{18}{18} = 1.00 = 100\%
$$

If $T_m = 20$ min, $T_h = 6$ min: $N = \lfloor 20/6 \rfloor = 3$ machines, with slack $= 20 - 18 = 2$ min in the longest cycle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Standard Time with Rating & Allowances

**Worked Example 2 (Full Time Study Calculation):**

> A worker is observed on a stopwatch time study. Element-wise observed times and rating factors are:
> 
> | Element | Observed Time (min) | Rating (%) |
> |---|---|---|
> | A — Pick up blank | 0.20 | 90 |
> | B — Insert in chuck | 0.30 | 95 |
> | C — Tighten chuck | 0.15 | 110 |
> | D — Start machine | 0.10 | 100 |
> | E — Inspect part | 0.45 | 85 |
> 
> The job has a relaxation allowance of **15 %** and a contingency allowance of **5 %**.
> 
> Compute: (a) Normal Time, (b) Standard Time.

**Step 1 — Compute Normal Time for each element:**

$$
NT_i = OT_i \times \frac{R_i}{100}
$$

$$
NT_A = 0.20 \times \frac{90}{100} = 0.180 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_B = 0.30 \times \frac{95}{100} = 0.285 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_C = 0.15 \times \frac{110}{100} = 0.165 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_D = 0.10 \times \frac{100}{100} = 0.100 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_E = 0.45 \times \frac{85}{100} = 0.3825 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Normal Times:**

$$
NT = \sum NT_i = 0.180 + 0.285 + 0.165 + 0.100 + 0.3825
$$

$$
NT = 1.1125 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total allowance factor:**

$$
RA_{\text{total}} = 0.15 + 0.05 = 0.20
$$

$$
AF = 1 + RA_{\text{total}} = 1.20
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Standard Time:**

$$
ST = NT \times AF = 1.1125 \times 1.20 = 1.335 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Pieces per 8-hour shift:**

$$
\text{Pieces} = \frac{480 \text{ min}}{1.335 \text{ min/pc}} \approx 359 \text{ pieces/day}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Work Sampling Sample Size

**Worked Example 3:**

> A foreman estimates that approximately 30 % of a worker's time is spent on setup activities. If a precision of **±5 %** is required at 95.45 % confidence, how many random observations are needed?

**Step 1 — Use the sample-size formula with the 4 constant:**

$$
N = \frac{4 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{s^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $p = 0.30$ and $s = 0.05$:**

$$
N = \frac{4 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.05)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator and denominator:**

$$
N = \frac{4 \times 0.21}{0.0025} = \frac{0.84}{0.0025}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
N = 336 \text{ observations}
$$

**Step 5 — If the team wants ±3 % precision instead:**

$$
N = \frac{4 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.03)^2} = \frac{0.84}{0.0009} \approx 934 \text{ observations}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Doubling the precision roughly *quadruples* the sample size. This is a common KTU question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — PMTS (MTM) Time Conversion

**Worked Example 4:**

> An MTM analysis yields 1,250 TMU for an assembly operation. Convert this to:
> (a) seconds
> (b) minutes
> (c) hours

**Step 1 — Convert to seconds** (1 TMU = 0.036 s):

$$
T = 1250 \times 0.036 = 45 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to minutes:**

$$
T = 45 \div 60 = 0.75 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours:**

$$
T = 0.75 \div 60 = 0.0125 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using direct TMU → hour conversion:**

$$
T = 1250 \times 0.00001 = 0.0125 \text{ hours} \;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Standard Time from a Standard Data Formula

**Worked Example 5:**

> A standard data study on a turning operation gives the formula:
> $$T = 2.50 + 0.08 D + 0.005 D^2 \;\;\; \text{(minutes)}$$
> where $D$ is the diameter of the workpiece in mm. Calculate the standard time for $D = 50$ mm. Add a 12 % relaxation allowance.

**Step 1 — Substitute D = 50:**

$$
T_{\text{basic}} = 2.50 + 0.08(50) + 0.005(50)^2
$$

$$
T_{\text{basic}} = 2.50 + 4.00 + 12.50 = 19.00 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply allowance factor (AF = 1.12):**

$$
T_{\text{standard}} = 19.00 \times 1.12 = 21.28 \text{ min/piece}
$$

**Step 3 — Pieces per 8-hour shift:**

$$
\text{Pieces} = \frac{480}{21.28} \approx 22.5 \;\rightarrow\; 22 \text{ pieces/day}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Master Framework of Work Study (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Work Study] --> nodeB[Method Study]
    nodeA[Work Study] --> nodeC[Work Measurement]
    
    nodeB[Method Study] --> nodeB1[Step 1: Select]
    nodeB[Method Study] --> nodeB2[Step 2: Record]
    nodeB[Method Study] --> nodeB3[Step 3: Examine]
    nodeB[Method Study] --> nodeB4[Step 4: Develop]
    nodeB[Method Study] --> nodeB5[Step 5: Install and Maintain]
    
    nodeB2[Step 2: Record] --> nodeB2A[Process Charts]
    nodeB2[Step 2: Record] --> nodeB2B[String Diagram]
    nodeB2[Step 2: Record] --> nodeB2C[Multiple Activity Chart]
    nodeB2[Step 2: Record] --> nodeB2D[Therbligs and Micro Motion]
    
    nodeC[Work Measurement] --> nodeC1[Stopwatch Time Study]
    nodeC[Work Measurement] --> nodeC2[PMTS: MTM and MOST]
    nodeC[Work Measurement] --> nodeC3[Work Sampling]
    nodeC[Work Measurement] --> nodeC4[Analytical Estimating]
    nodeC[Work Measurement] --> nodeC5[Standard Data and Formulas]
    
    nodeB4[Step 4: Develop] --> nodeX[Improved Method]
    nodeC1[Stopwatch Time Study] --> nodeY[Standard Time]
    nodeC2[PMTS: MTM and MOST] --> nodeY
    nodeC3[Work Sampling] --> nodeY
    nodeC4[Analytical Estimating] --> nodeY
    nodeC5[Standard Data and Formulas] --> nodeY
    
    nodeX[Improved Method] --> nodeZ[Productivity Improvement]
    nodeY[Standard Time] --> nodeZ[Productivity Improvement]
```

## 4.2 Time Study Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observed Time OT] --> B[Apply Rating R]
    B --> C[Normal Time NT]
    C --> D[Apply Allowances RA]
    D --> E[Standard Time ST]
    E --> F[Daily Output]
    E --> G[Incentive Plan]
    E --> H[Cost Estimation]
```

## 4.3 Multiple Activity Chart — Conceptual Layout (Man-Machine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TIMELINE[Time Axis]
        T0[T0] --> T1[T1] --> T2[T2] --> T3[T3] --> T4[T4]
    end
    
    subgraph OPERATOR[Operator Lane]
        O1[Load Job] --> O2[Idle During Cut] --> O3[Unload Job] --> O4[Inspect]
    end
    
    subgraph MACHINE[Machine Lane]
        M1[Idle] --> M2[Cutting] --> M3[Idle] --> M4[Cutting]
    end
    
    O1[Load Job] -.-> M2[Cutting]
    O3[Unload Job] -.-> M4[Cutting]
```

## 4.4 The 17 Therbligs — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Therbligs: 17 Fundamental Motions] --> B[Effective: Advance the Work]
    A[AB]:::inactive --> C[Ineffective: Waste]
    A --> D[Neutral: Neither]
    
    B --> B1[Grasp]
    B --> B2[Transport Loaded]
    B --> B3[Transport Empty]
    B --> B4[Position]
    B --> B5[Assemble]
    B --> B6[Disassemble]
    B --> B7[Use]
    B --> B8[Release Load]
    B --> B9[Hold]
    
    C --> C1[Search]
    C --> C2[Find]
    C --> C3[Select]
    C --> C4[Plan]
    C --> C5[Avoidable Delay]
    
    D --> D1[Inspect]
    D --> D2[Pre-Position]
    D --> D3[Unavoidable Delay]
```

## 4.5 Standard Time Computation — Equation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Time OT] --> B[Multiply by R over 100]
    B --> C[Normal Time NT]
    C --> D[Multiply by 1 plus RA]
    D --> E[Standard Time ST]
    E --> F[ST determines Output per Shift]
    E --> G[ST determines Piece Rate Wage]
```

## 4.6 Productivity Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Method Study: Improve HOW] --> D[Productivity Gain]
    B[Work Measurement: Determine HOW MUCH TIME] --> D
    C[Ergonomics and Worker Welfare] --> D
    D --> E[Reduced Cost, Improved Quality, Higher Morale]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define Method Study. List its five basic steps in sequence.**

**Model Answer:**

> Method Study is the systematic recording, analysis, and critical examination of existing and proposed methods of doing work, with the objective of developing the most efficient and economic method.
>
> **Five Steps (in sequence):**
> 1. **Select** — the work to be studied.
> 2. **Record** — the present method (using charts and diagrams).
> 3. **Examine** — critically, using questioning techniques.
> 4. **Develop** — the most practical, efficient, and economic method.
> 5. **Install and Maintain** — implement and monitor the new method.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q: Define "Normal Time" and "Standard Time" with formulas. Why are both required?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Normal Time (NT):** The time taken by an *average* worker working at a *normal pace* (100 B.S. rating) to perform the specified operation.
> $$NT = OT \times \frac{R}{100}$$
>
> **Standard Time (ST):** The time allowed to a qualified worker to perform the task at a defined performance level, including *personal, rest, and contingency allowances*.
> $$ST = NT \times (1 + RA)$$
>
> **Why both required:** Normal Time removes operator variation, giving a fair work content. Standard Time includes mandatory physiological and contingency allowances, ensuring the worker is *not* overworked and the standard is *realistic* and *sustainable*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a **Two-Handed Process Chart** (Right-Hand / Left-Hand Chart). State its purpose and list any **four** principles of motion economy that can be derived from it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A worker is observed on a stopwatch time study for a four-element job. The data are:
- Element A: OT = 0.40 min, R = 110 %
- Element B: OT = 0.30 min, R = 95 %
- Element C: OT = 0.25 min, R = 100 %
- Element D: OT = 0.50 min, R = 90 %
- Relaxation Allowance = 12 %, Contingency = 5 %

Compute the **Normal Time**, **Standard Time**, and the **number of pieces per 8-hour shift**.

---

#### Solution to (a) — Two-Handed Process Chart

**Definition:** A Two-Handed Process Chart is a time-scaled chart that records the *simultaneous* motions of the right and left hands of an operator performing a repetitive bench task.

**Construction (ASCII Schematic):**

```
Element | LHS Description | LHS Time | RHS Description | RHS Time
-----------------------------------------------------------------
   1    | Reach blank     |  0.10    | Idle             |  ----
   2    | Hold blank      |  0.05    | Reach tool       |  0.10
   3    | Position blank  |  0.08    | Grasp tool       |  0.05
   4    | Insert blank    |  0.12    | Transport tool   |  0.10
   5    | Release blank   |  0.04    | Position tool    |  0.08
   6    | Idle            |  ----    | Operate tool     |  0.25
```

**Purpose:**
- Identify **idle time** in either hand.
- Ensure **simultaneous** use of both hands.
- **Balance** the workload between right and left hands.
- Eliminate **excess reach, search, and select** motions.

**Four Principles of Motion Economy (derived):**
1. Both hands should be active at the same time (Principle 1).
2. Both hands should not be idle simultaneously except for rest (Principle 2).
3. Hand motions should be **symmetric and simultaneous** where possible (Principle 3).
4. **Drop delivery** and pre-positioned tools eliminate search/select (Principles 10, 12).

---

#### Solution to (b) — Stopwatch Time Study

**Step 1 — Normal Time (element-wise):**

$$
NT_A = 0.40 \times \frac{110}{100} = 0.440 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_B = 0.30 \times \frac{95}{100} = 0.285 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_C = 0.25 \times \frac{100}{100} = 0.250 \text{ min}
$$

$$
NT_D = 0.50 \times \frac{90}{100} = 0.450 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Normal Time:**

$$
NT_{\text{total}} = 0.440 + 0.285 + 0.250 + 0.450 = 1.425 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Allowance Factor:**

$$
RA = 0.12 + 0.05 = 0.17
$$

$$
AF = 1 + RA = 1.17
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Time:**

$$
ST = NT \times AF = 1.425 \times 1.17 = 1.66725 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Pieces per 8-hour shift:**

$$
\text{Pieces} = \frac{480 \text{ min}}{1.66725 \text{ min/pc}} \approx 287.9 \approx 288 \text{ pieces/day}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **String Diagram** technique. How is it used to analyse and improve workplace layouts? Illustrate with one engineering example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a machine shop, the machine cycle time $T_m = 24$ min and the operator's hand time per cycle is $T_h = 6$ min. Determine:
- (i) The number of machines one operator can tend.
- (ii) The operator utilisation and machine utilisation.
- (iii) The number of operators required to tend **10 machines**.

---

#### Solution to (a) — String Diagram

**Definition:** A String Diagram is a **scale plan** of the work area on which a string (or thread) is pinned to trace the *path of a worker* (or material) during the work cycle. The string is then measured to determine the total distance travelled, and the diagram is annotated with backtracks, delays, and idle movements.

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare an accurate **scale drawing** of the work area (typically 1:50 or 1:100).
2. Place pins at the start of the worker's path.
3. Stretch a thread (or use pins and coloured tape) along the path as the worker moves.
4. **Measure** the total length of thread to compute distance.
5. **Count** the number of direction reversals, backtracks, and idle segments.
6. Compare **before** and **after** layout designs quantitatively.

**Engineering Example — Workshop of a Lathe Section:**

The layout has 8 lathes arranged in two rows. The operator's daily walk-pattern, traced by a string, reveals a total of **640 m/day** with 12 backtracks. By rearranging the tool crib to the centre of the layout, the new total reduces to **410 m/day** with 3 backtracks — a **35.9 % reduction in travel**.

**Engineering Use:**
- Minimising operator walking distance.
- Identifying bottlenecks caused by **fixed-location equipment** (tool cribs, gauges).
- Validating proposed layout changes (e.g., cellular vs. functional).

---

#### Solution to (b) — Operator-Machine Balancing

**Given:**
- $T_m = 24$ min
- $T_h = 6$ min

**Step 1 — Number of machines per operator:**

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{T_m}{T_h} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{24}{6} \right\rfloor = 4 \text{ machines}
$$

(The operator services 4 machines in 24 min.)

**Step 2 — Operator utilisation (1 operator, 4 machines):**

The operator is busy for $N \times T_h = 4 \times 6 = 24$ min per machine cycle.
The cycle period is $T_m = 24$ min.

$$
\eta_{\text{op}} = \frac{N \cdot T_h}{T_m} = \frac{24}{24} = 1.00 = 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Machine utilisation:**

Each machine runs for $T_m = 24$ min and is unattended for the full 24 min (operator is loading/unloading another). So:

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T_m}{T_m + T_{\text{idle}}} = \frac{24}{24} = 1.00 = 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Operators required for 10 machines:**

$$
\text{Operators} = \frac{10 \text{ machines}}{4 \text{ machines/operator}} = 2.5 \rightarrow 3 \text{ operators}
$$

(Fractional value rounded up because a partial operator cannot be deployed.)

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 1):**
> 1. **Confusing Normal Time with Standard Time.** Normal Time is *before* allowances; Standard Time is *after* allowances. Examiners check whether the rating factor is applied *before* or *after* the allowance factor. Correct order: $OT \rightarrow NT \rightarrow ST$.
> 2. **Wrong number of machines per operator.** Students forget to take the *floor* of $T_m / T_h$. Always round *down* for $N$ to ensure operator can complete all hand-time operations within the cycle.
> 3. **Missing the 5,000-TMU-to-hour conversion** (or the 1 TMU = 0.00001 h constant). This is a favourite KTU numerical.
> 4. **Symbols of Process Charts** must be drawn precisely: ○ (Operation), □ (Inspection), ▷ (Transport), ▽ (Delay), D (Storage). Half-mark is lost for each wrong symbol.
> 5. **In Work Sampling, students confuse precision (s) with confidence level.** The constant 4 corresponds to **95.45 % CI (2σ)**. A 90 % CI uses ~2.7; a 99.7 % CI uses 9.
> 6. **Therblig colour coding and effectiveness** (effective vs. ineffective) is a common 3-mark sub-question. Memorise the table.
> 7. **Allowance factor must include *all* the stated allowances** — students often miss the contingency or process allowance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Work Study = Method Study + Work Measurement.** Both are mandatory for a complete productivity improvement programme.
- The **five-step Method Study** procedure is: **Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Install & Maintain.** Memorise in this exact order.
- **Process Chart symbols:** Operation ○, Inspection □, Transport ▷, Delay ▽, Permanent Storage D. The *Five-Questioning* framework: **Purpose, Place, Sequence, Person, Means.**
- **String Diagram** = scale plan + thread → quantifies travel distance, backtracks, and layout efficiency.
- **Multiple Activity Chart** gives $N = \lfloor T_m / T_h \rfloor$ machines per operator; the *cycle* is the longer of the two.
- **Therbligs** are 17 fundamental motions, identified by Gilbreth (his name reversed). Effective therbligs advance work; ineffective ones (Search, Find, Select, Plan, Avoidable Delay) represent waste.
- **Principles of Motion Economy (22)** are grouped into: *Use of Human Body, Workplace Layout, Tool/Equipment Design.*
- **Stopwatch Time Study formula chain:**
  $$OT \xrightarrow{\times R/100} NT \xrightarrow{\times (1+RA)} ST$$
- **PMTS conversion constants:** 1 TMU = 0.00001 hr = 0.036 s = 27.8 TMU/s; 1 hr = 100,000 TMU.
- **Work Sampling sample size** at 95.45 % CI: $N = 4 p(1-p)/s^2$. The precision is in *decimal* form (5 % = 0.05).
- **Standard Data / Formula** approach: $T = a + bx^n$ — eliminates re-study of similar elements and enables rapid standard-setting.
- **Relaxation Allowance** typically includes Personal (5 %), Basic Rest (4 %), and Variable Rest (posture, lifting, monotony, climate).
- The output of Work Study feeds **incentive payment systems** (Halsey, Rowan, Bedeaux, Taylor Differential Piece-Rate).
- **Always show units** in numerical answers; KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- **Always use rounded-up** values for the *number of operators/machines* (you cannot have 0.5 of an operator).
- **The end-goal of Module 1 techniques** is to convert the *implicit knowledge* of a worker's movements into *explicit, optimisable, standardisable* work content.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Workplace Environment and Workplace Design in Productivity Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Workplace Environment and Workplace Design in Productivity Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Workplace Environment** refers to the sum of all surrounding internal and external conditions — physical, psychological, social, and organizational — within the boundary of a work setting that directly or indirectly influence the operator's physiological comfort, cognitive functioning, and behavioral output.

**Workplace Design** is the systematic planning and arrangement of workstations, equipment, layout, lighting, ventilation, noise-control measures, and human-machine interfaces to optimize the fit between the worker and the work system.

**Productivity** in a work system is formally defined as the ratio of output produced to the input consumed over a defined period.

$$P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic sits at the intersection of **Ergonomics**, **Industrial Engineering**, and **Human Factors Engineering**. The KTU examiner expects you to interlink *environment variables* with *measurable productivity outcomes* and to justify improvements using engineering logic — not just qualitative statements.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

Imagine two identical office spaces rented at the same price. In Office A, the lights flicker, the AC is set to 16 °C, the printer whines in the corner, and chairs are mismatched. In Office B, soft diffused light, a steady 24 °C, sound-absorbing ceiling tiles, and height-adjustable chairs are installed. Ten engineers in Office A deliver 8 units/day; ten in Office B deliver 13 units/day. **Same people. Same machines. Same input. Different output.**

That difference is the *effect of workplace environment and design on productivity*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Workplace design is not a "comfort perk" — it is a productivity investment.** A 1 % improvement in workplace conditions, when scaled across an industry, is worth millions.

## 1.3 Key Classification of Workplace Environment Factors

| Category | Sub-Factors | Productivity Linkage |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Lighting, Noise, Temperature, Humidity, Vibration, Air Quality | Direct effect on fatigue, errors, absenteeism |
| **Psychological** | Job satisfaction, Stress, Motivation, Autonomy | Affects discretionary effort |
| **Ergonomic** | Workstation height, Reach, Posture, Repetition | Prevents Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSD) |
| **Social** | Team cohesion, Communication, Supervision style | Affects collaboration throughput |
| **Organizational** | Work-rest cycles, Shift design, Safety climate | Determines sustained performance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** In Part A questions, examiners often ask to *"List the workplace environment factors"* — reproduce this table's classification to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Productivity Frameworks Used in Work System Design

The KTU syllabus maps productivity at three hierarchical levels:

1. **Labor Productivity (LP)** — measures operator output per unit human effort.
2. **Machine Productivity (MP)** — measures equipment output per unit machine-time.
3. **Total / Multifactor Productivity (TP / MFP)** — measures combined conversion efficiency of all inputs.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Tier

- **Why LP first?** Because human effort is the most variable and most easily influenced by workplace design.
- **Why MP next?** Because machines are the second-most expensive resource and their uptime is governed by ergonomic loading and environmental stress (e.g., heat degrades bearing life).
- **Why MFP at the top?** Because a workstation's true *system efficiency* is only visible when labor, machine, material, and energy are jointly analyzed.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Productivity | $P = O / I$ | $O$ = Output (units), $I$ = Input | Single-factor analysis |
| 2 | Labor Productivity | $LP = \frac{O}{L \cdot h}$ | $L$ = workers, $h$ = hours | Manual / assembly work |
| 3 | Machine Productivity | $MP = \frac{O}{M \cdot t}$ | $M$ = machines, $t$ = hours | CNC / process industries |
| 4 | Multifactor Productivity | $MFP = \frac{O}{L \cdot C \cdot M \cdot E}$ | $C$ = capital, $M$ = material, $E$ = energy | System-level benchmarking |
| 5 | Productivity Index | $PI = \frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{standard}}} \times 100$ | Percentage | KTU 14-mark problems |
| 6 | Productivity Improvement % | $\Delta P\% = \frac{P_{\text{new}} - P_{\text{old}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Before-After comparison |
| 7 | Effective Working Time | $EWT = AT \cdot (1 - ID)$ | $AT$ = Available Time, $ID$ = Idle % | Environmental downtime |
| 8 | Absenteeism-adjusted LP | $LP_{\text{adj}} = \frac{O}{L \cdot h \cdot (1 - A)}$ | $A$ = Absenteeism rate | Sick-building effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Examiners deduct marks if you use the *wrong input base*. Always state the input unit clearly (e.g., *man-hours* ≠ *man-days* ≠ *number of workers*).

## 2.3 Effect Mechanism — How Each Environment Variable Hits Productivity

### 2.3.1 Lighting
- **Below 300 lux** → eye strain → errors rise by **8–15 %** (CIE / ISO 8995 standards).
- **Glare** → pupil oscillation → cognitive load ↑ → output ↓.
- **Optimal range for precision work: 750–1500 lux** (ISO 8995:2002).

### 2.3.2 Noise
- **Above 85 dB(A)** → permanent hearing risk, cognitive performance degrades.
- For every **+10 dB** above the comfort zone (~50 dB), task accuracy drops by **5–10 %** in mental arithmetic tasks.

### 2.3.3 Temperature & Humidity
- **Optimal comfort band: 20–24 °C** with **40–60 % RH** (ASHRAE Standard 55).
- Outside this band, productivity drops roughly **2 % per °C** deviation for cognitive work.

### 2.3.4 Vibration
- Whole-Body Vibration (WBV) in vehicle cabins → driver fatigue → throughput ↓.
- Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV) → tool-handling accuracy ↓.

### 2.3.5 Air Quality (IAQ)
- CO₂ above 1000 ppm → drowsiness, poor concentration.
- **Sick Building Syndrome (SBS)** raises absenteeism by 10–30 % in poorly ventilated offices.

## 2.4 Workplace Design Elements Affecting Productivity

1. **Anthropometric Workstation Design** — adjusting height, reach, and visual angles to the operator.
2. **Layout Optimization** — minimizing motion, transport, and waiting (from Method Study principles).
3. **Tools & Jigs Design** — reducing Cycle Time $T_c$.
4. **Work-Rest Cycles** — preventing fatigue accumulation.
5. **Lighting Layout** — task-ambient balance.
6. **Acoustic Treatment** — sound-absorbing panels, isolation.
7. **Climate Control** — HVAC zoning.
8. **Color & Visual Cues** — guiding attention, reducing errors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Ergonomics is a *productive* discipline, not a *soft* discipline. A well-designed workstation can reduce cycle time by **10–25 %** in assembly lines (NIOSH & Boeing field studies).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linking Environmental Index to Productivity

Let us derive a **Composite Environmental Quality Index (CEQI)** that quantifies how workplace conditions feed into productivity.

**Step 1 — Define individual factor ratings.**

Each factor $f_i$ (lighting, noise, temperature, IAQ) is rated on a normalized scale $[0, 1]$ where 1 = ideal and 0 = worst case.

**Step 2 — Assign weight $w_i$** based on its relative impact on productivity.

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted geometric mean (more realistic than arithmetic mean for multiplicative effects):**

$$
CEQI = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_{i}^{w_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Model productivity response as a function of CEQI:**

$$
P_{\text{effective}} = P_{\text{max}} \cdot \left(1 - k \cdot (1 - CEQI)\right)
$$

Where $k$ is the sensitivity coefficient ($0 < k \le 1$) that captures how strongly the system responds to environmental degradation. A typical $k$ value for assembly work is **0.4 to 0.6**.

**Step 5 — Substitute.** The derived effective productivity is the productivity actually realized under sub-optimal conditions, benchmarked against the maximum achievable under ideal conditions.

**Conversion Logic:** The product-of-powers form rewards balanced excellence. A single zero in any factor collapses the entire index, reflecting the *weakest-link* nature of human comfort.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Productivity Improvement After Ergonomic Intervention

Consider a workstation producing $O_1$ units in time $T$ before redesign and $O_2$ units in the same time $T$ after redesign.

**Step 1 — Before-Intervention Productivity**

$$
P_1 = \frac{O_1}{L \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2 — After-Intervention Productivity**

$$
P_2 = \frac{O_2}{L \cdot T}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Improvement**

$$
\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = \frac{O_2 - O_1}{L \cdot T}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Improvement (KTU's favorite metric)**

$$
\Delta P\% = \frac{O_2 - O_1}{O_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Including Restructured Cycle Time $T_c$** (e.g., new jig reduces assembly motion by $\Delta m$ seconds):

$$
T_{c,2} = T_{c,1} - \Delta m
$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{1}{T_{c,2}} \quad \text{(units/second for one operator)}
$$

**Step 6 — Final % Improvement in Productivity**

$$
\Delta P\% = \left( \frac{T_{c,1}}{T_{c,2}} - 1 \right) \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Notice that a 10 % reduction in cycle time yields ~11.1 % improvement in productivity, not exactly 10 %. The relationship is *inverse* and *non-linear*. Examiners test this trap regularly.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Sample Pattern):** A workstation has 6 operators working 8 hours. They produce 480 units/day. After an ergonomic redesign, the same 6 operators produce 552 units/day in 8 hours.

**Step 1 — Calculate original productivity:**

$$
P_1 = \frac{480}{6 \times 8} = 10 \;\; \text{units/man-hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate new productivity:**

$$
P_2 = \frac{552}{6 \times 8} = 11.5 \;\; \text{units/man-hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute change:**

$$
\Delta P = 11.5 - 10 = 1.5 \;\; \text{units/man-hour}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage improvement:**

$$
\Delta P\% = \frac{1.5}{10} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

**Step 5 — Annual value (assume 300 working days, unit price ₹50):**

$$
\text{Extra output/year} = 1.5 \times 6 \times 8 \times 300 = 21{,}600 \;\; \text{units}
$$

$$
\text{Revenue gain} = 21{,}600 \times 50 = \text{₹}\,10{,}80{,}000
$$

**Conclusion:** A workplace redesign delivered ₹10.8 lakh of extra annual revenue — *without* hiring or buying machines.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Factor vs. Impact

| Workplace Factor | Sub-optimal Symptom | Estimated Productivity Loss | Mitigation Cost vs. Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Poor Lighting | Eye strain, errors | 8–15 % | Low cost, high ROI |
| High Noise (>85 dB) | Hearing loss, communication errors | 5–20 % | Moderate cost, high ROI |
| Bad Ergonomics | MSD, absenteeism | 15–25 % | High upfront, long-term high ROI |
| Poor IAQ / Ventilation | Drowsiness, sick leave | 10–30 % | Moderate cost, high ROI |
| Inefficient Layout | Excess motion, transport | 10–40 % | High cost, very high ROI |
| Monotonous Work | Boredom, low motivation | 5–15 % | Low cost (job rotation) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Environment → Productivity

The following Mermaid diagram maps the causal chain from workplace design inputs to productivity outcomes. Each block is a functional component; arrows indicate causal influence direction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workplace Design Inputs] --> B[Physical Environment]
    A --> C[Ergonomic Setup]
    A --> D[Layout and Flow]
    B --> E[Operator Comfort]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Reduced Fatigue]
    E --> G[Lower Error Rate]
    E --> H[Better Morale]
    F --> I[Higher Output per Hour]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Improved Productivity Index]
    J --> K[Lower Unit Cost]
    J --> L[Higher Quality Yield]
    K --> M[Organizational Profitability]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Productivity Calculation Hierarchy

This diagram represents the standard KTU productivity pyramid, moving from single-factor to multifactor analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Labor Productivity] --> S2[Machine Productivity]
    S2 --> S3[Material Productivity]
    S3 --> S4[Energy Productivity]
    S4 --> S5[Multifactor Productivity]
    S5 --> S6[Total Factor Productivity]
    S6 --> S7[System Level Profitability]
    style S1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style S7 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Ergonomic Intervention Decision Flow

This flow illustrates the structured sequence an Industrial Engineer should follow when proposing a workplace design change.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Identify Productivity Gap] --> Q2[Measure Baseline Performance]
    Q2 --> Q3[Analyze Work Environment Factors]
    Q3 --> Q4{Is the gap due to environment or method?}
    Q4 -->|Environment| Q5[Apply Ergonomic Redesign]
    Q4 -->|Method| Q6[Apply Method Study]
    Q5 --> Q7[Re-Measure Performance]
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q7 --> Q8{Is target achieved?}
    Q8 -->|Yes| Q9[Standardize and Document]
    Q8 -->|No| Q3
    style Q9 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
    style Q4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** Examiners often reward students who draw such causal flow diagrams in 14-mark answers. A single well-labeled block diagram can fetch 1–2 extra marks in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define productivity. List the various types of productivity measures used in a work system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Productivity is defined as the ratio of output produced to the input consumed in a given process.

$$
P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}
$$

Types of productivity measures used in work system design:
1. **Labor Productivity** — output per man-hour.
2. **Machine Productivity** — output per machine-hour.
3. **Material Productivity** — output per kg of material.
4. **Energy Productivity** — output per kWh.
5. **Multifactor Productivity (MFP)** — output per combined input bundle.
6. **Total Factor Productivity (TFP)** — output normalized by all factor inputs including technology.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is "Sick Building Syndrome"? How does it affect productivity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Sick Building Syndrome (SBS)** is a condition in which occupants of a building experience acute health and comfort effects — headaches, eye irritation, fatigue, respiratory issues — that appear linked to time spent inside the building, without any specific illness or cause being identified.

**Effect on productivity:**
- Absenteeism rises by **10–30 %** due to recurring symptoms.
- Concentration and cognitive throughput drop due to elevated CO₂ levels (often > 1000 ppm).
- Studies (WHO, 1984) estimate a **6–10 % productivity loss** in poorly ventilated offices.

SBS is mitigated by adequate fresh-air ventilation (ASHRAE 62.1 standard: **8–10 L/s per person**), regular filter changes, and humidity control between **40–60 % RH**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the various **physical factors of the workplace environment** that influence productivity. How do lighting, noise, temperature, and air quality individually impact worker output?

**(b) [7 marks]** A production line employs 12 workers on an 8-hour shift producing 960 units/day. After redesigning the workstation for better ergonomics and lighting, the output rises to 1104 units/day with the same workforce and shift length. Calculate the **percentage improvement in productivity** and discuss at least **three ergonomic interventions** that may have contributed to this gain.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

The physical workplace environment encompasses four major factors that directly govern worker physiological state and therefore productivity:

**1. Lighting (2 marks)**
- Insufficient lighting causes **eye strain, fatigue, errors, and accidents**.
- Recommended illumination per **ISO 8995:2002**:
  - General office: **500 lux**
  - Precision assembly: **1000–1500 lux**
  - Detailed inspection: **2000+ lux**
- Sub-optimal lighting reduces output by **8–15 %** and raises defect rates.

**2. Noise (2 marks)**
- Noise above **85 dB(A)** damages hearing (OSHA, Factories Act 1948).
- Cognitive tasks are affected at noise levels above **55 dB(A)**.
- For every **+10 dB** above the comfort band, mental arithmetic accuracy falls by **5–10 %**.
- High noise also increases **accident risk** due to missed verbal warnings.

**3. Temperature and Humidity (1.5 marks)**
- ASHRAE Standard 55 defines the **comfort band** as **20–24 °C** with **40–60 % RH**.
- Outside this band, productivity drops by **~2 % per °C** for cognitive work.
- Heat stress causes dehydration, fatigue; cold causes stiffness, reduced dexterity.

**4. Air Quality (Indoor Air Quality, IAQ) (1.5 marks)**
- CO₂ > 1000 ppm → drowsiness, headache, poor concentration.
- Particulates and VOCs (from paints, adhesives) → respiratory illness.
- **Sick Building Syndrome** can cause **10–30 % absenteeism** in offices with poor IAQ.

**Conclusion:** Together, these four factors can swing productivity by up to **20–30 %** in the same workforce, validating the engineering importance of workplace environment design.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute original productivity (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_1 = \frac{960}{12 \times 8} = 10 \;\; \text{units/man-hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute new productivity (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_2 = \frac{1104}{12 \times 8} = 11.5 \;\; \text{units/man-hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage improvement (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta P\% = \frac{11.5 - 10}{10} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

**Step 4 — Ergonomic interventions that may have contributed (2 marks):**

1. **Height-adjustable workbenches** — eliminated stooping and over-reaching, reducing cycle time per operation.
2. **Improved task lighting (1000 lux + glare-free diffusers)** — reduced visual fatigue, lowering error and rework rates.
3. **Anti-fatigue matting + footrests** — reduced musculoskeletal strain during standing operations, allowing longer sustained effort.
4. (Bonus) **Tool positioning within normal reach zone** — eliminated wasted motion per the *Principles of Motion Economy* (Maynard's industrial engineering handbook).

**Conclusion:** A **15 % productivity gain** with no additional labor, capital, or materials is achievable purely through workplace environment and ergonomic redesign — making this one of the highest-ROI interventions in industrial engineering.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a suitable **framework or block diagram**, explain how workplace design influences productivity. Discuss the role of *workstation ergonomics* and *layout optimization*.

**(b) [7 marks]** A workstation operator previously took **120 seconds** to complete one assembly cycle. After applying *motion economy principles* and rearranging the workplace, the cycle time dropped to **96 seconds**. The operator works 8 hours/day, 25 days/month, and the value added per assembly is ₹80. Calculate: (i) the new productivity in **assemblies/hour**, (ii) the **% improvement in productivity**, and (iii) the **monthly revenue gain** attributable to the redesign.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Framework — Workplace Design to Productivity Chain (3 marks):**

The influence chain is:
**Design Inputs → Physical Layout & Ergonomics → Operator Comfort & Safety → Reduced Fatigue & Errors → Higher Throughput → Improved Productivity → Lower Cost & Higher Quality**

A block diagram representation (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flow) shows causal arrows flowing from each design element to the productivity endpoint.

**Role of Workstation Ergonomics (2 marks):**
- Applying *anthropometric data* ensures the workstation fits the 5th–95th percentile operator.
- Reduces **work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WMSDs)**.
- Lowers *energy expenditure* per cycle → higher sustainable pace.
- Aligns the operator's **sight lines, reach envelopes, and force-application angles** with biomechanically safe postures.

**Role of Layout Optimization (2 marks):**
- Minimizes **transport, motion, and waiting** (the 3 wastes of Method Study).
- Co-locates **tools, jigs, and materials** within the *primary work zone*.
- Implements **U-shaped, straight-line, or cellular layouts** depending on production volume.
- Reduces non-value-added time, increasing the *value-added ratio* per shift.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- $T_{c,1} = 120$ seconds, $T_{c,2} = 96$ seconds
- Shift = 8 h, Working days = 25/month
- Value added = ₹80/assembly

**Step 1 — Original productivity in assemblies/hour (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_1 = \frac{3600}{120} = 30 \;\; \text{assemblies/hour}
$$

**Step 2 — New productivity in assemblies/hour (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_2 = \frac{3600}{96} = 37.5 \;\; \text{assemblies/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage improvement (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta P\% = \left( \frac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1} \right) \times 100 = \left( \frac{37.5 - 30}{30} \right) \times 100 = 25\%
$$

**Step 4 — Monthly output before and after (1 mark):**

$$
O_1 = 30 \times 8 \times 25 = 6000 \;\; \text{assemblies/month}
$$

$$
O_2 = 37.5 \times 8 \times 25 = 7500 \;\; \text{assemblies/month}
$$

**Step 5 — Monthly revenue gain (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Gain} = (7500 - 6000) \times 80 = \text{₹}\,1{,}20{,}000/\text{month}
$$

**Conclusion:** A modest 20 % reduction in cycle time translates into a **25 % productivity improvement** and **₹1.2 lakh additional monthly revenue** — a classic KTU-style payoff demonstration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Cycle-time vs. productivity trap** — many students wrongly assume a 20 % cycle-time reduction = 20 % productivity gain. It actually equals a **25 %** productivity gain (inverse relationship). Lose 1 mark if you miss this.
> 2. **Unit confusion** — always write *units/man-hour* or *assemblies/hour*. Bare numbers fetch no credit.
> 3. **No diagram, no relief** — for the 7-mark framework question, a block diagram is **mandatory**; not drawing one typically costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **The "Sick Building Syndrome" trap** — examiners often pose this as a distractor in lighting/IAQ questions. Memorize the **10–30 % absenteeism** figure.
> 5. **In Part A**, write the *formula* AND the *interpretation*. Writing only the formula is half-marked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Productivity** = Output / Input; always specify the input base.
- **Hierarchy of productivity measures** — Labor → Machine → Material → Energy → Multifactor → Total Factor.
- **Five physical factors** governing workplace productivity — lighting, noise, temperature, humidity, and air quality.
- **Comfort band** — 20–24 °C, 40–60 % RH, 500 lux (office) / 1000+ lux (precision work), < 55 dB(A) for cognitive work.
- **Sick Building Syndrome** is a productivity killer responsible for **10–30 % absenteeism**.
- **Ergonomic redesign** can yield **10–25 %** productivity gains without additional resources.
- **Cycle-time vs. productivity** has an *inverse* relationship — a $x\%$ reduction in cycle time gives a $\left(\frac{x}{100-x}\right) \times 100 \%$ productivity increase.
- **Anthropometry** (5th–95th percentile design) is the foundation of inclusive workplace design.
- **Motion economy principles** (Maynard) — minimize travel, combine operations, use jigs/fixtures.
- **Productivity Index** = $\frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{standard}}} \times 100$ — a key benchmarking metric for KTU 14-mark problems.
- **KPIs to remember** — 85 dB(A) OSHA limit, 1000 ppm CO₂ threshold, 500/1000/2000 lux tiered lighting, 20–24 °C thermal comfort.
- **Conclusion of the topic** — Workplace environment and design are not HR comforts; they are *engineering levers* for productivity and profitability, and KTU expects you to treat them as such — quantitatively.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and Objectives of Method Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition and Objectives of Method Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **METHOD STUDY (ILO Definition)**
> *Method Study is the systematic recording and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work, as a means of developing and applying easier and more effective methods and reducing costs.*

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** course *Work System Design (PCIET403)*, Method Study is positioned as the **foundational technique of Work Study** that deals exclusively with the **"HOW"** of doing work. It is concerned with the **reduction of work content** in any process by means of systematic investigation, logical reasoning, and structured analysis of every operation, movement, and delay.

Method Study is also interchangeably referred to as:
- **Methods Engineering** (North American terminology)
- **Work Method Design**
- **Process Analysis** (in manufacturing contexts)

The scope of Method Study covers every type of human, mechanical, or chemical activity occurring in an organization — from manufacturing a product on a shop floor to processing a customer loan in a bank.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The GPS for Work"**
> Imagine you are driving from your home in **Thrissur** to **KTU Campus, CET** in Thiruvananthapuram for your exam. A GPS app does **not invent a new destination**; it keeps the **destination fixed** but explores *every possible route*, compares them on **time, distance, fuel, and traffic**, and recommends the **most efficient path**.
>
> Method Study works **exactly like a GPS for work**:
> - **Destination** = The output/product (unchanged)
> - **Routes** = The various ways the work can be done
> - **GPS Algorithm** = The critical examination of present and proposed methods
> - **Efficiency Gain** = Reduced work content, lower cost, less fatigue

A second helpful analogy is a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. The doctor does not prescribe medicine blindly; they first *examine* (record facts), then *diagnose* (critical examination), then *recommend treatment* (develop new method), and finally *follow up* (maintain the new method).

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Core Metrics

The following standard productivity/efficiency metrics are **bold-highlighted** for KTU board examinations:

| Metric | Symbol | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Time** | $T_s$ | Total time allowed to perform a task under standard conditions |
| **Normal Time** | $T_n$ | Time taken by a qualified worker at normal pace |
| **Rating Factor** | $R$ | Worker speed relative to standard performance |
| **Work Content** | $W_c$ | Total amount of work required to complete a job |
| **Productivity** | $P$ | Output per unit input (labour/hour) |
| **Method Efficiency** | $\eta_m$ | Ratio of effective work time to total elapsed time |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Method Study focuses on reducing **$W_c$ (Work Content)**, while Time Study (covered later in Module 2) focuses on setting the **$T_s$ (Standard Time)** for whatever work content remains. Always remember this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Method Study? — The Core Rationale

Every organization, knowingly or unknowingly, accumulates **inefficiencies** over time. These inefficiencies arise from outdated procedures, poor layouts, redundant motions, or simply because *"this is how we have always done it."* Method Study provides a **structured, scientific, and replicable framework** to:

- **Question the status quo** of every operational step.
- **Quantify the gap** between current performance and theoretical ideal.
- **Engage workers** in continuous improvement.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Method Study

The fundamental logic of Method Study rests on three engineering pillars:

1. **Productivity Pillar** — Reduce non-value-adding activities so that output per labour-hour increases.
2. **Human Pillar** — Reduce physical and mental fatigue, improve safety, and elevate the dignity of labour.
3. **Economic Pillar** — Lower the unit cost of production/service without compromising quality.

### 2.3 Objectives of Method Study (ILO — KTU Board Favourite)

The **International Labour Organization (ILO)** outlines the following primary objectives, which are reproduced verbatim in the **KTU 2024 syllabus** for Module 2. Examiners expect students to list **6–8 of these** in any 7-mark or 14-mark question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ILO Objectives of Method Study**
> 1. **To improve existing work methods and procedures.**
> 2. **To develop new and improved methods** that are safer, easier, and more economical.
> 3. **To reduce unnecessary work content** — eliminate idle time, redundant motion, and waste.
> 4. **To achieve the most effective use of available resources** — manpower, materials, machines, and space.
> 5. **To improve the layout of the workplace** for better material flow and reduced handling.
> 6. **To reduce fatigue** by improving body mechanics, posture, and tool design.
> 7. **To improve safety** and reduce the risk of accidents and occupational illnesses.
> 8. **To improve the working environment** — lighting, ventilation, noise, and ergonomics.
> 9. **To reduce costs** and improve overall productivity and quality.
> 10. **To standardize** operations, methods, and working conditions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although Method Study is largely a **qualitative-analytical technique**, it interacts with several quantitative formulas used across Work Study. The table below is the **complete KTU high-yield formula bank** for this topic.

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Safeguard:** The vertical pipe `\vert` (not the raw `\vert` character) is used below to prevent markdown-table parser breakage. Do not use the raw pipe symbol `\vert` inside table cells.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input (Labour-Hours)}}$ | Basic Productivity | Output in units; Input in man-hours | Measures pre/post-Method Study gain |
| 2 | $\Delta P \,(\%) = \dfrac{P_{\text{new}} - P_{\text{old}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | % Productivity Increase | Dimensionless (%) | Exam problems on cost-saving |
| 3 | $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$ | Standard Time (with Allowance) | $T_s$, $T_n$ in min; $A$ = Allowance fraction | Module-2 combined problems |
| 4 | $T_n = T_o \times R$ | Normal Time | $T_o$ = Observed time; $R$ = Rating | Method + Time study integration |
| 5 | $W_c = T_s \times N$ | Work Content (batch) | $N$ = number of units | Used in cost reduction proofs |
| 6 | $C_u = \dfrac{T_s \times W_r}{N}$ | Unit Labour Cost | $W_r$ = wage rate ₹/min; $N$ = units | Justification for new method |
| 7 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{\text{effective}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Method Efficiency % | Time in min | Evaluates proposed vs. existing |
| 8 | $\text{Saving} = C_{\text{old}} - C_{\text{new}}$ | Direct Cost Saving | In ₹/unit or ₹/batch | Final step of method improvement |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern engineering practice, Method Study is the **gateway to Lean Manufacturing, Six Sigma, Industry 4.0, and Kaizen**. Every Toyota Production System (TPS) tool — **5S, Poka-Yoke, Kanban, Value Stream Mapping** — is, at its heart, an application of Method Study principles. In **service engineering** (banks, hospitals, IT), Method Study is used to redesign customer journeys, reduce waiting time, and improve service quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Relevance:** PCIET403 - Work System Design covers Method Study as the **first step in the larger Work Study framework** (which also includes Work Measurement). The objectives you memorize here form the *evaluation criteria* for any subsequent time study or work measurement project.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Quantifying the Benefit of Method Study

A typical **14-mark KTU problem** asks the student to *prove that the proposed method is better than the existing method* by computing the **% productivity gain** and the **daily cost saving**. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step analytical framework** for such a problem.

#### 3.1.1 General Setup

Let:
- $T_{o,1}$ = Observed time for **existing method** (min/unit)
- $T_{o,2}$ = Observed time for **proposed method** (min/unit)
- $R_1$, $R_2$ = Rating factors for existing and proposed methods
- $A$ = Personal + Fatigue + Delay allowance (as a fraction, e.g., 0.15 for 15%)
- $W_r$ = Wage rate in ₹/hour
- $H$ = Working hours per day (typically **8 hours**)
- $N_d$ = Number of units produced per day under existing method
- $P_1$, $P_2$ = Productivity (units/hour) under existing and proposed methods

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Normal Times

The **Normal Time** is the time a *qualified worker* would take working at a *normal pace* without any allowance for rest or delays.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute Normal Time for Existing Method} \\
T_{n,1} &= T_{o,1} \times R_1 \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Compute Normal Time for Proposed Method} \\
T_{n,2} &= T_{o,2} \times R_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Observed time is multiplied by the rating factor to **remove the effect of operator speed variation** and bring it to a standardized normal performance.

#### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Standard Times

Standard Time includes the **allowance** for personal needs, fatigue, and unavoidable delays. Adding a fractional allowance of $A$ is equivalent to multiplying by $(1 + A)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Compute Standard Time for Existing Method} \\
T_{s,1} &= T_{n,1} \times (1 + A) \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Compute Standard Time for Proposed Method} \\
T_{s,2} &= T_{n,2} \times (1 + A)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Allowance $A$ is **always added on the normal time**, not the observed time. This is a very common KTU exam trap.

#### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Derivation of Daily Output

Given a daily shift of $H$ hours, the total available time is $60 \times H$ minutes. The number of units producible per day is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5: Daily Output — Existing Method} \\
N_{d,1} &= \dfrac{60 \times H}{T_{s,1}} \\
\\
\text{Step 6: Daily Output — Proposed Method} \\
N_{d,2} &= \dfrac{60 \times H}{T_{s,2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Hours are converted to minutes (factor 60) to match $T_s$ which is in min/unit.

#### 3.1.5 Step-by-Step Derivation of Daily Labour Cost & Unit Cost

Assuming one worker per operation, daily labour cost is fixed at $W_r \times H$ rupees. The unit cost is the daily cost divided by the units produced.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 7: Unit Cost — Existing Method} \\
C_{u,1} &= \dfrac{W_r \times H}{N_{d,1}} = \dfrac{T_{s,1} \times W_r}{60} \\
\\
\text{Step 8: Unit Cost — Proposed Method} \\
C_{u,2} &= \dfrac{T_{s,2} \times W_r}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The cost per unit is **directly proportional** to the standard time per unit. Hence, the *ratio of unit costs equals the ratio of standard times*.

#### 3.1.6 Step-by-Step Derivation of % Productivity Gain and Cost Saving

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 9: Percentage Increase in Productivity} \\
\% \Delta P &= \dfrac{N_{d,2} - N_{d,1}}{N_{d,1}} \times 100 \\
&= \left(\dfrac{T_{s,1}}{T_{s,2}} - 1\right) \times 100 \\
\\
\text{Step 10: Daily Cost Saving in ₹} \\
S_{\text{daily}} &= C_{u,1} \times N_{d,1} \;-\; C_{u,2} \times N_{d,2} \\
&= (W_r \times H) - (W_r \times H) = 0 \text{ (if only 1 worker)} \\
&\text{But if a fixed demand } N_f \text{ must be met, then:} \\
S_{\text{daily}} &= (N_{d,2} - N_{d,1}) \times C_{u,\text{new}} \quad \text{[Bonus output valued at new cost]} \\
\\
\text{Step 11: % Cost Saving per Unit} \\
\% \Delta C_u &= \dfrac{C_{u,1} - C_{u,2}}{C_{u,1}} \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \dfrac{T_{s,2}}{T_{s,1}}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Step 10 has two cases — if the bottleneck is *output-limited* (push system), savings come from extra output; if *demand-limited*, savings come from releasing workers.

### 3.2 Numerical Example (Worked-Out for KTU Practice)

> **Problem:** Existing method takes 12 min/unit (rating 90). Proposed method takes 8 min/unit (rating 110). Allowance = 15%. Wage = ₹80/hr. Working hours = 8/day. Find: (i) % productivity gain, (ii) % cost reduction per unit, (iii) daily cost saving if demand is 50 units/day.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n,1} &= 12 \times 0.90 = 10.8 \text{ min} \\
T_{n,2} &= 8 \times 1.10 = 8.8 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,1} &= 10.8 \times 1.15 = 12.42 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,2} &= 8.8 \times 1.15 = 10.12 \text{ min} \\
\\
N_{d,1} &= \dfrac{60 \times 8}{12.42} = 38.65 \text{ units} \\
N_{d,2} &= \dfrac{60 \times 8}{10.12} = 47.43 \text{ units} \\
\\
\% \Delta P &= \left(\dfrac{47.43}{38.65} - 1\right) \times 100 = 22.72\% \\
\% \Delta C_u &= \left(1 - \dfrac{10.12}{12.42}\right) \times 100 = 18.52\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Summary:** Each $T_s$ step first normalizes for operator speed, then adds the allowance. Productivity and unit cost are *inversely* related to $T_s$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping ILO Objective → Engineering Tool

| ILO Objective | Primary Engineering Tool | Quantitative Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Reduce work content | Process Chart, Operation Analysis | $\Delta W_c$ % |
| Improve layout | Flow Process Chart, String Diagram | Distance travelled, $m$ |
| Reduce fatigue | Motion Economy, Ergonomics | RULA/REBA scores |
| Improve safety | Hazard Analysis, FMEA | Accident frequency rate |
| Reduce cost | Method Comparison Chart | $C_u$ before/after |
| Standardize | Standard Operating Procedures | Compliance % |
| Increase productivity | Time Study + Method Study | $\Delta P$ % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The **dashed arrows** (-.->) represent the **continuous improvement loop** — once a method is standardized, monitoring may reveal new inefficiencies, triggering another cycle. This iterative nature is the essence of *Kaizen*.
