<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Method Study. Mention any two of its primary objectives.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Method Study is the systematic recording and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work, with the aim of developing easier and more effective methods and reducing costs (ILO).

> **Any Two Objectives (1 Mark):**
> - To reduce unnecessary work content and eliminate waste.
> - To improve the layout of the workplace and material flow.
> - *(Acceptable alternatives: reduce fatigue, improve safety, lower costs, increase productivity, standardize operations.)*

*(Mark split: 2 marks for definition, 1 mark for objectives.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Method Study and Time Study in two points.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Method Study | Time Study |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Concerned with **"HOW"** the work is done — the method/process. | Concerned with **"HOW LONG"** it takes — the time required. |
| 2 | Aims to **reduce work content** by improving the method. | Aims to **set standard time** for the work content. |
| 3 | Comes **first** in the Work Study framework. | Comes **after** Method Study, once the best method is fixed. |

*(Full 3 marks awarded if both differences are crisp and tabular.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** State and explain the **definition of Method Study** as per the International Labour Organization (ILO). Discuss in detail the **ten major objectives** of Method Study in the context of a manufacturing industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **basic procedure of Method Study** in six systematic steps. For each step, state the **primary tools/techniques** used. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definition & Objectives (7 Marks)**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Method Study is the systematic recording and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work as a means of developing and applying easier and more effective methods and reducing costs. It is one of the two branches of Work Study, the other being Work Measurement.

> **Ten ILO Objectives (5 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Improve existing methods** — Refine outdated procedures.
> 2. **Develop new methods** — Engineer fundamentally better workflows.
> 3. **Reduce work content** — Strip out non-value-adding operations.
> 4. **Optimize resource use** — Man, machine, material, space.
> 5. **Improve layout** — Enable smooth material flow.
> 6. **Reduce fatigue** — Better posture, tool design, motion economy.
> 7. **Improve safety** — Lower accident and occupational-hazard risk.
> 8. **Improve work environment** — Lighting, ventilation, noise.
> 9. **Reduce cost & boost productivity** — Lower unit cost of output.
> 10. **Standardize operations** — Create SOPs and benchmarks.

> **Manufacturing Context (brief):** In a manufacturing unit, objectives 3, 4, and 9 directly impact the **bottom line**, while 6, 7, and 8 impact the **workforce well-being** and *Industry 5.0* human-centric goals.

**(b) Six-Step Procedure & Tools (7 Marks)**

| Step | Name | Description | Tools/Techniques (KTU Required) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Select | Identify the work/job to be studied based on bottlenecks, cost, or volume. | Pareto Analysis, ABC analysis |
| **2** | Record | Capture all facts of the *present method* using standardized symbols. | Operation Process Chart, Flow Process Chart, Two-Hand Process Chart, String Diagram, Cycle Graph, Multiple Activity Chart |
| **3** | Examine | Critically question every recorded fact using the **purpose-questioning** framework. | **Purpose, Place, Sequence, Person, Means** — the 5-questioning technique. |
| **4** | Develop | Synthesize the most practical, economical method from the alternatives. | Method Comparison Chart, Motion Economy Principles |
| **5** | Install | Implement the new method with worker training and management approval. | Training, Supervision, Communication |
| **6** | Maintain | Monitor and audit the new method; refine if drift occurs. | Follow-up audits, Statistical Process Control (SPC) |

*(Mark split: 1 mark per step name + description, 1 mark collectively for tools — flexible as per examiner.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A manufacturing process currently takes **15 minutes per unit** (operator rated at **80%**). After a Method Study improvement, the proposed process takes **10 minutes per unit** (operator rated at **110%**). Allowance is **20%**. Working hours per day = **8 hours**, and wage rate = **₹100/hour**.
Compute: (i) Standard time for each method, (ii) Daily output for each method, (iii) **% productivity increase**, and (iv) **% cost reduction per unit**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of Method Study in **improving productivity** in an engineering industry. Discuss how it differs from and complements **Work Measurement**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Numerical (7 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & T_{o,1} = 15 \text{ min}, \; R_1 = 0.80, \\
& T_{o,2} = 10 \text{ min}, \; R_2 = 1.10, \\
& A = 0.20, \; H = 8 \text{ hr}, \; W_r = ₹100/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Normal Times:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n,1} &= 15 \times 0.80 = 12.0 \text{ min} \\
T_{n,2} &= 10 \times 1.10 = 11.0 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Times:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{s,1} &= 12.0 \times 1.20 = 14.4 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,2} &= 11.0 \times 1.20 = 13.2 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily Output:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{d,1} &= \dfrac{60 \times 8}{14.4} = 33.33 \text{ units} \\
N_{d,2} &= \dfrac{60 \times 8}{13.2} = 36.36 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — % Productivity Increase:**
$$
\% \Delta P = \left(\dfrac{36.36}{33.33} - 1\right) \times 100 = 9.09\%
$$

**Step 5 — % Cost Reduction per Unit:**
$$
\% \Delta C_u = \left(1 - \dfrac{13.2}{14.4}\right) \times 100 = 8.33\%
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since allowance $A$ is a *fixed fractional add-on* on normal time, it scales both $T_s$ values **proportionally** and therefore *cancels out* in the productivity ratio. The **rating factor $R$**, however, has a direct multiplying effect and is the dominant lever in the improvement.

**(b) Role of Method Study & Relation to Work Measurement (7 Marks)**

- **Role in Productivity (3 Marks):** Method Study boosts productivity by *reducing the amount of work* required per unit — eliminating unnecessary motions, combining operations, and improving workplace layout. Productivity $P$ rises because the *denominator* (labour-hours) shrinks for the same *numerator* (output).
- **Difference from Work Measurement (2 Marks):** Work Measurement is the *quantitative* arm that sets standard times; Method Study is the *qualitative* arm that redesigns the process.
- **Complementarity (2 Marks):** Method Study **must precede** Work Measurement. It is illogical to time-study a wasteful process; first improve the method, then time the improved method.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Method Study Questions**
> 1. **Confusing "Method Study" with "Time Study":** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if a student uses these interchangeably. Memorize: Method Study = *reduce work content*; Time Study = *set standard time for that work content*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Allowance Term $(1 + A)$:** Many students compute $T_s = T_n$ without adding the allowance. This is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU papers.
> 3. **Rating Factor applied to the wrong time:** Rating is applied to **Observed Time** to get Normal Time — not to Standard Time. Marks lost: **1 mark per sub-part**.
> 4. **Missing the 6-step procedural order:** Examiners expect the sequence **Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Install → Maintain**. Out-of-order answers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Listing objectives without grouping:** A classified tabular list (Economy / Human / Process / Layout / Quality) scores higher than a flat bullet list. Always group your ILO objectives.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (ILO):** Systematic recording + critical examination → easier, more effective methods + reduced cost.
- **Method Study = "HOW"** of work; **Time Study = "HOW LONG"** of work.
- **Six-Step Procedure:** Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Install → Maintain.
- **Five-Questioning Technique (during Examine phase):** Purpose, Place, Sequence, Person, Means.
- **Key Tools:** Operation Process Chart, Flow Process Chart, Two-Hand Chart, String Diagram, Cycle Graph, Multiple Activity Chart, Method Comparison Chart.
- **ILO Objectives (10):** Improve methods, develop new methods, reduce work content, optimize resources, improve layout, reduce fatigue, improve safety, improve environment, reduce cost, standardize.
- **Formula to Memorize:** $T_s = T_o \times R \times (1 + A)$.
- **Productivity Relation:** $\%\Delta P = \left(\dfrac{T_{s,1}}{T_{s,2}} - 1\right) \times 100$.
- **Unit Cost Relation:** $\%\Delta C_u = \left(1 - \dfrac{T_{s,2}}{T_{s,1}}\right) \times 100$.
- **Sequence Rule:** Method Study *always precedes* Work Measurement in any Work Study project.
- **Mindset:** Method Study is *iterative* — every maintained standard is the starting point of the *next* improvement cycle (Kaizen).
- **2024 KTU Application:** Method Study is the foundational concept for upcoming topics — *Charts, Diagrams, Motion Economy, and Work Measurement* — all of which assume this definition and these objectives as their starting point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure of Method Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Procedure of Method Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Method Study** is the systematic recording, critical examination, and systematic approach adopted for developing improved methods of doing work, primarily to reduce worker effort, eliminate unnecessary operations/movements, and improve the quality/efficiency of output. The **Procedure of Method Study** refers to the universally accepted, standardized **six-step (or seven-step) sequential framework** formulated under the British Standards Institution (BS 3138:1979) and codified by the International Labour Organization (ILO), which an Industrial Engineer follows to analyze, redesign, and install a better work method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "Procedure of Method Study" is a **direct 14-mark ESE favorite**. Students must memorize all six/six-plus steps in their exact chronological order, including the **purpose (objective)** of each step and the **standard tools/recording techniques** used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor treating a patient. The doctor does not randomly prescribe medicine. Instead, the doctor follows a strict protocol:

1. **Examines the patient** (gathers symptoms)
2. **Diagnoses the problem** (analyzes data)
3. **Prescribes a new treatment** (designs solution)
4. **Implements the treatment** (surgery/medicine)
5. **Follows up to check recovery** (monitoring)

**Method Study is the "Doctor of Workplaces."** It follows an identical medical protocol — **Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Install → Maintain**. Just as a doctor cannot prescribe without first examining, an Industrial Engineer cannot redesign a process without first **systematically recording** the existing reality. Without this procedure, redesigning a workstation would be based on guesswork, leading to chaos, resistance from workers, and economic loss.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Recurring Constants

In KTU board examinations, the following constants and statistical standards are expected to be cited in exact numerical form:

- **Standard Working Time** = **8 hours/day** (or **480 minutes/day**)
- **Standard Working Hours/year** = **2080 hours** (approx)
- **Standard Performance Rating** = **100 (B.S.)** on the **0–100 Rating Scale** (Westinghouse System)
- **Standard Allowances (Rest, Personal, Contingency)** = typically **10%–15%** of basic time
- **Number of standard steps in Method Study procedure** = **6 primary steps** (with an optional Step 0 for objective definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Method Study vs. Work Measurement**
> - **Method Study:** *What* to do and *How* to do it. Concerned with **method improvement** (reducing unnecessary work).
> - **Work Measurement:** *How long* it should take. Concerned with **time reduction** and setting standard times.
> - The Procedure of Method Study is the **foundation** upon which Work Measurement (Module 3 of PCIET403) is applied.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Step (and Optional Seven-Step) Procedure

The British Standards Institution formalized the methodology into a rigid logical sequence. Each step has a **defined input**, a **defined process**, and a **defined output**.

### Step 0: Define the Problem / Objective (Pre-Investigation Stage)
- **Purpose:** To identify *why* the study is being conducted and clearly bound the scope.
- **Activities:** Meet the plant manager, identify the problem area (e.g., high cost, low quality, high rejection, low productivity, worker fatigue).
- **Output:** A formal "Terms of Reference" or investigation brief.

### Step 1: Select the Work to be Studied
- **Purpose:** To identify the specific job, operation, or process requiring analysis.
- **Selection Criteria (Economic, Technical, Human):**
  * Bottleneck operations (operations that delay the entire production line).
  * Operations with **high repetitive content** (greater ROI).
  * Operations with **poor quality** (high scrap/rejection).
  * Operations involving **excessive human effort** or **worker complaints**.
- **Output:** A defined job/operation to be analyzed.

### Step 2: Record the Present Method (The Data Collection Stage)
- **Purpose:** To accurately document the current state of the work using standardized recording techniques. This is the **most critical step** because flawed recording = flawed analysis.
- **Standard Recording Tools (KTU Examinable):**
  * **Charts (Macro-level recording):**
    - **Outline Process Chart** — records only the sequence of operations & inspections (no delays/transports/storage at major overview).
    - **Flow Process Chart (Two-handed / Material / Man / Equipment)** — records all events: Operation, Inspection, Transport, Delay, Storage (5 symbols).
    - **String Diagram** — 2D layout tracking of worker's path on a scaled layout.
    - **Flow Diagram** — scaled spatial diagram showing movement of subject between workstations.
  * **Diagrams (Micro-level recording):**
    - **Multiple Activity Chart** — simultaneous charting of multiple workers/machines on a common time scale.
    - **Simo Chart (Simultaneous Motion Chart)** — left & right hand motions of one worker (Therbligs).
    - **Cycle Graph & Chronocyclegraph** — photographic recording of motion paths.
    - **Motion Study Filming** — video analysis.

### Step 3: Examine the Present Method (Critical Analysis)
- **Purpose:** To subject the recorded data to rigorous questioning using the **systematic questioning technique**.
- **The "Questioning Technique" — Two Tiers:**

**Tier 1 — Purpose of each operation (5 W's and 1 H):**
  * **What** is done?
  * **Why** is it done? *(If no valid reason → eliminate)*
  * **Where** is it done?
  * **When** is it done?
  * **Who** does it?
  * **How** is it done?

**Tier 2 — Detailed questioning of Means, Sequence, Place, and Person:**
  * For each element: Is there an alternative **Means** (equipment/tool)? Alternative **Sequence**? Alternative **Place** (layout)? Alternative **Person** (skill level)?
- **Output:** Identified wasteful elements (e.g., delays, unnecessary transport, idle time, redundant inspections).

### Step 4: Develop the New (Improved) Method
- **Purpose:** To design a practical, economical, safer, and ergonomically superior method based on findings from Step 3.
- **Sub-steps:**
  1. Sketch the proposed layout and method.
  2. Calculate estimated cost savings, productivity gains, and ROI.
  3. Compare alternatives using **cost-benefit analysis**.
  4. Select the most feasible/practical method (considering technical, economic, and human factors).
- **Output:** A proposed/improved method blueprint.

### Step 5: Install the New Method
- **Purpose:** To put the new method into actual practice.
- **Critical Sub-activities:**
  * **Sell** the idea to workers, supervisors, and management (Change Management).
  * **Train** the operators on the new procedure.
  * **Ensure physical readiness** — tools, jigs, fixtures, machines in place.
  * **Phase implementation** in stages to avoid disruption.
  * Document the new Standard Operating Procedure (SOP).
- **Output:** Live, functional new method.

### Step 6: Maintain the New Method (Follow-up & Control)
- **Purpose:** To ensure that the new method continues to deliver the expected benefits and is not reverted by workers out of habit.
- **Activities:**
  * Periodic audits.
  * Performance comparison vs. standard.
  * Recognize/reward compliance.
  * Rectify any deviation from SOP.
  * Re-examine if conditions change (new technology, new materials).

## 2.2 KTU Framework Cheat Sheet — Procedure of Method Study

| Step # | Step Name | Core Purpose | Primary Tool / Output | Critical Output Document |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Define Problem/Objective | Bound the scope of investigation | Preliminary brief, cost analysis | Terms of Reference |
| **1** | Select the Work | Identify bottleneck/repetitive job | Selection criteria matrix | Approved investigation brief |
| **2** | Record the Present Method | Document *as-is* state | Process Charts, Diagrams, Flow Diagrams | Factual data record |
| **3** | Examine Critically | Question every element | Questioning Technique (5W1H) | List of waste elements |
| **4** | Develop New Method | Design *to-be* state | Sketches, Cost comparison | Proposed method layout |
| **5** | Install New Method | Implement the change | Training, SOP, Trials | Functional SOP |
| **6** | Maintain New Method | Sustain & audit benefits | Periodic audits, QC charts | Audit reports |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industries:** Designing assembly lines in automotive plants (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors).
- **Hospital Administration:** Streamlining patient flow in OP departments.
- **Software/IT:** Applied as **Business Process Re-engineering (BPR)** for process optimization in BPOs and banks.
- **Ergonomics:** Redesigning workplace layouts to reduce Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs).
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Warehouse layout optimization and reducing material handling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, the procedure of method study is embodied in the **"Kaizen"** philosophy — a continuous, incremental improvement cycle very similar to the Steps 3→4→5→6 loop of method study.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Decomposing Each Step

Since "Procedure of Method Study" is a procedural management topic, the analytical matrix below replaces mathematical derivation with a **rigorous logical dissection** of each step, mapping **Input → Process → Output → Pitfall**.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Decomposition

$$
\text{Each Step} \xrightarrow{\text{logical transition}} \text{Next Step}
$$

The procedure follows a strict dependency: the **Output of step $n$** is the **Input of step $n+1$**.

### Step 0 → Step 1 Transition

$$
\text{Problem Identified (Input)} \xrightarrow{\text{Selection Criteria (Process)}} \text{Approved Job (Output)}
$$

*Explanation:* The transition from Step 0 to Step 1 requires applying **selection criteria** (e.g., bottleneck index, repeatability). Without criteria, the analyst may select a non-impactful job.

### Step 1 → Step 2 Transition

$$
\text{Approved Job} \xrightarrow{\text{Recording Tools}} \text{Factual Record}
$$

*Explanation:* Recording must use **standardized symbols**. The five standard process chart symbols are:

$$
\text{Symbols} = \{\text{Operation } \bigcirc,\ \text{Inspection } \square,\ \text{Transport } \Rightarrow,\ \text{Delay } \triangle,\ \text{Storage } \nabla\}
$$

The count of each symbol in the Flow Process Chart yields the **Process Efficiency Metric**:

$$
\eta_{\text{process}} = \frac{N_O + N_I}{N_O + N_I + N_T + N_D + N_S} \times 100\%
$$

where $N_O$ = # Operations, $N_I$ = # Inspections, $N_T$ = # Transports, $N_D$ = # Delays, $N_S$ = # Storage events.

> **Plain-text logic:** A new method aims to increase the ratio of value-adding events ($O$ and $I$) over non-value-adding events ($T$, $D$, $S$). This is the mathematical heart of why method study works.

### Step 2 → Step 3 Transition

$$
\text{Factual Record} \xrightarrow{\text{Questioning Technique (5W1H + 4 sub-questions)}} \text{List of Waste Elements}
$$

*Explanation:* The questioning technique transforms raw data into **diagnostic insights**. Every "Why?" that has no valid economic/safety/quality answer leads to **elimination** of that element.

### Step 3 → Step 4 Transition

$$
\text{Waste Elements} \xrightarrow{\text{Ideation + Cost-Benefit}} \text{Proposed Method}
$$

*Explanation:* For each waste element, at least one alternative must be generated and evaluated. If ROI is positive, the new method is adopted.

### Step 4 → Step 5 Transition

$$
\text{Proposed Method} \xrightarrow{\text{Trial + Training + Communication}} \text{Installed Method}
$$

*Explanation:* The most common failure point. Without **Change Management** (operator buy-in), workers revert to old habits within 48 hours.

### Step 5 → Step 6 Transition

$$
\text{Installed Method} \xrightarrow{\text{Audit + Feedback}} \text{Sustained Method}
$$

*Explanation:* Periodic audits ensure no regression. The cycle is closed, and a **new study** may be initiated if conditions evolve (loop back to Step 1).

## 3.2 The Closed-Loop Continuous Improvement Equation

$$
\text{Method Study Procedure} \equiv \lim_{n \to \infty} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow S_6 \right) \right]
$$

where each iteration $i$ represents one full improvement cycle. The procedure is therefore **continuous**, not terminal — this is why it forms the basis of **Kaizen** in Japanese manufacturing and **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** in TQM.

## 3.3 Critical Analytical Distinctions (Frequently Confused by KTU Students)

| Confusion Pair | Correct Distinction |
|---|---|
| Outline Process Chart vs. Flow Process Chart | Outline: Major events only (O, I). Flow: All 5 events (O, I, T, D, S). |
| Flow Diagram vs. String Diagram | Flow Diagram: tracks **material**. String Diagram: tracks **worker/man**. |
| Multiple Activity Chart vs. Simo Chart | Multiple Activity: **multiple workers/machines** vs. time. Simo Chart: **two hands of one worker** (Therbligs). |
| Cycle Graph vs. Chronocycle Graph | Cycle Graph: path of motion (light trail). Chronocycle Graph: path **with timing dots** (slow/fast visible). |
| Step 5 (Install) vs. Step 6 (Maintain) | Install = *put it in place once*. Maintain = *keep it in place forever*. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Six-Step Procedure (with Closed-Loop Feedback)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step0["Step 0: Define Problem and Objective"]
    step1["Step 1: Select the Work to be Studied"]
    step2["Step 2: Record the Present Method"]
    step3["Step 3: Examine the Present Method Critically"]
    step4["Step 4: Develop the New Improved Method"]
    step5["Step 5: Install the New Method"]
    step6["Step 6: Maintain the New Method"]
    
    step0 --> step1
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 -->|"Periodic Audit / New Bottleneck Identified"| step1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Method Study Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage["INPUT STAGE - Steps 0 to 1"]
        A0["Problem Statement"]
        A1["Selection Criteria: Bottleneck, Repetitiveness, Quality, Cost"]
        A0 --> A1
    end
    
    subgraph RecordingStage["RECORDING STAGE - Step 2"]
        B1["Process Charts: Outline / Flow"]
        B2["Diagrams: Flow Diagram / String Diagram"]
        B3["Motion Charts: Multiple Activity / Simo Chart"]
        B4["Photographic: Cycle / Chronocyclegraph"]
        B1 & B2 & B3 & B4 --> B5["Standardized Factual Record"]
    end
    
    subgraph AnalysisStage["ANALYSIS STAGE - Step 3"]
        C1["Questioning Technique: 5W 1H"]
        C2["Sub-Questions: Means, Sequence, Place, Person"]
        C1 --> C2
        C2 --> C3["Identified Waste Elements"]
    end
    
    subgraph DesignStage["DESIGN STAGE - Step 4"]
        D1["Generate Alternatives"]
        D2["Cost-Benefit Analysis"]
        D3["Feasibility Check: Technical / Economic / Human"]
        D1 --> D2 --> D3
        D3 --> D4["Final Proposed Method"]
    end
    
    subgraph ImplementationStage["IMPLEMENTATION STAGE - Steps 5 to 6"]
        E1["Sell Idea to Stakeholders"]
        E2["Train Workers"]
        E3["Establish SOPs"]
        E4["Periodic Audits and Continuous Improvement"]
        E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4
    end
    
    InputStage --> RecordingStage
    RecordingStage --> AnalysisStage
    AnalysisStage --> DesignStage
    DesignStage --> ImplementationStage
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Mapping of Recording Tools (Step 2 Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["Step 2: Record the Present Method"]
    R0 --> C1["CHART TYPE: Macro Recording"]
    R0 --> C2["DIAGRAM TYPE: Spatial Recording"]
    R0 --> C3["MOTION TYPE: Micro Recording"]
    
    C1 --> C1a["Outline Process Chart"]
    C1 --> C1b["Flow Process Chart - Material Type"]
    C1 --> C1c["Flow Process Chart - Man Type"]
    C1 --> C1d["Two Handed Process Chart"]
    
    C2 --> C2a["Flow Diagram"]
    C2 --> C2b["String Diagram"]
    
    C3 --> C3a["Multiple Activity Chart"]
    C3 --> C3b["Simo Chart with Therbligs"]
    C3 --> C3c["Cycle Graph"]
    C3 --> C3d["Chronocyclegraph"]
```
