<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Method Study. List the six steps of the procedure of Method Study.

**Model Answer:**
**Method Study** is the systematic recording, critical examination, and systematic approach adopted for developing improved methods of doing work to reduce unnecessary operations, eliminate waste, and improve efficiency.

**Six Steps of Procedure:**
1. Select the work to be studied.
2. Record the present method.
3. Examine the present method critically.
4. Develop the new improved method.
5. Install the new method.
6. Maintain the new method.

*(An optional Step 0 — Define Problem/Objective — may precede.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between Outline Process Chart and Flow Process Chart.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Outline Process Chart | Flow Process Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol Count** | 2 symbols (Operation, Inspection) | 5 symbols (Operation, Inspection, Transport, Delay, Storage) |
| **Detail Level** | Macro / broad overview | Detailed / comprehensive |
| **Use Case** | First stage overview of assembly | Detailed analysis of any process |
| **Output** | Identifies inspection-heavy stages | Identifies all non-value-adding events |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure of Method Study in detail, with emphasis on the critical examination stage (Step 3).

**(b) [7 Marks)** Differentiate between String Diagram and Flow Diagram. Explain how Multiple Activity Chart is recorded.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 0 — Define the Problem:** Identify the area of concern (cost, quality, productivity, fatigue) and define the boundary of the study.

**Step 1 — Select the Work:** Select jobs that are bottlenecks, repetitive, high-cost, or causing worker complaints.

**Step 2 — Record the Present Method:** Use Flow Process Charts, Diagrams, and Motion Charts to document the *as-is* state. Recording must use the **5 standardized symbols**.

**Step 3 — Examine the Present Method Critically** *(Most important step — carry 4–5 marks weight):* Apply the **Questioning Technique**:

*Primary Questions (Purpose of each operation):* What? Why? Where? When? Who? How?

*Secondary Questions (Detailed alternatives):* For each element, ask — is there an alternative **Means (equipment/tool)?**, alternative **Sequence?** (combine, rearrange, invert), alternative **Place?** (layout redesign), alternative **Person?** (skill level reassignment)?

Each question that yields no satisfactory answer leads to **elimination** of that element.

**Step 4 — Develop the New Method:** Generate alternatives for each wasteful element; perform cost-benefit analysis; select the most economical, safe, and ergonomic option.

**Step 5 — Install the New Method:** Sell the idea to management and workers, train operators, ensure tools/jigs are ready, pilot-run, then full implementation.

**Step 6 — Maintain the New Method:** Periodic audits, performance monitoring, and ensuring the new method is sustained.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Difference between String Diagram and Flow Diagram:**

| Feature | String Diagram | Flow Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject Tracked** | Worker's path on the floor | Material's path between workstations |
| **Symbols** | Pins and a continuous thread | Symbols (O, I, T, D, S) connected by lines |
| **Scale** | Scaled layout of workplace | Scaled spatial layout |
| **Purpose** | Minimize worker movement distance | Minimize material handling & transport delays |

**Multiple Activity Chart Recording:**
The Multiple Activity Chart is a **time-scaled chart** showing the activities of **two or more workers (or worker + machine)** simultaneously.

*Recording procedure:*
1. Draw a vertical time axis (with measured time intervals, typically in seconds or minutes).
2. For each subject (worker/machine), draw a horizontal bar/column.
3. As the process unfolds, plot **Working** (shaded) and **Idle** (unshaded) periods for each subject on the same time scale.
4. From the chart, identify: idle time of workers, idle time of machines, and the **overlap** between manual and machine time.

*Purpose:* To balance the workload across multiple workers and improve operator-machine utilization.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the various recording techniques used in Method Study. Explain any one chart in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing unit produces 500 units/day through a process that involves 8 operations, 4 inspections, 3 transports, 2 delays, and 1 storage. Calculate the process efficiency and suggest improvements.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The recording techniques in Method Study are classified into three categories:

**1. Process Charts (Macro Recording):**
- **Outline Process Chart:** Records only Operations and Inspections using 2 symbols.
- **Flow Process Chart (Material Type):** Records all events of a material/object as it progresses.
- **Flow Process Chart (Man Type):** Records the activities of a worker.
- **Two-Handed Process Chart:** Records the simultaneous activities of both hands of a worker.

**2. Diagrams (Spatial Recording):**
- **Flow Diagram:** Spatial representation of the layout, tracking the material path.
- **String Diagram:** Pin-and-thread technique on a scaled layout to track worker movement.

**3. Motion Study Charts (Micro Recording):**
- **Multiple Activity Chart:** Time-scaled recording of multiple workers/machines.
- **Simo Chart:** Records Therbligs (basic elemental motions) of the left and right hands.
- **Cycle Graph & Chronocyclegraph:** Photographic recording of motion paths, with chronocyclegraph showing speed through light-gap intervals.

**Detailed Explanation — Flow Process Chart (Material Type):**
The Flow Process Chart (Material Type) uses **5 symbols** (Operation $\bigcirc$, Inspection $\square$, Transport $\Rightarrow$, Delay $\triangle$, Storage $\nabla$) to record every event in the life of a material as it passes through the process. It is presented in tabular form with **columns**: Event Number $\vert$ Symbol $\vert$ Event Description $\vert$ Distance/Time $\vert$ Remarks. It reveals non-value-adding activities (transports, delays, storage) that form the basis for improvement.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$N_O = 8$ (Operations), $N_I = 4$ (Inspections), $N_T = 3$ (Transports), $N_D = 2$ (Delays), $N_S = 1$ (Storage).

**Process Efficiency Formula:**

$$
\eta_{\text{process}} = \frac{N_O + N_I}{N_O + N_I + N_T + N_D + N_S} \times 100\%
$$

**Substituting values:**

$$
\eta_{\text{process}} = \frac{8 + 4}{8 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{process}} = \frac{12}{18} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{process}} = 66.67\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **66.67%** of the events are value-adding (Operations + Inspections). The remaining **33.33%** of activities are non-value-adding wastes.

**Suggested Improvements:**
1. **Combine adjacent operations** to reduce the operation count and eliminate in-between transports.
2. **Relocate workstations** (using Flow Diagram) to reduce transport distances and minimize delays.
3. **Eliminate or combine redundant inspections** (use Statistical Quality Control / SPC charts instead of 100% inspection).
4. **Reduce storage** through Just-In-Time (JIT) inventory practices.
5. **Investigate root cause of delays** — they may be due to material unavailability, machine breakdown, or unbalanced line — and resolve them with preventive maintenance or line balancing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Wrong symbol association:** Students often confuse the symbols for Delay ($\triangle$) and Storage ($\nabla$). Delay = temporary halt; Storage = held for later use. Examiners deduct 1 mark for mis-stating symbols.
> 2. **Missing Step 0:** Many students write only 6 steps, forgetting the optional Step 0 (Define Objective) that scores a half-mark in some questions.
> 3. **Generic questioning technique:** Writing only "What? Why?" without mentioning the **secondary questioning** of Means, Sequence, Place, and Person is considered incomplete and loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing String vs. Flow Diagram:** Saying "both track material" loses full mark.
> 5. **Skipping Maintain step:** Step 6 is repeatedly omitted, but KTU explicitly tests whether the student understands that the procedure does NOT end at installation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ The **Procedure of Method Study** has **6 standard steps** (Select, Record, Examine, Develop, Install, Maintain), preceded optionally by **Step 0 (Define Objective)**.
- ⭐ The procedure is **sequential, continuous, and cyclic** — each cycle of 6 steps yields incremental improvements (foundation of Kaizen/PDCA).
- ⭐ **Step 2 (Record)** is the data foundation — uses **5 standardized symbols**: Operation, Inspection, Transport, Delay, Storage.
- ⭐ **Step 3 (Examine)** is the analytical heart — uses the **Questioning Technique (5W1H)** plus secondary questioning (Means, Sequence, Place, Person).
- ⭐ **Step 5 (Install)** is the human-factors heart — requires **Change Management**, training, and SOPs to overcome worker resistance.
- ⭐ **Step 6 (Maintain)** is the sustainability heart — without audits, workers revert to old habits.
- ⭐ **Recording Tools Taxonomy:** Charts (macro) → Diagrams (spatial) → Motion Charts (micro).
- ⭐ **Process Efficiency Formula:** $\eta_{\text{process}} = \dfrac{N_O + N_I}{N_O + N_I + N_T + N_D + N_S} \times 100\%$
- ⭐ **Method Study = Method improvement**; **Work Measurement = Time standard setting.** Do NOT confuse the two in exams.
- ⭐ **String Diagram** tracks the **man**; **Flow Diagram** tracks the **material**.
- ⭐ **Multiple Activity Chart** tracks **multiple workers/machines** vs. time; **Simo Chart** tracks **two hands of one worker** (Therbligs).
- ⭐ The procedure is **not linear-only**; after Step 6, the cycle restarts (continuous improvement philosophy).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Selection of Work # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Selection of Work** is the systematic and analytical process of identifying, evaluating, and choosing the most economically viable and operationally feasible jobs, products, processes, or projects to be undertaken by an organization, given its limited resources (capital, labor, machines, and time). It forms the very first and most critical phase of the broader **Work System Design** framework, where strategic decisions regarding *what* to produce, *how* to produce, and *whether to produce in-house or outsource* are made before any detailed method study, time study, or ergonomic design is initiated. In the context of KTU's 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework for **PCIET403 – Work System Design**, the *Selection of Work* topic encompasses: - **Product/Project Selection** (choosing among competing market opportunities) - **Process Selection** (identifying the appropriate production technology) - **Make-or-Buy Decisions** (in-house manufacturing vs. outsourcing) - **Economic Feasibility Analysis** (Present Worth, Annual Worth, Rate of Return, Break-Even) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** Selection of Work is treated as a **decision-making pillar** of industrial engineering. It directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Analyse work systems and apply economic principles for selection of work and resources."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **chef running a small restaurant** with one stove, one helper, and a fixed daily budget. Each morning, you must decide: - Which dishes to put on today's menu? (Product selection) - Should you make the bread in-house or buy it from the bakery next door? (Make-or-buy) - Which cooking method — firewood, gas, or induction — gives the best taste per rupee spent? (Process selection) - Will a new dish recover its recipe-development cost within a month? (Capital budgeting) **Selection of Work** in manufacturing is exactly this — but at an industrial scale, where the "stove" is a CNC machine, the "helper" is a worker, and the "daily budget" is capital investment running into crores. The engineer uses quantitative tools (break-even charts, present worth, IRR) to make these decisions **objectively**, just as a chef would weigh cost, taste, and effort. --- ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Selection The following metrics are used universally across KTU board examinations for this topic: - **Interest Rate (i):** The cost of capital, expressed as a decimal (e.g., **10% = 0.10**). - **Time Horizon (n):** The service life of the project, measured in **years** or **cycles**. - **Present Worth (PW):** Today's value of all future cash flows, in **₹ (rupees)**. - **Annual Equivalent (AE):** Uniform annual cash flow equivalent to all PW costs, in **₹/year**. - **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** The discount rate that makes **PW = 0**, expressed as a **%**. - **Break-Even Point (BEP):** The production volume where total cost equals total revenue, in **units/year**. > [!NOTE] > **Why this topic matters:** A poor work-selection decision made in year zero can lock an organization into losses for the next 10–20 years (the typical project life). Selection of Work is therefore the **highest-leverage** decision in the entire work-system lifecycle. --- ## 1.4 Prerequisite Knowledge Map Before diving into selection models, the student must be conversant with: 1. **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — single payment, uniform series, gradient series 2. **Compound Interest Factors** — $(P/F, i, n)$, $(F/P, i, n)$, $(P/A, i, n)$, $(A/P, i, n)$ 3. **Cost Behavior Patterns** — Fixed Cost (FC), Variable Cost (VC), Total Cost (TC) 4. **Elementary Cash-Flow Diagrams** — inflows (+) and outflows (−) plotted against time --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of "Selection of Work" The KTU 2024 syllabus structures *Selection of Work* around four interconnected decision layers. Each must be analysed independently and then reconciled. ### Pillar 1 — Product / Project Selection Determines **which** products or projects a firm should pursue. - Driven by market demand, strategic fit, and **profit potential** - Often evaluated using **scoring models** or **ranking indices** ### Pillar 2 — Process Selection Determines **how** the chosen product will be made. - Choices range from job-shop, batch, assembly line, to fully continuous flow - Heavily influenced by **volume vs. variety** trade-off ### Pillar 3 — Make-or-Buy Decision Determines **whether** the firm manufactures a component in-house or procures it from a vendor. - Based on cost comparison, capacity availability, quality control, and strategic confidentiality ### Pillar 4 — Economic Justification Determines **whether the chosen alternative is financially viable**. - Uses engineering economy tools: PW, AE, IRR, B/C ratio, payback period --- ## 2.2 Engineering-Economy Foundation (KTU's Most-Tested Sub-Topic) ### 2.2.1 Present Worth (PW) Method A project is acceptable if **PW ≥ 0** at the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**. When comparing two **mutually exclusive** alternatives (e.g., Machine A vs. Machine B), the one with the **higher PW** is selected — provided the PW of the *lower-cost* alternative is also ≥ 0. ### 2.2.2 Annual Equivalent (AE) Method A project is acceptable if **AE ≥ 0** at MARR. For mutually exclusive alternatives, choose the alternative with the **largest positive AE**. This method is mathematically equivalent to PW, but is often preferred for comparing alternatives with **unequal service lives** using the **least-common-multiple (LCM)** of lives. ### 2.2.3 Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Method A project is acceptable if **IRR ≥ MARR**. For mutually exclusive alternatives, an **incremental analysis** must be performed: 1. Order alternatives by increasing initial investment. 2. Compute the incremental IRR ($\Delta i^*$) on the extra investment. 3. If $\Delta i^* \geq$ MARR, choose the higher-investment alternative; otherwise retain the lower. ### 2.2.4 Break-Even Analysis (BEA) BEA finds the production quantity where **Total Cost = Total Revenue** (or where two cost alternatives tie). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Method | Decision Rule | Core Formula | Variables | |---|--------|---------------|--------------|-----------| | 1 | Present Worth | Choose highest PW (if PW ≥ 0) | $PW = -P + A(P/A, i, n) + S(P/F, i, n)$ | P = Initial cost, A = Annual net cash flow, S = Salvage | | 2 | Annual Equivalent | Choose highest AE (if AE ≥ 0) | $AE = -P(A/P, i, n) + A + S(A/F, i, n)$ | Same as above | | 3 | IRR | Accept if IRR ≥ MARR | $0 = -P + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{A_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | Solved by trial/interpolation | | 4 | Incremental IRR | Invest extra if $\Delta$IRR ≥ MARR | $0 = -\Delta P + \sum \frac{\Delta A_t}{(1+\Delta i^*)^t}$ | Δ = Difference between two alternatives | | 5 | Break-Even Quantity | $Q_{BE}$ where costs/revenues tie | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC_1 - FC_2}{VC_2 - VC_1}$ | FC = Fixed Cost, VC = Variable Cost/unit | | 6 | Payback Period | Accept if $T_{PB} \leq$ target | $T_{PB} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}$ | Years (simple payback, ignores TVM) | | 7 | Benefit–Cost Ratio | Accept if BCR ≥ 1 | $BCR = \dfrac{PW(\text{benefits})}{PW(\text{costs})}$ | Dimensionless ratio | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Compound Interest Factor Notations Used in KTU Papers:** > - $(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — Uniform-Series Present Worth > - $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — Capital Recovery > - $(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$ — Single Payment Present Worth > - $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}$ — Single Payment Compound Amount --- ## 2.4 Make-or-Buy Decision — Analytical Logic A make-or-buy analysis compares: $$ \text{Cost to Make} = FC_{m} + VC_{m} \cdot Q + \text{Overhead}_{m} $$ $$ \text{Cost to Buy} = P_{b} \cdot Q + \text{Ordering/Logistics Cost} $$ **Decision Rule:** - If **Cost-to-Make < Cost-to-Buy**, manufacture in-house. - If **Cost-to-Buy < Cost-to-Make**, outsource. - **Tie-breakers** (qualitative): quality, lead time, supplier reliability, strategic secrecy, capacity availability. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** In Indian manufacturing (e.g., Maruti, Tata Motors, Bosch), 60–70% of components by count are vendor-supplied. The make-or-buy decision directly determines the **vertical integration strategy** of the firm. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Quantity (Q_BE) Between Two Alternatives Consider two machines, **A** and **B**, with the following cost structures: - Machine A: Fixed Cost = $FC_A$, Variable Cost per unit = $VC_A$ - Machine B: Fixed Cost = $FC_B$, Variable Cost per unit = $VC_B$ Let $Q$ be the annual production volume. The total annual cost of each machine is: $$ C_A(Q) = FC_A + VC_A \cdot Q $$ $$ C_B(Q) = FC_B + VC_B \cdot Q $$ At the **break-even volume**, the two cost lines intersect, so $C_A(Q_{BE}) = C_B(Q_{BE})$: $$ FC_A + VC_A \cdot Q_{BE} = FC_B + VC_B \cdot Q_{BE} $$ Rearranging the terms: $$ FC_A - FC_B = VC_B \cdot Q_{BE} - VC_A \cdot Q_{BE} $$ $$ FC_A - FC_B = (VC_B - VC_A) \cdot Q_{BE} $$ $$ Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC_A - FC_B}{VC_B - VC_A} $$ This is the production volume at which both machines are economically equivalent. For **$Q < Q_{BE}$**, choose the machine with the **lower fixed cost**. For **$Q > Q_{BE}$**, choose the machine with the **lower variable cost**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Present Worth of a Uniform Annual Series A sum of $A$ rupees is received at the **end of every year** for $n$ years. We need its equivalent value at year 0. The first payment $A$ occurs at the **end of year 1**, so its present worth is $A(1+i)^{-1}$. The second payment occurs at the end of year 2, with PW = $A(1+i)^{-2}$, and so on. Summing the geometric series: $$ PW = A(1+i)^{-1} + A(1+i)^{-2} + \dots + A(1+i)^{-n} $$ $$ PW = A \sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+i)^{-t} $$ Multiply both sides by $(1+i)$: $$ PW(1+i) = A \sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+i)^{-(t-1)} = A \sum_{t=0}^{n-1} (1+i)^{-t} $$ Subtract the original equation from the shifted equation: $$ PW(1+i) - PW = A \left[ 1 - (1+i)^{-n} \right] $$ $$ PW \cdot i = A \left[ 1 - (1+i)^{-n} \right] $$ $$ \boxed{(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}} $$ $$ PW = A \cdot (P/A, i, n) $$ This is the single most-tested formula in **Engineering Economy** questions at KTU. --- ## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem: Machine Selection using AE Method > **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark):** A firm must select between two machines, A and B. Machine A costs **₹5,00,000** with an annual operating cost of **₹80,000** and a salvage of **₹1,00,000** after **5 years**. Machine B costs **₹7,50,000** with an annual operating cost of **₹55,000** and a salvage of **₹1,50,000** after **8 years**. The MARR is **12%**. Recommend the better machine using the **Annual Equivalent method** with LCM of lives. ### Step 1 — Compute the LCM of Service Lives $$ LCM(5, 8) = 40 \text{ years} $$ So Machine A will be replaced **8 times** (every 5 years) and Machine B will be replaced **5 times** (every 8 years) over the 40-year horizon. ### Step 2 — Compute AE of Machine A (life = 5 years) First, compute the PW over one 5-year cycle, then convert to AE. $$ PW_A = -5{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 (P/A, 12\%, 5) + 1{,}00{,}000 (P/F, 12\%, 5) $$ Look up / calculate the factors at $i = 12\%$, $n = 5$: $$ (P/A, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{(1.12)^5 - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^5} = \dfrac{1.7623 - 1}{0.12 \cdot 1.7623} = \dfrac{0.7623}{0.2115} \approx 3.6048 $$ $$ (P/F, 12\%, 5) = (1.12)^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{1.7623} \approx 0.5674 $$ Substitute: $$ PW_A = -5{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000(3.6048) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.5674) $$ $$ PW_A = -5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}88{,}384 + 56{,}740 = -7{,}31{,}644 \text{ ₹} $$ Convert to AE using $(A/P, 12\%, 5)$: $$ (A/P, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{1}{3.6048} \approx 0.2774 $$ $$ AE_A = -7{,}31{,}644 \times 0.2774 \approx -2{,}02{,}960 \text{ ₹/year} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute AE of Machine B (life = 8 years) $$ PW_B = -7{,}50{,}000 - 55{,}000 (P/A, 12\%, 8) + 1{,}50{,}000 (P/F, 12\%, 8) $$ Factors at $i = 12\%$, $n = 8$: $$ (P/A, 12\%, 8) = \dfrac{(1.12)^8 - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^8} = \dfrac{2.4760 - 1}{0.12 \cdot 2.4760} = \dfrac{1.4760}{0.2971} \approx 4.9676 $$ $$ (P/F, 12\%, 8) = (1.12)^{-8} = \dfrac{1}{2.4760} \approx 0.4039 $$ Substitute: $$ PW_B = -7{,}50{,}000 - 55{,}000(4.9676) + 1{,}50{,}000(0.4039) $$ $$ PW_B = -7{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}73{,}218 + 60{,}585 = -9{,}62{,}633 \text{ ₹} $$ Convert to AE using $(A/P, 12\%, 8)$: $$ (A/P, 12\%, 8) = \dfrac{1}{4.9676} \approx 0.2013 $$ $$ AE_B = -9{,}62{,}633 \times 0.2013 \approx -1{,}93{,}772 \text{ ₹/year} $$ ### Step 4 — Compare and Decide | Machine | AE (₹/year) | Decision | |---------|-------------|----------| | A | −2,02,960 | Worse | | **B** | **−1,93,772** | **Better (less negative cost)** | > **Conclusion:** Machine **B** is the more economical choice because its annual equivalent cost is **₹9,188/year lower** than Machine A's. The savings of nearly ₹9,200 per year, compounded over 40 years, will amount to a substantial lifetime gain. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method | Selection Method | Best Suited For | Limitation | |------------------|-----------------|------------| | Present Worth (PW) | Single-project go/no-go, equal-life alternatives | Cannot easily handle unequal lives | | Annual Equivalent (AE) | Unequal-life alternatives | Requires LCM assumption (repeatability) | | IRR | Comparing the **rate of return** of a project | Multiple IRRs possible with non-conventional cash flows | | Incremental IRR | Mutually exclusive alternatives with different investments | Computationally heavier; sign changes cause errors | | Break-Even Analysis | Make-or-buy, two-machine choice | Ignores time value of money | | Payback Period | Quick screening, high-risk environments | Ignores cash flows beyond payback | | B/C Ratio | Public-sector / infrastructure projects | Difficult to separate benefits from dis-benefits | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Flow of "Selection of Work" Decision-Making The following block-level architecture illustrates the **sequence of logical steps** an industrial engineer must follow when undertaking work selection in a manufacturing organization. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Identify the Decision Trigger] --> A1[New Product Demand OR
Capacity Expansion OR
Obsolescence of Existing Setup] A1 --> B0[Define the Decision Boundary] B0 --> B1[Gather Data: Demand, Costs,
Service Life, MARR, Salvage] B1 --> C0{Select the Economic Method} C0 -->|Equal Lives| C1[Present Worth Method] C0 -->|Unequal Lives| C2[Annual Equivalent Method] C0 -->|Strategic Comparison| C3[Incremental IRR] C0 -->|Volume-Sensitive| C4[Break-Even Analysis] C0 -->|Make or Buy| C5[Cost-of-Manufacture vs
Cost-of-Procurement] C1 --> D0[Compute PW for Each Alternative] C2 --> D0 C3 --> D0 C4 --> D0 C5 --> D0 D0 --> E0[Apply Decision Rule] E0 -->|PW is max positive| F0[Select Best Alternative] E0 -->|All PW negative| F1[Reject all or Re-scope] E0 -->|Tie| F2[Qualitative Tiebreakers] F0 --> G0[Document and Implement] F1 --> G0 F2 --> G0 G0 --> H0[Post-Implementation Review] ``` --- ## 4.2 Break-Even Concept — Schematic Mapping This Mermaid diagram represents the **behaviour of total-cost lines** for two competing alternatives. The intersection is the break-even volume. ```mermaid flowchart LR Q0[Production Q = 0 units] --> RL[Region Left of QBE] QBE[Break-Even Volume QBE] --> RR[Region Right of QBE] subgraph LOW_FIXED[Low Fixed Cost Machine] LF1[Low FC, High VC Line] end subgraph HIGH_FIXED[High Fixed Cost Machine] HF1[High FC, Low VC Line] end LF1 --- RL HF1 --- RR RL --> DEC1[Choose LOW-FIXED Machine] RR --> DEC2[Choose HIGH-FIXED Machine] ``` --- ## 4.3 Make-or-Buy Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD D0[Component X Required: Q units/yr] --> D1[Compute Cost to Make] D0 --> D2[Compute Cost to Buy] D1 --> D3{Cost to Make vs Cost to Buy} D2 --> D3 D3 -->|Make is cheaper| D4[In-house Manufacture] D3 -->|Buy is cheaper| D5{Strategic Factors Check} D5 -->|Quality and Lead Time OK| D6[Outsource to Vendor] D5 -->|Critical / Confidential| D4 D3 -->|Tie within 5 percent| D7[Consider Qualitative Factors] D7 --> D4 D7 --> D6 ``` --- ## 4.4 Selection-of-Work Process Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD SW[Selection of Work] --> P1[Product Selection] SW --> P2[Process Selection] SW --> P3[Make or Buy] SW --> P4[Economic Justification] P1 --> T1[Scoring Model] P1 --> T2[Market Potential Index] P2 --> T3[Volume vs Variety Matrix] P2 --> T4[Process Flow Choice] P3 --> T5[Cost Comparison] P4 --> T6[Present Worth] P4 --> T7[Annual Worth] P4 --> T8[Internal Rate of Return] P4 --> T9[Break-Even Analysis] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define "Selection of Work" and list any four criteria used in the selection of a product or project.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Selection of Work is the systematic process of identifying and choosing the most economically and operationally feasible alternative among competing products, processes, or projects, given the organization's resource constraints. Four common selection criteria are: 1. **Market potential** — size, growth rate, and stability of demand 2. **Profitability** — measured by NPV, IRR, or payback 3. **Technical feasibility** — availability of process technology 4. **Resource availability** — capital, labor, machinery, and time *(Acceptable alternatives: strategic fit, risk, social/regulatory considerations)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between the Present Worth (PW) method and the Annual Equivalent (AE) method of economic analysis.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Present Worth (PW) | Annual Equivalent (AE) | |--------|---------------------|-------------------------| | Meaning | Converts all cash flows to a single sum at year 0 | Converts all cash flows to a uniform series over the life | | Formula | $PW = -P + A(P/A, i, n) + S(P/F, i, n)$ | $AE = -P(A/P, i, n) + A + S(A/F, i, n)$ | | Unequal lives | Difficult unless LCM is used | Naturally handled via LCM repeatability | | Result interpretation | A lump-sum ₹ value | A uniform ₹/year value | Both are mathematically equivalent; AE is preferred when comparing alternatives with **different service lives**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** A manufacturing company is evaluating two machines, **M1** and **M2**, for a new work assignment. | Parameter | Machine M1 | Machine M2 | |-----------|------------|------------| | Initial Cost (₹) | 3,00,000 | 5,00,000 | | Annual Operating Cost (₹) | 60,000 | 30,000 | | Salvage Value (₹) | 50,000 | 80,000 | | Service Life (years) | 5 | 7 | | Annual Revenue (₹) | 1,80,000 | 1,80,000 | | MARR | 10% | 10% | **(a)** Compute the **Annual Equivalent** for both machines. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Which machine should be selected, and why? Use the **LCM method** for unequal lives. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution ##### (a) Computing AE of Machine M1 (life = 5 years) At $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$: $$ (P/A, 10\%, 5) = \dfrac{(1.10)^5 - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^5} = \dfrac{1.6105 - 1}{0.10 \cdot 1.6105} = \dfrac{0.6105}{0.16105} \approx 3.7908 $$ $$ (P/F, 10\%, 5) = (1.10)^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{1.6105} \approx 0.6209 $$ $$ (P/A, 10\%, 5)^{-1} = (A/P, 10\%, 5) = \dfrac{1}{3.7908} \approx 0.2638 $$ Net Annual Cash Flow of M1: $$ A_{\text{net,M1}} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Operating Cost} = 1{,}80{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr} $$ Total Annual Equivalent Cost of M1 (cost side only — including capital recovery and minus salvage recovery): $$ AE_{\text{cost,M1}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2638 + 60{,}000 - 50{,}000 \times (A/F, 10\%, 5) $$ $$ (A/F, 10\%, 5) = \dfrac{0.10}{(1.10)^5 - 1} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.6105} \approx 0.1638 $$ $$ AE_{\text{cost,M1}} = 79{,}140 + 60{,}000 - 50{,}000 \times 0.1638 = 79{,}140 + 60{,}000 - 8{,}190 = 1{,}30{,}950 \text{ ₹/yr} $$ Net Annual Worth of M1: $$ NAW_{M1} = 1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}30{,}950 = 49{,}050 \text{ ₹/yr} $$ --- ##### (a) Computing AE of Machine M2 (life = 7 years) At $i = 10\%$, $n = 7$: $$ (P/A, 10\%, 7) = \dfrac{(1.10)^7 - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^7} = \dfrac{1.9487 - 1}{0.10 \cdot 1.9487} = \dfrac{0.9487}{0.19487} \approx 4.8684 $$ $$ (P/F, 10\%, 7) = (1.10)^{-7} = \dfrac{1}{1.9487} \approx 0.5132 $$ $$ (A/P, 10\%, 7) = \dfrac{1}{4.8684} \approx 0.2054 $$ $$ (A/F, 10\%, 7) = \dfrac{0.10}{0.9487} \approx 0.1054 $$ Net Annual Cash Flow of M2: $$ A_{\text{net,M2}} = 1{,}80{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr} $$ Total Annual Equivalent Cost of M2: $$ AE_{\text{cost,M2}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2054 + 30{,}000 - 80{,}000 \times 0.1054 $$ $$ AE_{\text{cost,M2}} = 1{,}02{,}700 + 30{,}000 - 8{,}432 = 1{,}24{,}268 \text{ ₹/yr} $$ Net Annual Worth of M2: $$ NAW_{M2} = 1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}24{,}268 = 55{,}732 \text{ ₹/yr} $$ ##### (b) Decision LCM of 5 and 7 = **35 years**. With MARR = 10% and repeatability assumed, we compare NAW over a single life cycle (which is the basis of AE), and the decision is the same for any number of repeated cycles. | Machine | NAW (₹/year) | Rank | |---------|---------------|------| | M1 | 49,050 | Lower | | **M2** | **55,732** | **Higher — Select** | **Conclusion:** Select **Machine M2**. Its annual net worth is **₹6,682/year higher** than M1, confirming it is the more profitable long-term investment. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A firm needs to decide whether to **make** a component in-house or **buy** it from a vendor. The demand is **10,000 units/year**. Data: | Parameter | Make | Buy | |-----------|------|-----| | Fixed Cost / Setup (₹/yr) | 8,00,000 | 2,00,000 | | Variable Cost (₹/unit) | 200 | 250 | | Quality rejection rate | 2% | 0.5% | **(a)** Compute the **break-even quantity** and recommend the decision at the given demand. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss **three qualitative (non-monetary) factors** that could override the break-even conclusion. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution ##### (a) Break-Even Analysis Let $Q$ = annual production volume. Total cost of "Make": $$ C_M(Q) = 8{,}00{,}000 + 200 \cdot Q $$ Total cost of "Buy": $$ C_B(Q) = 2{,}00{,}000 + 250 \cdot Q $$ At break-even, $C_M = C_B$: $$ 8{,}00{,}000 + 200Q = 2{,}00{,}000 + 250Q $$ $$ 8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 250Q - 200Q $$ $$ 6{,}00{,}000 = 50Q $$ $$ Q_{BE} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{50} = 12{,}000 \text{ units/yr} $$ **Decision at Q = 10,000 units (which is *below* 12,000):** Since $Q < Q_{BE}$, the **"Buy"** option is economically cheaper. The cost difference at $Q = 10{,}000$ is: $$ C_M - C_B = (8{,}00{,}000 + 200 \cdot 10{,}000) - (2{,}00{,}000 + 250 \cdot 10{,}000) $$ $$ C_M - C_B = 28{,}00{,}000 - 27{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr (extra cost of Make)} $$ **→ Recommend BUY** (saves ₹1,00,000/year). ##### (b) Three Qualitative Override Factors 1. **Quality and Rejection Rate (4 marks weightage):** Make has 2% rejection → expected rejections = 200 units, Buy has 0.5% → 50 units. If the cost of a rejected unit is high (e.g., warranty, recall, brand damage), the actual effective cost of "Make" may exceed "Buy" even at volumes above 12,000 units. The break-even analysis above **ignored rejection cost** — a major limitation. 2. **Lead Time and Supply Chain Risk (4 marks weightage):** If vendors are located overseas or have unreliable delivery, in-house production avoids stock-outs. A "Buy" decision in a Make-or-Buy model is only as good as the supplier's reliability. Strategic inventory buffers add hidden cost. 3. **Strategic Confidentiality and Capability Building (4 marks weightage):** Some components carry **intellectual property** (e.g., a patented engine part). Outsourcing risks technology leakage. Also, a firm may choose to "Make" at a small loss to **build internal capability** for future high-margin products — a strategic trade-off the break-even chart cannot capture. *(3 marks for proper articulation, 1 mark each for distinct examples and real-world relevance.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Selection of Work Questions (KTU Board):** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract salvage** — Salvage value adds to the cash flow; students often omit $(S)(P/F, i, n)$ and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Sign convention errors** — Costs are negative, revenues positive. Mixing signs gives the wrong decision. Always re-state the decision rule at the end. > 3. **Ignoring unequal lives** — Using PW directly on machines with different lives gives a *biased* answer. Always use **LCM + AE** or **replacement-chain PW**. > 4. **Incremental IRR misuse** — The IRR method on individual alternatives is **not** valid for mutually exclusive choices. You **must** do an incremental analysis. > 5. **Arithmetic slip in compound-interest factors** — Always cross-check with the factor table provided in the question paper. Do not approximate early factors and carry forward the error. > 6. **Break-even with no validation of the denominator's sign** — If $VC_B - VC_A < 0$ and the result is negative, the lines never cross in the feasible region — flag it explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Selection of Work** is the *strategic entry point* of work system design; it precedes method study, time study, and ergonomics. - The **four pillars** are: Product Selection, Process Selection, Make-or-Buy, and Economic Justification. - The **Present Worth (PW)** method requires all cash flows to be at year 0; $(P/A, i, n)$ is the most frequently used factor. - The **Annual Equivalent (AE)** method is mathematically equivalent to PW but is naturally suited for **unequal-life** alternatives via the **LCM** trick. - The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is the discount rate that makes PW = 0; for mutually exclusive alternatives, use **incremental IRR**. - **Break-Even Quantity**: $Q_{BE} = (FC_1 - FC_2) / (VC_2 - VC_1)$; works only when the denominator is positive. - **Make-or-Buy** is not only a cost decision — qualitative factors (quality, lead time, IP, capability) often dominate. - Always include **salvage value** in your PW or AE expression; the term is $(S)(P/F, i, n)$ in PW and $(S)(A/F, i, n)$ in AE. - **MARR** is the threshold; accept a project only if its earning rate ≥ MARR. - **Payback** is a *screening* tool, not a *decision* tool — never use it alone for KTU's 14-mark questions. - For KTU board papers, **show all factor values** and **state the decision rule explicitly** at the end of every numerical — both are checked by examiners. - When asked to "compare two mutually exclusive alternatives," you may use PW (with LCM), AE, or incremental IRR — pick the one you are most fluent in. ---

topics.title: Recording the Work and Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recording the Work and Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Recording the Work and Activities** is the systematic documentation and data-collection phase of Work Study, in which an analyst captures the *method* (what is done, where, when, how, by whom) and the *time* (how long it takes) of human, machine, and process activities using standardized forms, charts, and measurement instruments. It transforms qualitative observations into **quantitative, analyzable engineering data** that become the foundation for method improvement, standard-setting, and productivity benchmarking.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, this topic sits at the intersection of:
- *Method Study* (recording techniques) and
- *Work Measurement* (time recording instruments & techniques).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Doctor's Stethoscope"**
> Imagine a doctor examining a patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor must *record* the patient's pulse rate, blood pressure, and symptoms on a chart. Without this *recording*, the doctor has only memory — and memory is unreliable, non-shareable, and unverifiable.
>
> In the same way, an **Industrial Engineer** is a "doctor" diagnosing a workplace. The recording sheets and stopwatches are the "stethoscopes and charts." The doctor (engineer) records pulse rate (**cycle time**), blood pressure (**rating factor**), and symptoms (**delays, waste, non-productive time**) before recommending treatment (**method improvement** + **standard time fixation**).

### 1.3 Why Recording is the Heart of Work Study
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Outcome Alignment**
> - **CO2 (Module 2):** *Apply recording techniques to document work methods and measure work content.*
> - Without accurate recording, **method study degenerates into guesswork** and **work measurement becomes arithmetic on flawed data**.
> - Recording is the **only step** that converts an **observed reality** into **engineered, communicable, and auditable** information.

### 1.4 The Two Pillars of Recording

| Pillar | What it Captures | Primary Tools | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Method Recording** | WHAT, WHERE, WHEN, WHO, HOW | Process charts, flow diagrams, two-handed charts, SIMO charts, string diagrams | Improved method, layout, motion economy |
| **Time Recording** | HOW LONG, HOW FAST | Stopwatch, time study board, rating sheets, activity sampling forms | Standard time, output norms, manpower planning |

### 1.5 Standard Recording Media (KTU Syllabus List)
1. **Charts used in Method Study:** Outline Process Chart, Flow Process Chart (Material/Man), Two-Handed Process Chart, Multiple Activity Chart, SIMO Chart, String Diagram, Cycle Graph, Chronocyclegraph, Travel Chart, Flow Diagram.
2. **Forms used in Time Study:** Time Study Observation Sheet (Stopwatch Record), Time Study Rating Sheet, Allowance Sheet, Standard Data Sheet, Activity Sampling / Work Sampling Observation Sheet.
3. **Instruments:** Stopwatch (decimal-minute, decimal-hour, split-action, cumulative), Camera, Camcorder, Chalk lines, Templates, Stopwatch boards.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:** *Method Recording = "Visual tools that show movement & flow."* *Time Recording = "Numerical tools that quantify duration & pace."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Method Study Recording Hierarchy
Recording techniques are applied in increasing levels of **detail and complexity** depending on the problem. The KTU syllabus follows the **progressive depth** logic:

```
Level 1 (Macro View)   →  Outline Process Chart
Level 2 (Detailed)     →  Flow Process Chart (Material or Man)
Level 3 (Operator)     →  Two-Handed Process Chart / SIMO Chart
Level 4 (Multi-Resource) →  Multiple Activity Chart
Level 5 (Spatial/Path) →  String Diagram / Flow Diagram / Travel Chart
Level 6 (Micro-Motion) →  Cycle Graph / Chronocyclegraph / Memo-Motion Study
```

### 2.2 Outline Process Chart (OPC)
- **Definition:** A chart that records only the *main operations* and *inspections* in sequence, omitting delays, transport, storage, and temporary storage.
- **Use Case:** Initial survey of a process, long-cycle overviews, presentation to management.
- **Symbols used:** Only **Operation (○)** and **Inspection (□)**.
- **Limitation:** Too coarse for detailed improvement; cannot identify hidden waste.

### 2.3 Flow Process Chart (FPC)
- **Definition:** A chart that records **every event** (operation, inspection, transport, delay, storage, temporary storage) using the **5 (or 6) standard process symbols** recommended by ASME.
- **Two Variants:**
  - *Flow Process Chart – Material Type:* Tracks the material/product through the process.
  - *Flow Process Chart – Man Type:* Tracks the worker through the process (typically for maintenance, store-keeping, hospital staff).
- **Why it is the workhorse of method study:** It exposes the **5 wastes (TIMWO)**:
  - **T**ransport
  - **I**nventory (storage)
  - **M**otion (of operator)
  - **W**aiting (delay)
  - **O**ver-processing

### 2.4 Standard Process Symbols (ASME / ILO Convention)

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| ○ | Operation | A purposeful change in an object (drill, weld, mix) | Drilling a hole |
| □ | Inspection | Verifying quality or quantity | Checking diameter with gauge |
| → | Transport | Moving object from place to place (arrow shows direction) | Trolley carrying part |
| D | Delay | Object waiting, not being worked on | Part idle at machine |
| ▽ | Storage | Object kept & protected against unauthorized removal | Finished goods in bonded warehouse |
| ☐ (Dashed D) | Temporary Storage | Object held for short period, may be removed | Part waiting on bench |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Students often confuse **Delay (D)** and **Temporary Storage (Dashed D)**. Recall: *Delay = unauthorized or undesired wait*; *Temporary storage = planned short hold (e.g., cooling, drying).*

### 2.5 Two-Handed Process Chart (THPC)
- **Definition:** A simultaneous motion chart of both hands (and sometimes feet) of an operator at a single workplace, recorded to a **scale of 1 cm = 0.05 m (or 1 inch = 1 second)**.
- **Purpose:** Detect simultaneous idle time of one hand while the other is working, leading to balanced two-handed work.
- **Key columns:** Left hand (TH) symbol, Right hand (TH) symbol, Description, Time scale.

### 2.6 Multiple Activity Chart (MAC)
- **Definition:** A chart that records the *simultaneous activities* of **several operators (or operator + machine)** on a common time scale.
- **Symbol convention:** A line drawn at a level indicates the operator/machine is **working**; a gap in the line indicates **idle** time.
- **Use case:** Determining optimum crew size, balancing line, removing bottlenecks.

### 2.7 SIMO Chart (Simultaneous Motion Cycle Chart)
- **Definition:** A micro-motion recording technique introduced by **Marvin E. Mundel (circa 1940s)** combining the **Therblig analysis** with a synchronized time scale.
- **Recording:** Both hands are recorded on a single sheet using **17 therbligs** (the fundamental micro-motions: Reach, Grasp, Move, Position, Release, etc.).
- **Use case:** Highly repetitive, short-cycle jobs (< 2 min) where micro-motions dominate.

### 2.8 String Diagram
- **Definition:** A plan-view (top-down) drawing of the workplace with a **continuous thread (or pins + string)** laid out to trace the path of worker/material.
- **Use case:** Layout planning, identifying backtracking, congested aisles, and excessive travel.

### 2.9 Time Study Recording – Stopwatch Methods
Time study (a subset of work measurement) records the time for each **element** of a job, then converts to a **standard time** using rating and allowances.

Three principal stopwatch methods:

| Method | Reset Behavior | Reading | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous (Fly-back) Method** | Stopwatch runs continuously; fly-back button returns hands to zero at end of each element. Total time = element time + fly-back accumulator reading. | **Cumulative reading** at element end; subtract consecutive readings. | Repetitive, short-cycle jobs. Most widely used. |
| **Repetitive (Snap-back / Split-second) Method** | Watch is read and reset to zero at every element end. | **Direct element time**; no subtraction needed. | Quick reading, but loses total-cycle check. |
| **Cumulative (Adding-machine) Method** | Watch is read at every element end but **not reset**; element time is the *difference* of consecutive readings (continuous = adding-machine). | **Differences** between cumulative readings. | Long, non-repetitive jobs; equipment-mounted tasks. |

### 2.10 Time Study Procedure (10 Steps — ILO Standard)
1. **Define the job** to be studied.
2. **Select the operator** (qualified, cooperative, performing the standard method).
3. **Break the job into elements** (distinct, recognizable, with clear start/end points).
4. **Inform the operator** of the purpose (build trust, avoid artificial speed-up).
5. **Record method data** (method details, workplace layout) on the observation sheet.
6. **Record time for each element** using the chosen stopwatch method.
7. **Rate the operator's performance** (Pace Rating) against the standard.
8. **Compute Normal Time** for each element.
9. **Add Relaxation + Contingency + Special Allowances** to get Standard Time.
10. **Issue the Standard Time** to Planning, Costing, and Incentive departments.

### 2.11 Performance Rating (Pace Rating)
**Performance Rating (R)** is the **assessor's judgment of the operator's working speed relative to the observer's concept of "normal" pace (BS = 100, or Standard Rating = 100).**

**Common Rating Scales:**
- **60–80 (Westinghouse System)** – Slow / Low skill / Inconsistent.
- **90–110 (Standard)** – Normal pace.
- **120–130** – Fast but sustainable.
- **130+** – Unsustainable for long periods.

**Pace Rating Methods:**
1. **Speed Rating (Wing's Method):** Pure pace/speed judgment (developed by S.B. Franks at BSA).
2. **Effort Rating (Westinghouse):** Combined skill + effort + consistency + conditions judgment.
3. **Objective Rating (100 = walking 3 mph, 0.1 mph = 4 units, etc. — Lowry, Maynard, Stegemerten).**
4. **Synthetic Rating:** Compared with pre-determined motion-time-system values.

### 2.12 Westinghouse System of Rating (4 Factors)
Each factor is rated with sub-categories (A1, A2, B1, …) and an **adjustment value (algebraic)**:

- **Skill (S):** +0.11 to -0.16
- **Effort (E):** +0.10 to -0.12
- **Conditions (C):** +0.06 to -0.07
- **Consistency (D):** +0.04 to -0.05

**Overall Rating Factor (R):**
$$R = 1 + \frac{(S + E + C + D)}{1}$$
where each is read as a **decimal multiplier (algebraic)**.

### 2.13 Activity Sampling (Work Sampling)
**Definition:** A statistical technique in which a **large number of instantaneous, random observations** are made of a worker, machine, or process; each observation classifies the activity into one of several pre-defined categories. The proportion of observations in each category estimates the **proportion of time** spent in that category.

**Why use Activity Sampling?**
- Cheap, requires only paper/pen (or a tally counter).
- Operator-friendly — does not require a stopwatch glued to the operator.
- Suitable for long-period, non-repetitive, or multi-machine studies.
- Provides data for **utilization, delay analysis, allowance estimation, performance index**.

**Randomization:** Observations are scheduled at **random instants** (random number table, calculator, or app), spaced through the day to avoid bias.

### 2.14 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Observed Time (OT)** | $OT_i$ = time recorded for element $i$ | minutes (decimal), seconds |
| 2 | **Average Observed Time** | $\overline{OT} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} OT_i}{n}$ | per element |
| 3 | **Normal Time (NT)** | $NT_i = \overline{OT_i} \times R$ | where $R$ = Rating factor |
| 4 | **Basic Time** | $BT = \sum_{i=1}^{k} NT_i$ | sum of all element NTs in job |
| 5 | **Standard Time (ST)** | $ST = BT \times \dfrac{1}{1 - A}$ | $A$ = total allowance (decimal) |
| 6 | **ST (alternative)** | $ST = BT + BT \times A$ | $A$ in fraction form |
| 7 | **Westinghouse Rating** | $R = 1 + (S + E + C + D)$ | decimal algebraic sum |
| 8 | **Activity Sample Size (proportion)** | $N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$ | $Z$=confidence, $p$=proportion, $E$=absolute error |
| 9 | **Activity Sample Size (continuous)** | $N = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | for measuring mean values |
| 10 | **Standard Error of Proportion** | $s_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{N}}$ | reduces as $\sqrt{N}$ increases |
| 11 | **Pace Rating Conversion (Walking)** | $Rating = (Observed \; Pace \; Speed / 3 \; mph) \times 100$ | Lowry, Maynard, Stegemerten |
| 12 | **Output (pieces per hour)** | $Output = \dfrac{60}{ST_{minutes}} \times Efficiency$ | if efficiency is given |
| 13 | **Allowance per Element** | $Allowance_i = BT_i \times a_i$ | $a_i$ = fractional allowance for that element |
| 14 | **Number of Cycles Observed (general)** | $N_{min} = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma_{element}}{k \cdot \bar{x}}\right)^2$ | $k$ = desired accuracy (e.g., ±5%) |
| 15 | **Conversion: Decimins to Seconds** | $1 \; decimins = 6 \; sec$ | widely used; $1$ decimins = $0.1$ min |
| 16 | **SIMO Cycle Time Computation** | $T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} t_i$ (dominant hand) | sync with time scale; therblig-based |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict Notation Rule (KTU Valuation):** When solving exam problems, always show units throughout. A formula like $NT = OT \times R$ with no units loses ½ mark even if the answer is correct. Use $NT$ in *min*, $R$ as a *ratio* (1.10, not 110), and $A$ as a *decimal* (0.20, not 20%).

### 2.15 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Manufacturing:** Setting piece-rate wages, line balancing, capacity planning.
- **Hospital Management:** Nurse activity sampling, bed-occupancy studies.
- **Software Industry:** Process recording for Agile sprints (cycle-time vs. takt-time analogy).
- **Logistics:** Forklift utilization, dock-to-stock time.
- **Banking/Service:** Customer queue analysis, teller productivity.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Discipline Note:** Activity sampling is the *father* of modern **observational ergonomics** and **digital time-and-motion studies** used in Industry 4.0 sensor-driven work measurement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard Time from Elemental Time Study
**Given:** A job has $k=4$ elements with observed times $OT_1, OT_2, OT_3, OT_4$ from $n=5$ cycles; the operator was rated at $R$; the total relaxation + contingency allowance is $A$ (as a fraction). Derive $ST$.

**Step 1 — Average Observed Time per element (cycle-wise summation).**
For each element $i$, compute the arithmetic mean of the $n$ observed readings:
$$
\overline{OT_i} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} OT_{ij}, \quad i = 1,2,3,4
$$
*Logic:* This removes random variation (Gaussian noise) of cycle time; element-time distributions are approximately normal.

**Step 2 — Apply Performance Rating to obtain Normal Time.**
$$
NT_i = \overline{OT_i} \times R
$$
*Logic:* Rating converts *what the operator actually did* into *what a standard, trained operator would do at normal pace (R = 1.00)*.

**Step 3 — Compute Basic Time (sum of normal times).**
$$
BT = \sum_{i=1}^{k} NT_i
$$
*Logic:* Basic time is the pure work content without any allowance for personal needs, fatigue, or contingencies.

**Step 4 — Compute Total Allowance.**
$$
A_{total} = a_{relax} + a_{conting} + a_{special}
$$
*Logic:* ILO recommends splitting allowance into the three sub-categories for traceability.

**Step 5 — Compute Standard Time (the deliverable).**
$$
ST = BT + BT \cdot A_{total} = BT \times (1 + A_{total}) = \frac{BT}{1 - A_{total}}
$$
*Logic:* $1 - A_{total}$ is the productive fraction of total time. Dividing BT by this fraction inflates BT to a *work-day-feasible* value.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Derivation 1.** *Both forms are equivalent; use whichever the examiner's mark scheme favours. The fraction form $\frac{BT}{1-A}$ is generally preferred in KTU solutions.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Sample Size in Activity Sampling
**Given:** A supervisor wishes to estimate, with **95 % confidence** (Z = 1.96), the proportion of time a CNC machine is **idle**, where a pilot study suggested $p_0 = 0.30$. The desired absolute error is $E = \pm 0.04$. How many random observations are needed?

**Step 1 — State the confidence and Z value.**
Confidence = 95 %  $\Rightarrow$  $Z = 1.96$ (two-tailed normal).

**Step 2 — Plug into the proportion sample-size formula.**
$$
N = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**
$$
N = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.30)(0.70)}{(0.04)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**
$$
(1.96)^2 = 3.8416
$$
$$
3.8416 \times 0.30 \times 0.70 = 3.8416 \times 0.21 = 0.806736
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator.**
$$
(0.04)^2 = 0.0016
$$

**Step 6 — Final division.**
$$
N = \frac{0.806736}{0.0016} = 504.21
$$

**Step 7 — Round up (always).**
$$
N_{required} = 505 \; \text{observations}
$$

*Logic Check:* If $p$ is unknown and no pilot study is available, use the **worst-case** $p = 0.5$, which maximises $p(1-p) = 0.25$. Then:
$$
N_{worst} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.25}{E^2} = \frac{0.9604}{0.0016} \approx 600
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Cycles for a Time Study (Rounded to Required Accuracy)
**Given:** A time study on a packing operator had a pilot of $n_0 = 10$ cycles. The element-wise mean and standard deviation are $\bar{x} = 0.45$ min and $\sigma = 0.06$ min. We need $\pm 5\%$ accuracy with 95% confidence. Find $N$.

**Step 1 — State desired accuracy and Z.**
$k = 0.05$, $Z = 1.96$.

**Step 2 — Formula.**
$$
N = \left(\frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{k \cdot \bar{x}}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**
$$
N = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 0.06}{0.05 \times 0.45}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator and Denominator.**
$$
\text{Num} = 0.1176, \quad \text{Den} = 0.0225
$$

**Step 5 — Ratio.**
$$
\frac{0.1176}{0.0225} = 5.2267
$$

**Step 6 — Square.**
$$
N = (5.2267)^2 \approx 27.32
$$

**Step 7 — Round up.**
$$
N = 28 \; \text{cycles minimum}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Recording Tool

| Scenario | Best Recording Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Operator drills 8 holes — repetitive, 90 sec cycle | Stopwatch, continuous method | Cycle is short & repetitive; element breakdown easy |
| Nurse rounds in a ward — non-repetitive, 2 hours | Activity Sampling | Long duration, varied tasks; sampling is non-intrusive |
| Maintenance crew fixes breakdowns in a plant | Flow Process Chart (Man-type) | Sequence of moves, delays, transport dominant |
| Packing operator on conveyor, 5 sec cycle | SIMO Chart | Micro-motions matter; balance both hands |
| Forklift route in a 10,000 m² warehouse | String Diagram | Spatial path & backtracking analysis |
| Operator on a forging press — 12 min cycle | Multiple Activity Chart (Operator + 2 Machines) | Idle time of operator/machine visible |
| Estimate utilization of 25 CNC machines | Activity Sampling | Many machines, long period, low cost per observation |
| Compare two methods (old vs. new) | Outline Process Chart + BT comparison | Quick, executive-readable summary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** *Use the **simplest chart** that exposes the **waste** in question. Do not start with a SIMO chart if an Outline Process Chart can do the job.*

---

### 3.5 Worked Example (Comprehensive — KTU Mark-Scheme Style)

**Problem:** A machining operation has three elements. Pilot study data (in decimal minutes) and Westinghouse rating factors are:

| Element | $\bar{OT}$ (min) | Skill | Effort | Conditions | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.40 | +0.06 | +0.05 | -0.03 | +0.02 |
| 2 | 0.25 | +0.04 | +0.04 | 0.00 | +0.01 |
| 3 | 0.15 | +0.03 | +0.02 | -0.02 | 0.00 |

Total allowance $A = 20\%$. Compute **Standard Time**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute rating factor for each element.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 1 + (0.06 + 0.05 - 0.03 + 0.02) = 1.10 \\
R_2 &= 1 + (0.04 + 0.04 + 0.00 + 0.01) = 1.09 \\
R_3 &= 1 + (0.03 + 0.02 - 0.02 + 0.00) = 1.03
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Normal time per element.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_1 &= 0.40 \times 1.10 = 0.440 \;\text{min} \\
NT_2 &= 0.25 \times 1.09 = 0.2725 \;\text{min} \\
NT_3 &= 0.15 \times 1.03 = 0.1545 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Basic Time (BT).**

$$
BT = 0.440 + 0.2725 + 0.1545 = 0.867 \;\text{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Time (ST).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= \frac{BT}{1 - A} = \frac{0.867}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{0.867}{0.80} \\
ST &= 1.08375 \;\text{min per piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to per-hour output (KTU bonus).**

$$
Output = \frac{60 \;\text{min/h}}{1.08375 \;\text{min/pc}} \approx 55.36 \;\text{pcs/h}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern, 14 marks):**
> *1) Rating (4 marks) — 3 elements × 1 mark each + sum step 1 mark.*
> *2) Normal Time (3 marks) — 1 mark per element.*
> *3) Basic Time (2 marks) — summation + unit.*
> *4) Standard Time (3 marks) — formula + substitution + final answer.*
> *5) Bonus (2 marks) — Output, efficiency interpretation.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Recording Techniques — Master Classification Tree
```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Recording Work and Activities]
    A1[Method Recording]
    A2[Time Recording]

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2

    A1 --> B1[Process Charts]
    A1 --> B2[Diagrams]
    A1 --> B3[Micro Motion Studies]

    A2 --> C1[Stopwatch Time Study]
    A2 --> C2[Activity Sampling]
    A2 --> C3[Predetermined Motion Time Systems]

    B1 --> D1[Outline Process Chart]
    B1 --> D2[Flow Process Chart Material]
    B1 --> D3[Flow Process Chart Man]
    B1 --> D4[Two Handed Process Chart]

    B2 --> D5[String Diagram]
    B2 --> D6[Flow Diagram]
    B2 --> D7[Travel Chart]

    B3 --> D8[SIMO Chart]
    B3 --> D9[Cycle Graph]
    B3 --> D10[Chronocyclegraph]
    B3 --> D11[Memo Motion Study]

    C1 --> E1[Continuous Method]
    C1 --> E2[Repetitive Method]
    C1 --> E3[Cumulative Method]

    C2 --> E4[Random Observation Tally]

    C3 --> E5[MTM]
    C3 --> E6[Most]
    C3 --> E7[PMTS]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Time Study Recording Workflow
```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Define Job and Break Into Elements] --> nodeB[Select Trained Operator]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Record Method on Time Study Board]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Stopwatch Reading per Element]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Performance Rating Using Westinghouse]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Normal Time per Element]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Sum to Obtain Basic Time]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Add Allowances]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Final Standard Time Output]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Issue to Planning and Costing]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Activity Sampling Cycle
```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[Define Activity Categories] --> s2[Conduct Pilot Study]
    s2 --> s3[Estimate Initial Proportion p]
    s3 --> s4[Compute Required Sample Size N]
    s4 --> s5[Schedule Random Observations]
    s5 --> s6[Conduct Tally Sheets in Field]
    s6 --> s7[Compute Proportions per Category]
    s7 --> s8[Statistical Validation]
    s8 --> s9[Issue Utilization and Delay Report]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Flow Process Chart Symbol Convention

| Symbol | Function |
|---|---|
| O circle | Operation |
| Square | Inspection |
| Arrow | Transport |
| Capital D | Delay |
| Inverted triangle | Storage |
| Dashed D | Temporary Storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these symbols?** Standardized by ASME (American Society of Mechanical Engineers, 1947) and adopted by ILO. They make charts **language-independent**: a Japanese, German, and Indian engineer can read the same chart with the same meaning.

### 4.5 Comparative Matrix — Stopwatch Methods (Visual Summary)

```mermaid
graph TD
    m0[Stopwatch Time Study]
    m0 --> m1[Continuous Method]
    m0 --> m2[Repetitive Method]
    m0 --> m3[Cumulative Method]
    m1 --> desc1[Watch runs nonstop. Fly back at element end. Read cumulative.]
    m2 --> desc2[Watch reset to zero at each element end. Direct element time read.]
    m3 --> desc3[Watch never reset. Element time = difference of consecutive readings.]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic — "CRC":** *C*ontinuous = fly-back, *R*epetitive = reset, *C*umulative = clock.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between *Outline Process Chart* and *Flow Process Chart* with examples.

**Model Answer:**
- **Outline Process Chart:** Records only the *main operations and inspections* of a process using symbols ○ and □. Useful for a high-level overview of a long process. Example: For an engine assembly line, OPC would list 6–8 broad operations (e.g., fit cylinder head, fit pistons, fit crank).
- **Flow Process Chart:** Records *every event* including transport, delay, storage, and temporary storage using the full 5 (or 6) process symbols. Useful for detailed analysis of waste. Example: Same engine assembly would be broken into 30+ events including "conveyor transport between stations" and "delay waiting for hoist."

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** List the **five standard process symbols** and state what each represents.

**Model Answer:**
| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| ○ | Operation |
| □ | Inspection |
| → | Transport |
| D | Delay |
| ▽ | Storage |
| Dashed D | Temporary Storage (optional 6th) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q3A]**
> **Q3A.** *(a)* Explain the procedure for conducting a **stopwatch time study** with a neatly labelled **Time Study Observation Sheet** description. *(7 marks)*
> **Q3A.** *(b)* A worker is rated at **110** (BS Scale). The observed elemental times in decimins are: 4.5, 6.0, 3.2, 2.8. Total allowance is **15%**. Compute the **Standard Time** and the **expected output per 8-hour shift** (assume efficiency 100%). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
1. **Define the job** to be studied (1 mark).
2. **Select the operator** — qualified, cooperative, working at standard method (1 mark).
3. **Break the job into elements** — distinct, recognizable, clear start/end (1 mark).
4. **Inform the operator** of purpose to build trust (0.5 mark).
5. **Record method data** on the time study board — symbols, layout, equipment (1 mark).
6. **Record time** for each element using stopwatch (1 mark).
7. **Rate the operator's pace** against standard (0.5 mark).
8. **Compute Normal Time, add allowances, issue Standard Time** (1 mark).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert decimins to minutes.**
$$
4.5 \;\text{dmin} = 0.45 \;\text{min}, \quad 6.0 = 0.60, \quad 3.2 = 0.32, \quad 2.8 = 0.28
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Basic Time (sum of OT × R, here R = 1.10).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_1 &= 0.45 \times 1.10 = 0.495 \;\text{min} \\
NT_2 &= 0.60 \times 1.10 = 0.660 \;\text{min} \\
NT_3 &= 0.32 \times 1.10 = 0.352 \;\text{min} \\
NT_4 &= 0.28 \times 1.10 = 0.308 \;\text{min} \\
BT &= 0.495 + 0.660 + 0.352 + 0.308 = 1.815 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time (A = 0.15).**
$$
ST = \frac{1.815}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{1.815}{0.85} = 2.135 \;\text{min/piece}
$$

**Step 4 — Output per 8-hour shift.**
$$
\text{Shift time} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \;\text{min}
$$
$$
\text{Output} = \frac{480}{2.135} = 224.82 \approx 225 \;\text{pieces/8 h}
$$

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q3B]** *(Internal Choice)*
> **Q3B.** *(a)* Describe the **Multiple Activity Chart** and **SIMO Chart** with their typical applications. *(7 marks)*
> **Q3B.** *(b)* A factory has 5 machines. A work-sampling study of machine utilization is planned with a **pilot proportion** of 0.35 (machine busy). Determine the **number of random observations** required for ±4% accuracy at 95% confidence. How many observations are needed if no pilot data exists? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Multiple Activity Chart (MAC):** Records *simultaneous* activities of multiple workers or worker + machine on a common time scale (3 marks). Use: crew-size optimization, bottleneck identification, line balancing (1 mark).
- **SIMO Chart:** Micro-motion chart recording **both hands of one operator** using 17 therbligs to a synchronized time scale (2 marks). Use: short-cycle (< 2 min) jobs, balance both hands (1 mark).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Z = 1.96, E = 0.04, p = 0.35.**
$$
N = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**
$$
N = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.35 \times 0.65}{(0.04)^2} = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.2275}{0.0016}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**
$$
3.8416 \times 0.2275 = 0.87396
$$

**Step 4 — Final division.**
$$
N = \frac{0.87396}{0.0016} = 546.23
$$

**Step 5 — Round up.**
$$
N = 547 \;\text{observations}
$$

**Step 6 — No pilot data → use p = 0.5.**
$$
N = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.25}{0.0016} = \frac{0.9604}{0.0016} = 600.25 \Rightarrow 601
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Decimins vs. minutes confusion:** Many students forget that 1 decimins = 0.1 min = 6 sec. Always convert to a consistent unit (preferably minutes) *before* summing.
> 2. **Rounding down the sample size:** The sample-size formula gives a *minimum*; always **round up** the next integer (e.g., 546.23 → 547). KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for rounding down.
> 3. **Rating as a percentage vs. ratio:** Rating **110** must be written as **1.10** in the formula. Writing $0.45 \times 110$ is **wrong**; it gives 49.5 min instead of 0.495 min.
> 4. **Allowance form:** $A = 15\%$ must be entered as **0.15** (decimal), not 15, in the formula.
> 5. **Activity sampling Z value:** Use $Z = 1.96$ for 95% confidence and $Z = 2.58$ for 99% confidence. Mixing these costs full credit.
> 6. **Westinghouse sub-category signs:** Students forget that some sub-categories are *negative* (e.g., poor conditions, low skill). Algebraic addition is mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision — Bullet-Point Power Pack**

- **Recording** is the **bridge** between observation and analysis in Work Study.
- **Method recording** tools: Outline Process Chart, Flow Process Chart, Two-Handed Chart, Multiple Activity Chart, SIMO Chart, String Diagram, Flow Diagram, Travel Chart, Memo-Motion.
- **Time recording** tools: Stopwatch (continuous, repetitive, cumulative), Activity Sampling, PMTS (MTM, MOST).
- **5 process symbols (ASME/ILO):** Operation ○, Inspection □, Transport →, Delay D, Storage ▽, Temporary Storage ☐.
- **Two-handed process chart** scale: 1 cm = 0.05 m (or 1 inch = 1 sec) on a time axis.
- **Multiple activity chart** uses a line for "working" and a gap for "idle."
- **SIMO chart** uses 17 therbligs and is by **Mundel**.
- **Stopwatch methods:** Continuous (fly-back), Repetitive (snap-back), Cumulative (adding-machine).
- **Time study steps (10):** Define → Select → Break → Inform → Record method → Record time → Rate → Normal Time → Allowances → Standard Time.
- **Performance rating scales:** 60–80 slow, 100 normal, 120+ fast; Westinghouse uses 4 factors S, E, C, D.
- **Normal Time:** $NT = \overline{OT} \times R$.
- **Standard Time:** $ST = \dfrac{BT}{1 - A}$.
- **Activity sampling sample size:** $N = \dfrac{Z^2 p(1-p)}{E^2}$.
- **Worst-case p:** Use $p = 0.5$ if no pilot data.
- **Standard Error of proportion:** $s_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{N}}$.
- **Cycles for time study:** $N = \left(\dfrac{Z \sigma}{k \bar{x}}\right)^2$.
- **Decimins conversion:** 1 decimins = 0.1 min = 6 seconds.
- **Output per hour:** $60 / ST_{minutes}$.
- **Use String Diagram for spatial path analysis.**
- **Use SIMO for micro-motion and short cycles (< 2 min).**
- **Use Activity Sampling for long, non-repetitive, multi-machine studies.**
- **Final rule:** Always **round up** sample sizes; always show **units**; always **convert** decimins to minutes.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Recording the Work and Activities**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCIET403, CO2.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Work System Design** and **Work Study**, an **Activity** is defined as any distinguishable, observable, and measurable unit of human or machine effort that consumes time and contributes (positively, neutrally, or negatively) toward the achievement of a defined work objective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Classification of Activities* is the systematic process of categorizing each elementary or collective activity performed during a process into well-defined groups based on its **contribution to value addition**, **frequency of occurrence**, **necessity**, and **origin of effort**, so that wasteful, idle, and unnecessary operations can be identified, isolated, and eliminated to improve overall system productivity.

In the **BS 3138:1979 (British Standard Glossary of Work Study Terms)** and the **ILO (International Labour Organization) standard for Work Study**, activities are fundamentally classified into three canonical categories:

1. **Effective Activity (Productive)** — adds direct value to the product/service.
2. **Necessary/Supportive Activity (Auxiliary)** — does not add direct value but is indispensable to permit effective work.
3. **Incidental Activity (Waste/Unproductive)** — adds neither value nor is required; it represents waste.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🍳 **Real-World Analogy — The Kitchen Chef**
>
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a dish in a restaurant kitchen. While making a meal, you perform many small tasks:
> - *Chopping vegetables over a hot pan* → This directly converts raw ingredients into the final dish (this is an **Effective/Productive** activity).
> - *Washing your hands, fetching ingredients from the fridge, cleaning your knife* → These are not part of the final dish but are required for safety, hygiene, and workflow (these are **Necessary/Supportive** activities).
> - *Daydreaming, scrolling your phone, taking an unnecessary coffee break* → These neither add value to the dish nor are they required (these are **Incidental/Waste** activities).
>
> A work-study engineer observes the kitchen exactly like this and asks: *"Which actions can be removed or reduced without harming the final outcome?"* The classification of activities is the tool that lets the engineer answer that question rigorously.

## 1.3 Why Classification is a Prerequisite in Work System Design

Every productivity improvement initiative (method study, work measurement, line balancing, time study, predetermined motion-time systems, value engineering) **begins** with classification. Without a structured taxonomy:

- **Waste cannot be quantified** because it is mixed with productive work.
- **Standards cannot be set** because the reference content is undefined.
- **Improvement opportunities** are hidden inside complex activity networks.
- **Cost accounting** for labour cannot be performed accurately.

The standard **activity indices** used in classification are:

- **Basic Time** ($T_b$) — time required to perform an activity at standard performance.
- **Standard Time** ($T_s = T_b \times \text{Rating} \times \text{Allowances}$).
- **Activity Ratio** = $\dfrac{\text{Productive Time}}{\text{Total Observed Time}} \times 100\%$
- **Waste Ratio** = $\dfrac{\text{Incidental Time}}{\text{Total Observed Time}} \times 100\%$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 2, *Classification of Activities* is the **first step** before applying any technique of Method Study (Process Charts, Flow Diagrams, Two-Hand Process Charts, Multiple Activity Charts). You cannot draw a process chart meaningfully unless each symbol represents a classified activity.
