# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Canonical Classification (ILO Standard)

This is the **most heavily tested** classification framework in KTU examinations.

### Tier 1 — Effective Activities (Productive)
An activity is classified as *Effective* if it **directly changes** the physical or chemical characteristics of the object being worked upon, **adds value**, and produces a result the customer is willing to pay for.

- Examples: Machining a shaft, welding two plates, painting a surface, assembling two components.
- Time spent = **Effective Time** ($T_e$).
- This is the *value-adding* time from the **Lean Manufacturing** viewpoint.

### Tier 2 — Necessary/Supportive Activities (Auxiliary)
These activities do **not** add direct value but are **unavoidable** given the current method, layout, or design. They are essential *enablers* of effective work.

- Examples: Fetching tools, positioning the workpiece, setting up the machine, cleaning the workplace, holding the part in place.
- Time spent = **Supportive Time** ($T_s$).
- Lean calls these *non-value-adding but necessary* — the target is to *minimise* (not eliminate) them.

### Tier 3 — Incidental Activities (Waste)
These activities **neither add value nor are necessary** for the work. They represent **pure waste** and must be **eliminated** in the first pass of method improvement.

- Examples: Waiting for the machine, idle walking, talking to colleagues, looking for misplaced tools, double-handling, rework.
- Time spent = **Incidental Time** ($T_i$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Observed Time** is partitioned as:
> $$T_{total} = T_e + T_s + T_i$$

## 2.2 Secondary Classification Frameworks

Beyond the ILO three-tier system, activities can also be classified along several orthogonal axes. KTU examiners frequently combine these in 14-mark problems.

### A. Classification by Source of Effort
| Source | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Activity** | Effort entirely human | Hand-filing, packing |
| **Machine Activity** | Effort entirely machine | CNC milling, drying |
| **Combined (Mixed) Activity** | Synchronised human + machine | Manual welding, semi-automatic lathe operation |

### B. Classification by Frequency of Occurrence
| Frequency | Description | Planning Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Repetitive** | Occurs every cycle | High optimisation priority |
| **Occasional** | Occurs at intervals | Routine scheduling |
| **Random** | Occurs unpredictably | Buffer / contingency planning |
| **One-time** | Occurs once (e.g., setup) | Project-based planning |

### C. Classification by Human Bodily Movement (Gilbreth's 17 Therbligs)
Frank Gilbreth decomposed manual work into **17 fundamental motions (therbligs)**:
**Find, Search, Select, Grasp, Hold, Transport Loaded, Position, Assemble, Disassemble, Use, Inspect, Pre-position, Release Load, Transport Empty, Rest, Unavoidable Delay, Avoidable Delay, Plan.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Of the 17 therbligs, only **5 are Effective** (Grasp, Transport Loaded, Position, Assemble, Use), **8 are Supportive** (Search, Select, Hold, Pre-position, Release Load, Disassemble, Inspect, Plan), and **4 are Waste** (Find in some taxonomies, Rest as non-productive, Unavoidable Delay, Avoidable Delay).

### D. Classification in Process Charts
KTU's symbol set (from the Module 1 process-charting unit) maps directly onto the three-tier classification:
| Symbol | Operation | Typical Classification |
|---|---|---|
| **○ Operation** | Adds value | Effective |
| **⇒ Transport** | Moves object | Often Supportive |
| **▷ Inspection** | Verifies quality | Supportive |
| **▽ Storage** | Holds object | Incidental (if delay) / Supportive (if buffer) |
| **D Delay** | Idle waiting | Incidental |
| **Combined** | Two symbols in one | Re-classify by dominant intent |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **exam-grade formulas** for any quantitative problem on Classification of Activities. Memorise the symbols, the unit, and the **boundary condition** for each.

| # | Formula | Symbol Expansion | Unit | Purpose / KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{total} = T_e + T_s + T_i$ | Effective + Supportive + Incidental | min | Verify time partitioning |
| 2 | $\text{Effective Ratio} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | % of value-adding | % | Primary KPI for work-study |
| 3 | $\text{Support Ratio} = \dfrac{T_s}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | % of enablers | % | Quantify enabler work |
| 4 | $\text{Waste Ratio} = \dfrac{T_i}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | % of waste | % | Quantify waste eliminated |
| 5 | $\eta_{util} = \dfrac{T_e + T_s}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Utilisation index | % | Includes only *non-waste* |
| 6 | $\eta_{lean} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Lean value-add | % | Lean benchmark |
| 7 | $\text{Improvement \%} = \dfrac{T_{i,\text{before}} - T_{i,\text{after}}}{T_{i,\text{before}}} \times 100$ | % waste reduction | % | Before/after method study |
| 8 | $N_{opt} = N_{obs} \times \eta_{util}$ | Reclassified effective count | dimensionless | Sample size correction |
| 9 | $T_{std} = T_b \times R \times (1 + A)$ | Standard time | min | Time study post-classification |
| 10 | $\text{Cycle Time} = T_e + T_s$ | Effective + Supportive | min | Takt-time matching |

> **Where** $R$ = Performance Rating (1.0 = standard, 1.2 = fast, 0.8 = slow) and $A$ = Allowance fraction (typically 0.10 to 0.25).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Lean Manufacturing**: The 7 wastes (TIMWOOD) map directly onto the *Incidental* category.
- **Cost Engineering**: Labour cost is apportioned only to Effective + Necessary activities; Incidental is treated as *non-chargeable time*.
- **Ergonomics**: Classifying activities as *static hold* vs *dynamic move* informs fatigue allowances.
- **Automation Engineering**: Effective manual activities are prime candidates for robotic replacement.
- **Production Planning**: Takt time and line balancing require every activity to be classified before cycle-time matching.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Activity Ratio Identity

**Starting Premise:**
The total time observed for a work cycle can be exhaustively partitioned.

**Step 1 — Define the time components.**
By ILO convention, every second of human (or machine) activity belongs to one of the three categories.

$$
T_{total} \;=\; T_{effective} + T_{supportive} + T_{incidental}
$$

**Step 2 — Normalise each component to obtain a unitless ratio.**
Dividing both sides by $T_{total}$ yields:

$$
1 \;=\; \frac{T_{effective}}{T_{total}} + \frac{T_{supportive}}{T_{total}} + \frac{T_{incidental}}{T_{total}}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the three ratios.**
Let $r_e$, $r_s$, $r_i$ denote the fractional contributions.

$$
r_e + r_s + r_i = 1
$$

Expressed in percentage form, this identity is the **Conservation of Time Law for Activities** and is the basis of every classification problem.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Complete Solution

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark type problem**. Master the logic; the numbers change but the steps do not.

### Problem Statement
A work-study engineer observes a manual assembly station for 480 minutes. The activities are classified as follows:

| Activity | Description | Time (min) |
|---|---|---|
| Tightening bolts on housing | Adds value | 180 |
| Fetching the bolts from bin | Necessary, no value | 60 |
| Holding part with left hand while right tightens | Necessary, no value | 70 |
| Walking to fetch the torque wrench | Waste (could be placed at station) | 50 |
| Talking to colleague | Waste | 40 |
| Waiting for the next part to arrive | Waste | 50 |
| Final inspection of assembly | Necessary, no value | 30 |

**Required:**
1. Classify each activity.
2. Compute the three time components ($T_e$, $T_s$, $T_i$).
3. Compute the Effective Ratio, Support Ratio, Waste Ratio.
4. Compute the utilisation index and Lean value-add ratio.
5. If method study eliminates 70% of the waste, what is the new cycle time and the percentage improvement in waste?

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Classification**
- Tightening bolts → **Effective**
- Fetching bolts, Holding part, Final inspection → **Supportive**
- Walking for wrench, Talking, Waiting for parts → **Incidental**

**Step 2 — Aggregate the time components.**

$$
T_e = 180 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_s = 60 + 70 + 30 = 160 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_i = 50 + 40 + 50 = 140 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the partition.**

$$
T_e + T_s + T_i = 180 + 160 + 140 = 480 \text{ min} = T_{total} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the three ratios.**

$$
r_e = \frac{180}{480} \times 100 = 37.5\%
$$

$$
r_s = \frac{160}{480} \times 100 = 33.33\%
$$

$$
r_i = \frac{140}{480} \times 100 = 29.17\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the utilisation index.**

$$
\eta_{util} = \frac{T_e + T_s}{T_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{180 + 160}{480} \times 100 = 70.83\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Lean value-add ratio.**

$$
\eta_{lean} = \frac{T_e}{T_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{180}{480} \times 100 = 37.5\%
$$

**Step 7 — Apply 70% waste reduction.**

$$
\text{New Incidental Time} = T_i \times (1 - 0.70) = 140 \times 0.30 = 42 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 8 — New cycle time for the equivalent production quantity.**

$$
T_{cycle,new} = T_e + T_s + T_{i,new} = 180 + 160 + 42 = 382 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 9 — Percentage improvement in cycle time.**

$$
\text{Improvement \%} = \frac{T_{before} - T_{after}}{T_{before}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Improvement \%} = \frac{480 - 382}{480} \times 100 = \frac{98}{480} \times 100 = 20.42\%
$$

**Step 10 — New Lean value-add ratio.**

$$
\eta_{lean,new} = \frac{180}{382} \times 100 = 47.12\%
$$

> This is a **9.62 percentage-point** jump in value-adding efficiency — typical of low-hanging waste elimination.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Classify Any Activity

The following sequential decision logic is the **practitioner-grade algorithm** used by industrial engineers during a method-study observation.

| Step | Question to Ask | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Does the activity **physically/chemically change** the object? | Go to Step 2 | Go to Step 3 |
| 2 | **Is the change what the customer pays for?** | **EFFECTIVE** | Go to Step 3 |
| 3 | **Is the activity required for the current method to function?** | **SUPPORTIVE** | Go to Step 4 |
| 4 | **Could the activity be removed with no impact on output, safety, or method?** | **INCIDENTAL** | Re-evaluate from Step 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Step-2 question is the **Lean value-add test**. It is the single most diagnostic question in the entire classification system and is frequently asked as a 3-mark definition question in KTU Part A.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the three classifications of activities used in Work Study and give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Activities in Work Study are classified into three categories per the ILO standard:
- **Effective Activity:** An operation that *adds direct value* to the product. *Example:* Welding two steel plates in a fabrication shop.
- **Necessary/Supportive Activity:** An activity that *does not add value but is required* for the method to function. *Example:* Fetching the welding rod from the rack.
- **Incidental Activity:** An activity that *neither adds value nor is necessary*. *Example:* Idle conversation with a co-worker during machine cycle.

> The classification is the first step in any method-study exercise because it isolates *value-adding* from *waste* time. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Effective and Supportive activities with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**
The key differentiator is the **value-add criterion**:
- *Effective* activities **change** the object in a way the customer values — e.g., painting a finished product.
- *Supportive* activities are **enablers** that do not alter the object but cannot be skipped with the current method — e.g., holding the part while painting.
- An activity moves from *Supportive* to *Incidental* when a *method change* (e.g., using a fixture to hold the part) eliminates the need for human holding. Hence the classification is **method-dependent**, not absolute. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 2 Model Paper]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain in detail the classification of activities based on **(i) contribution to value addition** and **(ii) frequency of occurrence**, with examples for each.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification by Contribution to Value Addition (ILO 3-tier):**
- **Effective/Productive** — Adds direct value. E.g., drilling a hole in a bracket.
- **Supportive/Necessary** — Does not add value but is essential. E.g., clamping the bracket in the vice.
- **Incidental/Waste** — Adds neither. E.g., idle waiting between cycles.

This classification enables calculation of **Effective Ratio** $r_e = T_e / T_{total}$ and the **Waste Ratio** $r_i = T_i / T_{total}$. The first goal of method study is to drive $r_i \to 0$.

**(ii) Classification by Frequency of Occurrence:**
- **Repetitive** — Occurs every cycle (e.g., tightening a bolt on every unit).
- **Occasional** — Occurs at fixed intervals (e.g., lubrication every 100 units).
- **Random** — Occurs unpredictably (e.g., tool breakage).
- **One-time** — Occurs only at start (e.g., machine setup).

Frequency classification drives *prioritisation* in method improvement: repetitive activities yield the highest cumulative savings.

**(7 marks = 3 for value-add + 3 for frequency + 1 for example)**

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A worker is observed for 8 hours (480 min). The breakdown of activities is given below. Classify them, compute the Effective Ratio and the Waste Ratio, and suggest two method improvements to reduce the waste.

| Activity | Time (min) | Your Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Drilling 6 holes on a plate | 200 | |
| Picking up drill bit from rack | 40 | |
| Clamping the plate in the vice | 30 | |
| Talking to the supervisor | 25 | |
| Idle waiting for next plate | 55 | |
| Cleaning chips from workpiece | 30 | |
| Inspecting hole diameter | 20 | |
| Walking to a distant tool crib | 80 | |

**Model Answer:**

| Activity | Time (min) | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Drilling 6 holes | 200 | **Effective** |
| Picking up drill bit | 40 | **Supportive** |
| Clamping plate | 30 | **Supportive** |
| Talking to supervisor | 25 | **Incidental** |
| Idle waiting for next plate | 55 | **Incidental** |
| Cleaning chips | 30 | **Supportive** |
| Inspecting hole | 20 | **Supportive** |
| Walking to tool crib | 80 | **Incidental** |

**Compute totals:**

$$
T_e = 200 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_s = 40 + 30 + 30 + 20 = 120 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_i = 25 + 55 + 80 = 160 \text{ min}
$$

**Check:** $200 + 120 + 160 = 480$ min $\checkmark$

**Ratios:**

$$
r_e = \frac{200}{480} \times 100 = 41.67\%
$$

$$
r_i = \frac{160}{480} \times 100 = 33.33\%
$$

**Two Method Improvements:**
1. **Relocate the tool crib closer to the workstation** (or place a small tool rack at the station) to eliminate the 80-min walking waste.
2. **Implement a kanban / part-buffer system** so the next plate is always available, eliminating the 55-min idle wait.
3. **Restrict personal conversations to break time** through supervisor policy to remove the 25-min incidental.

After these improvements, waste could fall to ≈ 25 min, raising $r_e$ to ≈ 44.2 %.

**(7 marks = 2 table + 2 calculation + 3 method improvements)**

---

### Question B — Alternative (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a neat classification chart, explain **Gilbreth's 17 therbligs** and group them under Effective, Supportive, and Incidental categories. Why is this classification important in **micro-motion study**?

**Model Answer:**

Frank Gilbreth identified **17 fundamental motions (therbligs)** that constitute any manual work. They are:

| # | Therblig | Symbol | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Search | SH | Supportive |
| 2 | Find | FN | Incidental |
| 3 | Select | ST | Supportive |
| 4 | Grasp | G | Effective |
| 5 | Hold | H | Supportive |
| 6 | Transport Loaded | TL | Effective |
| 7 | Position | P | Effective |
| 8 | Assemble | A | Effective |
| 9 | Disassemble | DA | Supportive |
| 10 | Use | U | Effective |
| 11 | Inspect | I | Supportive |
| 12 | Pre-position | PP | Supportive |
| 13 | Release Load | RL | Supportive |
| 14 | Transport Empty | TE | Supportive |
| 15 | Rest | R | Incidental |
| 16 | Unavoidable Delay | UD | Incidental |
| 17 | Avoidable Delay | AD | Incidental |
| 18 | Plan | PN | Supportive |

*Note: Some taxonomies combine Search+Find, giving 17 total. Both versions are accepted.*

**Importance in Micro-Motion Study:**
- Allows decomposition of any manual task into a standardised library of micro-motions.
- Each therblig has a **predetermined time** (via PMTS systems like MTM or MOST).
- By classifying each therblig as Effective/Supportive/Incidental, the engineer can target *Eliminate the Incidental (Find, Delays), Combine the Supportive, and Optimise the Effective*.
- It enables the construction of **Simo Charts** and **Two-Hand Process Charts** with quantitative improvement targets.

**(7 marks = 2 list + 3 classification + 2 importance)**

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A process has the following time distribution: Effective = 35 %, Supportive = 40 %, Incidental = 25 %. If a method-improvement program eliminates 60 % of the incidental time, redistribute the saved time to reduce supportive time by 25 % (the rest is added to productive time). Compute the **new ratios** and the **percentage improvement in cycle time** for a 1000-unit batch where the original total time per unit is 10 minutes.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Original component times (per unit).**

$$
T_{e,old} = 0.35 \times 10 = 3.5 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_{s,old} = 0.40 \times 10 = 4.0 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_{i,old} = 0.25 \times 10 = 2.5 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate 60 % of incidental time.**

$$
T_{i,new} = 2.5 \times (1 - 0.60) = 1.0 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Time saved from incidental = 1.5 min.**

**Step 4 — Reduce supportive time by 25 % of the saved time.**

$$
T_{s,new} = 4.0 - (0.25 \times 1.5) = 4.0 - 0.375 = 3.625 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Remaining 75 % of saved time added to effective.**

$$
T_{e,new} = 3.5 + (0.75 \times 1.5) = 3.5 + 1.125 = 4.625 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 6 — New total time per unit.**

$$
T_{new} = 4.625 + 3.625 + 1.0 = 9.25 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 7 — New ratios.**

$$
r_{e,new} = \frac{4.625}{9.25} \times 100 = 50.0\%
$$

$$
r_{s,new} = \frac{3.625}{9.25} \times 100 = 39.19\%
$$

$$
r_{i,new} = \frac{1.0}{9.25} \times 100 = 10.81\%
$$

**Step 8 — Improvement in cycle time per unit.**

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{10 - 9.25}{10} \times 100 = 7.5\%
$$

**Step 9 — Improvement on 1000-unit batch.**

$$
\text{Saved time} = 0.75 \text{ min/unit} \times 1000 = 750 \text{ min} = 12.5 \text{ hours}
$$

**(7 marks = 1 setup + 2 elimination + 2 redistribution + 2 ratios + improvement verification)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Classification-of-Activities Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Supportive" with "Incidental":** Fetching a tool is *Supportive* (you need it for the work); walking to a *wrong* tool crib is *Incidental* (caused by poor layout). The criterion is **method-necessity**, not personal effort.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify the time partition:** Always show $T_e + T_s + T_i = T_{total}$. Examiners allocate 1 mark for this check.
> 3. **Quoting percentages without "of what":** "Effective ratio is 37.5 %" without specifying *37.5 % of total time* loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Mixing the three-tier and Gilbreth classifications:** They are *complementary*, not interchangeable. Use ILO for total time analysis, therbligs for micro-motion.
> 5. **Assuming classification is absolute:** In reality, an activity can move between categories after a *method change*. State this in your answer to earn the application-level marks.
> 6. **Skipping the "real-world example" in 14-mark answers:** A 14-mark Part (a) without an example is docked 1 mark by most KTU valuators.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classification of Activities**

- **Three-Tier ILO Classification:** *Effective → Supportive → Incidental.* Memorise the order and the test for each.
- **Conservation of Time Law:** $T_e + T_s + T_i = T_{total}$ and $r_e + r_s + r_i = 1$ — *inviolable.*
- **Key Ratios:** Effective Ratio, Waste Ratio, Utilisation Index, Lean Value-Add Ratio — formulae must be at your fingertips.
- **Method-Dependency:** The classification of an activity is *not absolute*; it depends on the current method, layout, and tooling.
- **Gilbreth's 17 Therbligs:** 5 Effective · 9 Supportive · 3 Incidental (with rest/delay/plan nuances).
- **Process-Chart Symbol Mapping:** Operation = typically Effective; Transport/Inspection = often Supportive; Delay/Storage = usually Incidental.
- **Frequency Classification:** Repetitive (high priority) → Occasional → Random (buffers needed) → One-time (project plan).
- **Source of Effort Classification:** Manual · Machine · Combined — affects standard-time formulation.
- **Step-2 Lean Test:** *"Is the change what the customer pays for?"* — the single most diagnostic question in classification.
- **Improvement Strategy Order:** **Eliminate Incidental first → Minimise Supportive next → Preserve and Optimise Effective.**
- **Numerical Problems:** Always show (1) classification table, (2) summed components, (3) verification check, (4) ratio calculation, (5) actionable improvement suggestions.
- **Common Error to Avoid:** "Fetching a tool" = Supportive; "Walking for a misplaced tool" = Incidental. The difference is *layout quality.*
- **Higher-Order Thinking:** When asked "How can the ratio be improved?", always answer with *method change examples* (fixture, layout, kanban, automation) — not vague statements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representation of Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Activity Representation** in Work System Design refers to the systematic, standardized graphical and diagrammatic techniques used to record, analyze, and communicate the sequence of operations, inspections, transports, delays, and storages performed on a workpiece or by a worker/machine during the execution of a process. These representations form the backbone of **Method Study** and are essential tools for visualizing the flow of work, identifying non-value-adding elements, and designing improved methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> Activity representation is the **charting and diagramming** phase of Method Study. It transforms the *physical reality of work* into *standardized paper-based records* using globally accepted symbols (proposed by the **American Society of Mechanical Engineers – ASME**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Film Script of Work"

Imagine you are directing a movie but you cannot see the actors. The only way to understand the story is to read the **script** — a sequential, symbolic record of who does what, when, and where.

- Each **symbol** in work study (○, ☐, ⬇, D, ▽) is a *line in the script*.
- The **chart** is the *script page* that shows the chronological story of the job.
- The **analyst** is the *director*, who reads the script to find scenes that are too long, redundant, or illogical — and rewrites them for a better movie.

Without these symbols and charts, analyzing a job is like trying to direct a film by just listening to the sound — you miss the visuals of motion, waiting, and transportation.

## 1.3 Why is Activity Representation Needed?

| Purpose | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Visibility** | Makes invisible mental and physical activities visible. |
| **Standardization** | Allows global communication (one operator in Japan can read a chart from India). |
| **Analysis** | Forms the baseline for critical examination. |
| **Cost Reduction** | Highlights delays & transport, which add no value. |
| **Documentation** | Acts as legal/quality record of approved methods. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term – "Value-Adding" vs "Non-Value-Adding"**: Out of the five basic activities, only **Operation** and sometimes **Inspection** add value. **Transport, Delay, and Storage** are the three *non-value-adding* activities that representation techniques are designed to expose.

## 1.4 The Five Fundamental Symbols of Activity Representation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These **ASME Standard Symbols** are the *alphabet* of Work System Design. Master them — every chart, every diagram, and every Part B answer revolves around them.

| Symbol | Shape | Activity | Value-Added? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **○ (Circle)** | Small circle | **Operation** | YES |
| **☐ (Square)** | Square | **Inspection** | YES (sometimes) |
| **→ (Arrow)** | Thick arrow | **Transport / Movement** | NO |
| **D (Capital D)** | Letter D | **Delay / Temporary Storage** | NO |
| **▽ (Triangle)** | Inverted triangle | **Permanent Storage** | NO |

A **combined symbol** (a circle inside a square, ○/☐) represents an operation *combined* with inspection — meaning the worker performs and inspects in one step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Activity Representation Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these techniques into two broad families:

### A. Process Charts (Record WHAT happens to the *material/workpiece*)

1. **Outline Process Chart** – Lists only the *main operations* and inspections in sequence; ignores transport, delay, and storage. Used at the *early stage* of method study to get a quick overview.
2. **Flow Process Chart** – The most commonly asked chart. Records *all five* events in chronological order. Three variants:
   - **Material-type** (tracks the workpiece)
   - **Man-type** (tracks the worker — e.g., doctor making ward rounds)
   - **Equipment/Machine-type** (tracks the machine)
3. **Two-Handed Process Chart (Right-Hand/Left-Hand Chart)** – Simultaneously records the activities of both hands of a single operator on a symmetrical layout.

### B. Activity Diagrams (Record WHEN events happen — *time & motion*)

1. **Multiple Activity Chart (Man-Machine Chart)** – Plots operator and machine activities on a common time scale to find idle time of either.
2. **Travel Chart** – A scaled floor plan showing paths of workers/materials as straight lines between workstations.
3. **Simo Chart (Simultaneous Motion Cycle Chart)** – Micro-motion study; records therbligs (17 basic body motions) of both hands at *very small time scales* (typically 0.01 s).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Tip**: In Part B, questions often ask: *"Prepare a Flow Process Chart and a Two-Handed Process Chart for the given job and suggest improvements."* Memorize the symbols and the layout — they are *directly evaluated* for **3 to 5 marks** in the chart-drawing step alone.

## 2.2 Construction Logic of a Flow Process Chart

The construction follows a **6-step logical flow**:

1. **Define the boundary** — Decide the *start* and *end* of the process under study (e.g., "from receipt of raw blank to packed finished component").
2. **Observe the actual work** — Use direct observation or video recording; do not rely on memory.
3. **Record entries chronologically** — Each row corresponds to one event: a column for *distance moved*, *time taken*, and a *remarks* column.
4. **Apply the correct symbol** — Strict adherence to ASME standards.
5. **Summarize totals** — Count operations, inspections, transports, delays, storages; sum up time and distance.
6. **Critically examine & question** — Apply the *5W 1H 2W* technique (What? Why? Where? When? Who? How? How much?).

## 2.3 Two-Handed Process Chart — Layout Principle

- Page is divided **vertically into two equal columns** — Left column for *Left Hand*, Right column for *Right Hand*.
- A **central vertical line** holds the activity symbols; the *symbol is drawn in the column of the hand performing it*.
- A **joint symbol** (e.g., circle bisected by the line) denotes *both hands* working together.
- A **horizontal scale (time)** is drawn at the bottom in standard units (seconds or minutes).
- **Delay symbols (D)** are placed against the idle hand — exposing imbalance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters**: The chart reveals the **idle time of the slower hand**, which is the foundation for **workstation redesign** (rebalancing, jig/fixture introduction, foot-pedal use, etc.).

## 2.4 Multiple Activity Chart (Man-Machine Chart) — Layout Principle

- **Two horizontal bars** stacked vertically: one for *Operator*, one for *Machine*.
- **Common horizontal time axis** (e.g., seconds, with 1 cm = 1 s).
- Vertical lines separate each *cycle* of the machine.
- **Cross-hatching / different shades** distinguish *working* vs *idle* periods.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are the most-tested calculations in KTU University Exams for activity representation. **Memorize the table** — they appear in nearly every 14-mark Part B question involving man-machine charts.

| Metric | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operator Cycle Time (T_op)** | $\text{Total time of one operator cycle}$ | seconds | Time between two consecutive starts of the operator |
| **Machine Cycle Time (T_m)** | $\text{Total time of one machine cycle}$ | seconds | Time between two consecutive auto-starts of the machine |
| **Effective Working Time (EWT)** | $\sum \text{(Working periods of operator)}$ | seconds | Sum of all working intervals in the chart |
| **Operator Idle Time** | $\text{Total chart time} - \text{EWT of operator}$ | seconds | Waiting time of the operator |
| **Machine Idle Time** | $\text{Total chart time} - \text{EWT of machine}$ | seconds | Waiting time of the machine |
| **Operator Utilization (η_op)** | $\dfrac{\text{EWT of operator}}{\text{Total chart time}} \times 100$ | % | Percentage of time operator is productive |
| **Machine Utilization (η_m)** | $\dfrac{\text{EWT of machine}}{\text{Total chart time}} \times 100$ | % | Percentage of time machine is productive |
| **Number of Machines an Operator Can Tend (n)** | $\dfrac{\text{T_op}}{\text{T_m}}$ (rounded down) | integer | Key result of man-machine balancing |
| **Balanced Cycle Time (T_balanced)** | $\text{LCM} \left( \text{T_op}, \text{T_m} \right)$ | seconds | Time after which the pattern perfectly repeats |
| **Total Production in T_balanced** | $\dfrac{\text{T_balanced}}{\text{T_m}}$ | pieces | Output per balanced cycle |
| **Throughput / Hour** | $\dfrac{3600 \times \text{Total Production}}{\text{T_balanced}}$ | pieces/hr | Production rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Rule (KTU Valuation)**: If the calculated *n* has a fraction (e.g., 2.7), the **floor value (2)** is the actual number of machines tended. The decimal remainder (0.7) is the *overlap margin* that an engineer uses to add a *small auxiliary task* to fully load the operator.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Activity Representation

- **Automotive Assembly Lines** — Multiple Activity Charts determine the *optimal number of machines* an operator can manage, directly affecting plant layout cost.
- **Hospital Workflow Design** — Man-type Flow Process Charts track a *nurse* through 6 hours of ward rounds; delays of 45 minutes are exposed and eliminated.
- **CNC Machining Cells** — Two-Handed Charts reveal that one hand waits during tool-change; *foot-pedal chucks* are introduced as the improvement.
- **Ergonomic Redesign** — Simo Charts identify 17 wasteful *therbligs* (e.g., *search*, *select*, *hold*) that a robot can eliminate entirely.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **complete worked-out analytical chain** for the two most important derivations in this module — (A) Man-Machine balancing for *n* and Throughput, and (B) Critical examination of a Two-Handed chart to compute *hand balance ratio*.

## 3.1 Derivation: Man-Machine Balancing — Finding the Number of Machines an Operator Can Tend (n)

### Step 1 — Define the parameters from the observed chart

Let:
- $T_{op}$ = Operator's total cycle time (seconds)
- $T_m$ = Machine's automatic cycle time (seconds)
- $t_{l}$ = Loading time (operator places workpiece on machine)
- $t_{u}$ = Unloading time (operator removes finished part)
- $t_{s}$ = Supervising time (operator watches machine running)

The total operator time in one machine cycle is:

$$
T_{op} = t_{l} + t_{s} + t_{u}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the number of machines tended

The operator's available time per machine equals the machine's automatic cycle $T_m$. If one operator services multiple identical machines arranged in a "ring" topology, the operator can service:

$$
n = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{op}}{T_m} \right\rfloor
$$

where $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ denotes the *greatest integer function* (floor).

### Step 3 — Compute the balanced (repeat) cycle time

The chart will perfectly repeat only at the **Least Common Multiple** of $T_{op}$ and $T_m$:

$$
T_{bal} = \text{LCM}\left( T_{op},\; T_m \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Compute total production in one balanced cycle

$$
N_{bal} = \frac{T_{bal}}{T_m}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute throughput (pieces per hour)

$$
\eta_{thr} = \frac{3600 \times N_{bal}}{T_{bal}} = \frac{3600}{T_m} \quad \text{pieces/hr}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: Throughput is *governed by the slower of the two* — typically the machine cycle $T_m$ in fully automatic cells. The operator's *only* role is to keep the machine fed; once the operator's *n* is matched to the machine's $T_m$, the cell is *balanced*.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Given**: Operator cycle $T_{op} = 42$ s (Loading = 10 s, Supervising = 22 s, Unloading = 10 s); Machine automatic cycle $T_m = 28$ s.
> **Find**: $n$, $T_{bal}$, total pieces in one balanced cycle, throughput per hour, operator and machine utilization.

**Solution**:

**Step A — Compute n:**

$$
n = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{op}}{T_m} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{42}{28} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 1.5 \rfloor = 1
$$

So **one operator can tend one machine** in this case. The operator has **14 s of slack** (0.5 × 28) — opportunity to do auxiliary work.

**Step B — Compute $T_{bal}$:**

$$
T_{bal} = \text{LCM}(42, 28) = 84 \text{ s}
$$

**Step C — Total production in $T_{bal}$:**

$$
N_{bal} = \frac{84}{28} = 3 \text{ pieces}
$$

**Step D — Throughput per hour:**

$$
\eta_{thr} = \frac{3600 \times 3}{84} = 128.57 \approx 129 \text{ pieces/hr}
$$

**Step E — Operator utilization:**

$$
\eta_{op} = \frac{T_{op}}{T_{bal}} \times 100 = \frac{42}{84} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step F — Machine utilization:**

$$
\eta_{m} = \frac{T_{bal}}{T_{bal}} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

The machine is fully loaded; the operator is **50% utilized** — a *classic imbalance* requiring either a faster cycle, an auxiliary task, or a second machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Enhancement Tip for KTU 14-Mark Answers**: If a *second identical machine* is added, $T_{op}$ scales to $2 T_m = 56$ s, leaving $84 - 56 = 28$ s margin — still not enough for $T_{op} = 42$ s for *two* machines. The cell can never balance with $T_{op} = 42$ s and $T_m = 28$ s for $n = 2$. Therefore the only way to use two machines is to *reduce $T_{op}$ to 28 s* through jig/fixture redesign.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hand Balance Ratio (HBR) from a Two-Handed Chart

The **Hand Balance Ratio** quantifies how evenly both hands are loaded. It is a *new metric* often asked in KTU Part B (for 7 marks) when a Two-Handed Chart is given.

### Step 1 — Total Left-Hand Working Time

$$
T_L = \sum \text{(Working time of all left-hand activities)}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Right-Hand Working Time

$$
T_R = \sum \text{(Working time of all right-hand activities)}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Hand Balance Ratio

$$
\text{HBR} = \frac{\min(T_L, T_R)}{\max(T_L, T_R)} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation of HBR

| HBR Value | Workload Balance | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **100%** | Perfect balance | Both hands equally loaded — ideal workstation |
| **75% – 99%** | Good balance | Minor improvement possible |
| **50% – 74%** | Moderate imbalance | Redesign one hand's task |
| **< 50%** | Severe imbalance | Major ergonomic redesign required |

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Given**: From a Two-Handed Chart of a PCB assembly task: Left hand works for 38 s, Right hand works for 26 s, both within a 45 s cycle.
> **Find**: HBR, idle time of each hand.

**Solution**:

**Step A — HBR:**

$$
\text{HBR} = \frac{26}{38} \times 100\% = 68.4\%
$$

**Step B — Idle time of Left Hand:**

$$
\text{Idle}_L = 45 - 38 = 7 \text{ s}
$$

**Step C — Idle time of Right Hand:**

$$
\text{Idle}_R = 45 - 26 = 19 \text{ s}
$$

**Step D — Imbalance severity:**

HBR = 68.4% → **Moderate imbalance**. The right hand waits 19 s; the engineer should *redesign* the right-hand task by introducing a *holding fixture* so the right hand is freed for a productive auxiliary operation.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Chart to Use?

| Situation | Best Chart to Use |
|---|---|
| Track the journey of a *raw material* through a workshop | **Flow Process Chart (Material-type)** |
| Track a *nurse/doctor/maintenance crew* moving between patients/machines | **Flow Process Chart (Man-type)** |
| Quick overview of *main operations only* | **Outline Process Chart** |
| Analyze *both hands of a single operator* at a bench | **Two-Handed Process Chart** |
| Analyze *one operator + one machine* on a common time line | **Multiple Activity Chart** |
| Visualize *movement paths on a shop floor* | **Travel Chart** |
| Analyze *microscopic hand motions* (less than 0.1 s) for very short jobs | **Simo Chart** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Symbol Library – Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
nodeStart[START of Process] --> nodeOp
nodeOp[OPERATION - Circle Symbol - Adds Value to Material] --> nodeInsp
nodeInsp[INSPECTION - Square Symbol - Verifies Quality] --> nodeTrn
nodeTrn[TRANSPORT - Arrow Symbol - Moves Material - NO Value] --> nodeDel
nodeDel[DELAY - Letter D - Temporary Wait - NO Value] --> nodeSto
nodeSto[STORAGE - Inverted Triangle - Long-term Hold - NO Value] --> nodeEnd[END of Process]

style nodeOp fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
style nodeInsp fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
style nodeTrn fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
style nodeDel fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
style nodeSto fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
style nodeStart fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
style nodeEnd fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

## 4.2 Two-Handed Process Chart – Functional Layout

```mermaid
graph TD
subgraph leftCol[LEFT HAND COLUMN]
lhA[Pick up blank from tray - t=0 to 4s] --> lhB[Hold blank vertically - t=4 to 8s] --> lhC[Idle - waiting for right hand - t=8 to 14s] --> lhD[Place part in box - t=14 to 18s]
end
subgraph rightCol[RIGHT HAND COLUMN]
rhA[Idle - waiting - t=0 to 4s] --> rhB[Pick up tool - t=4 to 8s] --> rhC[Drill hole on blank - t=8 to 14s] --> rhD[Return tool to rack - t=14 to 18s]
end
lhA -.- rhA
lhB -.- rhB
lhC -.- rhC
lhD -.- rhD

style lhA fill:#bbdefb
style lhB fill:#bbdefb
style lhC fill:#ffcdd2
style lhD fill:#bbdefb
style rhA fill:#ffcdd2
style rhB fill:#bbdefb
style rhC fill:#c8e6c9
style rhD fill:#bbdefb
```

## 4.3 Multiple Activity Chart – Man-Machine Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
subgraph operator[OPERATOR BAR]
opA[Load blank on machine - t=0 to 10s] --> opB[Supervise automatic cycle - t=10 to 32s] --> opC[Unload finished part - t=32 to 42s] --> opIdle[Idle - 42 to 60s]
end
subgraph machine[MACHINE BAR]
mcA[Idle - waiting for load - t=0 to 10s] --> mcB[Automatic drilling cycle - t=10 to 32s] --> mcC[Idle - waiting for unload - t=32 to 42s] --> mcD[Next load arrives - t=42 to 60s = wait + reset]
end
opA -.- mcA
opB -.- mcB
opC -.- mcC
opIdle -.- mcD

style opA fill:#c8e6c9
style opB fill:#c8e6c9
style opC fill:#c8e6c9
style opIdle fill:#ffcdd2
style mcA fill:#ffcdd2
style mcB fill:#bbdefb
style mcC fill:#ffcdd2
style mcD fill:#fff9c4
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree – Choosing the Right Representation

```mermaid
graph TD
qStart[What do you want to record?] --> q1{Is the focus on the MATERIAL or the WORKER moving through a process?}
q1 -->|Material moves| q2{Do you need a quick overview or full detail?}
q1 -->|Worker moves| q3[Use MAN-TYPE Flow Process Chart]
q2 -->|Quick overview| q4[Use OUTLINE Process Chart]
q2 -->|Full detail with all 5 symbols| q5[Use MATERIAL-TYPE Flow Process Chart]
qStart --> q6{Is the focus on the MOTION of HANDS at a single workstation?}
q6 -->|Yes - both hands| q7[Use TWO-HANDED Process Chart]
q6 -->|Yes - micro motion in seconds| q8[Use SIMO Chart]
qStart --> q9{Is the focus on TIME-SYNCHRONIZATION of operator and machine?}
q9 -->|Yes| q10[Use MULTIPLE ACTIVITY Chart - Man-Machine Chart]
qStart --> q11{Is the focus on PHYSICAL PATHS on the shop floor?}
q11 -->|Yes| q12[Use TRAVEL CHART on scaled layout]

style qStart fill:#fff9c4
style q5 fill:#c8e6c9
style q7 fill:#c8e6c9
style q10 fill:#c8e6c9
style q12 fill:#c8e6c9
style q3 fill:#c8e6c9
style q4 fill:#c8e6c9
style q8 fill:#c8e6c9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – April 2024]`**

### Q1. List any **five standard symbols** used in activity representation with a one-line meaning of each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **○ (Circle) – Operation:** A purposeful change in the physical or chemical characteristics of the workpiece.
2. **☐ (Square) – Inspection:** An activity that verifies the quality or quantity of an object without altering it.
3. **→ (Arrow) – Transport:** Movement of the workpiece from one location to another (non-value-adding).
4. **D (Capital D) – Delay:** A temporary wait where no operation or inspection occurs (non-value-adding).
5. **▽ (Inverted Triangle) – Storage:** A long-term holding of the workpiece under controlled conditions (non-value-adding).

A sixth optional symbol is the **combined operation-and-inspection symbol** (○/☐).

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

### Q2. Differentiate between an **Outline Process Chart** and a **Flow Process Chart**.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Outline Process Chart | Flow Process Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbols used** | Only Operation and Inspection | All five symbols (Op, Insp, Trn, Delay, Storage) |
| **Detail level** | Macro-level summary | Micro-level exhaustive record |
| **Time/Distance columns** | Not present | Time, distance, and remarks columns present |
| **Use** | Early stage of method study, quick overview | Full method study, detailed analysis, and improvement |
| **Complexity** | Simple to construct | Time-consuming; requires careful observation |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *Option 1*

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction of a Two-Handed Process Chart**. State its applications and limitations. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Two-Handed Process Chart (also called *Right-Hand/Left-Hand Chart*) is a synchronized, mirror-image diagram that records the activities of *both hands* of a single operator on a *common time scale* during the performance of a repetitive job at a workstation.

**Construction Steps (3 marks):**

1. The chart paper is divided into **three vertical sections**:
   - **Left column** – symbols for the *Left Hand*.
   - **Central column** – narrow strip for *joint activities* (when both hands do the same thing) and to hold a vertical reference line.
   - **Right column** – symbols for the *Right Hand*.
2. A **time scale** is drawn at the bottom (e.g., 1 cm = 1 second).
3. The job is observed, and each hand's activity is recorded in its column with the *correct ASME symbol* (○, ☐, D, etc.).
4. For **joint work** (e.g., both hands lifting a heavy plate), the symbol is drawn **on the central line**.
5. **Idle periods** of either hand are marked with the **D symbol** in the column of the *idle* hand.
6. A *summary box* is added at the bottom showing **total working time** of each hand and **idle time** of each hand.

**Applications (2 marks):**

- Identifying **hand imbalance** and redesigning the workstation.
- Improving **ergonomic efficiency** of repetitive bench jobs (e.g., assembly, packing, PCB soldering).
- Detecting **wasteful micro-motions** like *search, hold, and select*.
- Setting the foundation for **workstation layout, jigs, and fixtures**.
- Training new operators on the *preferred method*.

**Limitations (1 mark):**

- Applicable only to **short-cycle, repetitive jobs** (typically < 2 min).
- Cannot record **leg, foot, or body movements** in detail.
- Construction is **time-consuming** and requires high skill in motion classification.

---

**Q. (b)** A machine has an **automatic cycle of 30 s**. An operator takes **10 s to load**, the **machine runs automatically for 30 s**, and the operator then takes **8 s to unload** and **12 s to inspect** the part. Construct a **Multiple Activity Chart**, find the **number of machines** the operator can tend, and compute the **operator and machine utilization**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify operator and machine activities (1 mark):**

- $t_{l} = 10$ s (Load), $t_s = 30$ s (Supervise during auto-cycle), $t_{inspect} = 12$ s (Inspect finished part), $t_{u} = 8$ s (Unload).
- $T_{op} = 10 + 30 + 12 + 8 = 60$ s
- $T_m = 30$ s (auto-cycle)

**Step 2 — Construct the chart conceptually (2 marks):**

| Time (s) | 0–10 | 10–40 | 40–48 | 48–60 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operator** | Load | Supervise | Inspect | Unload |
| **Machine** | Idle | Auto-run | Idle (next load) | Idle (continues) |

**Step 3 — Compute number of machines tended (1 mark):**

$$
n = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{op}}{T_m} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{60}{30} \right\rfloor = 2
$$

The operator can tend **2 machines**.

**Step 4 — Operator and Machine utilization (3 marks):**

For *n* = 2 machines, the operator's effective time per cycle becomes $T_{op} = 60$ s, and total time per cycle is $T_{bal} = 60$ s (since $T_m = 30$ s and 2 cycles fit in 60 s).

$$
\eta_{op} = \frac{T_{op}}{n \cdot T_m} \times 100 = \frac{60}{2 \times 30} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{m} = \frac{T_m}{T_{op}} \times 100 = \frac{30}{60} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

(Each machine is busy only 30 s out of the 60-s operator cycle.)

**Conclusion:** The operator is **fully loaded**; the machines are **50% utilized**. To improve machine utilization, the *automatic cycle* should be increased (e.g., by adding a second operation) to bring $T_m$ closer to **60 s**.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Option 2 (Internal Choice)*

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Multiple Activity Chart** in detail. How is it used to find the **number of machines an operator can tend**? **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Multiple Activity Chart (also called a *Man-Machine Chart* or *Simultaneous Activity Chart*) is a graphical record that displays the activities of **one or more operators** and **one or more machines** on a **common time axis**, side by side, for one or more cycles of the operation.

**Construction Steps (2 marks):**

1. The chart is drawn with **horizontal bars** — one for each *operator* and one for each *machine*.
2. The **horizontal axis** represents time (with a chosen scale, e.g., 1 cm = 1 s or 5 s).
3. **Vertical lines** are drawn at the end of every cycle.
4. *Working* periods are marked with **solid lines / dark shade**; *idle* periods are marked with **blank or cross-hatched** segments.
5. The chart is constructed for *one complete balanced cycle* and then *extended* to a balanced repeat time.

**Procedure to find n (3 marks):**

1. Calculate the operator's total cycle time $T_{op} = t_l + t_s + t_u$.
2. Calculate the machine's automatic cycle time $T_m$.
3. Compute $n = \left\lfloor T_{op} / T_m \right\rfloor$.
4. Plot the chart for the *n machines*; check that the operator never has overlapping tasks and the machines are not starved.
5. Compute the *balanced cycle time* $T_{bal} = \text{LCM}(T_{op}, T_m)$ and the *throughput* per hour.

**Advantages (1 mark):**

- Identifies **idle time of operator and machines** — the most valuable insight in cell design.
- Helps in **deciding the number of machines** to install in a work cell.
- Reveals *opportunities for an operator to tend multiple machines*, increasing labour productivity.

---

**Q. (b)** The following data were obtained for an assembly task. Construct a **Two-Handed Process Chart**, compute the **Hand Balance Ratio (HBR)**, and **suggest two improvements**. **\[7 Marks\]**

| Element | Left Hand Time (s) | Right Hand Time (s) |
|---|---|---|
| Pick blank from tray | 4 | – |
| Hold blank in position | – | 6 |
| Insert screw | – | 5 |
| Tighten screw with screwdriver | 8 | – |
| Place assembly in bin | 5 | – |
| Return tool to rack | – | 3 |

*Total cycle time = 12 s (one complete job)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate left and right hand working times (2 marks):**

- $T_L = 4 + 8 + 5 = 17$ s
- $T_R = 6 + 5 + 3 = 14$ s
- Total working time = 17 + 14 = 31 s across both hands.

**Step 2 — Compute HBR (2 marks):**

$$
\text{HBR} = \frac{\min(T_L, T_R)}{\max(T_L, T_R)} \times 100 = \frac{14}{17} \times 100 = 82.4\%
$$

**Step 3 — Idle time per hand (1 mark):**

The total *cycle* time is 12 s, so the chart must be re-examined. In typical two-handed charts, the two hands *work in parallel* during the 12-s cycle. Therefore, *each hand's working time within one 12-s cycle* is determined by the *activity pattern*, not the simple sum above (which is the sum over multiple cycles).

Assuming the pattern repeats every 12 s with both hands active for the listed times *within* that window, the typical interpretation in KTU is:

- **Left Hand active for ~8 s** (Pick + Tighten + Place) → **Idle = 12 − 8 = 4 s**
- **Right Hand active for ~6 s** (Hold + Insert + Return tool) → **Idle = 12 − 6 = 6 s**

$$
\text{HBR} = \frac{6}{8} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

**Step 4 — Two Improvements (2 marks):**

1. **Use a holding fixture (jig):** Replace the right hand's *hold* action with a *mechanical fixture* that grips the blank. This frees the right hand to perform an *auxiliary task* (e.g., applying thread-locking fluid) — improving right-hand utilization.
2. **Use a power screwdriver / foot-pedal chuck:** Replace the manual *tighten* action (left hand) with a *powered tool* triggered by a foot pedal. This reduces left-hand time and removes the need for a separate "return tool" action.

**Conclusion:** With both improvements, HBR can be raised above **90%** and the *effective cycle time* can be reduced from 12 s to about 8 s — a **33% productivity gain**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–3 Marks per Mistake)**
> 1. **Wrong symbol** in a chart – using a *square* for *operation* costs you 1 mark each time.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the time column** in a Two-Handed Chart – the chart is *invalid* without a horizontal time scale.
> 3. **Mixing up n and T_m** – the *integer* result of $\lfloor T_{op}/T_m \rfloor$ is *n*; do not put it in a percentage formula.
> 4. **Using the raw ratio** $T_{op}/T_m$ (e.g., 1.5) as the *number of machines*. The **floor function** is mandatory.
> 5. **Omitting the summary box** at the end of a Flow Process Chart — examiners deduct **1 mark** specifically for the missing *total time / total distance* summary.
> 6. **Confusing "Permanent Storage (▽)" with "Delay (D)"** — Storage is *controlled and long-term*; Delay is *uncontrolled and short-term*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Five ASME symbols** — ○ (Op), ☐ (Insp), → (Trn), D (Delay), ▽ (Storage) — must be memorized perfectly.
- ✅ Only **Operation and Inspection** are *value-adding*; the other three are *non-value-adding*.
- ✅ **Flow Process Chart** is the *most common* chart in KTU exams — three variants: *Material, Man, Equipment*.
- ✅ **Outline Process Chart** uses only *Op and Insp*; used for *quick overview*.
- ✅ **Two-Handed Process Chart** is divided into *left column* and *right column*; uses a *central line for joint work*; always has a *horizontal time axis*.
- ✅ **Hand Balance Ratio (HBR)** = $\min(T_L, T_R) / \max(T_L, T_R) \times 100$. Ideal value is **100%**.
- ✅ **Multiple Activity Chart (Man-Machine Chart)** plots operator and machine on a *common time axis*.
- ✅ **Number of machines an operator can tend:** $n = \lfloor T_{op}/T_m \rfloor$ (use *floor function*, not simple ratio).
- ✅ **Balanced cycle time:** $T_{bal} = \text{LCM}(T_{op}, T_m)$ — the time after which the entire chart pattern perfectly repeats.
- ✅ **Throughput** is *limited by the slower of the two* — typically the machine cycle $T_m$.
- ✅ **Travel Chart** uses a *scaled floor plan* with straight lines representing movement paths.
- ✅ **Simo Chart** is for *micro-motion* (therbligs, 17 basic body motions) on jobs shorter than ~5 s.
- ✅ **5W 1H 2W** questioning technique is the *next step* after recording the chart.
- ✅ **Critical Examination** = asking *What, Why, Where, When, Who, How, How much* for *every* event on the chart.
- ✅ Always include a **summary box** (totals of time, distance, count of each symbol) at the end of a Flow Process Chart.
- ✅ Mention **at least 2 improvements** in any Part B answer involving a chart — the examiner awards a *dedicated mark* for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Different Charts and Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Different Charts and Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Charts and Diagrams in Work System Design

In **Work System Design**, charts and diagrams constitute the **primary visual language** of *Methods Engineering*. They are standardized graphical tools that record, analyse, and improve the sequence of work performed by **men, machines, or materials** during a process. Each chart abstracts a real production process into a standardized set of symbols, lines, and time bars so that **waste (idle time, redundancy, unnecessary movement)** can be visually detected and eliminated.

### Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charts and Diagrams in Work Study** are predefined schematic representations prescribed by the *British Standards Institution (BS 3138:1979)* and *International Labour Organization (ILO)* for documenting the flow of work, operator movements, and machine–operator relationships. They are classified under the **Recording Techniques** of Methods Analysis.

### Major Charts and Diagrams Covered Under Module 2

| # | Chart / Diagram | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Outline Process Chart | Overall process at a glance |
| 2 | Operation Process Chart | Sequence of operations & inspections |
| 3 | Flow Process Chart (Man / Material / Equipment) | Detailed recording of one subject |
| 4 | Two-Hand Process Chart | Both hands of a single operator |
| 5 | Multiple Activity Chart | Multiple workers / machines together |
| 6 | SIMO Chart | Sub-second micro-motions (therbligs) |
| 7 | String Diagram | Movement patterns over an area |
| 8 | Cycle Graph / Travel Chart | Movement over a time-distance grid |
| 9 | Gang / Worker Chart | Team activity synchronization |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint of Work"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a *Google Maps route view* between two cities. A chart in work design is exactly that — a **map of work**. Just as a road map shows where you wasted kilometres on wrong turns, a process chart shows where the worker wasted seconds on unnecessary steps. The symbols are the *icons*, the lines are the *roads*, and the time values are the *kilometres travelled*.

### 1.3 Standard Symbols (BS 3138 / ASME)

| Symbol | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|
| `○` Operation | A productive task alters the workpiece |
| `▢` Inspection | Checking quality or quantity |
| `→` Transport | Movement of subject between locations |
| `▽` Delay / Storage | Temporary halt (waiting) |
| `D` Combined Op + Insp | Performed together |

> **Mandatory:** All flow process charts must use **vertical lines (top-to-bottom)** with operation symbols drawn **against the vertical line** and others **on the line**, exactly as per KTU reference conventions.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Purpose of Using Charts and Diagrams

Charts and diagrams serve **four core purposes** that examiners consistently test:

1. **Recording** — capturing *what is currently being done* (the "As-Is").
2. **Examining** — critically reviewing each step for necessity.
3. **Improving** — proposing the "Should-Be / Improved" method.
4. **Installing** — communicating the new method to supervisors.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Examine → Develop → Install → Maintain (EDIM) cycle** of methods engineering is the conceptual parent framework within which all charts sit.

## 2.2 Classification Hierarchy

Charts can be categorised along **two axes**:

- **Axis 1 (Scope):** *Process-level* (Outline, Operation, Flow Process) → *Operator-level* (Two-Hand, SIMO) → *Multi-resource* (Multiple Activity, Gang Chart).
- **Axis 2 (Subject tracked):** *Man* / *Material* / *Machine* / *Multiple*.

## 2.3 Selection Logic — Which Chart to Use?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **decision tree** for chart selection (a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU papers):

```
START → Is work repetitive?
        ├─ NO  → Outline Process Chart (top-level overview)
        └─ YES → Is the focus on ONE operator?
                  ├─ YES → Is the cycle short & detailed?
                  │        ├─ YES → SIMO Chart
                  │        └─ NO  → Two-Hand Process Chart
                  └─ NO  → Multiple workers/machines?
                            ├─ YES → Multiple Activity Chart / Gang Chart
                            └─ NO  → Layout analysis? → String Diagram / Cycle Graph
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All numeric problems in Module 2 revolve around the **time-study–based metrics** that appear *inside* the charts (cycle time, rating, allowances). The following cheat-sheet is mandatory memorisation.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Normal Time (NT)} = \text{Observed Time (OT)} \times \text{Rating}/100$ | Performance-rating correction | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $\text{Standard Time (ST)} = \text{NT} \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$ | Adding rest/contingency allowance | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $\text{Allowance} = \text{Allowance \%} \times \text{NT} / 100$ | Recovering fatigue, personal, contingency | s |
| 4 | $\% \text{Utilisation} = \frac{\text{Productive (working) Time}}{\text{Total Available Time}} \times 100$ | How busy the resource was | % |
| 5 | $\text{Activity Ratio} = \frac{\text{NT of productive symbols}}{\text{ST of full cycle}} \times 100$ | Useful work vs total time | % |
| 6 | $\text{No. of Observations } N = \left(\frac{z \cdot s}{L}\right)^{2}$ | Statistical sample size | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Machine Cycle Time (CT)} = \text{Machining Time} + \text{Idle/Handling Time}$ | For Multiple Activity Chart evaluation | s |
| 8 | $\text{Balance Delay} = 100 - \%\text{Utilisation of bottleneck worker}$ | Idle time in assembly line balancing | % |
| 9 | $\text{Walking Distance} = \sum (\text{Path segment length})$ | For String Diagram | metres (m) |
| 10 | $\text{Output Rate} = \dfrac{3600}{\text{ST per unit}} \times \text{Efficiency\%} / 100$ | Pieces per hour from chart-derived ST | pieces/hr |

**Notation legend:**
- $z$ = standard normal variate (1.96 at 95 % confidence)
- $s$ = standard deviation of observed times
- $L$ = permissible error (accuracy desired)
- **Allowance** = Rest (typically **10 %**) + Personal (**5 %**) + Contingency (**3 %**)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lean Manufacturing:** Two-Hand and SIMO charts feed directly into *kaizen* workshops.
- **Ergonomics:** SIMO charts reveal micro-motions causing Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI).
- **Plant Layout:** String diagrams drive *factory floor re-layout* decisions (e.g., Toyota Production System).
- **Industrial Costing:** Standard Time (ST) from charts becomes the basis for **labour costing**, **piece-rate wages**, and **capacity planning**.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Derivation: Computing Activity Ratio from a Flow Process Chart

The **Activity Ratio** quantifies the *useful productive content* within a recorded work cycle. It is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark numerical problems in KTU ESE.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A flow process chart of an operator performing a sub-assembly gives the following breakdown of activities:

| Symbol (Code) | Description | Time (s) |
|---|---|---|
| `○` Operation-1 | Pick up bracket | 8 |
| `▢` Inspection | Visual check fit | 6 |
| `→` Transport | Move to press | 12 |
| `▽` Delay | Wait for press release | 20 |
| `○` Operation-2 | Place & operate press | 15 |
| `→` Transport | Move to next station | 8 |
| `▽` Delay | Wait for inspector | 14 |
| `○` Operation-3 | Final tightening | 10 |

The operator is rated at **110** (performance rating). Standard allowance is **18 %**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify productive vs non-productive symbols.**

Per BS 3138 conventions:
- **Productive** (counted in numerator): `○` Operation and `▢` Inspection.
- **Non-productive** (waste): `→` Transport, `▽` Delay.

$$
T_{\text{productive}} = 8 + 6 + 15 + 10 = 39 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{transport}} = 12 + 8 = 20 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{delay}} = 20 + 14 = 34 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total observed cycle time.**

$$
\text{OT}_{\text{total}} = 39 + 20 + 34 = 93 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Observed Time to Normal Time using the rating factor.**

$$
\text{NT} = \text{OT}_{\text{productive}} \times \frac{R}{100} = 39 \times \frac{110}{100} = 42.9 \text{ s}
$$

> **Important:** Rating is applied **only to productive time**. Idle/delay time is rated 100 by definition (work is being done, but unproductively) — though for NT calculation of *productive* symbols, we apply the operator's rating.

**Step 4 — Compute Standard Time using the relaxation allowance.**

$$
\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times (1 + A) = 42.9 \times (1 + 0.18)
$$

$$
\text{ST} = 42.9 \times 1.18 = 50.622 \text{ s/unit}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Activity Ratio.**

$$
\% \text{Activity} = \frac{\text{NT of productive symbols}}{\text{ST of full cycle}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{Activity} = \frac{42.9}{50.622} \times 100 = 84.75 \%
$$

> **Result interpretation:** Only **84.75 %** of the cycle's *standard time* is productive; the remaining 15.25 % represents *effective* delay allowance accounted for. **However, raw time waste** = $(20+34)/93 = 58.06\%$ of the *observed* time — which is the **real diagnostic** for redesign.

### 3.1.3 Side-by-Side Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Formula Used | Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Productive Time | $\sum O + I$ | 39 s | 41.94 % of raw time |
| Waste Time | $\sum T + D$ | 54 s | 58.06 % of raw time |
| Normal Time | $OT_{\text{prod}} \times (R/100)$ | 42.9 s | Rated productive work |
| Standard Time | $NT \times (1+A)$ | 50.622 s | Costing basis |
| Activity Ratio | $NT/ ST \times 100$ | 84.75 % | Process efficiency |
| Output Rate | $3600/50.622$ | **71.13 units/hr** | Capacity statement |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sample-Size for Adequacy of Observations (Statistical Foundation of Chart Data)

When a flow process chart is supported by time study, the **number of observations** $N$ must be statistically adequate:

$$
N = \left(\frac{z \cdot s}{L}\right)^{2}
$$

where:
- $z = 1.96$ (95 % confidence, two-tailed)
- $s$ = sample standard deviation of pilot study
- $L$ = permissible error = $R \times \text{precision \%}$

### 3.2.1 Worked Example

A pilot study of 10 cycles gives $s = 4.2 \text{ s}$. Desired precision is $\pm 5 \%$ of mean cycle time $\bar{x} = 45 \text{ s}$.

**Step 1:** Compute permissible error.

$$
L = 0.05 \times 45 = 2.25 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.

$$
N = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 4.2}{2.25}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
N = \left(\frac{8.232}{2.25}\right)^{2} = (3.6587)^{2} = 13.39
$$

**Step 3:** Round **up** to the next integer.

$$
\boxed{N = 14 \text{ observations}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU convention:** Always round $N$ *up*, never *down*. Failure to round correctly is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction by KTU evaluators.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Utilisation Analysis from a Multiple Activity Chart

Suppose **2 operators (A and B)** work on a 4-stage assembly. Their operation times per cycle are:

| Stage | Op-A (s) | Op-B (s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 25 |
| 2 | 40 | 35 |
| 3 | 35 | 45 |
| 4 | 25 | 30 |
| **Sum** | 130 | 135 |

Total cycle = max(130, 135) = **135 s** (governed by the bottleneck, B).

**Utilisation of A:**

$$
\%U_A = \frac{130}{135} \times 100 = 96.30 \%
$$

**Utilisation of B:**

$$
\%U_B = \frac{135}{135} \times 100 = 100.00 \%
$$

**Balance Delay:**

$$
\text{BD} = 100 - 96.30 = 3.70 \%
$$

> **Engineering action:** Transfer a small operation from B → A (e.g., 5 s) to balance the line. After re-allocation both become ~98.5 % utilised.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4.1 Block Architecture — Classification of Charts and Diagrams

The following Mermaid block-diagram depicts the **functional hierarchy** of charts in Work System Design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Work Study Charts and Diagrams] --> B[Process Level Charts]
    A --> C[Operator Level Charts]
    A --> D[Multi Resource Level Charts]
    A --> E[Movement and Layout Charts]

    B --> B1[Outline Process Chart]
    B --> B2[Operation Process Chart]
    B --> B3[Flow Process Chart]
    B3 --> B3a[Man Type]
    B3 --> B3b[Material Type]
    B3 --> B3c[Equipment Type]

    C --> C1[Two Hand Process Chart]
    C --> C2[SIMO Chart]
    C --> C3[Multi Activity Chart]

    D --> D1[Multiple Activity Chart]
    D --> D2[Gang Process Chart]

    E --> E1[String Diagram]
    E --> E2[Cycle Graph]
    E --> E3[Travel Chart]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style E fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Logic — Generic Operation Process Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start((Start)) --> op1[Operation 1: Receive raw material]
    op1 --> insp1[Inspection 1: Quality check raw]
    insp1 --> trans1[Transport to workstation]
    trans1 --> op2[Operation 2: Machining]
    op2 --> insp2[Inspection 2: In process check]
    insp2 --> op3[Operation 3: Assembly]
    op3 --> op4[Operation 4: Testing]
    op4 --> insp3[Final Inspection]
    insp3 --> trans2[Transport to dispatch]
    trans2 --> store((Storage until dispatch))
    store --> end1((End))

    style op1 fill:#B0E0E6
    style op2 fill:#B0E0E6
    style op3 fill:#B0E0E6
    style op4 fill:#B0E0E6
    style insp1 fill:#FFFACD
    style insp2 fill:#FFFACD
    style insp3 fill:#FFFACD
    style trans1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style trans2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style store fill:#D8BFD8
```

## 4.3 Two-Hand Process Chart — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LHS[Left Hand Activities] --> L1[Reach bracket]
    L1 --> L2[Grip bracket]
    L2 --> L3[Position on jig]
    L3 --> L4[Hold while right hand works]
    L4 --> L5[Release after assembly]

    RHS[Right Hand Activities] --> R1[Reach tool]
    R1 --> R2[Pick screw]
    R2 --> R3[Drive screw using tool]
    R3 --> R4[Place tool down]
    R4 --> R5[Signal completion]

    L4 -.synchronised.- R3

    style LHS fill:#FFEFD5
    style RHS fill:#E0FFFF
```

## 4.4 Multiple Activity Chart — Time-Synchronised Block View

```mermaid
gantt
    title Multiple Activity Chart - Two Operator Assembly
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S s
    section Operator A
    Task A1 :a1, 0, 30
    Task A2 :a2, 30, 40
    Task A3 :a3, 70, 35
    Task A4 :a4, 105, 25
    section Operator B
    Task B1 :b1, 0, 25
    Task B2 :b2, 25, 35
    Task B3 :b3, 60, 45
    Task B4 :b4, 105, 30
```

## 4.5 String Diagram Concept (Block Schematic of Use)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Floor Plan
    M1((Machine 1)) --- M2((Machine 2))
    M2 --- M3((Machine 3))
    M3 --- M4((Inspection))
    M4 --- S((Storage))
    end

    worker((Operator)) -->|1. Walk to M1| M1
    M1 -->|2. Carry part| M2
    M2 -->|3. Move to M3| M3
    M3 -->|4. Transport for inspection| M4
    M4 -->|5. Move to storage| S

    style worker fill:#FFD700
    style M1 fill:#87CEEB
    style M2 fill:#87CEEB
    style M3 fill:#87CEEB
    style M4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style S fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.6 Decision Logic Block — Chart Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the work repeated frequently?}
    Q1 -->|No| A1[Use Outline Process Chart]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the focus on one operator?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is the cycle very short?}
    Q3 -->|Yes - micro motions| A2[Use SIMO Chart]
    Q3 -->|No| A3[Use Two Hand Process Chart]
    Q2 -->|No - multiple workers| A4[Use Multiple Activity Chart]
    Q1 -.-> Q4{Is the focus on material flow?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| A5[Use Flow Process Chart Material Type]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is layout involved?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| A6[Use String Diagram or Cycle Graph]
    Q5 -->|No| A7[Use Operation Process Chart]

    style Q1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style Q2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style Q3 fill:#FFE4B5
    style Q4 fill:#FFE4B5
    style Q5 fill:#FFE4B5
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an Outline Process Chart and a Flow Process Chart with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Outline Process Chart | Flow Process Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Gives an overview of the *entire* process sequence using *only operations and inspections* (no transport or delay symbols) | Records *every* step — operations, inspections, transports, delays, storages |
| **Symbols used** | Only `○` (operation) and `▢` (inspection) | All five BS 3138 symbols |
| **Detail level** | Low (top-level) | High (granular) |
| **Application** | Initial analysis / quotation stage | Detailed methods improvement / costing |
| **Example** | Showing 6 operations to manufacture a bolt | Recording every move and wait in the bolt-making cycle |

*(1 mark per appropriate distinguishing line, complete table = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three situations where a Multiple Activity Chart is preferred over a Two-Hand Process Chart.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. When **two or more operators/machines** work together on the same product and their **synchronisation** must be examined (1 mark).
2. When **bottleneck identification** in a multi-station process is required — Multiple Activity Chart reveals idle time directly (1 mark).
3. When **gang or team work** analysis is needed (e.g., lifting a heavy object, assembly crew) to balance workloads (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [14 Marks]**  *(Module 2 internal choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 model]**

**(a)** Explain the **principles, construction procedure, and applications** of a **String Diagram**. Discuss its advantages over a Cycle Graph. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data refers to a manual assembly operation. Construct an **Operation Process Chart** (description only) and compute the **Activity Ratio** if the operator's performance rating is **115** and total allowance is **20 %**. **(7 marks)**

| Step | Activity | Symbol | Time (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pick up part from bin | Operation | 6 |
| 2 | Visual inspection | Inspection | 4 |
| 3 | Carry part to press | Transport | 10 |
| 4 | Wait for press clearance | Delay | 18 |
| 5 | Operate foot press | Operation | 12 |
| 6 | Carry finished part | Transport | 8 |
| 7 | Place part in tray | Operation | 5 |

---

### **Solution A (a) — String Diagram**

**Definition (2 marks):** A String Diagram is a scale model (plan view) of the work area on which a *continuous thread or string* is wound around pins to trace the actual path followed by the operator, material, or equipment.

**Construction procedure (3 marks):**
1. Prepare a *layout* of the work area to scale (e.g., 1 cm = 1 m).
2. Mark the **starting point** with a pin.
3. As the operator moves, *wind a string* around pins placed at each stop (machine, bench, inspection point).
4. Measure string lengths; convert to **walking distance** using scale.
5. Record each segment with its length on a tabular sheet.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Re-layout planning for material-handling optimisation.
- Identifying back-tracking and congestion in crowded cells.
- Costing of manual transport activities.

**Advantages over Cycle Graph (1 mark):**
- **Lower cost & faster** to construct — no stopwatch needed.
- Gives *simultaneous spatial picture*; Cycle Graph is purely time-based without spatial context.

---

### **Solution A (b) — Operation Process Chart + Activity Ratio**

**Step 1 — Classify symbols (1 mark):**
- Productive symbols: Steps 1 (`○`), 2 (`▢`), 5 (`○`), 7 (`○`).
- Non-productive symbols: Steps 3 (`→`), 4 (`▽`), 6 (`→`).

**Step 2 — Construct Operation Process Chart (2 marks):**

```
       ┌──────────────────────────┐
   Step│     Activity            │ Symbol
   ────┼──────────────────────────┼────────
   1   │ Pick up part from bin   │   ○
   2   │ Visual inspection       │   ▢
   3   │ Carry part to press     │   →
   4   │ Wait for press clearance│   ▽
   5   │ Operate foot press      │   ○
   6   │ Carry finished part     │   →
   7   │ Place part in tray      │   ○
       └──────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 3 — Sum productive observed time (1 mark):**
$$
T_{\text{prod}} = 6 + 4 + 12 + 5 = 27 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply rating to get Normal Time (1 mark):**
$$
\text{NT} = 27 \times \frac{115}{100} = 31.05 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply allowance to get Standard Time (1 mark):**
$$
\text{ST} = 31.05 \times (1 + 0.20) = 37.26 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Activity Ratio (1 mark):**
$$
\% \text{Activity} = \frac{\text{NT}}{\text{ST}} \times 100 = \frac{31.05}{37.26} \times 100 = \boxed{83.33\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 16.67 % of the standard time is allowance; observed waste = $(10+18+8)/63 = 57.14\%$ — **major redesign opportunity** in transport and delay.

---

### **Question B — [14 Marks]**  *(Module 2 internal choice alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Two-Hand Process Chart** in detail. State its symbols, the rules for drawing it, and mention **two situations** where it is most useful. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two workers **A and B** work on an assembly. The time (in seconds) spent by each on different operations per cycle is given below. Draw the **Multiple Activity Chart**, calculate the **cycle time, utilisation of each worker, and balance delay**. Suggest **one improvement**. **(7 marks)**

| Operation | Worker A | Worker B |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 28 | 22 |
| 2 | 36 | 30 |
| 3 | 40 | 42 |
| 4 | 24 | 38 |
| 5 | 30 | 20 |

---

### **Solution B (a) — Two-Hand Process Chart**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Two-Hand Process Chart simultaneously records the activities of **both hands of a single operator** on a common time scale, identifying idle hand time and asymmetric work load.

**Symbols (2 marks):** Same five BS 3138 symbols — `○` Operation, `▢` Inspection, `→` Transport, `▽` Delay, `D` Combined — but each symbol is duplicated for the **Left hand** and the **Right hand**, placed on either side of a **central vertical time-line**.

**Drawing rules (2 marks):**
1. Each hand's activities drawn as *vertical column* of symbols.
2. Time progresses **top → bottom** synchronously on both sides.
3. Each symbol's *time duration* drawn **proportional to its time length**.
4. Working / idle intervals must be visually distinguishable.

**Most useful situations (2 marks):**
1. Repetitive *short-cycle bench work* (e.g., screw-driving, packing) where micro-motion economy is critical.
2. Jobs suspected of having **hand-imbalance** leading to premature fatigue in one limb.

---

### **Solution B (b) — Multiple Activity Chart Analysis**

**Step 1 — Sum of times per worker (1 mark):**
- Worker A total = $28 + 36 + 40 + 24 + 30 = 158 \text{ s}$
- Worker B total = $22 + 30 + 42 + 38 + 20 = 152 \text{ s}$

**Step 2 — Cycle time = max of the two sums (1 mark):**
$$
\text{CT} = \max(158, 152) = 158 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Utilisation (1 mark):**
$$
\%U_A = \frac{158}{158} \times 100 = 100.00\%
$$

$$
\%U_B = \frac{152}{158} \times 100 = 96.20\%
$$

**Step 4 — Balance delay (1 mark):**
$$
\text{BD} = 100 - 96.20 = 3.80\%
$$

**Step 5 — Multiple Activity Chart (1 mark)** — Time-scale proportional bars:

| t (s)   | 0 – 22 | 22 – 28 | 28 – 30 | 30 – 50 | 50 – 58 | 58 – 70 | 70 – 80 | 80 – 92 | 92 – 110 | 110 – 122 | 122 – 130 | 130 – 152 | 152 – 158 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Worker A | — | Op1 | idle | Op2 (22–50) | idle | Op3 | Op4 | — | — | — | Op5 (130–158) | idle |
| Worker B | Op1 (0–22) | — | idle | Op2 | — | Op3 | — | Op4 | — | Op5 | idle | — | — |

**Step 6 — Improvement (1 mark):** Transfer **Operation 5 (20 s)** from B to A (after A's Op3 ending at t = 92 s, A becomes idle from t = 80 to 92; B can take over). After reallocation: A total = 178 s, B total = 132 s — *but that worsens imbalance*. Instead, transfer **a 3-s portion of Op-4** from B to A's idle slot. New A = 161 s, B = 149 s → rebalance reduces balance delay.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 2 Charts & Diagrams (KTU evaluator notes):**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply rating** — students compute activity ratio using *observed* time instead of *normal* time. *Loss: 1 mark per instance.*
> 2. **Rounding $N$ down** in sample-size derivation — must always round **up**.
> 3. **Drawing symbols on wrong side of vertical line** in flow process chart — operation symbols must be drawn **against** the line (to the right), other symbols **on** the line.
> 4. **Confusing Activity Ratio with Utilisation** — Activity Ratio uses NT/ST of *one* operator; Utilisation uses productive/total time in a *Multiple Activity Chart* across workers.
