> 5. **Forgetting allowance in Standard Time** — ST is **not** equal to NT unless allowance = 0.
> 6. **Cycle time = bottleneck operator's total**, NOT average of workers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Five standard symbols** (`○` Operation, `▢` Inspection, `→` Transport, `▽` Delay, `D` Combined) — universal across all flow charts.
- **Outline Process Chart** uses only **2 symbols** (Operation + Inspection); gives bird's-eye view.
- **Operation Process Chart** uses **all 5 symbols** but for *process description*; **Flow Process Chart** adds **time** values to each step.
- **Two-Hand Process Chart** = single operator, **both hands** on a *common time axis*.
- **Multiple Activity Chart** = multiple workers / machines on a *common time axis* — used for *balance delay* and *line balancing*.
- **SIMO Chart** = simultaneous motion chart using **therbligs** (17 micro-motion symbols); for sub-second analysis.
- **String Diagram** = scale layout + thread/string route; cheapest layout-improvement tool.
- **Cycle Graph** = time-distance chart for *one* moving worker on a *grid*; expensive (needs stopwatch + measuring tape).
- **Activity Ratio formula:** $\% \text{Activity} = (NT_{prod}/ST_{cycle}) \times 100$.
- **Standard Time formula:** $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ where $A$ is decimal allowance (e.g., 0.18 for 18 %).
- **Sample size formula:** $N = (z \cdot s / L)^2$, always **round up**.
- **Cycle time of a multi-worker chart** = **maximum** of all workers' total times.
- **Balance Delay** = $100 - \%U_{\text{bottleneck predecessor}}$.
- Charts integrate with **EDIM cycle**: Examine → Develop → Install → Maintain.
- Standard references: **ILO Introduction to Work Study**, **BS 3138:1979**, **Niebel & Freivalds — Motion and Time Study**.

> **Final 7-point golden rule for any chart-based KTU problem:**
> *Classify symbols → Sum productive time → Apply rating → Apply allowance → Compute target metric (Activity Ratio / Utilisation / Output Rate) → Interpret for redesign.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examining the Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Examining the Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Examining the Work"** is the systematic, critical, and structured investigation of a defined work activity — its operations, movements, sequence, and human–machine interactions — using standardized recording techniques, charts, and analytical questioning frameworks. The objective is to develop a complete factual picture of the *present method* so that the most *economical, ergonomic, and productive method* can be designed, installed, and maintained.

In the language of **Work Study** (O.I.T. classification), *Examining the Work* is the **investigative and analytical arm of the Methods Study** phase. It answers the central question:

> *"What is being done? Why is it being done? Where, when, how, and by whom is it being done — and could it be done more economically?"*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Understanding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — A Detective at a Crime Scene**
> Imagine a detective arriving at a cluttered crime scene. Before suggesting any improvement (cleaning, rearranging), the detective must first **photograph, sketch, and document every detail** as it is — footprints, fingerprints, broken glass, the position of furniture. Only *after* this exhaustive, factual recording can the detective deduce *what happened* and propose a better state of affairs.
>
> *Examining the Work* is exactly this — the **detective phase** of work design. We do **not** jump to solutions. We meticulously **record → examine critically → question → improve → install → maintain**.

In a factory setting, this means observing a worker assembling a product, drawing the sequence of small steps, timing them, asking "Why this step? Why this tool? Why this hand?" — and only then suggesting a better method.

## 1.3 Why "Examining the Work" Matters in Work System Design

> [!NOTE]
> Without an accurate, documented understanding of the *current* work method, any redesign, automation, or ergonomic intervention becomes a **guess** rather than a **data-driven decision**. KTU examiners repeatedly emphasize this concept because it is the *foundation* of:
> - Productivity improvement
> - Method standardization
> - Cost reduction
> - Ergonomics and worker safety
> - Capacity planning and line balancing

## 1.4 Standard Metrics / Constants in Examining the Work

- **Normal Working Pace** = **100 B.S. (Basic Seconds)** in PMTS systems (Methods Time Measurement)
- **Performance Rating (PR)** typically ranges from **60 to 135** depending on worker effort
- **Standard Time (ST)** = Normal Time × (1 + Allowances)
- **Allowance** is normally between **10% and 25%** of Normal Time

These constants are essential when *examining* measured work to convert observed time into standard time.

## 1.5 Scope of the Topic (KTU 2024 Module-2 Coverage)

The topic *Examining the Work* typically covers:

1. Purpose and objectives of examining work
2. The logical **6-step procedure** of examining work
3. **Recording techniques** — charts, diagrams, motion study
4. **Critical examination** — the questioning technique
5. Selection of the **most economical method**
6. Installation and **follow-up** of the new method

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6-Step Procedure of Examining the Work (Curtis & Cole Universal Approach)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus follows the **classical Work Study procedure** of the British Standards Institution (B.S.I.) and ILO. There are **six sequential steps**:

### Step 1 — **SELECT** (Choose the work to be studied)
- Identify the job, operation, or process with **highest cost / highest repetition / bottleneck potential**.
- Selection criteria: **economic significance, frequency of occurrence, ergonomic risk, and scope for measurable improvement**.

### Step 2 — **RECORD** (Document the present method)
- Capture *all* relevant facts about the existing method using standardized techniques.
- Records must be **objective, fact-based, and quantitative**.
- Common recording tools:
  - **Charts:** Operation Process Chart, Flow Process Chart (Material/Man), Two-Handed Process Chart, Man-Machine Chart, Multiple Activity Chart.
  - **Diagrams:** String Diagram, Travel Chart, Cyclegraph, Chronocyclegraph.
  - **Motion Analysis:** Therblig-based micromotion study.

### Step 3 — **EXAMINE** (Critical examination of facts)
- Subject every recorded fact to **rigorous questioning** to determine necessity, place, sequence, person, and means.
- This is the *heart* of "Examining the Work."

### Step 4 — **DEVELOP** (Create the improved method)
- Synthesize the answers to the questioning phase into a *new, more economical* method.
- Compare alternatives with cost–benefit logic.

### Step 5 — **INSTALL** (Implement the new method)
- Gain worker/supervisor acceptance, train operators, arrange tools, and roll out the new method in a controlled manner.

### Step 6 — **MAINTAIN** (Follow-up and standardization)
- Periodically audit, control drift, and update standards.
- This step ensures the gains are *sustained*, not just achieved once.

## 2.2 Critical Examination — The Questioning Technique

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most important *examiner-favorite* sub-topic in KTU ESE papers. Every recorded element of work is interrogated using **two levels of questioning**:

### Primary Questions (the "Big Five")
For every activity, ask:

| # | Question | What it challenges |
|---|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **What is being done?** (Purpose) | Necessity of the activity itself |
| 2 | **Where is it being done?** (Place) | Layout, transport, location |
| 3 | **When is it being done?** (Time/Sequence) | Order, scheduling, synchronization |
| 4 | **Who is doing it?** (Person) | Skill match, ergonomic fit, automation |
| 5 | **How is it being done?** (Means) | Tools, methods, technique |

### Secondary Questions (the "Investigative Drill-Down")
After each primary question, the analyst asks *three* deeper sub-questions:

- *What is the purpose?* — *Is it necessary?* — *What else could be done?*
- *Where should it be done?* — *Why there?* — *Where else might be better?*
- *When should it be done?* — *Why then?* — *When might be better?*
- *Who should do it?* — *Why them?* — *Who else could?*
- *How is it done?* — *Why this way?* — *What other method?*

This produces a **5 × 3 = 15-cell decision matrix** that forces the analyst to test every element against alternative possibilities.

## 2.3 Recording Techniques — Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *"Differentiate between a process chart and a flow diagram"* or *"When do you use a two-handed process chart?"* Memorize this taxonomy:

| Recording Tool | Symbol Set | Best Used For | Level of Detail |
|----------------|------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Operation Process Chart** | Operation ⭕, Inspection ▢, Transport ⇒, Delay ▽, Storage ▽ | Overview of process — many operations | Macro |
| **Flow Process Chart (Material)** | Same 5 symbols | Tracing the *material* through the process | Macro–Meso |
| **Flow Process Chart (Man)** | Same 5 symbols | Tracing the *operator* through the process | Macro–Meso |
| **Two-Handed Process Chart** | TMU codes + body-symbols | Right/left hand simultaneous activity | Micro |
| **Man–Machine Chart** | Activity bars | Coordination of man & multiple machines | Meso |
| **Multiple Activity Chart** | Activity bars | 2+ workers / machines in parallel | Meso |
| **String Diagram** | Pins & string on plan | Worker movement over a fixed layout | Meso |
| **Travel Chart** | Matrix grid | Inter-departmental movement volumes | Macro |
| **Simo-chart** | TMU codes on time scale | Micromotion with timing | Micro |
| **Cyclegraph / Chronocyclegraph** | Light-trail photo | Three-dimensional path + speed of motion | Micro |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Observed Time (OT) | $OT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i / n$ | $T_i$ = individual readings, $n$ = number of cycles | seconds (s) | Average of multiple cycle readings |
| 2 | Normal Time (NT) | $NT = OT \times R$ | $R$ = Performance Rating (decimal, e.g., 1.05) | seconds | R is the worker's pace vs. standard |
| 3 | Standard Time (ST) | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | $A$ = Allowance (decimal) | seconds | KTU most-tested formula |
| 4 | Allowance Factor | $A = A_r + A_p + A_c$ | Relaxation, Personal, Contingency | fraction | Typical total ≈ 15% |
| 5 | Work Sampling Sample Size | $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$ | $Z$ = confidence coefficient, $p$ = observed proportion, $E$ = acceptable error | dimensionless | For 95% confidence, $Z$ = **1.96** |
| 6 | Number of Random Observations (Work Sampling) | $N = \frac{4 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{s^2}$ | $p$ = preliminary proportion, $s$ = desired accuracy | dimensionless | Quick-estimate form using $Z$=2 |
| 7 | Standard Hour (SH) | $1\ SH = 3600\ \text{seconds of Standard Time}$ | — | seconds | Productivity metric |
| 8 | Productivity Index | $P = \frac{ST_{\text{old}} - ST_{\text{new}}}{ST_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Old vs. new standard time | % | Measures % improvement |
| 9 | Performance Rating (synthetic) | $R = \frac{\text{OT}_{\text{standard}} + \text{OT}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{OT}_{\text{standard}}}$ | — | ratio | Used when standard pace is known |
| 10 | Activity Ratio (Work Sampling) | $AR = \frac{\text{Favourable observations}}{\text{Total observations}} \times 100$ | — | % | Used to compute % utilization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-symbol safeguard applied:** all table cells use `\vert` or `\mid` notation in the original LaTeX rather than `|` so the markdown table renders correctly.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of "Examining the Work"

- **Manufacturing:** Redesign assembly lines, balance takt times, eliminate non-value-added steps.
- **Healthcare:** Streamline patient flow, reduce waiting time in OT, ER, OPD.
- **Services:** Optimize banking queue, e-commerce warehouse picking, call-centre workflows.
- **Ergonomics:** Identify micro-movements causing Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI).
- **Lean & Six Sigma:** Forms the *Measure* and *Analyze* phase data foundation.
- **Digital Twin / Industry 4.0:** The recorded facts become the *digital baseline* before any simulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard Time from Observed Work

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation shows the *complete* chain — from raw stopwatch reading to published Standard Time — with **no step skipped**.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Record raw stopwatch times for $n$ cycles.**

$$T_1,\ T_2,\ T_3,\ \ldots,\ T_n \quad [\text{in seconds}]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the arithmetic mean observed time.**

$$OT = \frac{T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + \cdots + T_n}{n} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

**Step 3 — Judge the operator's pace relative to the standard pace (100 BS).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Rating ($R$)** is a dimensionless factor: $R = 1.00$ means *normal pace*; $R = 1.20$ means 20% faster than normal.

**Step 4 — Convert Observed Time to Normal Time by removing the effect of operator skill/pace.**

$$NT = OT \times R$$

**Step 5 — Add Relaxation, Personal, and Contingency Allowances to cover fatigue, breaks, and unavoidable delays.**

$$A = A_r + A_p + A_c$$

where $A_r$ = Relaxation allowance, $A_p$ = Personal allowance, $A_c$ = Contingency allowance.

**Step 6 — Compute the Standard Time (the time an *average skilled worker* should take at *normal pace* including all legitimate allowances).**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A)$$

**Step 7 — Final closed-form substitution:**

$$ST = \left[ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i \right] \times R \times (1 + A)$$

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A stopwatch study of a drilling operation produces the following times in seconds: 0.48, 0.52, 0.50, 0.49, 0.51. The operator is rated at 110% and the total allowance is 18%. Compute the Standard Time per piece.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$OT = \frac{0.48 + 0.52 + 0.50 + 0.49 + 0.51}{5} = \frac{2.50}{5} = 0.500\ \text{s}$$

$$NT = OT \times R = 0.500 \times 1.10 = 0.550\ \text{s}$$

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 0.550 \times (1 + 0.18)$$

$$ST = 0.550 \times 1.18 = 0.649\ \text{s per piece}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion to a per-hour output rate:** $\text{Output/hr} = 3600 / ST = 3600 / 0.649 \approx 5548\ \text{pieces/hour}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Work Sampling Sample Size

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Work sampling is a *statistical* method of examining work. The sample size equation is derived from the **binomial standard error** of a proportion.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — In a random Bernoulli process, the standard error of a sample proportion is:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

**Step 2 — For a stated confidence level $Z$ and an acceptable absolute error $E$:**

$$E = Z \cdot \sigma_p = Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides:**

$$E^2 = Z^2 \cdot \frac{p(1-p)}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$:**

$$\boxed{N = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}}$$

**Step 5 — For the quick-estimate approach at 95% confidence ($Z \approx 2$), using a *preliminary* proportion $p$:**

$$N = \frac{4 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{s^2}$$

where $s$ = desired relative accuracy (e.g., $s = 0.05$ means $\pm 5\%$).

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A preliminary work-sampling study of 100 random observations shows that 30% of the time the machine is idle. How many additional observations are needed for $\pm 4\%$ absolute accuracy at 95% confidence?

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
$Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.30$, $E = 0.04$.

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**

$$N = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.30) \cdot (0.70)}{(0.04)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator.**

$$\text{Num} = 3.8416 \times 0.21 = 0.8067$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator.**

$$\text{Den} = 0.0016$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$N = \frac{0.8067}{0.0016} \approx 504.2 \rightarrow 505\ \text{observations (round up)}$$

**Step 6 — Subtract preliminary observations.**

$$N_{\text{additional}} = 505 - 100 = 405\ \text{more observations}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Productivity Improvement (Method Change)

**Step 1 — Compute old Standard Time $ST_{old}$ from the existing method.**

$$ST_{old} = OT_{old} \times R \times (1 + A)$$

**Step 2 — Compute new Standard Time $ST_{new}$ after redesign.**

$$ST_{new} = OT_{new} \times R \times (1 + A)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the % improvement in standard time.**

$$P_{\%} = \frac{ST_{old} - ST_{new}}{ST_{old}} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Compute the productivity index (pieces per standard hour).**

$$SH_{\text{old}} = \frac{3600}{ST_{old}}, \quad SH_{\text{new}} = \frac{3600}{ST_{new}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the % increase in output rate.**

$$P_{\text{output}} = \frac{SH_{\text{new}} - SH_{\text{old}}}{SH_{\text{old}}} \times 100 = \frac{ST_{old} - ST_{new}}{ST_{new}} \times 100$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Recording Technique

> [!NOTE]
> This **decision matrix** is a high-yield KTU answer for questions that ask: *"Which technique would you recommend for a given scenario?"*

| Recording Technique | When to Use | Advantage | Limitation |
|----------------------|-------------|-----------|------------|
| **Operation Process Chart** | 10–100+ operations overview | Quick high-level snapshot | Loses micro-detail |
| **Flow Process Chart (Material)** | Tracking raw material/piece | Reveals transport & delay | Doesn't show human motion |
| **Flow Process Chart (Man)** | Operator route, ergonomics audit | Highlights operator waste | Static snapshot |
| **Two-Handed Process Chart** | Repetitive short-cycle work | Reveals asymmetric hand use | Time-consuming |
| **Man–Machine Chart** | Operator oversees ≥1 machine | Optimizes machine utilization | Ignores inter-worker flow |
| **Multiple Activity Chart** | Crew of 2+ workers / 2+ machines | Detects idle time of crew | Complex layout |
| **String Diagram** | Layout/movement design | Visual, intuitive | Single-operator limit |
| **Travel Chart** | Inter-department / inter-bench | Volume-based routing | Needs movement data |
| **Simo-chart** | Micromotion with timing | Adds time dimension to therbligs | Requires film/camera |
| **Cyclegraph / Chronocyclegraph** | Ergonomic + speed study | 3-D path & velocity visualized | Specialized equipment |

## 3.5 Critical Examination — Sample Worked Application

**Scenario:** A worker assembles a small plastic knob. Current sequence is:
1. Pick cap (left hand)
2. Pick body (right hand)
3. Press cap onto body (right hand, while left hand holds)
4. Place assembly in box (right hand)

**Application of the 5 × 3 Questioning Matrix:**

| Activity | What? | Where? | When? | Who? | How? |
|----------|-------|--------|-------|------|------|
| Pick cap (LH) | Necessary? Yes. Combine with later step? Maybe. | At bin? Could be hopper-fed. | Before body? Could be simultaneous. | LH only? Could be both. | Manual? Could be auto-feeder. |
| Pick body (RH) | Necessary? Yes. | At bin? Yes. | After cap? Should be parallel. | RH? Yes. | Manual? Yes. |
| Press cap on body | Necessary? Yes. | On bench? Could be pneumatic press. | After both picks? Yes. | RH? Could be 2-handed press. | Hand-pressing? Press tool better. |
| Place in box | Necessary? Yes. | In box at end of line? Yes. | Last step. | RH? Yes. | Manual? Could be chute. |

**Outcome of the examination — Improved method:**

- Add a gravity-fed hopper for caps → eliminates "Pick cap" step.
- Use a foot-pedal press → both hands free → parallelism.
- Add a discharge chute → eliminates manual placing.

**Result:** Cycle time reduced, left-hand idle time eliminated, ergonomics improved.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The 6-Step Procedure of Examining the Work

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: SELECT the Work Job"] --> B["Step 2: RECORD the Present Method"]
    B --> C["Step 3: EXAMINE Critically"]
    C --> D["Step 4: DEVELOP the Improved Method"]
    D --> E["Step 5: INSTALL the New Method"]
    E --> F["Step 6: MAINTAIN the New Method"]
    F --> A

    subgraph REC["RECORDING TOOLS (Step 2)"]
        R1["Operation Process Chart"]
        R2["Flow Process Chart"]
        R3["Two Handed Process Chart"]
        R4["Man Machine Chart"]
        R5["String Diagram and Travel Chart"]
    end

    subgraph EXAM["CRITICAL EXAMINATION (Step 3)"]
        Q1["WHAT Purpose"]
        Q2["WHERE Place"]
        Q3["WHEN Sequence"]
        Q4["WHO Person"]
        Q5["HOW Means"]
    end

    B --> REC
    C --> EXAM
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The 5×3 Questioning Matrix (Critical Examination)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P["PRIMARY QUESTIONS"]
        P1["WHAT Purpose"]
        P2["WHERE Place"]
        P3["WHEN Time"]
        P4["WHO Person"]
        P5["HOW Means"]
    end

    subgraph S["SECONDARY DRILL DOWN for EACH"]
        S1a["Why done?"]
        S1b["Is it necessary?"]
        S1c["What else?"]

        S2a["Why here?"]
        S2b["Why not elsewhere?"]
        S2c["Better place?"]

        S3a["Why now?"]
        S3b["Why this order?"]
        S3c["Better timing?"]

        S4a["Why this person?"]
        S4b["Why not someone else?"]
        S4c["Automation option?"]

        S5a["Why this method?"]
        S5b["What alternative?"]
        S5c["Best practice?"]
    end

    P1 --> S1a --> S1b --> S1c
    P2 --> S2a --> S2b --> S2c
    P3 --> S3a --> S3b --> S3c
    P4 --> S4a --> S4b --> S4c
    P5 --> S5a --> S5b --> S5c
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of "Examining the Work"

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT["Input: Work Activity or Process Under Study"] --> SELECT["Select Work by Economic Criteria"]
    SELECT --> DATA["Data Acquisition Layer"]
    DATA --> R1["Stopwatch Time Study"]
    DATA --> R2["Work Sampling"]
    DATA --> R3["Video Capture"]
    DATA --> R4["Layout Maps"]

    R1 --> RECORD["Recording Layer: Charts and Diagrams"]
    R2 --> RECORD
    R3 --> RECORD
    R4 --> RECORD

    RECORD --> EXAM["Critical Examination Layer: 5 by 3 Questioning"]
    EXAM --> IDEATE["Idea Generation: Alternative Methods"]
    IDEATE --> EVAL["Economic and Ergonomic Evaluation"]
    EVAL --> NEW["Selected Improved Method"]
    NEW --> INSTALL["Install: Training and Tooling"]
    INSTALL --> MAINTAIN["Maintain: Standardize, Audit, Sustain"]
    MAINTAIN --> OUTPUT["Output: Documented Standard Method and Time"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Selecting a Recording Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Recording the Work"] --> q1{"What is the level of detail needed?"}
    q1 -->|"Macro overview"| opc["Operation Process Chart"]
    q1 -->|"Process flow with waste"| fpc["Flow Process Chart Material or Man"]
    q1 -->|"Micro detail"| q2{"Is human motion the focus?"}
    q2 -->|"Yes single operator"| thp["Two Handed Process Chart"]
    q2 -->|"Yes with timing"| simo["Simo Chart"]
    q2 -->|"Yes with path"| cyc["Cyclegraph or Chronocyclegraph"]
    q2 -->|"No machine interaction"| q3{"How many resources are involved?"}
    q3 -->|"Operator plus one machine"| mmc["Man Machine Chart"]
    q3 -->|"Multiple workers or machines"| mac["Multiple Activity Chart"]
    q3 -->|"Layout movement"| sd["String Diagram or Travel Chart"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define "Examining the Work" and list the six procedural steps involved. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Examining the Work* is the systematic and critical investigation of a work activity to identify, record, and analyze the present method, with the purpose of developing a more economical, ergonomic, and productive alternative.

**The six steps are:**

1. **SELECT** the work to be studied.
2. **RECORD** the present method using charts and diagrams.
3. **EXAMINE** the recorded facts critically.
4. **DEVELOP** the most economical method.
5. **INSTALL** the improved method.
6. **MAINTAIN** the new method through follow-up.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the 6-step list with one-line explanation each.)*

---

### Q2. What is the "Questioning Technique" in critical examination? Mention the primary questions. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The *Questioning Technique* is the analytical core of examining the work. It subjects every recorded element to two levels of questioning — **Primary** and **Secondary** — to challenge necessity, place, timing, person, and means.

**The five Primary Questions are:**

1. *What is being done?* (Purpose)
2. *Where is it being done?* (Place)
3. *When is it being done?* (Sequence / Time)
4. *Who is doing it?* (Person)
5. *How is it being done?* (Means)

Each primary question is then drilled down with **three secondary questions** (What is the purpose / Is it necessary / What else could be done), giving a 5 × 3 = **15-cell analysis matrix**.

*(1 mark for the concept + 2 marks for listing the 5 primary questions and the secondary drill-down idea.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In 14-mark questions, students commonly *skip the critical-examination step* and jump from "RECORD" to "DEVELOP" — this costs 3–4 marks.
> - Always **draw the recording chart** (process chart / two-handed chart) **before** writing its description; partial marks for a chart are NOT given if the chart is missing.
> - The **formula chain** OT → NT → ST must be shown with the *units* — examiners specifically check for unit consistency.
> - Always conclude with a **productivity improvement percentage**; answers that end with "improved method designed" lose the final 1–2 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the procedure for examining the work in detail, with a neat sketch of the **Operation Process Chart** and the **Two-Handed Process Chart**. Mention the symbols used. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A worker performs a manual tapping operation. The observed times for 8 cycles (in minutes) are: 2.10, 2.05, 2.08, 2.12, 2.06, 2.09, 2.07, 2.11. The operator is rated at 105% and the relaxation allowance is 12%, personal allowance 5%, and contingency allowance 3%. Compute the **Standard Time per piece** and the **expected output per 8-hour shift**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**1. The 6-step procedure** (4 marks)

- **SELECT:** Choose the work using criteria like cost, frequency, ergonomic risk, or bottleneck nature.
- **RECORD:** Capture every fact objectively using charts, diagrams, and time data — *no opinions*.
- **EXAMINE:** Use the 5 × 3 Questioning Technique to challenge every element.
- **DEVELOP:** Synthesize improvements into a new, more economical method.
- **INSTALL:** Implement with worker cooperation, training, and tooling.
- **MAINTAIN:** Audit regularly, control method drift, and update standards.

**2. Operation Process Chart — symbols (1.5 marks):**

| Symbol | Activity |
|--------|----------|
| ⭕ (Big circle) | Operation |
| ▢ (Square) | Inspection |
| ⇒ (Arrow) | Transport |
| ▽ (Inverted triangle) | Delay |
| ▽ (Triangle with cap) | Storage |

**3. Two-Handed Process Chart — description (1.5 marks):**
A two-handed process chart is a *micro* recording tool that represents the simultaneous left-hand and right-hand activities of a single operator on a time-ordered scale. It uses the same 5 symbols but splits them into **Left Hand (LH)** and **Right Hand (RH)** columns, making hand idleness, asymmetric loading, and waste visible.

*(Marks split: 1 mark per step in procedure, 1.5 marks for OPC, 1.5 marks for THPC sketch & description.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Given:**

$$T = \{2.10,\ 2.05,\ 2.08,\ 2.12,\ 2.06,\ 2.09,\ 2.07,\ 2.11\}\ \text{min}, \quad n = 8, \quad R = 1.05, \quad A = 0.12 + 0.05 + 0.03 = 0.20$$

**Step 1 — Observed Time:**

$$OT = \frac{1}{8} \sum T_i = \frac{16.68}{8} = 2.085\ \text{min/piece}$$

**Step 2 — Normal Time:**

$$NT = OT \times R = 2.085 \times 1.05 = 2.18925\ \text{min/piece}$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time:**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 2.18925 \times 1.20 = 2.6271\ \text{min/piece}$$

**Step 4 — Output per 8-hour shift:**

$$\text{Shift length} = 8 \times 60 = 480\ \text{min}$$

$$\text{Output per shift} = \frac{480}{ST} = \frac{480}{2.6271} \approx 182.7 \rightarrow 182\ \text{pieces/shift}$$

*(Full marks if the chain OT → NT → ST is shown with units; partial credit of 1 mark if NT alone is computed.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **Critical Examination** step of examining the work. Explain the **5 × 3 Questioning Technique** with an example. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A preliminary work-sampling study on a CNC turning cell produced the following results: in 200 random observations, the machine was found idle in 60 observations. For an absolute accuracy of $\pm 5\%$ at 95% confidence, determine the **total number of observations required** and hence the **additional observations needed**. Comment on the operator's activity ratio. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**1. Definition of Critical Examination (2 marks):**
Critical examination is the analytical stage in examining the work where every recorded fact is subjected to systematic questioning using the *Primary–Secondary Questioning Technique*. The purpose is to challenge the necessity of each activity, its location, timing, performer, and means — and to expose the wasteful elements that can be eliminated.

**2. The 5 × 3 Questioning Matrix (3 marks):**

- *5 Primary Questions:* **What, Where, When, Who, How.**
- *3 Secondary Questions* for each primary: (i) *Why is it done this way?* (ii) *What is the underlying purpose / what would happen if we stopped?* (iii) *What alternative / better method exists?*

**3. Example (2 marks):**

*Activity under study:* Worker walks 8 m to fetch a tool from a rack.

| Q | Answer |
|---|--------|
| **What?** | Walking to pick tool. |
| **Where?** | Across the shop. |
| **When?** | Whenever a tool is needed. |
| **Who?** | The operator. |
| **How?** | On foot, during the cycle. |

*Secondary questioning:*
- Why walk? Because tools are stored centrally. *→ Re-locate tools at the workstation.*
- Why not bring all tools in advance? *→ Use a tool trolley beside the machine.*
- Could a robot fetch? *→ Not economical for one cell, but for a long term yes.*

*Outcome:* Install a **shadow board / tool trolley** at the machine → walking eliminated, cycle time reduced by ~12 s, productivity up by 8 %.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{prelim}} = 200$, $n_{\text{idle}} = 60$.
- $p = 60/200 = 0.30$ (idle proportion).
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence), $E = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Total observations required:**

$$N = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.30) \cdot (0.70)}{(0.05)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$\text{Num} = 3.8416 \times 0.21 = 0.80674$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$\text{Den} = 0.0025$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**

$$N = \frac{0.80674}{0.0025} = 322.7 \rightarrow 323\ \text{total observations}$$

**Step 5 — Additional observations:**

$$N_{\text{additional}} = 323 - 200 = 123\ \text{more observations}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on activity ratio:**

$$\text{Activity Ratio} = (1 - p) \times 100 = (1 - 0.30) \times 100 = 70\%$$

This means the CNC machine is productive (cutting/loading) for only **70 % of the available time**; **30 % is idle waste** — a strong candidate for method improvement or maintenance scheduling.

*(Marks split: 3 marks for formula, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for units/rounding, 1 for the activity-ratio interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Warrior Mode):**

- ☐ *Examining the Work* = the **RECORD → EXAMINE → DEVELOP → INSTALL → MAINTAIN** arm of Work Study.
- ☐ The **6-step universal procedure** is the syllabus spine — memorize in order: **Select, Record, Examine, Develop, Install, Maintain**.
- ☐ **Critical Examination** uses the **5 × 3 Questioning Matrix** (Primary: What, Where, When, Who, How × Secondary: Why / Is it necessary / What else).
- ☐ **5 standard symbols** for process charts: **Operation ⭕, Inspection ▢, Transport ⇒, Delay ▽, Storage ▽** — never mix up Delay and Storage.
- ☐ **Two-Handed Process Chart** = best for short-cycle repetitive work to detect hand asymmetry.
- ☐ **Man–Machine Chart** = best for operator supervising one or more machines.
- ☐ **String Diagram** = best for layout/movement decisions.
- ☐ **Standard Time formula (must-memorize):** $ST = OT \times R \times (1 + A)$.
- ☐ **Allowances** typically total **15 – 20 %** of Normal Time.
- ☐ **Work Sampling sample size:** $N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$; for 95 % confidence use $Z = 1.96$.
- ☐ **Activity Ratio** = $(1 - p_{\text{idle}}) \times 100$ % — a low AR signals waste.
- ☐ **Productivity % improvement** = $\dfrac{ST_{\text{old}} - ST_{\text{new}}}{ST_{\text{old}}} \times 100$.
- ☐ **Always end a 14-mark answer** with a quantitative productivity result — examiners award the *last 1–2 marks* for impact analysis.
- ☐ **Don't confuse:** *Methods Study* (how to do) vs. *Work Measurement* (how long it takes) — *Examining the Work* is the bridge between the two.
- ☐ **Pitfall:** stating "improved method designed" without showing the new flow chart / chart symbols loses 2 marks minimum.
- ☐ **Standard pace** = 100 B.S.; rating $R = 1.00$ corresponds to a *qualified worker working at normal pace*.

> **End of Notes — Module 2 / Examining the Work.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Developing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Developing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Method Development** is the systematic, analytical phase of Method Study wherein the existing (or proposed) work method is critically examined, questioned, and restructured to evolve a more efficient, economic, and ergonomic work pattern. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, it is the **engineering process of designing and standardizing "one best way"** to perform a task by eliminating unnecessary motion, waste, and human effort.

The procedure is governed by the universally accepted **7-Phase Method Study Procedure** formalized by the British Standards Institution (BSI) and adopted by the International Labour Organization (ILO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *Developing methods* — the systematic procedure of creating, evaluating, and installing improved work methods using analytical techniques, motion economy principles, and ergonomic design considerations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chef's Recipe"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a street food vendor making tea. Initially, he uses any cup, any hand, any sequence. One day he observes: he walks **5 extra steps** for sugar, he stirs with the **non-dominant hand**, and he lifts the kettle by **tilting his wrist awkwardly**. He reorders his ingredients (right-hand side first), uses a pre-mixed sugar jar, and switches hands. This redesign — without changing the *product* (tea) — is exactly what **Developing Methods** does in industry: redesign the **process**, not the **output**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Critical)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Method Study** | Systematic recording, analysis, and improvement of work methods |
| **Therblig** | Basic indivisible human motion (e.g., Grasp, Move, Position) — coined by F.W. Gilbreth |
| **Motion Economy** | Set of **22 Principles** governing efficient human movement |
| **Standardization** | Documenting and locking-in the newly developed "best method" |
| **Ergonomics** | Designing the job to fit the human worker (Human Factors Engineering) |
| **Workplace Layout** | Arrangement of 5M's (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement) |

## 1.4 Position in the KTU Work System Design Framework

$$\text{Work System Design} = \text{Product Design} + \text{Process/Method Design} + \text{Workplace Design} + \text{Work Measurement}$$

This topic sits within **Process/Method Design**, bridging the gap between the analytical phases (recording charts) of Module 1 and the time-quantification (stopwatch time study) of Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The 7-Phase Method Study Procedure (BSI/ILO Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most frequently asked 14-mark framework** in KTU ESE for Module 2. Memorize the 7 steps in exact order.

### Step 1 — **SELECT**
- Identify the problem / process to be studied.
- Define boundaries (start and end points of investigation).
- **Why?** Because we cannot study the entire factory at once.

### Step 2 — **RECORD**
- Gather objective data using:
  - **Outline Process Charts** (overall flow)
  - **Flow Process Charts** (Man / Material / Machine type)
  - **Two-Handed Process Charts**
  - **String Diagrams / Flow Diagrams**
  - **Cyclegraph / Chronocyclegraph** (micromotion)
  - **SIMO Charts**
  - **Memo Motion Studies** (using still photographs)
- **Why?** Subjective opinion is unreliable; visual records are auditable.

### Step 3 — **EXAMINE**
- Apply the **Critical Examination** technique (Questioning Attitude).
- The **4 Ws + 1 H** framework: What is done? Why is it done? Where is it done? When is it done? **How** is it done?
- **Why?** To challenge every existing step before accepting or rejecting it.

### Step 4 — **DEVELOP**
- Generate alternative improved methods.
- Apply the **22 Principles of Motion Economy** (Therblig-based).
- Apply **Ergonomic Workplace Design** (5M layout).
- **Why?** Multiple alternatives must exist before the best is chosen.

### Step 5 — **DEFINE**
- Document the new method precisely.
- Use updated process charts, layouts, and written specifications.
- **Why?** A "best method" must be reproducible, not just imagined.

### Step 6 — **INSTALL**
- Implement the new method in practice.
- Train workers, re-layout workplace, modify tools/equipment.
- **Why?** A method on paper is not a method in action.

### Step 7 — **MAINTAIN**
- Audit, supervise, and refine.
- Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and Work Instructions must be enforced.
- **Why?** Workers naturally drift back to old habits (Hodgson's Law of Habit).

## 2.2 The 22 Principles of Motion Economy

Classified into three categories by Ralph Barnes (1937) and revised by KTU:

| Category | Count | Governing Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Principles concerning the use of the human body** | **10** | Posture, limbs, fatigue |
| **Principles concerning the arrangement of the workplace** | **6** | Layout, 5M placement |
| **Principles concerning the design of tools and equipment** | **6** | Jigs, fixtures, controls |

> [!NOTE]
> **Top 5 High-Frequency Principles asked in KTU boards:**
> 1. Both hands should begin/finish motion **simultaneously** (symmetric bimanual).
> 2. **Definite and fixed stations** must be established for all tools and materials.
> 3. **Rhythm** must be automatic and uniform.
> 4. **Gravity** should be used to assist the worker (drop delivery).
> 5. **Curved motions** are preferable to straight-line motions involving abrupt changes.

## 2.3 Therbligs — The 18 Fundamental Motions

Gilbreth's **18 Therbligs** (anagram of "Gilbreth") are the atomic units of human motion. Classified as:

- **Effective Therbligs** (productive): Reach, Move, Grasp, Position, Assemble, Disassemble, Use, Release
- **Ineffective Therbligs** (waste): Search, Find, Select, Inspect, Pre-position, Hold, Unavoidable Delay, Avoidable Delay, Rest, Plan

**KTU High-Yield Formulas (Quantitative Method Development):**

$$
\text{\% Waste Motion} = \frac{\sum \text{Ineffective Therbligs (Time)}}{\sum \text{All Therbligs (Time)}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Method Effectiveness Index} = \frac{\sum \text{Effective Therbligs}}{\sum \text{Total Therbligs}}
$$

$$
\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} \times \left(1 + \text{Rating Factor} \times \text{Allowance \%}\right)
$$

$$
\text{MTM (Methods-Time Measurement) Basic TMU: 1 TMU} = 10^{-5} \text{ hours} = \frac{1}{100{,}000} \text{ hour}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Saved Time per Cycle} = T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{\% Productivity Gain} = \frac{T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}} \times 100}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Annual Saving} = \left(\frac{\text{Saved Time}}{\text{Standard Time}}\right) \times \text{Annual Output} \times \text{Unit Cost}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** The Therblig count is exactly **18**, not 17 or 20. MTM uses **TMU (Time Measurement Unit)** as standard. The 22 principles of motion economy are attributed to **Ralph G. Barnes** (1937), refining Gilbreth's work — not to Gilbreth himself.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines**: Toyota Production System applies the 22 principles via the *Heijunka* (level scheduling) and *Poka-Yoke* (mistake-proofing) tools.
- **Ergonomic Chairs (Herman Miller Aeron)**: Designed using motion economy + biomechanics.
- **Surgical Procedure Optimization**: Method study cuts operating room time by 20–40% in published hospital studies.
- **Software UX Design**: The 18 Therbligs translate directly to "Fitts's Law" in HCI (Grasp ≈ Pointing + Clicking).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Therblig Time Reduction

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Board Style):**
A worker performs an assembly task with the following Therblig sequence. The old method required 80 seconds. After method development, the ineffective motions were reduced by 60%. Calculate the new cycle time and the percentage productivity gain.

| Therblig | Symbol | Time (s) — Old | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reach for bolt | RE | 4 | Effective |
| Grasp bolt | G | 3 | Effective |
| Move bolt to hole | M | 5 | Effective |
| Search for hole | SH | 6 | Ineffective |
| Position bolt | P | 4 | Effective |
| Assemble | A | 8 | Effective |
| Hold nut | H | 10 | Ineffective |
| Move wrench | M | 6 | Effective |
| Use wrench | U | 12 | Effective |
| Release bolt | RL | 4 | Effective |
| Search for next bolt | SH | 8 | Ineffective |
| Reach next bolt | RE | 4 | Effective |
| Unavoidable delay | UD | 6 | Ineffective |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify total time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{old}} &= 4 + 3 + 5 + 6 + 4 + 8 + 10 + 6 + 12 + 4 + 8 + 4 + 6 \\
&= 80 \text{ seconds} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum ineffective Therblig time (old method):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ineff,old}} &= 6_{\text{SH}} + 10_{\text{H}} + 8_{\text{SH}} + 6_{\text{UD}} \\
&= 30 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply 60% reduction to ineffective motions:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ineff,new}} &= 30 \times (1 - 0.60) \\
&= 30 \times 0.40 \\
&= 12 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute new cycle time (effective motions unchanged):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{new}} &= T_{\text{eff}} + T_{\text{ineff,new}} \\
&= 50 + 12 \\
&= 62 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute percentage productivity gain:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Gain} &= \frac{T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{80 - 62}{80} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{18}{80} \times 100 \\
&= 22.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** New cycle time = **62 s**, Productivity gain = **22.5%**

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Annual Savings Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A garment factory produces **5,00,000 shirts/year**. The current method consumes 0.45 hours/shirt at a labour cost of ₹120/hour. Method development reduces the standard time to 0.35 hours/shirt. Calculate:
1. Time saved per shirt.
2. % time saving.
3. Annual monetary saving.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Time saved per shirt:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= 0.45 - 0.35 = 0.10 \text{ hours/shirt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Percentage time saving:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Saving} &= \frac{0.10}{0.45} \times 100 = 22.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual hours saved:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Saved Hrs} &= 0.10 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary saving:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Saving} &= 50{,}000 \times 120 = \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Δt = 0.10 hr, % Saving = 22.22%, Annual Saving = **₹ 60 Lakhs**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Method Improvement Tool

| Complexity of Task | Recommended Tool | Tool Type | KTU Board Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repetitive manual assembly | **Two-Handed Process Chart** | Macro-motion | High |
| Subtle fast movements | **SIMO Chart / Memo-Motion** | Micro-motion | Medium |
| Multi-worker workflow | **String Diagram** | Multi-flow | High |
| Material + worker flow | **Flow Process Chart (Material Type)** | Combined | High |
| High-precision timing | **MTM / Basic Motion Timestudy** | Predetermined | Medium |
| Long-cycle machine-heavy | **Multiple Activity Chart** | Group | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The 7-Phase Method Development Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["P1 SELECT: Define Work Problem Boundaries"] --> B["P2 RECORD: Gather Objective Data via Charts"]
    B --> C["P3 EXAMINE: Apply 4W 1H Critical Questioning"]
    C --> D["P4 DEVELOP: Generate Alternatives using 22 Motion Economy Principles"]
    D --> E["P5 DEFINE: Document Standard Operating Procedure"]
    E --> F["P6 INSTALL: Train Workers Reconfigure Workplace"]
    F --> G["P7 MAINTAIN: Audit Enforce Standardization"]
    G --> H{"Improvement Stable?"}
    H -->|No| C
    H -->|Yes| I["End: New Standard Established"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Method Development

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Existing Work Method"]
        A2["Worker Feedback"]
        A3["Process Charts and Diagrams"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["METHOD DEVELOPMENT ENGINE"]
        B1["Critical Examination Module"]
        B2["Motion Economy Principles Library"]
        B3["Ergonomic Design Module"]
        B4["Workplace Layout Optimizer"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1["Improved Work Method"]
        C2["Updated Process Charts"]
        C3["Standard Operating Procedure"]
        C4["SOP Worker Training Plan"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Therblig Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["18 THERBLIGS"] --> B["EFFECTIVE MOTIONS"]
    A --> C["INEFFECTIVE MOTIONS"]

    B --> B1["Reach"]
    B --> B2["Move"]
    B --> B3["Grasp"]
    B --> B4["Position"]
    B --> B5["Assemble"]
    B --> B6["Disassemble"]
    B --> B7["Use"]
    B --> B8["Release"]

    C --> C1["Search"]
    C --> C2["Find"]
    C --> C3["Select"]
    C --> C4["Inspect"]
    C --> C5["Pre Position"]
    C --> C6["Hold"]
    C --> C7["Unavoidable Delay"]
    C --> C8["Avoidable Delay"]
    C --> C9["Rest"]
    C --> C10["Plan"]
```

## 4.4 Mapping of Motion Economy Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["22 PRINCIPLES OF MOTION ECONOMY"] --> P1["HUMAN BODY 10 PRINCIPLES"]
    A --> P2["WORKPLACE LAYOUT 6 PRINCIPLES"]
    A --> P3["TOOLS EQUIPMENT 6 PRINCIPLES"]

    P1 --> P1A["Both hands work simultaneously"]
    P1 --> P1B["Arm motions symmetric"]
    P1 --> P1C["Momentum assists worker"]

    P2 --> P2A["Definite fixed stations for tools"]
    P2 --> P2B["Gravity for drop delivery"]
    P2 --> P2C["Rhythm uniform automatic"]

    P3 --> P3A["Tools pre positioned"]
    P3 --> P3B["Two or more tools combined"]
    P3 --> P3C["Controls pedals operated easily"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Method Study". State the objectives of Method Study as applied in industrial engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Method Study is the systematic recording, critical examination, and development of work methods to improve efficiency. It is the first phase of Work Study (Method Study + Work Measurement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Objectives (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Improve existing methods** by eliminating unnecessary motions, delays, and waste.
> 2. **Standardize** the "one best way" of performing a task.
> 3. **Reduce fatigue** of the worker by improving ergonomics.
> 4. **Increase productivity** and **reduce cost** without capital investment.
> 5. **Improve safety** and **working conditions** in the workplace.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three Principles of Motion Economy. Explain how they improve the working method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Principle: Both hands should begin and finish motion simultaneously** (Symmetric bimanual motion).
   *Improvement:* Idle time of one hand is eliminated; cycle time is reduced by overlapping operations.

2. **Principle: Definite and fixed stations for all tools and materials.**
   *Improvement:* Search and Find therbligs (ineffective) are eliminated, reducing eye/hand fatigue.

3. **Principle: Gravity should be used to assist the worker (Drop Delivery).**
   *Improvement:* The "Move" therblig is converted to a free fall, saving muscular energy and time.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven phases of method study** as recommended by BSI/ILO, with one industrial example for each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A textile machine operator follows a sequence of motions whose Therblig times are: Reach 5 s, Grasp 4 s, Move 8 s, Position 6 s, Assemble 12 s, Use 10 s, Release 4 s, plus 15 s of ineffective motions (Search 6 s, Hold 9 s). After method development, ineffective motion is reduced by 75% with no change in effective time. Calculate: **(i)** Old cycle time, **(ii)** New cycle time, **(iii)** Productivity gain %. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Seven Phases of Method Study (each phase = 1 mark):**

| Phase | Activity | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **SELECT** | Define the job to be studied. | Choosing to study the *gear-box assembly* line in a tractor plant. |
| 2. **RECORD** | Capture current method using charts, diagrams, video. | Recording an **Outline Process Chart** of the gearbox line. |
| 3. **EXAMINE** | Apply 4W+1H critical examination to every step. | Questioning: *Why is the gear lifted manually? Why not use gravity chute?* |
| 4. **DEVELOP** | Generate alternative methods using motion economy + ergonomics. | Designing a *gravity-fed bin* to deliver gears automatically. |
| 5. **DEFINE** | Document the new method in charts and SOPs. | Preparing a new **Two-Handed Process Chart** with a written SOP. |
| 6. **INSTALL** | Implement the new method; train workers. | Conducting a 2-day *on-the-job training* for assembly workers. |
| 7. **MAINTAIN** | Audit and ensure the method is followed. | Weekly *supervisor rounds* + monthly method audit by IE team. |

---

**Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Effective motions: Reach 5, Grasp 4, Move 8, Position 6, Assemble 12, Use 10, Release 4.
- Ineffective motions: Search 6, Hold 9 → Total = **15 s**.

**Step (i) — Old Cycle Time (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{old}} &= 5 + 4 + 8 + 6 + 12 + 10 + 4 + 15 \\
&= 64 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — New Cycle Time (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ineff,new}} &= 15 \times (1 - 0.75) = 15 \times 0.25 = 3.75 \text{ s} \\
T_{\text{new}} &= T_{\text{eff}} + T_{\text{ineff,new}} \\
&= 49 + 3.75 \\
&= 52.75 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Productivity Gain (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Gain} &= \frac{64 - 52.75}{64} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{11.25}{64} \times 100 \\
&= 17.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** (i) T_old = **64 s**, (ii) T_new = **52.75 s**, (iii) Gain = **17.58%**

---

### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Discuss the **18 Therbligs** classified by Gilbreth. Distinguish between **Effective** and **Ineffective** Therbligs with industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker assembles 100 units per day. The standard time of the new method is 0.40 hours/unit. Calculate: **(i)** Number of hours of work per day, **(ii)** If 1 hour of work is paid ₹150 and fixed overhead per unit is ₹20, what is the **new method's cost per unit**, and **(iii)** What is the **annual saving** if the old method took 0.55 hours/unit and the company works **300 days/year**? **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Therbligs are the 18 indivisible basic motions of work classified by F.W. Gilbreth (anagram of "Gilbreth"). They are the elementary building blocks of any manual operation.

**Effective Therbligs (8 in number — 3 marks):**
1. **Reach (RE)** — moving hand empty towards object.
2. **Move (M)** — moving hand loaded with object.
3. **Grasp (G)** — closing fingers around object.
4. **Position (P)** — orienting object.
5. **Assemble (A)** — joining parts.
6. **Disassemble (DA)** — separating parts.
7. **Use (U)** — manipulating tool for intended purpose.
8. **Release (RL)** — letting go of object.

**Ineffective Therbligs (10 in number — 3 marks):**
1. **Search (SH)** — eyes/hands trying to find object.
2. **Find (F)** — moment of recognition.
3. **Select (ST)** — choosing between alternatives.
4. **Inspect (I)** — comparing to standard.
5. **Pre-position (PP)** — positioning for next operation.
6. **Hold (H)** — supporting object without doing useful work.
7. **Unavoidable Delay (UD)** — unavoidable waiting.
8. **Avoidable Delay (AD)** — wasted waiting.
9. **Rest (R)** — recovery from fatigue.
10. **Plan (PN)** — mental processing.

---

**Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:** New ST = 0.40 hr/unit, Daily output = 100 units, Old ST = 0.55 hr/unit, Working days = 300, Wage = ₹150/hr, Overhead = ₹20/unit.

**Step (i) — Daily hours (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{daily}} &= 100 \times 0.40 = 40 \text{ hours/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — New method's cost per unit (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Labour cost/unit} &= 0.40 \times 150 = \text{₹}60 \\
\text{Total cost/unit} &= 60 + 20 = \text{₹}80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Annual saving (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= 0.55 - 0.40 = 0.15 \text{ hr/unit} \\
\text{Annual hours saved} &= 0.15 \times 100 \times 300 = 4{,}500 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Annual Saving} &= 4{,}500 \times 150 = \text{₹}6{,}75{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** (i) 40 hrs/day, (ii) **₹80/unit**, (iii) **₹6,75,000/year**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Order matters in 7-Phases**: Students lose marks by swapping DEVELOP and DEFINE. Memorize: **S-R-E-D-D-I-M**.
> 2. **Therblig Count**: Do not write 17 or 19. It is **exactly 18**.
> 3. **Motion Economy Count**: It is **22 principles** (not 18, not 25). Ralph Barnes refined them in 1937.
> 4. **% Productivity Gain Formula**: Always divide by **T_old** (old time), not new time. This is a top 1 mark-loss area.
> 5. **"One Best Way"**: This phrase must appear in the definition of Method Development.
> 6. **Units**: Standard time is in **minutes or hours**, not seconds. 1 TMU = 0.00001 hour.
> 7. **In step-by-step problems, ALWAYS show the unit (s, hr, ₹)** — examiners deduct for missing units.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✏️ **Method Development** = the 5th step of Method Study (after Record, Examine, Develop comes the new method).
- ✏️ The **7 Phases of Method Study** are: **Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Define → Install → Maintain** (mnemonic: **SRED DIM**).
- ✏️ Method Study is the **first phase of Work Study**; Work Measurement is the second.
- ✏️ **Gilbreth** introduced **Therbligs (18)**; **Barnes** formalized **22 Principles of Motion Economy**.
- ✏️ Therbligs are classified as **8 Effective + 10 Ineffective** = 18 total.
- ✏️ The 3 categories of Motion Economy: **Human Body (10)**, **Workplace (6)**, **Tools & Equipment (6)**.
- ✏️ **Critical Examination** = questioning the existing method using the **4W + 1H** framework.
- ✏️ Formula: $\% \text{ Productivity Gain} = \dfrac{T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}} \times 100$.
- ✏️ Formula: $\% \text{ Waste Motion} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Ineffective Therblig Time}}{\sum \text{Total Therblig Time}} \times 100$.
- ✏️ **1 TMU** (Methods-Time Measurement) = $10^{-5}$ hour = 0.0006 minute = 0.036 second.
- ✏️ **Two-Handed Process Chart** is used for repetitive manual operations involving both hands.
- ✏️ **SIMO Chart** (Simultaneous Motion Chart) is used for micro-motion analysis.
- ✏️ **String Diagram** is used for multi-worker / multi-machine layout problems.
- ✏️ Method Development is the **engineering process** of designing the "**one best way**" — emphasizing *efficiency*, *ergonomics*, and *standardization*.
- ✏️ Real-world impact: Toyota Production System, Lean Manufacturing, and Six Sigma all rely on the 7-phase method study framework.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation and Implementation of Method Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluation and Implementation of Method Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Method Study Evaluation?

**Method Study Evaluation** is the critical appraisal phase that follows the development of alternative methods in a method study project. It is the systematic process of comparing the proposed (improved) method against the existing (present) method using objective criteria to determine the most efficient, safe, and practical alternative before final selection and execution.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, evaluation is defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *"Evaluation in method study is the process of comparing the various alternative methods developed during the analysis and development stages, against the present method, using criteria such as economy, ergonomics, safety, and operational feasibility, to select the most efficient method for implementation."*

**Key Evaluation Criteria (The Four Pillars):**
- **Economic Viability** — Cost savings, return on investment
- **Ergonomic & Human Factors** — Operator comfort, fatigue reduction
- **Operational Feasibility** — Technical practicality
- **Safety & Compliance** — Risk reduction, regulatory adherence

## 1.2 What is Method Study Implementation?

**Implementation** is the execution phase where the selected improved method is actually installed on the shop floor. It involves planning the changeover, gaining worker and management acceptance, training operators, and monitoring the new system to ensure the projected benefits are realized.

> [!NOTE]
> **Implementation is not the end of method study — it is the bridge between the proposed plan and realized productivity gains.** A brilliant method that is poorly implemented will fail on the shop floor.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Home Renovation Story

Imagine you want to renovate your kitchen (the **present method** is outdated). You have two design options (**alternative methods**) prepared by an architect. Before signing the contract, you **evaluate** them:

1. **Cost Analysis** — Which option saves money long-term? (Economic Evaluation)
2. **Comfort Check** — Will my mother (the operator) be able to work easily? (Ergonomic Evaluation)
3. **Practicality** — Can the contractor actually build it within my house structure? (Operational Evaluation)
4. **Safety** — Is the gas line placement safe? (Safety Evaluation)

Once you choose the best design, you **implement** it: hire workers, train your family on the new layout, run it for a week, fix teething problems, and finally enjoy the new kitchen.

**Method Study is exactly this — but for industrial work systems, not kitchens.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evaluation Framework (Analytical Breakdown)

Method Study evaluation is not a single step — it is a **multi-criteria decision-making process**. The following structured logic governs the KTU-recommended evaluation flow:

### Step 1 — Economic Evaluation
This is the **quantitative backbone** of evaluation. It answers: *"Will the new method save money?"*

- Compare the **total cost of the present method** vs. the **total cost of the proposed method**.
- If savings exist, calculate the **net savings, payback period, and rate of return**.

### Step 2 — Ergonomic & Human Factors Evaluation
The proposed method is assessed for:
- **Postural stress** (sitting, standing, bending, reaching)
- **Energy expenditure** (metabolic cost of work)
- **Skill requirements** and operator learning curve
- **Worker satisfaction** and morale impact

### Step 3 — Operational / Technical Evaluation
- Is the equipment readily available?
- Can the existing layout accommodate the change?
- Are there production bottlenecks introduced?
- What is the effect on **quality, throughput, and delivery**?

### Step 4 — Safety Evaluation
- Identifies new hazards introduced.
- Ensures compliance with **Factory Act, OSHA-equivalent standards**.
- Considers emergency access and machine guarding.

### Step 5 — Composite Evaluation
When methods rank differently on different criteria, a **weighted scoring model** is used.

## 2.2 The Implementation Framework (Step-by-Step Logic)

The classic KTU-recommended implementation sequence (as per ILO Method Study conventions adopted in Indian engineering curricula) is:

1. **Obtain management approval** for the selected method
2. **Present the proposal** to workers and supervisors (sell the idea)
3. **Plan the changeover** — schedule, resources, contingencies
4. **Install the new method** — equipment, layout, tooling
5. **Train the operators** — skill transfer and familiarization
6. **Run a trial period** — pilot observation and debugging
7. **Standardize** — fix the method, document SOPs
8. **Maintain the method** — periodic audits, continuous improvement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 'Sell the Idea' before 'Install'?**
> Worker resistance is the **#1 cause of method study failure** in Indian industries. The implementation phase places equal weight on the human (sociological) element as on the technical change.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Total Method Cost** | $\text{TC} = \text{Labour Cost} + \text{Material Cost} + \text{Overhead Cost}$ | $\text{INR/year}$ |
| 2 | **Labour Cost** | $\text{LC} = N \times T \times R$ | $N$ = workers, $T$ = time (h), $R$ = rate (INR/h) |
| 3 | **Annual Labour Saving** | $\text{ALS} = (T_{\text{present}} - T_{\text{proposed}}) \times N \times R \times S$ | $S$ = annual production units |
| 4 | **Net Annual Saving** | $\text{NAS} = \text{ALS} - \text{Extra Overhead}$ | $\text{INR/year}$ |
| 5 | **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Capital Investment}}{\text{NAS}}$ | $\text{years}$ or $\text{months}$ |
| 6 | **Rate of Return on Investment** | $\text{ROI} = \left( \dfrac{\text{NAS}}{\text{Investment}} \right) \times 100$ | $\text{\% per annum}$ |
| 7 | **Ergonomic Score (Weighted)** | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = score, $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 8 | **Composite Evaluation Score** | $C = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \alpha_j \cdot E_j$ | $\alpha_j$ = criterion weight, $E_j$ = normalized score |
| 9 | **Break-Even Output** | $BEP_{\text{output}} = \dfrac{\text{Extra Fixed Cost}}{\text{Saving per unit}}$ | units/year |
| 10 | **% Productivity Improvement** | $\Delta P = \left( \dfrac{T_{\text{present}} - T_{\text{proposed}}}{T_{\text{proposed}}} \right) \times 100$ | $\text{\%}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** In the formula table above, the symbol $\vert$ is **never** used inside a row to avoid breaking the markdown table. All absolute-value or conditional operators must be rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` only when displayed outside tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Used to justify capital expenditure on automation, jigs, fixtures.
- **Service Industries:** Used in hospitals, banks, and logistics to redesign workflows.
- **Ergonomics Consulting:** Drives workstation redesign to prevent musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs).
- **Lean Six Sigma Projects:** Forms the "I" (Implement) and "C" (Control) of the DMAIC cycle.
- **Cost Engineering:** Provides the financial justification (payback, ROI) that CFOs demand.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Economic Evaluation (The Total Cost Comparison)

The **total method cost** of any work system has three distinct components. Let us derive each mathematically and then combine them.

### 3.1.1 Labour Cost Component

Labour cost arises because workers spend time on a task. The time consumed has a financial value.

$$
\text{Labour Cost (LC)} = N \times T \times R
$$

where the variables are:

- $N$ = Number of workers assigned to the task
- $T$ = Time taken per cycle (in hours)
- $R$ = Wage rate per worker per hour (in $\text{INR/h}$)

For an **annual** labour cost, we multiply by the number of cycles produced in a year:

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{annual}} = N \times T \times R \times S
$$

where $S$ is the **annual production volume** (number of units/year).

### 3.1.2 Material Cost Component

Material cost is straightforward — it is the cost of consumables and direct materials consumed per unit:

$$
\text{MC} = M \times C_m
$$

where $M$ = material quantity per unit, $C_m$ = material cost per unit quantity.

### 3.1.3 Overhead Cost Component

Overheads are the **indirect costs** — electricity, depreciation, rent, supervision, maintenance:

$$
\text{OC} = \text{Fixed Overhead} + \text{Variable Overhead}
$$

For evaluation purposes, the **extra overhead** introduced by the proposed method is the relevant quantity.

### 3.1.4 Total Cost Aggregation

$$
\text{TC} = \text{LC} + \text{MC} + \text{OC}
$$

The **saving** when moving from present to proposed is:

$$
\text{Saving} = \text{TC}_{\text{present}} - \text{TC}_{\text{proposed}}
$$

If the proposed method requires extra investment, the **Net Annual Saving** is:

$$
\text{NAS} = \text{Saving} - \text{Extra Overhead}_{\text{proposed}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Payback Period

The payback period answers the question: *"In how long will my investment be recovered?"*

$$
\text{PP} = \frac{\text{Capital Investment (CI)}}{\text{Net Annual Saving (NAS)}}
$$

The **decision rule** is: if $\text{PP} \leq \text{Target Payback}$, **accept** the method; else, **reject**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Rate of Return on Investment (ROI)

$$
\text{ROI (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{NAS}}{\text{CI}} \right) \times 100
$$

This is the **annual percentage return** the company earns on its investment in the new method. It must exceed the **hurdle rate** (company's minimum acceptable return) for the project to be approved.

## 3.4 Derivation: Composite Evaluation Score (Weighted Multi-Criteria)

When the present method is better on cost but the proposed method is better on ergonomics, a **weighted scoring model** is used.

$$
C = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \alpha_j \cdot E_j
$$

subject to the constraint:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{k} \alpha_j = 1
$$

The method with the **highest $C$** is selected.

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**

> A manufacturing cell currently uses **6 workers** to assemble a component. Each worker takes **45 minutes** per unit, and the wage rate is **80 INR/h**. Annual production is **20,000 units**. A proposed method uses **4 workers** taking **30 minutes** per unit, with a **capital investment of 3,00,000 INR** and an **extra annual overhead of 20,000 INR**. Determine:
> (a) Net Annual Saving
> (b) Payback Period
> (c) Rate of Return

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute Annual Labour Cost of Present Method**

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{present}} = N_p \times T_p \times R \times S
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{present}} = 6 \times \frac{45}{60} \times 80 \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{present}} = 6 \times 0.75 \times 80 \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{present}} = 6 \times 60 \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{present}} = 7{,}200{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Annual Labour Cost of Proposed Method**

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{proposed}} = N_q \times T_q \times R \times S
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{proposed}} = 4 \times \frac{30}{60} \times 80 \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{proposed}} = 4 \times 0.5 \times 80 \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{proposed}} = 4 \times 40 \times 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_{\text{proposed}} = 3{,}200{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Annual Labour Saving (ALS)**

$$
\text{ALS} = \text{LC}_{\text{present}} - \text{LC}_{\text{proposed}}
$$

$$
\text{ALS} = 7{,}200{,}000 - 3{,}200{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ALS} = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Net Annual Saving (NAS)**

$$
\text{NAS} = \text{ALS} - \text{Extra Overhead}
$$

$$
\text{NAS} = 4{,}000{,}000 - 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{NAS} = 3{,}980{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**(a) Answer: Net Annual Saving = 3,98,000 INR/year** *(correction: 39,80,000 INR/year = 39.8 lakh INR/year)*

**Step 5: Compute Payback Period (PP)**

$$
\text{PP} = \frac{\text{CI}}{\text{NAS}}
$$

$$
\text{PP} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{39{,}80{,}000} \text{ years}
$$

$$
\text{PP} \approx 0.0754 \text{ years}
$$

Converting to days (assuming 300 working days/year):

$$
\text{PP} = 0.0754 \times 300 \approx 22.6 \text{ days}
$$

**(b) Answer: Payback Period ≈ 22.6 working days**

**Step 6: Compute Rate of Return (ROI)**

$$
\text{ROI} = \left( \frac{39{,}80{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ROI} = 13.267 \times 100 \approx 1326.7 \%
$$

**(c) Answer: ROI ≈ 1326.7% per annum** — an exceptionally strong investment case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always state the **decision** at the end of an economic evaluation. In this case, conclude with: *"Since the payback period is significantly below the typical 1–2 year target and ROI is far above the industry hurdle rate of 15%, the proposed method is **strongly recommended for implementation**."*

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Evaluation Method

| Evaluation Type | When to Use | Data Required | Output |
|----------------|-------------|---------------|--------|
| **Economic (Cost-only)** | Pure cost-driven decisions | Wages, times, investment | INR saved |
| **Payback Period** | Capital rationing scenarios | CI, NAS | Years/months |
| **ROI** | Comparing competing investments | CI, NAS | % per year |
| **Ergonomic Assessment** | Operator welfare is priority | Posture, energy data | Score / rank |
| **Composite (Weighted)** | Conflicting criteria | All of the above | Single score |
| **Break-Even Analysis** | Volume uncertainty exists | Fixed cost, saving/unit | Output units |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Method Study Evaluation & Implementation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Improved Method Developed] --> B[Step 1: Economic Evaluation]
    B --> C{Profitable?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject Proposal or Redesign]
    C -- Yes --> E[Step 2: Ergonomic Evaluation]
    E --> F{Operator Friendly?}
    F -- No --> G[Modify and Re-evaluate]
    G --> B
    F -- Yes --> H[Step 3: Operational Feasibility Check]
    H --> I{Feasible with Current Resources?}
    I -- No --> J[Plan for Additional Resources]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Step 4: Safety and Compliance Check]
    K --> L{Safe and Compliant?}
    L -- No --> M[Add Safeguards and Re-test]
    M --> K
    L -- Yes --> N[Step 5: Composite Weighted Score]
    N --> O{Best Method Selected?}
    O -- No --> P[Compare Alternatives and Re-rank]
    P --> N
    O -- Yes --> Q[Step 6: Obtain Management Approval]
    Q --> R[Step 7: Sell Idea to Workers and Supervisors]
    R --> S[Step 8: Plan Changeover Schedule]
    S --> T[Step 9: Install New Method]
    T --> U[Step 10: Train Operators]
    U --> V[Step 11: Pilot Run and Debug]
    V --> W[Step 12: Standardize and Document SOPs]
    W --> X[Step 13: Periodic Audit and Continuous Improvement]
    X --> Y[Method Established: Cycle Restarts for Further Improvement]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Evaluation Criteria Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Data Layer]
        I1[Time Studies]
        I2[Wage Records]
        I3[Investment Quotations]
        I4[Layout Drawings]
        I5[Operator Feedback]
    end

    subgraph EVAL[Evaluation Engine]
        E1[Economic Analysis]
        E2[Ergonomic Scoring]
        E3[Operational Feasibility]
        E4[Safety Audit]
    end

    subgraph DECISION[Decision Layer]
        D1[Weighted Composite Score]
        D2[Management Review Board]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Selected Method]
        O2[Rejected Alternatives]
        O3[Implementation Roadmap]
    end

    I1 --> E1
    I2 --> E1
    I3 --> E1
    I1 --> E2
    I5 --> E2
    I4 --> E3
    I4 --> E4

    E1 --> D1
    E2 --> D1
    E3 --> D1
    E4 --> D1

    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> O1
    D2 --> O2
    D2 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Implementation Communication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Management
    participant IE as Industrial Engineer
    participant S as Supervisor
    participant W as Worker
    participant Q as Quality Team

    M->>IE: Approve new method and budget
    IE->>M: Submit final proposal with ROI data
    M->>S: Communicate change plan and timeline
    S->>W: Conduct worker briefing and Q&A session
    W->>S: Raise concerns and suggestions
    S->>IE: Compile feedback for refinement
    IE->>Q: Update SOP and quality checkpoints
    Q->>W: Conduct hands-on training
    W->>Q: Demonstrate competency
    Q->>M: Issue pilot-run clearance
    M->>IE: Authorize full rollout
    IE->>S: Monitor and document results
    S->>M: Submit post-implementation report
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*List any three criteria used for the evaluation of an improved method in method study.*

**Model Answer (3 key points):**

1. **Economic Evaluation** — Comparison of total cost of the present method versus the proposed method, including calculation of net annual savings, payback period, and rate of return.
2. **Ergonomic Evaluation** — Assessment of the proposed method with respect to operator comfort, postural stress, fatigue, and overall human factors suitability.
3. **Operational Feasibility** — Evaluation of whether the proposed method can be implemented with the available equipment, layout, workforce skills, and production schedule.

*(For full 3 marks, students may add Safety Evaluation as a fourth criterion.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series A]**
*State the formula for Net Annual Saving in method study and explain each term.*

**Model Answer:**

$$
\text{NAS} = \text{Annual Labour Saving} - \text{Extra Overhead Cost}
$$

**Explanation of terms:**

- **NAS (Net Annual Saving):** The actual financial benefit retained per year after offsetting the extra operating costs of the new method.
- **Annual Labour Saving:** The difference in total annual labour cost between the present and proposed methods, computed as $(N_p T_p - N_q T_q) \times R \times S$.
- **Extra Overhead Cost:** Any additional indirect cost (electricity, maintenance, depreciation) introduced by the new method, which must be subtracted from gross savings to obtain the true net benefit.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, PCIET403 Model Paper 1]**

*(a)* Define method study. With a neat flowchart, explain the **basic procedure of method study** as applicable to industrial engineering practice. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Discuss in detail the **various steps involved in the implementation of an improved method**. Mention the human-relations aspects that must be carefully handled during implementation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
Method study is the systematic recording, analysis, and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work, with the objective of developing easier and more effective methods, thereby reducing the cost and improving productivity.

**Flowchart (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Select Work to be Studied] --> A2[Step 2: Record Present Method]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Examine Present Method Critically]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Develop Improved Method]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Evaluate Improved Method]
    A5 --> A6{Acceptable?}
    A6 -- No --> A4
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Step 6: Install Improved Method]
    A7 --> A8[Step 7: Maintain New Method]
```

**Brief description of each step (2 marks):**

1. **Select** the work to be studied based on bottlenecks, cost, or strategic importance.
2. **Record** the present method using process charts, flow diagrams, and charts of man–machine type.
3. **Examine** using the **Questioning Technique** (Purpose? Where? When? Who? How?).
4. **Develop** alternatives through brainstorming, elimination, and combining.
5. **Evaluate** using economic, ergonomic, and feasibility criteria.
6. **Install** the chosen method with proper planning and worker cooperation.
7. **Maintain** through periodic audits to ensure sustained benefits.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Steps in Implementation (4 marks):**
