1. **Obtain approval** from top management after presenting the proposal with full economic justification (savings, payback, ROI).
2. **Sell the idea** to supervisors, workers, and union representatives through meetings and demonstrations.
3. **Plan the changeover** — schedule, tools, materials, and contingency plans.
4. **Install the method** — set up equipment, rearrange layout, prepare jigs/fixtures.
5. **Train operators** — hands-on training, skill demonstration, allow a learning curve period.
6. **Run a pilot study** — trial production run, monitor output and quality.
7. **Standardize** — issue written SOPs, fix time standards, update work instructions.
8. **Follow up** — periodic audits, address teething problems, and ensure benefits are realized.

**Human-Relations Aspects (3 marks):**

- **Worker resistance** must be addressed by explaining the benefits (less fatigue, easier work) without threatening jobs.
- **Union involvement** is critical — the change must be discussed with union leaders to avoid conflict.
- **Supervisor training** ensures they can guide workers through the transition.
- **Communication** must be two-way — listen to operator feedback and incorporate valid suggestions.
- **Recognition** of workers who contribute ideas boosts morale and acceptance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, PCIET403 Model Paper 1, Question B Choice]**

*(a)* Explain the **various criteria used for evaluating alternative methods** in method study. Briefly describe the **economic evaluation** with suitable formulas. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A manufacturing process currently requires **8 workers** to perform an operation taking **40 minutes per unit**. The wage rate is **75 INR/hour**, and the annual production is **15,000 units**. A proposed method uses **5 workers** taking **25 minutes per unit**, with a **capital investment of 2,50,000 INR** and an **extra annual overhead of 15,000 INR**.

Calculate:
   - (i) Net Annual Saving
   - (ii) Payback Period (in months)
   - (iii) Rate of Return

Recommend whether the proposed method should be implemented. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Evaluation Criteria (4 marks):**

1. **Economic Criteria:** Comparison of labour, material, and overhead costs; payback period; ROI.
2. **Ergonomic / Human Factors:** Operator comfort, reduced fatigue, lower energy expenditure, improved morale.
3. **Operational / Technical Feasibility:** Compatibility with existing layout, equipment availability, production flow, and quality impact.
4. **Safety Criteria:** Elimination or reduction of hazards; compliance with statutory regulations; safer working environment.
5. **Composite / Weighted Evaluation:** Used when criteria conflict — assign weights to each criterion and compute a composite score.

**Economic Evaluation with Formulas (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Annual Labour Saving (ALS)} = (N_p T_p - N_q T_q) \times R \times S
$$

$$
\text{Net Annual Saving (NAS)} = \text{ALS} - \text{Extra Overhead}
$$

$$
\text{Payback Period (PP)} = \frac{\text{Capital Investment}}{\text{NAS}}
$$

$$
\text{Return on Investment (ROI)} = \left( \frac{\text{NAS}}{\text{CI}} \right) \times 100 \text{ \%}
$$

The method with the **lowest payback period** and **highest ROI** (subject to ergonomic and safety compliance) is recommended for implementation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Present method: $N_p = 8$, $T_p = 40 \text{ min}$, $R = 75 \text{ INR/h}$, $S = 15{,}000 \text{ units/year}$
- Proposed method: $N_q = 5$, $T_q = 25 \text{ min}$, $\text{CI} = 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}$, $\text{Extra OH} = 15{,}000 \text{ INR/year}$

**Step 1: Present Method Annual Labour Cost (1 mark):**

$$
\text{LC}_p = 8 \times \frac{40}{60} \times 75 \times 15{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_p = 8 \times 0.6667 \times 75 \times 15{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_p = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**Step 2: Proposed Method Annual Labour Cost (1 mark):**

$$
\text{LC}_q = 5 \times \frac{25}{60} \times 75 \times 15{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_q = 5 \times 0.4167 \times 75 \times 15{,}000
$$

$$
\text{LC}_q = 2{,}34{,}375 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**Step 3: Annual Labour Saving (0.5 mark):**

$$
\text{ALS} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}34{,}375 = 3{,}65{,}625 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**Step 4: Net Annual Saving (0.5 mark):**

$$
\text{NAS} = 3{,}65{,}625 - 15{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}625 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**(i) Net Annual Saving = 3,50,625 INR/year**

**Step 5: Payback Period (1 mark):**

$$
\text{PP (years)} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{3{,}50{,}625} = 0.713 \text{ years}
$$

Converting to months:

$$
\text{PP} = 0.713 \times 12 \approx 8.55 \text{ months}
$$

**(ii) Payback Period ≈ 8.55 months**

**Step 6: Rate of Return (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ROI} = \left( \frac{3{,}50{,}625}{2{,}50{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 140.25 \text{ \%}
$$

**(iii) ROI ≈ 140.25% per annum**

**Recommendation (1 mark):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommended: YES, the proposed method should be implemented.** The payback period of approximately 8.5 months is well below the typical 1–2 year industry target, and the ROI of 140% far exceeds the standard hurdle rate. Subject to ergonomic confirmation and management approval, the proposal is financially and operationally strong.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert minutes to hours** — Many students write $T = 40$ instead of $T = 40/60 = 0.667$ hours. This is the **#1 calculation error** in KTU papers. Always check units.
> 2. **Omitting extra overhead** — Students often compute Annual Labour Saving and directly call it Net Annual Saving. **NAS = ALS − Extra Overhead.** Losing this step costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Not stating the decision/recommendation** — Always end with: *"The method is recommended / not recommended because..."* The decision statement carries 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring the human factor** — In implementation questions, students focus only on the technical steps. KTU examiners award at least 2–3 marks for the human-relations / change-management angle.
> 5. **No mention of pilot run or standardization** — These are KTU-favourite points worth 1 mark each.
> 6. **Mixing up Payback and ROI interpretations** — Payback is in *time* (years/months); ROI is a *percentage* (% per year).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Method Study Evaluation** is the appraisal of alternative methods using **economic, ergonomic, operational, and safety** criteria.
- **Economic Evaluation** relies on the master formula: $\text{NAS} = \text{ALS} - \text{Extra Overhead}$.
- **Payback Period** = Capital Investment / Net Annual Saving — lower is better.
- **ROI** = (NAS / Investment) × 100% — higher is better; must beat the company's hurdle rate.
- **Implementation** is a **socio-technical** process: equal weight on technical change and worker cooperation.
- **The 8 Implementation Steps** are: Approve → Sell → Plan → Install → Train → Pilot → Standardize → Maintain.
- **Worker resistance is the leading cause of method-study failure** — human-relations management is non-negotiable.
- **Composite (weighted) evaluation** is used when criteria give conflicting rankings.
- **Always state the final recommendation** in any KTU numerical problem.
- **Unit conversion (minutes → hours)** is the most common pitfall — verify before every calculation.
- **The Questioning Technique** (Purpose, Place, Sequence, Person, Means) is the intellectual engine of method study; remember it for theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and Objectives of Work Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definition and Objectives of Work Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Measurement (WM)** is the systematic application of techniques designed to determine the amount of time required for a **qualified worker**, working at a **defined level of performance**, to carry out a specified task under specified conditions. It is also called as **Time Study** in its broader application.

As per the **British Standards Institution (BS 3138:1979)** — the universally accepted industrial engineering definition adopted by KTU syllabus:

> *"Work Measurement is the application of techniques designed to establish the time for a qualified worker to carry out a specified task at a defined level of performance."*

The key operative words in this definition are:
1. **Qualified Worker** — a worker who possesses the necessary skill, training, and physical/mental capability.
2. **Defined Level of Performance** — a benchmark rate of working (typically **Standard Rating = 100%** or the **Normal Pace**).
3. **Specified Task** — a clearly delineated element of work.
4. **Specified Conditions** — standard environmental, ergonomic, and procedural conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tea Stall Timing Method**
>
> Imagine you run a small tea stall. You want to know exactly how long it takes your best tea-maker to brew one cup of masala chai from start to finish so that you can schedule workers, quote prices, and plan delivery times.
>
> Instead of guessing, you **time each sub-step**: boiling water (1 min), adding tea leaves (10 sec), adding milk (15 sec), adding masala (10 sec), straining into cup (20 sec), serving (30 sec). You sum these, then **add allowances** for the worker's fatigue and small personal breaks (say 12% extra).
>
> The result — **Standard Time = 2 min 25 sec per cup** — is the *output of Work Measurement*. It removes guesswork from planning and forms the scientific backbone of wage incentives, production scheduling, and cost estimation.

### 1.3 Work Measurement in the Context of Work Study

Work Measurement is the **second arm** of the broader **Work Study** methodology (the first being **Method Study**). Together they form the core of Industrial Engineering:

- **Method Study** → "Doing the work in the **best way**" (improves the *method*).
- **Work Measurement** → "Determining the **correct time** to do it" (improves the *productivity* metric).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Observed Time (OT)** | $OT$ | Time actually recorded by the stopwatch during the study |
| **Basic Time (BT)** | $BT$ | Time adjusted for worker's performance rating |
| **Normal Time (NT)** | $NT$ | Synonymous with Basic Time (used in KTU textbooks) |
| **Standard Time (ST)** | $ST$ | Normal Time + Relaxation + Contingency Allowances |
| **Performance Rating (R)** | $R$ | Worker's pace expressed as a percentage of standard pace |
| **Allowance Factor (A)** | $A$ | Extra time expressed as a percentage for personal, fatigue, and contingency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Fact for KTU Board Exams:** In most KTU-recommended textbooks (Kanawaty, ILO; Mahajan), the terms *Basic Time* and *Normal Time* are used **interchangeably** to mean the same quantity — i.e., the time taken by a worker at the **standard rating (100)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Step Logical Flow of Work Measurement

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats Work Measurement as a **three-stage logical pipeline**. Each stage has a clearly defined purpose:

**Stage 1 — Observation and Recording**
- The worker's activity is observed using a stopwatch, video, work-sampling, or PMTS (Predetermined Motion Time System).
- **Goal:** Capture raw observed time for each work element.

**Stage 2 — Performance Rating**
- The observer (a qualified Work Study Engineer) compares the worker's pace against a **standard rating scale** (e.g., the **100 = Normal Pace** or **BS 100 = Walking 3 mph / 4.8 km/h on level ground**).
- **Goal:** Normalize the observed time for fairness.
- **Core 'Why':** No two workers work at exactly the same speed — rating removes this **worker variability**.

**Stage 3 — Allowance Addition**
- Extra time is added to cover **personal needs, fatigue, and contingencies** (PFD allowances).
- **Goal:** Make the standard time **realistic, humane, and achievable** over an 8-hour shift.

### 2.2 The Work Measurement Hierarchy

```
Raw Observed Time (OT)
        │
        ▼  × (Rating Factor R)
Basic / Normal Time (BT)
        │
        ▼  × (1 + Allowance Factor A)
Standard Time (ST)
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **verbatim KTU-board-tested** material. Memorize every formula and its units.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Purpose / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Time (BT)** | $BT = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100}$ | minutes or seconds | After rating the worker's pace |
| 2 | **Standard Time (ST)** | $ST = BT + Allowances = BT \times (1 + A)$ | minutes or seconds | Final time used for planning, costing, scheduling |
| 3 | **Allowance Factor (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Allowance Time}}{BT}$ | dimensionless (decimal or %) | Expressed as a fraction of BT |
| 4 | **Standard Time (Alternate form)** | $ST = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times (1 + A)$ | minutes or seconds | Direct one-step calculation in numerical problems |
| 5 | **Performance Rating Index** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Observed Speed}}{\text{Normal Speed}} \times 100$ | percentage | When speed method of rating is used |
| 6 | **Number of Cycles (Work Sampling)** | $N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$ | dimensionless count | To determine number of observations required |
| 7 | **Standard Hours Calculation** | $SH = \text{Units Produced} \times ST$ | hours | To compute earned hours for incentive wages |
| 8 | **Machine Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Standard Hours Produced}}{\text{Clock Hours Available}} \times 100$ | percentage | To assess efficiency of work centers |
| 9 | **Normal Time in PMTS (MTM)** | $NT = \sum TMU_i \times 0.00001 \times 60$ | seconds | When using Methods Time Measurement tables |

> **Units of Time in Work Study:**
> - **1 TMU (Time Measurement Unit)** = 0.00001 hour = 0.036 seconds = 1/100,000 hour
> - **1 Decimal Hour** = 100 decimal minutes = 10,000 decimal seconds

### 2.4 The Eight Principal Objectives of Work Measurement

This is the **highest-weightage topic in Module 3** for KTU 2024 examinations. The standard 8 objectives, as prescribed in the KTU syllabus and derived from ILO/Kanawaty's framework, are:

1. **To compare alternative methods** — Select the most efficient method among several proposed methods.
2. **To establish standard times** — Provide benchmarks for production planning and scheduling.
3. **To determine plant and equipment utilization** — Calculate machine and labor efficiency indices.
4. **To balance the work of operators and machines** — Eliminate bottlenecks by line balancing.
5. **To determine labour costs** — Establish accurate costing for tenders, budgets, and quotations.
6. **To provide a basis for incentive wage schemes** — Fair payment by output (Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, Emerson plans).
7. **To assist in production planning and control** — Fix manning levels, delivery dates, and dispatch schedules.
8. **To set a fair and consistent wage structure** — Reduce arbitrariness in piece-rate fixation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Tip — "Compare, Standard, Utilize, Balance, Cost, Incentivize, Plan, Pay":**
> Memorize using the mnemonic **"C-S-U-B-C-I-P-P"** (Compare-Standard-Utilize-Balance-Cost-Incentivize-Plan-Pay).

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Application Domain | Role of Work Measurement |
|---|---|
| **Garment Manufacturing (Kerala's Hub)** | Standard minutes per operation for line balancing and SMV (Standard Minute Value) fixation |
| **Automotive Assembly (Maruti, Tata)** | Cycle time balancing across stations using MTM-1 or MOST |
| **CNC Machining Industry** | Estimating setup time + machining time for quotations |
| **Software Industry (ITES in Technopark/Infopark)** | Effort estimation per story point or per task in agile sprints |
| **Healthcare (NABH Accreditation)** | Nurse-to-patient ratio and turnaround time standards |
| **Public Sector (BHEL, ONGC)** | Setting piece-rate wages and incentive earnings |

### 2.6 Techniques of Work Measurement (KTU Classification)

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes Work Measurement techniques into **four families**:

1. **Direct / Stopwatch Time Study** (continuous and snap-back methods)
2. **Work Sampling (Activity Sampling)** — statistical method based on laws of probability
3. **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** — MTM, MOST, Basic Motion Timestudy
4. **Standard Data Systems / Analytical Estimating** — building standard times from pre-established data

> [!NOTE]
> Each of these techniques is elaborated in **subsequent modules**. This module focuses on the **definition, objectives, and need** for Work Measurement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Standard Time Formula

> [!NOTE]
> Although Work Measurement is not a heavily algebraic topic, the following derivation is **frequently asked** in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Be thorough.

**Step 1 — Define Observed Time (OT)**

Let the stopwatch reading for an element of work be $t_i$ seconds. The arithmetic mean observed time across $n$ cycles is:

$$
\bar{t} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i
$$

The bar over $t$ denotes the **average observed time** per cycle.

**Step 2 — Apply the Performance Rating**

The worker's pace may be faster or slower than the standard. To normalize, the observer assigns a **Performance Rating $R$** on a 100-point scale (where 100 represents standard pace). The Basic Time becomes:

$$
BT = \bar{t} \times \dfrac{R}{100}
$$

**Why divide by 100?**
Because the rating $R$ is expressed as a **percentage**. To convert it into a dimensionless multiplying factor, we divide by 100.

**Step 3 — Add Allowances**

The worker needs time for personal needs (P), fatigue (F), and contingencies (C). The total allowance time $A_t$ is:

$$
A_t = \dfrac{A}{100} \times BT
$$

where $A$ is the **allowance factor in percentage** (typically 12% to 25% depending on working conditions).

**Step 4 — Compute Standard Time**

Adding allowance time to the Basic Time:

$$
ST = BT + A_t
$$

Substituting $A_t$:

$$
ST = BT + \dfrac{A}{100} \times BT = BT \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute BT from Step 2**

$$
ST = \bar{t} \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)
$$

**This is the Master Formula of Work Measurement — frequently tested in KTU numerical problems.**

### 3.2 Worked Example — A Standard KTU Numerical

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Part B Style):**
> A work study engineer conducted a stopwatch time study on a drilling operation. The **average observed time** per cycle was **1.20 minutes**. The **performance rating** assigned was **110%**. The **allowance factor** for the operation is **15%**. Calculate:
> (a) Basic Time
> (b) Standard Time

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**

$$
\bar{t} = 1.20 \text{ minutes}, \quad R = 110\%, \quad A = 15\%
$$

**(a) Basic Time Calculation:**

$$
BT = \bar{t} \times \dfrac{R}{100}
$$

$$
BT = 1.20 \times \dfrac{110}{100}
$$

$$
BT = 1.20 \times 1.10
$$

$$
\boxed{BT = 1.32 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**(b) Standard Time Calculation:**

$$
ST = BT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)
$$

$$
ST = 1.32 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{15}{100}\right)
$$

$$
ST = 1.32 \times 1.15
$$

$$
\boxed{ST = 1.518 \text{ minutes per cycle}}
$$

**Verification using the Master Formula directly:**

$$
ST = \bar{t} \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)
$$

$$
ST = 1.20 \times 1.10 \times 1.15 = 1.518 \text{ min}
$$

Both methods agree → **result is consistent and correct**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Work Measurement Technique

| Technique | Best Application | Accuracy | Cost | Time Required | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stopwatch Time Study** | Repetitive, short-cycle manual/semi-manual jobs (assembly, machine operation) | High (±5%) | Low | Low (few hours) | Very High |
| **Work Sampling** | Long-cycle, irregular jobs (maintenance, office, hospital) | Moderate (±5–10%) | Low | High (several days) | High |
| **PMTS (MTM, MOST)** | New product design, ergonomic studies, short elements | Very High (±2%) | High (requires trained analyst) | Medium | Medium |
| **Standard Data / Analytical Estimating** | When standard data already exists for components | High | Very Low | Very Low | High |

### 3.4 Detailed Allowance Breakdown (KTU-Recommended)

| Allowance Component | Typical % of BT | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal Allowance** | 5–7% | Tea breaks, restroom, washing, conversation |
| **Basic Fatigue Allowance** | 4% | Static muscular effort, monotony |
| **Variable Fatigue Allowance** | 0–22% | Added based on working conditions (heat, noise, light, posture) |
| **Contingency Allowance** | 2–5% | Small irregularities, minor delays |
| **Total Typical Allowance** | **12–25%** | Combined PFD for an average Indian shop-floor job |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When a KTU problem states "allowance factor = 20%", it means the **combined PFD allowance**, not separate percentages. Apply it as a single multiplication factor $(1 + 0.20)$ on Basic Time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Work Measurement Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end flow** of a Work Measurement study, from initial observation to final standard time output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: Identify Task and Define Scope"]
    nodeB["Select Qualified Worker and Conditions"]
    nodeC["Record Observed Time OT via Stopwatch or PMTS"]
    nodeD{"Is Observation Data Sufficient?"}
    nodeE["Apply Performance Rating R and Compute BT"]
    nodeF["Determine Allowance Factor A Personal Fatigue Contingency"]
    nodeG["Compute Standard Time ST Equal to BT times 1 plus A"]
    nodeH["Document Standard Time and Update Database"]
    nodeI["Use ST for Planning Costing Incentives Balancing"]
    nodeJ["End: Output Reports and Continuous Monitoring"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE
    nodeD -- No --> nodeC
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Mind-Map — The Eight Objectives of Work Measurement

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Work Measurement Objectives))
    node1["Compare Alternative Methods"]
    node2["Establish Standard Times"]
    node3["Determine Plant Utilization"]
    node4["Balance Work of Operators and Machines"]
    node5["Determine Labour Costs"]
    node6["Provide Basis for Incentive Wage Schemes"]
    node7["Assist in Production Planning and Control"]
    node8["Set Fair and Consistent Wage Structure"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Relationship between Work Study Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeWS["WORK STUDY"]
    nodeMS["METHOD STUDY"]
    nodeWM["WORK MEASUREMENT"]

    nodeTS["Time Study Stopwatch"]
    nodeWS1["Work Sampling"]
    nodePMTS["PMTS MTM MOST"]
    nodeSD["Standard Data"]

    nodeWS --> nodeMS
    nodeWS --> nodeWM
    nodeWM --> nodeTS
    nodeWM --> nodeWS1
    nodeWM --> nodePMTS
    nodeWM --> nodeSD
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Conversion of Observed Time to Standard Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeOT["Observed Time OT seconds"]
    nodeR["Performance Rating R percent"]
    nodeBT["Basic Time BT"]
    nodeA["Allowance Factor A percent"]
    nodeST["Standard Time ST seconds"]

    nodeOT --> nodeBT
    nodeR --> nodeBT
    nodeBT --> nodeST
    nodeA --> nodeST

    nodeBT@{ shape: rect, label: "BT = OT times R divided by 100" }
    nodeST@{ shape: rect, label: "ST = BT times 1 plus A divided by 100" }
```

### 4.5 Hierarchy of Work Measurement Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["Work Measurement Outputs"]
    nodeL1["Day to Day Operations"]
    nodeL2["Strategic Decisions"]

    nodeRoot --> nodeL1
    nodeRoot --> nodeL2

    nodeL1 --> nodeL1a["Manpower Planning"]
    nodeL1 --> nodeL1b["Production Scheduling"]
    nodeL1 --> nodeL1c["Cost Estimation"]

    nodeL2 --> nodeL2a["Plant Capacity Decisions"]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL2b["Incentive Wage Policy"]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL2c["Make or Buy Decisions"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> **Define Work Measurement. List any four of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

Work Measurement is the systematic application of techniques designed to determine the time required for a qualified worker, working at a defined level of performance, to complete a specified task under specified conditions.

**Four Objectives:**
1. To compare alternative methods and select the most efficient one.
2. To establish standard times for production planning.
3. To determine plant and equipment utilization.
4. To provide a sound basis for incentive wage schemes.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> **Differentiate between Method Study and Work Measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Method Study | Work Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Improving the *method* of doing work | Determining the *time* required to do work |
| **Question Asked** | "What is the best way to do this job?" | "How long should this job take?" |
| **Stage in Work Study** | First (precedes Work Measurement) | Second (follows Method Study) |
| **Output** | Improved method, flow diagram, layout | Standard time, performance rating |
| **Quantification** | Mostly qualitative | Fully quantitative |

---

### 5.2 PART B Question — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> *Note: KTU ESE follows Module Internal Choice — answer ANY ONE full question from the given pair.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various techniques of Work Measurement with their relative merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A worker operates a CNC lathe. A time study reveals an **average observed time of 2.40 minutes per piece**. The engineer's performance rating is **115%**. The allowances applicable are:
- Personal allowance = 6%
- Fatigue allowance = 9%
- Contingency allowance = 3%

Calculate:
(i) Basic Time
(ii) Total Allowance Time
(iii) Standard Time per piece **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A (a)**

The four principal techniques of Work Measurement are:

**1. Stopwatch Time Study**
- Most widely used. Uses a stopwatch to record times for each work element.
- **Merits:** Low cost, quick, high accuracy for repetitive jobs.
- **Demerits:** Observational bias in rating, not suitable for very long or irregular jobs.

**2. Work Sampling (Activity Sampling)**
- Statistical method using random observations over a long period.
- **Merits:** Economical for long-cycle jobs, less observer fatigue.
- **Demerits:** Lower accuracy, requires careful sample size determination.

**3. Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)**
- Uses published time standards for basic human motions (e.g., MTM, MOST).
- **Merits:** No rating required, consistent, useful in design stage.
- **Demerits:** Expensive, requires highly trained analysts.

**4. Standard Data Systems**
- Time standards derived from previously established data for elements.
- **Merits:** Very fast, low cost when data exists.
- **Demerits:** Only as accurate as the original data; needs careful maintenance.

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A (b)**

**Given Data:**

$$
\bar{t} = 2.40 \text{ min}, \quad R = 115\%, \quad A = 6\% + 9\% + 3\% = 18\%
$$

**(i) Basic Time Calculation:**

$$
BT = \bar{t} \times \dfrac{R}{100}
$$

$$
BT = 2.40 \times \dfrac{115}{100}
$$

$$
BT = 2.40 \times 1.15
$$

$$
\boxed{BT = 2.76 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**(ii) Total Allowance Time Calculation:**

$$
A_t = \dfrac{A}{100} \times BT = 0.18 \times 2.76
$$

$$
\boxed{A_t = 0.4968 \text{ minutes} \approx 0.50 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**(iii) Standard Time Calculation:**

$$
ST = BT + A_t = 2.76 + 0.4968
$$

$$
\boxed{ST = 3.2568 \text{ minutes per piece} \approx 3.26 \text{ min}}
$$

**Cross-check using master formula:**

$$
ST = 2.40 \times 1.15 \times 1.18 = 3.2568 \text{ minutes} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define Work Measurement. State and explain any **six objectives** of Work Measurement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An assembly line worker takes an **average observed time of 0.85 minutes** to assemble a component. The performance rating is **90%** (worker is slower than standard). The **total allowance** is **12%**. Compute the **Standard Time** and hence determine the **expected output in an 8-hour shift**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B (a)**

**Definition:** (Refer to Section 1.1 of these notes — write in full.)

**Six Objectives (with one-line explanation each):**

1. **Compare Alternative Methods** — Work Measurement data enables management to numerically compare productivity of different methods.

2. **Establish Standard Times** — Provides benchmark times used universally in planning, scheduling, and delivery promises.

3. **Determine Plant Utilization** — Comparing standard hours produced against clock hours gives the efficiency index of equipment.

4. **Balance Work of Operators and Machines** — Standard times for each operation enable optimum line balancing.

5. **Determine Labour Costs** — Accurate costing of products for tenders and budgets requires standard times per operation.

6. **Provide Basis for Incentive Wage Schemes** — Fair piece-rate wages are calculated using scientifically determined standard times.

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B (b)**

**Given Data:**

$$
\bar{t} = 0.85 \text{ min}, \quad R = 90\%, \quad A = 12\%
$$

**Standard Time Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Basic Time:**

$$
BT = 0.85 \times \dfrac{90}{100} = 0.85 \times 0.90
$$

$$
BT = 0.765 \text{ minutes per piece}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Time:**

$$
ST = BT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{12}{100}\right)
$$

$$
ST = 0.765 \times 1.12
$$

$$
\boxed{ST = 0.8568 \text{ minutes per piece} \approx 0.857 \text{ min/piece}}
$$

**Step 3 — Output per 8-hour Shift:**

**Total shift duration in minutes:**

$$
T = 8 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hour} = 480 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Expected output:**

$$
N = \dfrac{T}{ST} = \dfrac{480}{0.8568}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 560 \text{ pieces per 8-hour shift}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide R by 100.** Rating is in percentage, so it MUST be divided by 100 before multiplying. Writing $BT = OT \times R$ directly is the #1 mistake — results in 10× inflated values.
>
> 2. **Confusing Basic Time and Standard Time.** Basic Time excludes allowances. Always add allowances explicitly.
>
> 3. **Adding individual allowances as separate multipliers.** Use the **combined allowance factor** as a single multiplication: $(1 + A/100)$. Do NOT write $BT \times 1.06 \times 1.09 \times 1.03$ — that compounds the allowance incorrectly.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency.** Observed time in seconds, allowance in % of Basic Time — keep units consistent throughout.
>
> 5. **Omitting the 'qualified worker' clause in definitions.** Examiners specifically look for the words *qualified*, *defined level of performance*, *specified conditions*.
>
> 6. **Confusing Method Study and Work Measurement.** Always state the key distinction in tabular form for clarity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Definition and Objectives of Work Measurement**

- ✅ **Definition (BS 3138 / ILO):** Application of techniques to determine the time for a qualified worker at a defined level of performance.
- ✅ **Three pillars of the definition:** Qualified worker + Defined performance level + Specified task & conditions.
- ✅ **Work Study = Method Study + Work Measurement.** Method Study comes first (improve the way); Work Measurement follows (measure the time).
- ✅ **Master Formula (must memorize):**
$$ST = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)$$
- ✅ **8 Objectives:** Compare methods, Establish standards, Determine utilization, Balance operators/machines, Determine labour costs, Basis for incentives, Assist production planning, Set fair wages.
- ✅ **Memory Mnemonic:** **"C-S-U-B-C-I-P-P"** (Compare, Standard, Utilize, Balance, Cost, Incentivize, Plan, Pay).
- ✅ **4 Techniques:** Stopwatch Time Study, Work Sampling, PMTS (MTM/MOST), Standard Data Systems.
- ✅ **Performance Rating = 100** is the standard pace (normal walk = 3 mph / 4.8 km/h on level ground).
- ✅ **Allowance Factor (A)** is typically **12%–25%**, combining Personal + Fatigue + Contingency (PFD).
- ✅ **TMU (Time Measurement Unit)** = 0.00001 hour = 0.036 seconds.
- ✅ **Work Sampling sample size formula:**
$$N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$$
- ✅ **Always state units clearly** — seconds, minutes, hours, pieces per shift.
- ✅ **Standard Minute Value (SMV)** in garment industry = Sum of standard times of all operations to complete one garment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Procedure of Stop Watch Time Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Procedure of Stop Watch Time Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stop Watch Time Study** is a classical, direct work measurement technique defined under Method Study and Work Measurement, in which a qualified practitioner uses a calibrated stop watch (or equivalent precision timing instrument) to record the elapsed time taken by a competent, trained operator to perform a specific, well-defined element of work at a defined level of performance. The recorded observed times are subsequently adjusted by a **Performance Rating Factor (PRF)**, allowances are added for rest, personal needs and contingencies, and the final output is the **Standard Time (ST)** — the time allowed for a qualified operator working at a normal pace to complete the specified work under specified conditions.

**Key formal entities:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Observed Time (OT)** | The raw time read directly from the stop watch for an element. |
| **Basic Time (BT)** | Time for an element at standard performance (no allowances). |
| **Normal Time (NT)** | Time for an element after applying rating — equivalent to Basic Time for single element. |
| **Standard Time (ST)** | Time allowed per piece including relaxation, personal, and contingency allowances. |
| **Performance Rating Factor (PRF)** | A dimensionless multiplier (typically 60 < PRF < 140) used to convert OT into NT. |
| **Allowance Factor (A)** | A fraction (or %) added to NT to derive ST. |
| **Rated Worker (Standard Performer)** | A hypothetical 100 % worker who possesses the skill, knowledge and attributes of a trained, competent operator. |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Cafeteria Spice Jar Line**
>
> Imagine a small factory in Kerala that fills spice jars with turmeric powder. The owner wants to know exactly how long each jar should take to fill, cap, label and pack — *not* the fastest worker, *not* the slowest, but the *fairly typical* trained worker so that wages and machine scheduling can be planned. He stands with a stop watch, watches an experienced worker, writes down the time for "open jar", "fill", "tap to settle", "cap", "label", "place in box", etc. The worker on day 1 was a little slow (because he had a cold), so the owner mentally adjusts the time upward. The worker on day 2 was unusually quick (because his manager was watching), so the owner mentally adjusts the time downward. He averages this over many cycles, adds a small allowance for tea breaks, eye blinking, stretching and the odd sneeze, and finally publishes a **Standard Time per Jar**. That is exactly what **Stop Watch Time Study** does — systematically, with statistical rigour, instead of by gut feel.

### 1.3 Prerequisites & Boundary Conditions

A legitimate Stop Watch Time Study is only conducted **after**:

1. The **method** has been standardized through Method Study (Motion Economy, Operation Process Chart, Flow Process Chart, Two-Handed Process Chart).
2. The operator has been **fully trained** on the standard method.
3. Working conditions, materials, tools and equipment are **stable and representative** of regular production.
4. The stop watch has been **calibrated** (accuracy $\pm 0.01$ s for fast elements, $\pm 0.1$ s otherwise).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Work Measurement:** *"You can never measure what you have not first defined."* Hence the operation must be split into **elements** before timing begins.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Canonical Steps of Basic Procedure

The classical procedure, as prescribed in ILO / British Standards / Niebel & Freivalds texts and followed in KTU Board examinations, consists of **eight sequential steps**:

**Step 1 — Define the objective of the study.**
Why is the study being conducted? Examples: setting standard times for wage incentive, balancing a production line, costing a job, evaluating a new method.

**Step 2 — Select the operator and workplace.**
The operator must be *qualified, experienced, cooperative, and representative* of the trained workforce. The workplace must be the *actual normal production* environment.

**Step 3 — Analyze the method.**
Confirm that the operator is using the *standard method*. If a deviation is observed, it must be recorded and either corrected (preferred) or the study is halted.

**Step 4 — Record the method in detail.**
Break the operation into **elements** — short, easily identifiable sub-cycles bounded by clear, repeatable *terminal points* (e.g., a sound, a flash, a contact). Each element should be:
- Short enough to be timed accurately (typically $0.5$ min to several minutes).
- Logically distinct (manual vs. machine, productive vs. preparatory).
- Bounded by a sharp sensory end-point (sound, motion completion).

A **Time Study Observation Sheet** is used, with columns: Element No., Element Description, R, T₁, T₂, T₃, … (repetitions), Average OT, Rating, NT.

**Step 5 — Take the observations (stop watch timing).**

Two principal stop watch techniques exist:

| Technique | Mechanism | When Preferred |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Timing** (Fly-back / Cumulative) | Stop watch runs continuously; on completion of each element, the *elapsed* time is read and recorded; watch is **not** reset until end of full cycle. | Most preferred in practice; less cumulative error. |
| **Snap-Back Timing** (Repetitive / Reset) | Stop watch is reset to **zero** at the end of each element; next element is timed afresh. | Fast, repetitive manual cycles; suffers cumulative error. |
| **Split-Timing** | Two stop watches — one for element time, one for cycle control. | Special cases (machine-dominated cycles). |

**Step 6 — Rate the operator's performance.**

The observer mentally compares the worker's pace with the **concept of a Normal Worker (100 %)** and assigns a **Performance Rating Factor (PRF)** to each element (or to the cycle as a whole, depending on the rating system).

Common rating systems:

| System | Logic |
|---|---|
| **Westinghouse System** | Four sub-factors: Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency. |
| **Synthetic / Speed Rating** | A single speed-only rating (e.g., 100 = normal walking pace, 110 = brisk). |
| **Objective Rating (British Standard)** | Rating = Walking Pace on B.M.S. scale. |
| **Pace Rating (Currie)** | Numerical pace $P$, with $P = 100$ for normal. |

For basic KTU problems, the most common formulation is:

$$\text{Performance Rating Factor (PRF)} = \frac{\text{Standard Performance}}{\text{Observed Performance}} \times 100$$

For example, if the operator appears to be working 20 % faster than normal, PRF = 120.

**Step 7 — Compute Normal Time (NT).**

$$\text{Normal Time} = \text{Observed Time} \times \frac{\text{PRF}}{100}$$

If the rating is applied element-wise and then summed, the result is the **Basic Time**. For a single element, *Normal Time = Basic Time*.

**Step 8 — Add allowances → Standard Time (ST).**

$$\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} + \text{Relaxation Allowance} + \text{Personal Allowance} + \text{Contingency Allowance}$$

In percentage form (more compact for KTU problems):

$$\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} \times \left(1 + \frac{A}{100}\right)$$

where $A$ is the **Allowance Factor** expressed as a percentage of NT.

### 2.2 Allowance Classification

| Category | Typical Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal Allowance (PA)** | **5 % – 7 %** of NT | Toilet, drinking water, washing. |
| **Relaxation / Rest Allowance (RA)** | **5 % – 15 %** of NT | Recovery from fatigue (heavier in hot/humid conditions). |
| **Contingency Allowance (CA)** | **2 % – 5 %** of NT | Minor, irregular delays not separately timed. |
| **Total Allowance Factor $A$** | usually **10 % – 25 %** | $A = PA + RA + CA$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often write *Allowance = NT − ST* or *NT = ST + Allowance*. The correct relationship is **ST > NT** and **Allowance = ST − NT** (allowance is *added* to NT to obtain ST).

### 2.3 Determining the Number of Cycles to Observe (Sample Size)

A frequently-asked KTU question concerns how many cycles $N$ must be timed. Two methods:

**Method A — Statistically-driven formula (most accurate):**

$$N =left(\frac{z \cdot s}{R \cdot \bar{X}} \cdot 100\right)^{2}$$

where:
- $z$ = standard normal variate for desired confidence (e.g., $z = 1.96$ for 95 % confidence, $z = 2.58$ for 99 %).
- $s$ = standard deviation of pilot observed times.
- $\bar{X}$ = mean observed time.
- $R$ = desired accuracy as a percentage of mean (e.g., $R = \pm 5$).

**Method B — British Standard / ILO Pre-determined Table (commonly used in KTU Board papers):**

The number of cycles is read from a table indexed by *cycle length* and *repetitive nature* of the task. Typical values:

| Cycle Time | No. of Cycles Recommended |
|---|---|
| $< 0.25$ min (< 15 s) | 200 |
| $0.25 - 0.75$ min | 100 |
| $0.75 - 2.0$ min | 50 |
| $2.0 - 5.0$ min | 25 |
| $5.0 - 10.0$ min | 15 |
| $10.0 - 20.0$ min | 10 |
| $> 20$ min | 5 |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{NT} = \text{OT} \times \dfrac{\text{PRF}}{100}$ | OT = Observed Time, PRF = Performance Rating Factor | Convert raw time into normal pace time. |
| 2 | $\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times \left(1+\dfrac{A}{100}\right)$ | $A$ = Allowance factor in % | Compute the published standard time. |
| 3 | $\text{Basic Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\text{OT}_i \cdot \text{PRF}_i)$ | Element-wise | When different elements are rated differently. |
| 4 | $\text{Standard Output} = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Standard Time per piece}}$ | Pieces/period | Output planning. |
| 5 | $N = \left(\dfrac{z \cdot s}{R \cdot \bar{X}} \cdot 100\right)^{2}$ | Sample size equation | Number of cycles required for accuracy $R$ %. |
| 6 | $\text{Cycle Time} = \dfrac{\text{Total Job Time}}{\text{Number of Pieces}}$ | Average time | Reverse-engineering OT from known production. |
| 7 | $\text{Efficiency} = \dfrac{\text{Standard Time}}{\text{Absorbed Time}} \times 100$ | % | Operator / plant efficiency. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas above are dimensionless-compatible — multiply **OT** (in minutes or seconds) by a dimensionless **PRF/100**, and the **NT** inherits the same unit.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stop Watch Time Study outputs are directly fed into:
- **Wage Incentive Schemes** (Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, Emerson).
- **Line Balancing** in assembly lines (automotive, electronics).
- **Capacity Planning** and **Machine Loading**.
- **Cost Estimation** of a job / tender.
- **Lean / Kaizen** baseline measurements (takt time, cycle time).
- **Ergonomic redesign** — to detect over-fatiguing elements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Stop Watch Time Study (Single Element, Single Operator)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A stop watch time study was conducted on the element *"Pick and place component on jig"*. The following observed times (in seconds) were recorded for 10 consecutive cycles: $7.2,\ 6.8,\ 7.5,\ 7.0,\ 7.3,\ 6.9,\ 7.4,\ 7.1,\ 7.6,\ 7.2$. The observer rated the operator at **110** (i.e., 10 % faster than normal). The total allowance factor for the element is **20 %** of Normal Time. Compute (a) Mean Observed Time, (b) Normal Time per piece, and (c) Standard Time per piece.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step (a): Mean Observed Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i \\
&= \frac{7.2 + 6.8 + 7.5 + 7.0 + 7.3 + 6.9 + 7.4 + 7.1 + 7.6 + 7.2}{10} \\
&= \frac{72.0}{10} \\
&= 7.20 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The arithmetic mean of the 10 cycles represents the central tendency of the observed pace.

**Step (b): Normal Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NT} &= \bar{X} \times \frac{\text{PRF}}{100} \\
&= 7.20 \times \frac{110}{100} \\
&= 7.20 \times 1.10 \\
&= 7.92 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Since the worker was 10 % faster than the rated (normal) pace, we *de-rate* the observed time by dividing mentally through a 110 % multiplier. The result is the time a *normal* (100 %) worker would have taken on the same task — this is the Normal Time.

**Step (c): Standard Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST} &= \text{NT} \times \left(1 + \frac{A}{100}\right) \\
&= 7.92 \times \left(1 + \frac{20}{100}\right) \\
&= 7.92 \times 1.20 \\
&= 9.504 \text{ seconds} \approx 9.50 \text{ s per piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A normal-rated worker is entitled to rest, personal needs and contingency time, totalling 20 % of NT. So the *allowed* time per piece (Standard Time) is 120 % of NT.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Element Operation with Element-Wise Rating

**Problem Statement:**
An operation consists of three elements. Time study observations (in minutes) and the observer's rating for each are tabulated below. Compute Normal Time, Basic Time and Standard Time (total allowance = 15 %).

| Element | Description | Avg. OT (min) | Rating | Allowance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reach and grasp blank | 0.20 | 90 | 15 % |
| 2 | Position on machine | 0.35 | 110 | 15 % |
| 3 | Activate and inspect | 0.45 | 100 | 15 % |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Element-wise Normal Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NT}_1 &= 0.20 \times \frac{90}{100} = 0.180 \text{ min} \\
\text{NT}_2 &= 0.35 \times \frac{110}{100} = 0.385 \text{ min} \\
\text{NT}_3 &= 0.45 \times \frac{100}{100} = 0.450 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Basic Time of the operation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Basic Time} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \text{NT}_i \\
&= 0.180 + 0.385 + 0.450 \\
&= 1.015 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Because different elements are rated differently (the operator was slow on E1, fast on E2 and exactly normal on E3), the Basic Time is the **sum of element-wise Normal Times**, *not* the cycle-mean times multiplied by a single global rating.

**Step 3 — Standard Time (uniform 15 % allowance on Basic Time):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST} &= \text{Basic Time} \times 1.15 \\
&= 1.015 \times 1.15 \\
&= 1.16725 \text{ min per piece} \\
&\approx 1.167 \text{ min} \;(70.05 \text{ s}) \text{ per piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Output (per hour):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pieces per hour} &= \frac{60 \text{ min}}{1.167 \text{ min/piece}} \\
&= 51.41 \text{ pieces/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Determining Number of Observations (Statistical Formula)

**Problem Statement:**
A pilot study of 9 cycles gave the following observed times (s): $5.2,\ 4.8,\ 5.5,\ 4.7,\ 5.0,\ 5.3,\ 4.9,\ 5.1,\ 5.0$. The observer requires accuracy within $\pm 5\%$ of the mean at 95 % confidence ($z = 1.96$). Determine the number of observations required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X} &= \frac{5.2+4.8+5.5+4.7+5.0+5.3+4.9+5.1+5.0}{9} \\
&= \frac{45.5}{9} \\
&= 5.0556 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the standard deviation $s$:**

First compute $\sum X_i^2$ and deviations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum X_i^2 &= 5.2^2 + 4.8^2 + 5.5^2 + 4.7^2 + 5.0^2 + 5.3^2 + 4.9^2 + 5.1^2 + 5.0^2 \\
&= 27.04 + 23.04 + 30.25 + 22.09 + 25.00 + 28.09 + 24.01 + 26.01 + 25.00 \\
&= 230.53
\end{aligned}
$$

Sample variance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^2 &= \frac{\sum X_i^2 - N\bar{X}^2}{N-1} \\
&= \frac{230.53 - 9 \times (5.0556)^2}{8} \\
&= \frac{230.53 - 9 \times 25.559}{8} \\
&= \frac{230.53 - 230.031}{8} \\
&= \frac{0.499}{8} \\
&= 0.0624
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{0.0624} = 0.2498 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left(\frac{z \cdot s}{R \cdot \bar{X}} \cdot 100\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\frac{1.96 \times 0.2498}{5 \times 5.0556} \times 100\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\frac{0.4896}{25.278} \times 100\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(1.9369\right)^{2} \\
&= 3.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore **$N = 4$** observations (rounded up to next whole integer). This means the initial 9 pilot observations are already more than sufficient.

> **Logic:** When $N$ comes out less than the pilot $N$, no further observations are needed; the pilot itself satisfies the required statistical accuracy.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Continuous vs. Snap-Back Timing

| Criterion | Continuous Timing | Snap-Back Timing |
|---|---|---|
| Watch running | Always | Reset every element |
| Error accumulation | Low (only at cycle end) | High (one missed reset → entire cycle wrong) |
| Data captured | Cumulative + individual | Individual only |
| Effort | Moderate | High (operator must reset fast) |
| Best for | Machine-dominated cycles | Short manual repetitive cycles |
| Examiner preference | **Higher** (preferred answer in KTU) | Lower |

---

### 3.5 Typical KTU Distinction: Normal Time vs. Standard Time

| Aspect | Normal Time | Standard Time |
|---|---|---|
| Allowance included? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Used for | Method comparison, costing | Production planning, scheduling, incentive |
| Formula | $\text{OT} \times \text{PRF}/100$ | $\text{NT} \times (1+A/100)$ |
| Always | $\text{ST} \ge \text{NT}$ | — |
| If $A = 0$ | $\text{ST} = \text{NT}$ | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Basic Procedure of Stop Watch Time Study

The figure below shows the canonical 8-step procedural flow, with feedback loops to indicate where re-iteration is necessary (e.g., if the number of observations is statistically inadequate).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1: Define Study Objective"]
    stepB["Step 2: Select Operator and Workplace"]
    stepC["Step 3: Analyze and Confirm Standard Method"]
    stepD["Step 4: Record Method - Break into Elements with Terminal Points"]
    stepE["Step 5: Observe and Record Times using Stop Watch"]
    stepF["Step 6: Rate Operator Performance PRF"]
    stepG["Step 7: Compute Normal Time NT = OT x PRF divided by 100"]
    stepH["Step 8: Add Allowances - Compute Standard Time ST"]

    checkA{"Pilot N sufficient?"}
    checkB{"Method standardized?"}

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> checkB
    checkB -- No --> stepA
    checkB -- Yes --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> checkA
    checkA -- No --> stepE
    checkA -- Yes --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
```

> [!NOTE]
> The feedback loop from **Step 5 → Step 5** corresponds to the **statistical sample-size check** — if pilot observations are inadequate, additional cycles are taken before proceeding to rating.

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Data-Flow in Time Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        worker["Qualified Worker"]
        method["Standard Method"]
        stopwatch["Calibrated Stop Watch"]
        observer["Trained Observer"]
    end

    subgraph processingLayer["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        rawOT["Raw Observed Times OT per element"]
        ratingLogic["Performance Rating Logic - Westinghouse or Synthetic"]
        prfOut["PRF per element"]
        ntCalc["NT = OT x PRF divided by 100"]
        sumBasic["Sum to Basic Time"]
        allowCalc["Allowance Factor A - PA + RA + CA"]
        stCalc["ST = Basic Time x 1 plus A divided by 100"]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        stdTime["Standard Time per piece"]
        stdOutput["Standard Output per hour"]
        wageBase["Wage Incentive Base"]
        planBase["Production Planning Base"]
    end

    worker --> rawOT
    method --> rawOT
    stopwatch --> rawOT
    observer --> ratingLogic
    rawOT --> ntCalc
    ratingLogic --> prfOut
    prfOut --> ntCalc
    ntCalc --> sumBasic
    sumBasic --> stCalc
    allowCalc --> stCalc
    stCalc --> stdTime
    stdTime --> stdOutput
    stdTime --> wageBase
    stdTime --> planBase
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing Continuous vs. Snap-Back Timing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Is cycle very short, less than 30 s?"}
    q2{"Is operation machine-dominated with long waits?"}
    q3{"Is observer highly trained?"}

    ans1["Use Snap-Back Timing - reset every element"]
    ans2["Use Continuous Timing - cumulative read preferred"]
    ans3["Either method acceptable - default to Continuous for lower error"]
    ans4["Use Continuous Timing with two stop watches split timing"]

    q1 -- Yes --> q3
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> ans4
    q2 -- No --> ans2
    q3 -- Yes --> ans1
    q3 -- No --> ans3
```

### 4.4 Schematic of an Element Split with Terminal Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    elA["Element 1: Reach and Grasp - Terminal Point A: Hand contacts blank"]
    elB["Element 2: Lift and Move - Terminal Point B: Hand positioned above jig"]
    elC["Element 3: Insert and Release - Terminal Point C: Blank seated and hand withdrawn"]
    elD["Element 4: Activate Machine - Terminal Point D: Both buttons pressed, sound heard"]

    elA --> elB
    elB --> elC
    elC --> elD
    elD --> nextCycle(("Cycle Repeats"))
    nextCycle -.-> elA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each **terminal point** must be *unambiguous and repeatable* — that is what allows an independent observer to score identical timings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Q1. (3 Marks)**
> *Define the term "Standard Time" as applied in Stop Watch Time Study. How is it different from Normal Time?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Standard Time (ST)** is the total time *allowed* to a qualified operator, working at a normal pace, to complete a specified unit of work *including* allowances for personal needs, relaxation and contingency. (1.5 marks)
- **Normal Time (NT)**, on the other hand, is the time required by a normal-rated worker *without* any allowance. The two are related by $\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times (1 + A/100)$ where $A$ is the allowance factor in percent. (1.5 marks)
- Thus, **NT** is the *fair-work* time, whereas **ST** is the *paid/allowed* time. ST is always greater than or equal to NT.

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Q2. (3 Marks)**
> *List any three characteristics of a good "element" into which an operation must be split before stop watch timing.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. The element must have a clearly defined and repeatable **terminal point** (sound, motion completion or contact). (1 mark)
2. The element should be **short enough** to be timed accurately — typically **0.5 min to a few minutes** — and must be **logically distinct** (manual vs. machine, productive vs. preparatory). (1 mark)
3. The element should be **observable with reasonable ease** by the time-study observer, and **stable in method** across cycles. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` — Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> *(a)* *"Explain, with the help of a neat flowchart, the basic procedure of conducting a Stop Watch Time Study."* **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* *During a stop watch time study, the following observations (in seconds) were recorded for an element across 8 cycles:* $12,\ 14,\ 11,\ 13,\ 15,\ 12,\ 14,\ 13$. *The operator was rated at 110 % and a total allowance of 15 % was given. Determine (i) Mean Observed Time, (ii) Normal Time, and (iii) Standard Time for the element.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Flowchart of Basic Procedure (7 Marks):**

The basic procedure consists of **eight sequential steps** as shown below. The student is expected to draw (or describe) a flowchart with the following boxes connected in sequence, with at least one feedback loop indicated for "number of cycles check":

| Step | Description |
|---|---|
| 1 | Define the objective of the time study (e.g., setting standard times for wage incentive, line balancing). |
| 2 | Select the operator and workplace — operator must be qualified and trained; workplace must be representative. |
| 3 | Analyze and confirm the **standard method** is being used. |
| 4 | Record the method and split the operation into **elements** with clear **terminal points**. |
| 5 | Observe and record times with stop watch — preferably **continuous timing**. |
| 6 | Rate the operator's performance using a recognized system (Westinghouse, Synthetic, Pace). |
| 7 | Compute Normal Time: $\text{NT} = \text{OT} \times \text{PRF}/100$. |
| 8 | Add allowance factor and compute Standard Time: $\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times (1 + A/100)$. |

(1 mark for the flowchart structure, 4 marks for correctly describing the eight steps, 1 mark for feedback loop / cyclical iteration note, 1 mark for neatness and labelling.) **= 7 Marks**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step (i): Mean Observed Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X} &= \frac{12 + 14 + 11 + 13 + 15 + 12 + 14 + 13}{8} \\
&= \frac{104}{8} \\
&= 13.00 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**Step (ii): Normal Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NT} &= \bar{X} \times \frac{\text{PRF}}{100} \\
&= 13.00 \times \frac{110}{100} \\
&= 13.00 \times 1.10 \\
&= 14.30 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**Step (iii): Standard Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST} &= \text{NT} \times \left(1 + \frac{A}{100}\right) \\
&= 14.30 \times \left(1 + \frac{15}{100}\right) \\
&= 14.30 \times 1.15 \\
&= 16.445 \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 16.45 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**Final Answer:**
- Mean Observed Time = **13.00 s**
- Normal Time = **14.30 s**
- Standard Time = **16.45 s**
**(1 Mark)** for presentation / correct units.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` — Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> *(a)* *Discuss the different methods of rating the operator's performance during a stop watch time study. Compare any two of them.* **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* *A machine operator performs an operation consisting of three elements. The time study data are:* | Element | Average OT (min) | Rating |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 0.30 | 90 |
> | 2 | 0.45 | 115 |
> | 3 | 0.25 | 100 |
>
> *The total allowance is 20 % of Normal Time. Calculate (i) the Basic Time of the operation, (ii) the Standard Time per piece, and (iii) the expected standard output in an 8-hour shift.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Performance Rating Methods (7 Marks):**

The objective of performance rating is to convert the *observed* time into the time a *normal* (100 %) worker would have taken. The main methods are:

1. **Westinghouse Rating System** — Rates four sub-factors:
   - **Skill** (training, dexterity, experience).
   - **Effort** (will to work).
   - **Conditions** (heat, light, noise).
   - **Consistency** (uniformity of pace).
   Each sub-factor is given a numerical code (positive or negative) which is summed to yield the rating.

2. **Synthetic (Speed) Rating** — A single overall speed rating is assigned based on observer's mental comparison with a known normal pace (e.g., normal walking pace = 100).

3. **Objective Rating (British Standard)** — Performance Rating = Walking Speed × 100 / Standard Walking Speed. Useful for very repetitive operations.

4. **Pace Rating (Currie)** — Numerical pace value $P$ assigned, with $P = 100$ for normal.

**Comparison — Westinghouse vs. Synthetic Rating:**

| Criterion | Westinghouse | Synthetic |
|---|---|---|
| Sub-factors considered | Four (Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency) | One (overall speed) |
| Objectivity | Higher (structured table) | Lower (subjective judgment) |
| Complexity | More complex; slower to apply | Quick; suitable for high-volume studies |
| Best for | Detailed, slow elements | Repetitive short cycles |
| Result format | Sum of four codes | Single number (e.g., 110) |

**(7 Marks — 3 for listing methods, 3 for comparison, 1 for conclusion.)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step (i): Basic Time of the operation.**

Element-wise Normal Times:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NT}_1 &= 0.30 \times \frac{90}{100} = 0.270 \text{ min} \\
\text{NT}_2 &= 0.45 \times \frac{115}{100} = 0.5175 \text{ min} \\
\text{NT}_3 &= 0.25 \times \frac{100}{100} = 0.250 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Basic Time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Basic Time} &= \text{NT}_1 + \text{NT}_2 + \text{NT}_3 \\
&= 0.270 + 0.5175 + 0.250 \\
&= 1.0375 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**Step (ii): Standard Time per piece.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST} &= \text{Basic Time} \times \left(1 + \frac{A}{100}\right) \\
&= 1.0375 \times 1.20 \\
&= 1.245 \text{ min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**Step (iii): Standard Output in 8-hour shift.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Available Time} &= 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ min} \\
\text{Standard Output} &= \frac{480}{1.245} = 385.54 \text{ pieces}
\end{aligned}
$$
**(2 Marks)**

**Final Answer:** Basic Time = **1.0375 min**; Standard Time = **1.245 min/piece**; Output in 8 h = **385.5 pieces**.
**(1 Mark)** for final presentation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Stop Watch Time Study**
>
> 1. **Conflating Normal Time and Standard Time.** Many students write *"Standard Time = Normal Time + Rating"* — wrong. Rating converts OT→NT. Allowance converts NT→ST. **Remember: OT → NT (via Rating) → ST (via Allowance).**
>
> 2. **Applying a single global rating to multi-element data.** When each element has its own rating, you MUST compute element-wise Normal Time and sum. Applying one average rating to the cycle-mean is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to round-up $N$.** If the statistical formula gives $N = 3.75$, write $N = 4$, not 3 or 3.75. Examiners expect *whole cycles*.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Snap-Back and Continuous timings.** Snap-Back **resets** the watch; Continuous **does not**. In KTU, **Continuous timing is the default preferred answer**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the units in the final answer.** Always state *"13.00 s"* not *"13"*. Same for *min/piece*.
>
> 6. **Mis-stating the role of Method Study.** Stop Watch Time Study is a *work measurement* technique; it presumes Method Study has *already* standardized the operation. The student who skips Step 3 (method analysis) loses a full 1-mark point.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Stop Watch Time Study = direct work measurement** technique — sets **Standard Time** for an operation.
- ☐ The canonical procedure has **8 steps**: Objective → Select Operator → Analyze Method → Record & Split into Elements → Observe (Stop Watch) → Rate → Compute NT → Compute ST.
- ☐ **Elements** must be split with **clear terminal points** (sound, motion, contact).
- ☐ Two timing methods: **Continuous (Flyback)** — preferred; **Snap-Back (Repetitive)** — alternate.
- ☐ **Observed Time (OT)** is the raw stop-watch reading — *unadjusted*.
- ☐ **Normal Time (NT) = OT × (PRF / 100)**. PRF is the Performance Rating Factor (e.g., 110 if 10 % faster than normal).
- ☐ **Basic Time = Σ (Element-wise Normal Times)** when elements are rated independently.
- ☐ **Standard Time (ST) = NT × (1 + A/100)**, where $A$ = Personal Allowance + Relaxation Allowance + Contingency Allowance.
- ☐ Typical $A$ values: PA ≈ 5–7 %, RA ≈ 5–15 %, CA ≈ 2–5 %, total ≈ 10–25 %.
- ☐ **Standard Time > Normal Time** always (unless $A = 0$, which is rare).
- ☐ **Number of observations** either from **statistical formula** $N = (z s / (R \bar{X}) \cdot 100)^2$ or **pre-determined table** (e.g., 200 for cycles < 15 s; 100 for 15–45 s; 50 for 45 s–2 min; etc.).
- ☐ **Standard Output** = Available Time / Standard Time per piece.
- ☐ Always state **units** (s, min, pieces/hour, pieces/shift).
- ☐ Method Study MUST be completed BEFORE Stop Watch Time Study begins.
- ☐ Examiners prefer **Westinghouse system** in theory and **continuous timing** in practice — quote them by name.
- ☐ **Incentive schemes** (Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux) all consume the Standard Time as their **base** — note this link for follow-on questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Classification of Elements # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Classification of Elements" in Work System Design? In Work System Design, an **element** is defined as a **distinct, identifiable, and measurable subdivision of a complete work task** — it is the smallest practical unit of work that a method analyst or time study engineer can observe, measure, and record without losing the logical sequence of the job. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):** > An *element* is a fundamental component of a job/operation, and *classification of elements* is the systematic grouping of these elements based on specific engineering criteria such as **nature, repeatability, productivity contribution, control mechanism (human/machine), and variability**. This classification is the bedrock of **Methods Analysis**, **Motion Study**, and **Time Study (Work Measurement)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Grocery Checkout Analogy" Imagine a cashier scanning groceries at a supermarket checkout counter. From a customer's eye, it looks like *one* activity. But for a Work Study engineer, that single job breaks down into multiple **elements**: | Real Action | Element Type | |---|---| | Hand moves to pick the item | **Reach / Move** (Effective therblig) | | Eye locates the barcode | **Search** (Ineffective therblig) | | Swiping the barcode | **Use** (Effective therblig) | | Placing item in the bag | **Position + Release** (Effective therblig) | | Waiting for the next customer | **Unavoidable Delay** | Just as a chemist classifies the periodic table into metals, non-metals, and noble gases, a Work System engineer classifies every job's micro-actions into well-defined categories. This makes analysis, measurement, and improvement **scientific** rather than **intuitive**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Classify Elements?** > - Helps in **separating value-adding activity from waste**. > - Enables **standardisation** of work methods. > - Forms the database for **predetermined motion time systems (PMTS)** like MTM, MOST, and Basic Motion Timestudy. > - Provides the **foundation for stopwatch time study** and **standard time calculation**. ## 1.3 The Five Major Classification Systems Used in KTU Industrial Engineering The KTU 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes **five** orthogonal classification systems. These are not competing alternatives — they are **complementary lenses** through which the same work element can be viewed. 1. **Therblig Classification** (Gilbreth's 18 Fundamental Motions) 2. **Productivity-Based Classification** (Productive / Incidental / Unproductive) 3. **Repeatability-Based Classification** (Repetitive / Non-Repetitive) 4. **Control-Based Classification** (Manual / Machine / Combined) 5. **Variability-Based Classification** (Constant / Variable) We will dissect each in the next sections. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification System #1 — Therbligs (Gilbreth's 18 Fundamental Motions) Frank B. Gilbreth (with his wife Lillian Gilbreth) decomposed every manual human motion into **18 indivisible units called *Therbligs*** — the name is *"Gilbreth"* spelled backwards with the *G* dropped. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Therbligs appear repeatedly in Module 3 questions. A frequent KTU examiner favourite is the question: *"Classify the 18 therbligs with examples and identify which are effective/ineffective."* ### The 18 Therbligs Grouped by Function #### A. Effective Therbligs (Value-Adding — Symbol: green in practice) These directly accomplish the work. 1. **Reach (R)** — Moving empty hand toward an object. 2. **Move (M)** — Moving an object with purpose. 3. **Grasp (G)** — Closing fingers around an object. 4. **Position (P)** — Orienting/aligning an object. 5. **Pre-Position (PP)** — Positioning an object for later use (preparatory). 6. **Assemble (A)** — Joining two parts together. 7. **Disassemble (DA)** — Separating joined parts. 8. **Use (U)** — Using a tool/device for its intended purpose. 9. **Release (RL)** — Letting go of an object. #### B. Ineffective Therbligs (Non-Value-Adding but Often Necessary) 10. **Search (Sh)** — Eyes/hand trying to find an object. 11. **Select (St)** — Choosing among several objects. 12. **Inspect (I)** — Comparing object with a standard. 13. **Plan (Pn)** — Deciding the next course of action. 14. **Hold (H)** — Stopping motion to hold an object stationary. #### C. Transport Therbligs (Special — Both Effective and Ineffective) 15. **Transport Empty (TE)** — Moving empty hand (similar to Reach but distinct). 16. **Transport Loaded (TL)** — Same as Move (synonym in some texts). #### D. Delay Therbligs (Purely Ineffective — Time Loss) 17. **Avoidable Delay (AD)** — Delay under worker's control (e.g., day-dreaming). 18. **Unavoidable Delay (UD)** — Delay beyond worker's control (e.g., machine breakdown). 19. **Rest (R)** — For overcoming fatigue (called *Rest for Overcoming Fatigue*). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Most texts list **18 Therbligs**, but some include *Rest* as the 18th (replacing one of the delays). Be consistent — KTU 2024 syllabus accepts the standard **18-therblig model with Rest counted separately as the 18th** in the fatigue category. ## 2.2 Classification System #2 — By Productivity Contribution This is the classification used directly in **Process Charts** and **Two-Handed Process Charts** in Methods Analysis. | Element Type | Symbol | Definition | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Operation** | O | Work being done on the job (primary purpose) | Drilling, painting, tightening | | **Inspection** | ▢ | Verifying quality/specifications | Checking dimensions, visual check | | **Transport** | → | Moving material from one place to another | Conveyor movement, hand-carrying | | **Storage** | ▽ | Holding material in a fixed location | Bin storage, waiting before next op | | **Delay** | D | Wait time (material or operator waiting) | Material drying, operator idle | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trick:** In a flow process chart, **only "Operation" adds direct value**. **Inspection** adds value only if the customer pays for it. Everything else is **non-value-adding** from a lean perspective — but is sometimes *necessary* non-value-adding work. ## 2.3 Classification System #3 — By Repeatability - **Repetitive Elements**: Occur in every cycle of the operation. Used in **standard time calculation** via time study. - **Non-Repetitive Elements (Occasional Elements)**: Occur intermittently (e.g., tool change, machine reset). Handled via **frequency factor (f)** in standard time formula. ## 2.4 Classification System #4 — By Control (Manual / Machine / Combined) | Type | Description | Symbol in PMTS | |---|---|---| | **Manual Element** | Completely controlled by the worker | M | | **Machine Element** | Completely controlled by machine | A (automatic) | | **Combined/Hand-Machine Element** | Worker + machine together | - | **Why it matters:** Machine elements are usually **rated on speed** (not effort), and a *rest allowance* is *not* typically applied to machine-only elements because the worker is not physically exerting. ## 2.5 Classification System #5 — By Variability - **Constant Element**: Time does not change from cycle to cycle (e.g., closing a lid). - **Variable Element**: Time changes from cycle to cycle (e.g., feeding sheets of different thickness — thicker sheets take longer). This classification is **crucial** when applying **predetermined motion time systems**, because variable elements require multiple time standards for different conditions. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers every formula connected to element classification in Module 3.** | # | Concept / Formula | Expression | Units | Where Used | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Normal Time** for an element | $NT_i = OT_i \times R_i$ | min | Time Study | | 2 | **Standard Time** (per element) | $ST_i = NT_i \times (1 + Allowance_i)$ | min | Work Measurement | | 3 | **Element Time Adjustment** (for variable elements) | $NT_{adj} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} NT_j}{n}$ | min | Averaging cycles | | 4 | **Standard Time for Cycle** | $ST = \sum_{i=1}^{k} NT_i \left(1 + A_i\right)$ | min | Whole job | | 5 | **Performance Rating** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Observed Speed}}{\text{Normal Speed}} \times 100$ | % | Time Study | | 6 | **Westinghouse Rating Factor** | $R_{total} = R_{Skill} \times R_{Effort} \times R_{Consistency} \times R_{Conditions}$ | unitless | Rating | | 7 | **Effective Work Index (Lean)** | $\text{EWI} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Effective Therbligs Time}}{\sum \text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100$ | % | Motion Study | | 8 | **Therblig Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\sum t_{effective}}{\sum t_{effective} + \sum t_{ineffective}} \times 100$ | % | Work Study | | 9 | **Allowance Composition** | $A = A_{PF} + A_{RM} + A_{S} + A_{U}$ | % | Time Study | | 10 | **Standard Hour Formula** | $SH = \dfrac{\text{Standard Time per piece}}{\text{Time per standard hour (60 min)}}$ | SH/piece | Wage Incentive | Where: - $OT$ = Observed Time, $R$ = Rating factor, $A$ = Allowance - $A_{PF}$ = Personal Fatigue, $A_{RM}$ = Relaxation/Machine, $A_S$ = Standard/Contingency, $A_U$ = Unavoidable delay - Indices: $i$ = element index, $j$ = cycle index, $k$ = total elements, $n$ = number of cycles > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Element classification is the operational foundation of **lean manufacturing**, **Six Sigma DMAIC**, **Toyota Production System**, and **Industrial Engineering compensation systems (e.g., Halsey Plan, Rowan Plan, Bedaux Plan)**. Every motion-study based ergonomic intervention (e.g., RULA, REBA assessments) traces its taxonomy back to Gilbreth's therbligs. ## 2.7 The "Why-How" Behind Classification — Structured Logic **Why classify elements at all?** - Because raw observation data is **chaotic and unstructured**. Classification imposes *order*. - Because **standardization is impossible without categorization** — you cannot set a standard time for an unknown quantity. **How is classification applied in practice?** 1. **Decompose** the job into elements via process chart (Operations / Inspection / Transport / Storage / Delay). 2. **Sub-decompose** manual elements into Therbligs (using filming / DART — Designated Aided Recall Technique). 3. **Time-rate-classify** each element (Manual, Machine, Combined). 4. **Rate each element** using Westinghouse or synthetic rating. 5. **Compute** Normal Time → Standard Time using the formulas above. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples Since "Classification of Elements" is largely conceptual, the **numerical depth** in this section focuses on **deriving classification indices** and **applying the formula sheet** from Section 2 to real KTU numerical problems. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Therblig Effectiveness Index (TEI) ### Problem Setup A worker performs a task broken into 9 elements. Observed times and therblig classification are recorded. We need to compute the **Therblig Effectiveness Index** (also called *Therblig Efficiency*). ### Given Data (illustrative) | Element # | Observed Time (sec) | Therblig Category | |---|---|---| | E1 | 8 | Effective (Reach) | | E2 | 5 | Effective (Move) | | E3 | 4 | Ineffective (Search) | | E4 | 2 | Effective (Grasp) | | E5 | 6 | Ineffective (Inspect) | | E6 | 3 | Effective (Position) | | E7 | 4 | Effective (Use) | | E8 | 7 | Effective (Release) | | E9 | 11 | Ineffective (Hold + Avoidable Delay) | ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Separate Effective from Ineffective Times** From the table, identify which elements are *effective* (value-adding) and which are *ineffective*. - **Effective elements** (E1, E2, E4, E6, E7, E8): contribute **directly** to the goal. $$T_{\text{effective}} = 8 + 5 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 7 = 29 \text{ sec}$$ - **Ineffective elements** (E3, E5, E9): do not add value. $$T_{\text{ineffective}} = 4 + 6 + 11 = 21 \text{ sec}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Total Cycle Time** $$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{effective}} + T_{\text{ineffective}}$$ $$T_{\text{total}} = 29 + 21 = 50 \text{ sec}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Therblig Efficiency Formula** $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\sum t_{\text{effective}}}{\sum t_{\text{effective}} + \sum t_{\text{ineffective}}} \times 100$$ Substituting the values: $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{29}{50} \times 100$$ $$\eta_{th} = 58\%$$ ### Interpretation - Only **58%** of the cycle time produces direct value. - The remaining **42%** is waste (Search, Inspect, Hold, Delay). - **Action**: Redesign workplace (5S), use jigs/fixtures, eliminate avoidable delays — push η above **85%** (world-class). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Standard Time for a Multi-Element Cycle (Full Worked Example) ### Problem Setup (a classic KTU-style question) A time study analyst records the following observed times for a 4-element cycle, each measured over 10 cycles. The rating factor ($R$) and allowance ($A$) for each element are given.
