Compute the **Standard Time per cycle** and the **Standard Hour Output**. | Element | Mean Observed Time (min) | Rating $R$ | Allowance $A$ | |---|---|---|---| | E1 (Manual) | 0.20 | 110% | 15% | | E2 (Manual) | 0.35 | 95% | 15% | | E3 (Machine) | 0.50 | 100% | 5% (machine allowance only) | | E4 (Combined) | 0.15 | 105% | 12% | ### Step 1 — Compute Normal Time ($NT$) per element Using: $$NT_i = OT_i \times \dfrac{R_i}{100}$$ For each element: $$NT_1 = 0.20 \times 1.10 = 0.220 \text{ min}$$ $$NT_2 = 0.35 \times 0.95 = 0.3325 \text{ min}$$ $$NT_3 = 0.50 \times 1.00 = 0.500 \text{ min}$$ $$NT_4 = 0.15 \times 1.05 = 0.1575 \text{ min}$$ **Total Normal Time:** $$NT_{total} = 0.220 + 0.3325 + 0.500 + 0.1575$$ $$NT_{total} = 1.210 \text{ min}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Standard Time per element Using: $$ST_i = NT_i \times (1 + A_i)$$ For each element: $$ST_1 = 0.220 \times 1.15 = 0.2530 \text{ min}$$ $$ST_2 = 0.3325 \times 1.15 = 0.3824 \text{ min}$$ $$ST_3 = 0.500 \times 1.05 = 0.5250 \text{ min}$$ $$ST_4 = 0.1575 \times 1.12 = 0.1764 \text{ min}$$ ### Step 3 — Sum for Cycle Standard Time $$ST_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} ST_i$$ $$ST_{cycle} = 0.2530 + 0.3824 + 0.5250 + 0.1764$$ $$\boxed{ST_{cycle} = 1.3368 \text{ min per piece}}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Standard Hours per Piece $$SH_{\text{per piece}} = \dfrac{ST_{cycle}}{60 \text{ min/SH}}$$ $$SH_{\text{per piece}} = \dfrac{1.3368}{60} = 0.02228 \text{ SH/piece}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to Output Rate (pieces per hour) $$\text{Output Rate} = \dfrac{60}{ST_{cycle}} = \dfrac{60}{1.3368} \approx 44.9 \text{ pieces/hour}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this worked example matters:** KTU examiners love asking this exact 14-mark numerical. If you forget to *rate each element separately* (Step 1) or apply *one global allowance* (common mistake), you lose **4 marks** out of 14. Always treat allowance **per element**. --- ## 3.3 Classification Decision Tree — Step-by-Step Logical Procedure To classify any element correctly in a KTU exam, follow this decision tree logic: **Step A** — Is the element doing something to the workpiece? → **Yes** → It's an **Operation** (O). → **No** → Go to Step B. **Step B** — Is the worker checking quality? → **Yes** → It's an **Inspection** (▢). → **No** → Go to Step C. **Step C** — Is material being relocated (without processing)? → **Yes** → It's a **Transport** (→). → **No** → Go to Step D. **Step D** — Is material being held but not actively worked on (active retention)? → **Yes** → It's a **Storage** (▽). → **No** → Go to Step E. **Step E** — Is the worker (or material) simply waiting? → **Yes** → It's a **Delay** (D). This **decision tree is examinable** — KTU 2024 frequently gives a paragraph describing a job and asks you to *classify each event* using this exact logic. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD A["Work Element: The Basic Unit of a Job"]:::root A --> B["By Productivity
Process Chart Symbols"] A --> C["By Motion Type
Gilbreth Therbligs - 18 units"] A --> D["By Repeatability"] A --> E["By Control Mechanism"] A --> F["By Variability"] B --> B1["Operation O
adds direct value"] B --> B2["Inspection ▢
verifies quality"] B --> B3["Transport arrow
moves material"] B --> B4["Storage triangle
holds material"] B --> B5["Delay D
wait state"] C --> C1["Effective Therbligs
9 units value adding"] C --> C2["Ineffective Therbligs
5 units non value adding"] C --> C3["Delay Therbligs
3 units pure waste"] C --> C4["Transport Sub-group
1 special unit"] C1 --> C1a["Reach"] C1 --> C1b["Move"] C1 --> C1c["Grasp"] C1 --> C1d["Position"] C1 --> C1e["Assemble"] C1 --> C1f["Disassemble"] C1 --> C1g["Use"] C1 --> C1h["Release"] C1 --> C1i["Pre-Position"] C2 --> C2a["Search"] C2 --> C2b["Select"] C2 --> C2c["Inspect"] C2 --> C2d["Plan"] C2 --> C2e["Hold"] C3 --> C3a["Avoidable Delay"] C3 --> C3b["Unavoidable Delay"] C3 --> C3c["Rest for Fatigue"] C4 --> C4a["Transport Empty"] D --> D1["Repetitive Elements
in every cycle"] D --> D2["Non Repetitive Elements
occasional only"] E --> E1["Manual Elements"] E --> E2["Machine Elements"] E --> E3["Combined Hand Machine Elements"] F --> F1["Constant Elements
time does not vary"] F --> F2["Variable Elements
time changes per cycle"] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px; classDef cat1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000; classDef cat2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000; classDef cat3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000; classDef cat4 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000; class A root; class B1,C1,C1a,C1b,C1c,C1d,C1e,C1f,C1g,C1h,C1i,C2,C2a,C2b,C2c,C2d,C2e,D1,E1,F1 cat1; class B2,B3,B5,D2,E3 cat2; class B4,C3,C3a,C3b,C3c,E2 cat3; class C4,C4a,F2 cat4; ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Element Classification ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Raw Job
Observed"] --> B["Decomposition
Methods Analysis"] B --> C["Process Chart
O I T S D Classification"] C --> D["Two Handed Chart
Manual vs Machine"] D --> E["Therblig Analysis
Gilbreth 18 Motions"] E --> F["Performance Rating
Westinghouse System"] F --> G["Normal Time
OT x R"] G --> H["Allowance Addition
1 plus A"] H --> I["Standard Time
Final Output"] I --> J["Work Standard Database"] J --> K["Wage Incentive Plan"] J --> L["Production Planning"] J --> M["Ergonomic Improvement"] J --> N["Lean Kaizen"] ``` ## 4.3 Element Decision Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Encounter a Work Activity"] --> Q1{"Doing something
to the part?"} Q1 -->|Yes| Op["Operation O"] Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Checking
quality?"} Q2 -->|Yes| Insp["Inspection"] Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Moving the
part?"} Q3 -->|Yes| Tr["Transport"] Q3 -->|No| Q4{"Storing for
later use?"} Q4 -->|Yes| St["Storage"] Q4 -->|No| Dl["Delay D"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Memorize the *question* asked at each node. If you can answer the flowchart questions in the right order, you will classify any element correctly in any KTU 2024 theory question. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Element" as used in Work Study. Mention any **two** classification criteria for work elements.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **element** is the smallest practical subdivision of a work activity that can be observed, timed, and measured separately while still retaining the logical flow of the overall job. Two classification criteria are: 1. **By Productivity Contribution** — Operation, Inspection, Transport, Storage, Delay. 2. **By Repeatability** — Repetitive elements (occur every cycle) vs. Non-repetitive (occasional elements). *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for two criteria with brief explanation)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **List the 18 Therbligs developed by Gilbreth. Identify which category is "ineffective" and give two examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Gilbreth identified **18 fundamental human motions** called **Therbligs**. They are: - **Effective (9):** Reach, Move, Grasp, Position, Pre-Position, Assemble, Disassemble, Use, Release. - **Ineffective (5):** Search, Select, Inspect, Plan, Hold. - **Delays (3):** Avoidable Delay, Unavoidable Delay, Rest for Overcoming Fatigue. - **Transport (1):** Transport Empty. Two **ineffective** examples: **Search** (eyes/hand searching for an object) and **Hold** (holding an object without using it). *(3 marks — 1 for listing, 1 for category identification, 1 for examples)* --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Coverage **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** With the aid of a clear classification table, explain **any four** different ways in which work elements can be classified in Work System Design. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 6-element cycle has the following observed times and classifications: | Element | Type | OT (min) | Rating R | Allowance A | |---|---|---|---|---| | E1 | Manual | 0.10 | 100% | 15% | | E2 | Manual | 0.15 | 110% | 15% | | E3 | Machine | 0.40 | 100% | 5% | | E4 | Manual | 0.20 | 95% | 15% | | E5 | Combined | 0.10 | 105% | 12% | | E6 | Machine | 0.25 | 100% | 5% | Calculate the **Standard Time per cycle** and the **number of pieces produced per standard hour**. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Four Classification Systems (7 marks)** 1. **Productivity-Based Classification** (2 marks): Elements are grouped by whether they add value, are necessary non-value-adding, or pure waste. - **Operation (O):** Direct value addition. - **Inspection (▢):** Quality verification. - **Transport (→):** Movement. - **Storage (▽):** Holding. - **Delay (D):** Waiting. 2. **Therblig Classification** (2 marks): Gilbreth's 18 basic motions are divided into **Effective (9)**, **Ineffective (5)**, **Delay (3)**, and **Transport (1)** categories — useful for motion economy and ergonomic redesign. 3. **Repeatability-Based Classification** (1.5 marks): - **Repetitive elements** occur every cycle (handle in time study as core). - **Non-repetitive (occasional) elements** occur intermittently (e.g., tool change) — handled with frequency factor. 4. **Control-Based Classification** (1.5 marks): - **Manual:** Worker-controlled. - **Machine:** Machine-controlled. - **Combined:** Both. Important for applying rest allowance (typically only to manual elements). **Part (b) — Standard Time Calculation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Compute Normal Time per element** (1 mark each for formula application, 3 marks total) $$NT_1 = 0.10 \times 1.00 = 0.100 \text{ min}$$ $$NT_2 = 0.15 \times 1.10 = 0.165 \text{ min}$$ $$NT_3 = 0.40 \times 1.00 = 0.400 \text{ min}$$ $$NT_4 = 0.20 \times 0.95 = 0.190 \text{ min}$$ $$NT_5 = 0.10 \times 1.05 = 0.105 \text{ min}$$ $$NT_6 = 0.25 \times 1.00 = 0.250 \text{ min}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Standard Time per element** (1 mark each for formula application, 3 marks total) $$ST_1 = 0.100 \times 1.15 = 0.1150 \text{ min}$$ $$ST_2 = 0.165 \times 1.15 = 0.1898 \text{ min}$$ $$ST_3 = 0.400 \times 1.05 = 0.4200 \text{ min}$$ $$ST_4 = 0.190 \times 1.15 = 0.2185 \text{ min}$$ $$ST_5 = 0.105 \times 1.12 = 0.1176 \text{ min}$$ $$ST_6 = 0.250 \times 1.05 = 0.2625 \text{ min}$$ **Step 3 — Total Standard Time per Cycle** (0.5 mark) $$ST_{cycle} = 0.1150 + 0.1898 + 0.4200 + 0.2185 + 0.1176 + 0.2625$$ $$ST_{cycle} = 1.3234 \text{ min/piece}$$ **Step 4 — Pieces per Standard Hour** (0.5 mark) $$\text{Output} = \dfrac{60}{1.3234} \approx 45.3 \text{ pieces/Std Hour}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Marks Are Lost:** > 1. **Using a single global allowance** (e.g., 15% for all elements) instead of element-wise allowance — **loses 2 marks**. > 2. **Not separating Machine elements** for allowance treatment — machine elements have lower allowance (5–7%) compared to manual (12–15%) — **loses 1 mark**. > 3. **Forgetting to convert rating** from % to decimal before multiplication — **loses 1 mark**. > 4. **Skipping the "pieces per standard hour" conversion** — examiners want both $ST$ *and* output — **loses 1 mark**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a)** What are **Therbligs**? Explain the classification of Therbligs into **Effective, Ineffective, Delay, and Transport** categories with **at least two examples** from each category. Why are ineffective therbligs given importance in work study? *(7 marks)* **(b)** A worker performs an operation consisting of 5 elements. The observed times and classifications are: | Element | OT (sec) | Therblig Type | |---|---|---| | E1 | 6 | Reach (Effective) | | E2 | 4 | Search (Ineffective) | | E3 | 5 | Move (Effective) | | E4 | 3 | Inspect (Ineffective) | | E5 | 12 | Use (Effective) | Compute the **Therblig Efficiency Index** and suggest **two practical improvements** to enhance it. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Therbligs Explanation (7 marks)** - **Definition (1 mark):** Therbligs are the 18 fundamental indivisible human motions identified by **Frank Gilbreth** in motion study, representing the smallest observable human work units. - **Effective Therbligs (2 marks):** Direct value addition. Examples: **Reach** (moving hand to object), **Move** (carrying object to destination), **Use** (using tool). - **Ineffective Therbligs (2 marks):** Do not add direct value but are often necessary. Examples: **Search** (locating object), **Inspect** (verifying quality), **Hold** (pausing without release). - **Delay Therbligs (1 mark):** Pure waste. Examples: **Avoidable Delay** (worker's voluntary pause), **Unavoidable Delay** (e.g., power failure), **Rest** (fatigue recovery). - **Importance of Ineffective Therbligs in Work Study (1 mark):** They are targets for **method improvement**. By reducing Search and Select, engineers redesign workplace layout (5S), introduce jigs/fixtures, improve lighting, and use poka-yoke. Reducing ineffective therbligs directly reduces cycle time and operator fatigue. **Part (b) — Therblig Efficiency Index (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Classify and sum the effective vs. ineffective times** (2 marks) - **Effective times:** E1 (Reach) = 6, E3 (Move) = 5, E5 (Use) = 12 → Sum = **23 sec** - **Ineffective times:** E2 (Search) = 4, E4 (Inspect) = 3 → Sum = **7 sec** **Step 2 — Total cycle time** (1 mark) $$T_{total} = 23 + 7 = 30 \text{ sec}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Therblig Efficiency formula** (2 marks) $$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{23}{30} \times 100 = 76.67\%$$ **Step 4 — Two Practical Improvements** (2 marks — 1 each) 1. **Reduce Search time (4 sec)**: Reorganize the workplace using the **5S methodology** (Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain). Place all tools within the worker's normal working area (within 30 cm). Use **shadow boards** and **labeled bins**. 2. **Reduce Inspect time (3 sec)**: Replace manual visual inspection with **poka-yoke (mistake-proofing)** devices, or use **sensors / gauges** that give instantaneous feedback. This converts inspection from human to automatic. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** > 1. **Not defining "Therblig" before classifying** — examiners want the etymology note ("Gilbreth backwards") — **loses 1 mark**. > 2. **Confusing Ineffective with Delay** — remember: Ineffective = Search/Select/Inspect/Plan/Hold (necessary); Delay = pure waiting — **loses 1 mark**. > 3. **Improvements not specific** — saying "improve the workplace" is too vague. KTU wants **5S, jigs, fixtures, poka-yoke, layout redesign** — **loses 2 marks** for vague suggestions. > 4. **Not linking improvements back to η_th** — every improvement should explicitly say "this will raise η_th" — **loses 1 mark**. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Classification of Elements** - ✅ **Element** = smallest practical unit of work (observable + measurable + logical). - ✅ **Gilbreth** = inventor of therbligs (18 fundamental motions). - ✅ **18 Therbligs** = 9 Effective + 5 Ineffective + 3 Delay + 1 Transport (some texts count Rest as 18th — be consistent). - ✅ **Effective Therbligs**: Reach, Move, Grasp, Position, Pre-Position, Assemble, Disassemble, Use, Release. - ✅ **Ineffective Therbligs**: Search, Select, Inspect, Plan, Hold. - ✅ **Delay Therbligs**: Avoidable Delay, Unavoidable Delay, Rest for Overcoming Fatigue. - ✅ **Process Chart Classification** = O, I, T, S, D (Operation, Inspection, Transport, Storage, Delay). - ✅ **Repeatitive vs Non-Repetitive** = cycle-frequency based. - ✅ **Manual vs Machine vs Combined** = control-based; rest allowance applied only to manual. - ✅ **Constant vs Variable** = variability-based; variable elements need averaged or multiple standards. - ✅ **Key formula**: $NT = OT \times R$ ; $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$. - ✅ **Therblig Efficiency**: $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_{effective}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ ; aim **> 85%**. - ✅ **Rating** uses Westinghouse or Synthetic (4 factors: Skill, Effort, Consistency, Conditions). - ✅ **Allowance** is **per element**, not global — machine allowance (~5–7%) is much less than manual (~12–15%). - ✅ **Output rate** $= \dfrac{60}{ST_{cycle}}$ pieces per standard hour. - ✅ **Lean interpretation**: Only **Operation** (and customer-paid **Inspection**) add value; everything else is candidate for elimination. - ✅ **Common KTU pitfall**: Forgetting element-wise allowance — **always rate and allow per element, not per cycle**. - ✅ **Common KTU pitfall**: Treating Hold as Effective — Hold is **Ineffective**. > 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** Before answering any classification question, write down the 5 classification systems in a small box and **label each element under each system** — KTU examiners award marks for *systematic thinking*, not just final answer.

topics.title: Breaking the Job into Elements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Breaking the Job into Elements

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Breaking the Job into Elements

### Formal Academic Definition

In **Work System Design** and **Methods Engineering**, **Breaking the Job into Elements** is defined as the systematic procedure of subdividing a complete work activity (job, operation, or task) into its smallest observable, measurable, and logically discrete sub-units called **Work Elements** (or **Therbligs** in motion study), so that each element can be independently studied, timed, rated, and analysed for method improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Verbatim):**
> *"A work element is a distinct, identifiable, and measurable portion of a job which has a definite beginning and a definite ending point, and which can be conveniently observed, timed, and recorded as a separate unit during methods and work measurement studies."*

### Key Terminology Used in KTU Syllabus

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Element** | Smallest practical division of a work cycle |
| **Sub-Element** | A further breakdown of a complex element |
| **Unit Element** | A short, repetitive portion common to many jobs |
| **Element Breakdown Sheet** | Tabular record of all elements of a job |
| **End Point** | The instant an element ends and the next begins |
| **Foreign Element** | An element not part of the regular work cycle |
| **Repetitive Element** | An element performed in every cycle |
| **Occasional Element** | An element not performed every cycle |
| **Constant Element** | Element time that does not vary from cycle to cycle |
| **Variable Element** | Element time that varies from cycle to cycle |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Recipe Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a chef trying to teach someone to cook Biryani. You wouldn't just say "cook Biryani." Instead, you break it down: *wash rice → soak rice → chop onions → fry spices → add chicken → simmer → layer rice → dum cook → serve.* Each step is a **work element** — small, observable, and measurable. Breaking the job into elements is exactly this, applied to industrial work. By analysing each "ingredient step" (element), the engineer (chef) can find which step is slow, wasteful, or incorrect, and improve it independently.

### Real-World Importance in Engineering

- **Productivity Engineering:** Reduces cycle time by identifying non-value-adding elements.
- **Cost Estimation:** Element-wise time × rate = accurate labour cost.
- **Work Measurement:** Forms the foundation of **Time Study**, **PMTS (Predetermined Motion Time Systems)**, and **Standard Data**.
- **Methods Improvement:** Helps in spotting **redundant motions, fatigue, and delays**.
- **Ergonomics & Safety:** Reveals hazardous sub-tasks requiring redesign.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Break a Job into Elements? (The "Why")

1. **Detailed Study** – A whole job is too complex to analyse at once.
2. **Accurate Timing** – Stopwatch readings are reliable only for short, distinct events.
3. **Selective Rating** – Performance rating can be applied element-wise because effort differs across elements.
4. **Identification of Foreign Elements** – Irregular activities can be separated.
5. **Building Standard Data** – Element times are stored and reused for new jobs.
6. **Methods Comparison** – Element-by-element comparison between old and proposed methods.

## 2.2 Principles for Breaking a Job into Elements

The KTU examiner expects these rules verbatim:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8 Golden Rules of Element Breakdown:**
> 1. Each element should have a **clear starting and ending point** (a definite *end point* or *terminal point*).
> 2. Elements should be as **short as possible** to enable accurate timing.
> 3. **Manual and machine time** should be separated.
> 4. **Constant-time elements** and **variable-time elements** should be kept separate.
> 5. Elements should be **logically and physically distinct**.
> 6. Each element should be **rated separately** (effort varies).
> 7. **Foreign elements** (irregular, non-cycle) should be segregated.
> 8. **Subjective elements** (involving judgement) should be kept separate from **automatic** (mechanical) elements.

## 2.3 Types of Elements (Classification Framework)

### A. By Frequency of Occurrence
- **Repetitive Element** – Performed every cycle (e.g., "press button").
- **Occasional Element** – Performed at intervals (e.g., "add coolant").

### B. By Nature of Activity
- **Manual Element** – Done by worker using body/muscle.
- **Machine Element** – Done automatically by machine (also called *process time* or *controlled time*).
- **Mixed Element** – Combined manual + machine.

### C. By Time Variability
- **Constant Element** – Time is essentially the same in every cycle (e.g., drill penetration depth fixed).
- **Variable Element** – Time changes from cycle to cycle (e.g., hand travel distance varies).

### D. By Cycle Membership
- **Regular (Normal) Element** – Part of the standard cycle.
- **Foreign / Irregular Element** – Not part of the standard cycle (e.g., tool breakage).

## 2.4 Therbligs – The Microscopic Elements (Motion Study)

When the engineer breaks a job into **micro-motions**, the elements are called **Therbligs** (the word "therblig" is "Gilbreth" spelled backwards, coined by Frank Gilbreth).

| # | Therblig | Symbol | Colour Code |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Search | Sh | Black |
| 2 | Find | F | Black |
| 3 | Select | St | Grey |
| 4 | Grasp | G | Lake Red |
| 5 | Hold | H | Gold Ochre |
| 6 | Transport Loaded | TL | Green |
| 7 | Transport Empty | TE | Green |
| 8 | Position | P | Blue |
| 9 | Pre-position | PP | Sky Blue |
| 10 | Release | RL | Carmine |
| 11 | Assemble | A | Violet |
| 12 | Disassemble | DA | Purple |
| 13 | Use | U | Purple |
| 14 | Inspect | I | Yellow Orange |
| 15 | Unavoidable Delay | UD | Yellow |
| 16 | Avoidable Delay | AD | Yellow |
| 17 | Plan | Pn | Brown |
| 18 | Rest | R | Orange |

- **Effective Therbligs** – Add value (Grasp, Transport Loaded, Position, Assemble, Use, Disassemble, Release).
- **Ineffective Therbligs** – Do not add value (Search, Find, Select, Hold, Transport Empty, Pre-position, Inspect, Plan, Delays).
- **Effective Therbligs** should be reduced; **Ineffective Therbligs** should be eliminated wherever possible.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_s = T_n + A$ | Standard Time = Normal Time + Allowance | minutes (min) |
| 2 | $T_n = T_o \times R$ | Normal Time = Observed Time × Rating Factor | min |
| 3 | $T_s = T_o \times R \times (1 + A_f)$ | Combined formula | min |
| 4 | $T_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{n,i} = \sum (t_{o,i} \times R_i)$ | Normal time = sum over all elements | min |
| 5 | $T_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{s,i}$ | Total standard time = sum of element standard times | min |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{T_s}$ | Standard output / number of pieces per day | pieces/day |
| 7 | $A = T_n \times A_f$ | Allowance amount = Normal time × Allowance factor | min |
| 8 | $R_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum (R_i \times t_{o,i})}{\sum t_{o,i}}$ | Weighted average rating factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Cycle Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{s,i}$ | Total time of one complete work cycle | min |

> Where:
> - $T_s$ = Standard Time
> - $T_n$ = Normal Time
> - $T_o$ = Observed (actual) Time
> - $R$ = Performance Rating Factor (typically $60\%$ to $130\%$, $100\% = \text{normal}$)
> - $A$ = Allowance (personal, fatigue, contingency)
> - $A_f$ = Allowance factor (e.g., $0.13$ for $13\%$)
> - $N$ = Number of standard pieces producible

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTATION RULE:** Never confuse **Average Time** with **Normal Time**. Average is arithmetic mean of observations; Normal is *Average × Rating*. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU exams.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Production Planning:** Standard time per piece → production rate → scheduling.
- **Costing & Quoting:** Labour cost = time × wage rate.
- **Incentive Wage Systems:** Halsey, Rowan, Bedeaux schemes all use element standard times.
- **Lean Manufacturing:** Identifying *muda* (waste) at element level.
- **Ergonomic Redesign:** Removing high-fatigue elements first.
- **Robotic Automation:** Each human element is mapped to a robot action.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Example

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure to Break a Job into Elements

### Step 1 — Select the Job
Choose a job that is repetitive, measurable, and representative of a typical work cycle.

### Step 2 — Preliminary Observation
Watch the operator several times without recording anything to understand the *whole picture* (orientation phase).

### Step 3 — Record the Method
Use a **Process Chart**, **Flow Process Chart**, or **Two-Hand Process Chart** to capture every step in sequence.

### Step 4 — Identify Cycle Boundaries
Determine the **start point** and **end point** of one complete cycle (e.g., from "pick up blank" to "place finished part on rack").

### Step 5 — Select End Points
End points should be at events where:
- A **clear audible, visible, or measurable event** occurs (e.g., machine starts, contact is made).
- Manual and machine time **separate naturally**.

### Step 6 — Break into Elements
Divide the cycle into logical elements following the 8 Golden Rules listed above.

### Step 7 — Number and Describe Elements
Assign each element a serial number and a concise verbal description.

### Step 8 — Classify Each Element
Tag each element as **Repetitive / Occasional**, **Manual / Machine**, **Constant / Variable**.

### Step 9 — Build the Element Breakdown Sheet
Tabulate all elements with their: Number, Description, Type, and later: Observed Time, Rating, Normal Time, Allowance, Standard Time.

### Step 10 — Compute Times
Apply formulas element-wise to obtain Normal Time → Standard Time.

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> An industrial engineer conducts a time study on a CNC operator performing a facing operation on a cylindrical workpiece. The job has been broken into **5 elements** with the following data:

| Element No. | Description | Type | Observed Time $t_o$ (min) | Rating $R$ (%) | Allowance $A_f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pick up blank and place on chuck | Manual, Variable | 0.18 | 110 | 0.13 |
| 2 | Tighten chuck and start spindle | Manual + Machine, Constant | 0.12 | 105 | 0.13 |
| 3 | Machine facing (cutting) | Machine, Constant | 1.40 | 100 | 0.13 |
| 4 | Stop spindle, loosen chuck, remove part | Manual, Variable | 0.20 | 95 | 0.13 |
| 5 | Inspect diameter with micrometer | Manual, Variable | 0.25 | 100 | 0.13 |

> **Required:**
> Compute Normal Time, Standard Time for each element, total standard time per piece, and number of pieces per 8-hour shift.

### Solution — Step-by-Step (Exhaustive)

#### Element 1: Pick up blank and place on chuck

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{o,1} &= 0.18 \text{ min} \\
R_1 &= 110\% = 1.10 \\
T_{n,1} &= T_{o,1} \times R_1 \\
&= 0.18 \times 1.10 \\
&= 0.198 \text{ min} \\
A_1 &= T_{n,1} \times A_f \\
&= 0.198 \times 0.13 \\
&= 0.02574 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,1} &= T_{n,1} + A_1 \\
&= 0.198 + 0.02574 \\
&= 0.22374 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Element 2: Tighten chuck and start spindle

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{o,2} &= 0.12 \text{ min} \\
R_2 &= 105\% = 1.05 \\
T_{n,2} &= 0.12 \times 1.05 = 0.126 \text{ min} \\
A_2 &= 0.126 \times 0.13 = 0.01638 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,2} &= 0.126 + 0.01638 = 0.14238 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Element 3: Machine facing (cutting — automatic)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{o,3} &= 1.40 \text{ min} \\
R_3 &= 100\% = 1.00 \\
T_{n,3} &= 1.40 \times 1.00 = 1.400 \text{ min} \\
A_3 &= 1.400 \times 0.13 = 0.182 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,3} &= 1.400 + 0.182 = 1.582 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Element 4: Stop spindle, loosen chuck, remove part

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{o,4} &= 0.20 \text{ min} \\
R_4 &= 95\% = 0.95 \\
T_{n,4} &= 0.20 \times 0.95 = 0.190 \text{ min} \\
A_4 &= 0.190 \times 0.13 = 0.0247 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,4} &= 0.190 + 0.0247 = 0.2147 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Element 5: Inspect diameter with micrometer

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{o,5} &= 0.25 \text{ min} \\
R_5 &= 100\% = 1.00 \\
T_{n,5} &= 0.25 \times 1.00 = 0.250 \text{ min} \\
A_5 &= 0.250 \times 0.13 = 0.0325 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,5} &= 0.250 + 0.0325 = 0.2825 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Aggregate Calculations

**Total Normal Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} T_{n,i} \\
&= 0.198 + 0.126 + 1.400 + 0.190 + 0.250 \\
&= 2.164 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Standard Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} T_{s,i} \\
&= 0.22374 + 0.14238 + 1.582 + 0.2147 + 0.2825 \\
&= 2.44532 \text{ min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalent via combined formula (verification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= T_n \times (1 + A_f) \\
&= 2.164 \times (1 + 0.13) \\
&= 2.164 \times 1.13 \\
&= 2.44532 \text{ min/piece} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Production Rate per 8-Hour Shift:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available Time} &= 8 \text{ hr} \times 60 = 480 \text{ min} \\
N &= \dfrac{480}{2.44532} \\
&\approx 196.3 \text{ pieces/shift} \\
&\approx 196 \text{ pieces/shift (rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result Table

| Element | $T_o$ (min) | $R$ (%) | $T_n$ (min) | $A_f$ | $T_s$ (min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.18 | 110 | 0.198 | 0.13 | 0.2237 |
| 2 | 0.12 | 105 | 0.126 | 0.13 | 0.1424 |
| 3 | 1.40 | 100 | 1.400 | 0.13 | 1.5820 |
| 4 | 0.20 | 95 | 0.190 | 0.13 | 0.2147 |
| 5 | 0.25 | 100 | 0.250 | 0.13 | 0.2825 |
| **Total** | **2.15** | — | **2.164** | — | **2.4453** |

> **Result:** Total Standard Time = **2.445 min/piece** → Standard Output = **196 pieces per 8-hour shift**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** 
*Define "Work Element" and list any **four** rules to be followed while breaking a job into elements.*

**Model Answer:**

> A **Work Element** is a small, distinct, and logically identifiable portion of a work cycle which has a clear beginning point and ending point and which can be observed, measured, and rated independently.

**Four Rules:**
1. Each element must have a **definite starting and ending point** (end point).
2. **Manual** and **machine** elements should be separated.
3. Elements should be **as short as possible** for accurate timing.
4. **Constant-time** and **variable-time** elements should be kept separate so that rating can be applied accurately.

*(Any four of the eight Golden Rules of Section 2.2 accepted.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between **Repetitive** and **Occasional Elements** with an example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Repetitive Element | Occasional Element |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Performed **in every work cycle** | Performed **at intervals**, not every cycle |
| Predictability | Highly predictable | Less predictable |
| Inclusion in Standard Time | Always included | Often separated or amortized |
| Example (CNC job) | "Press start button" every cycle | "Add coolant" once every 5 cycles |
| Example (Assembly) | "Pick screw from bin" every cycle | "Replace empty bin" once per hour |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Pattern (Internal Choice):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **principles and procedure** of breaking a job into elements. Discuss the **classification of elements** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data was collected during a time study on a drilling operation. Compute the **Normal Time**, **Standard Time** for each element, **Total Standard Time per piece**, and the **standard output per 8-hour shift**. Allowance factor = **13%**.

| Element | Description | $T_o$ (min) | $R$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pick up blank, place on table | 0.20 | 105 |
| 2 | Clamp blank | 0.15 | 110 |
| 3 | Drill hole (machine) | 1.80 | 100 |
| 4 | Unclamp and remove | 0.18 | 95 |
| 5 | Inspect with plug gauge | 0.22 | 100 |

**(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principles (write any 6 for full 7 marks):**
1. Each element must begin and end at a clearly observable event (sound, contact, motion completion).
2. Manual and machine operations must be **separated** as they have different rating characteristics.
3. Elements should be **logically short and distinct** to ensure accurate stopwatch reading.
4. **Constant-time** and **variable-time** elements must be separated.
5. **Repetitive** and **occasional** elements must be identified and segregated.
6. **Foreign elements** (irregular, non-cyclic) must be recorded separately, not added to standard time.
7. Element description must use **action verbs** (pick, place, start, stop, inspect).

**Procedure (10 steps):**
1. Select the job to be studied.
2. Preliminary orientation — observe without recording.
3. Record present method (Process Chart, Two-Hand Chart).
4. Define cycle start and end.
5. Identify end points for each element.
6. Subdivide the cycle into elements.
7. Number each element and write brief description.
8. Classify by type (manual/machine, constant/variable, repetitive/occasional).
9. Tabulate in **Element Breakdown Sheet**.
10. Compute $T_n$, $T_s$, output rate.

**Classification (any 3 classifications for full 7 marks):**
- **By Frequency:** Repetitive vs Occasional.
- **By Source of Activity:** Manual vs Machine vs Mixed.
- **By Time Variability:** Constant vs Variable.
- **By Cycle Membership:** Regular vs Foreign.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Element 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n,1} &= 0.20 \times 1.05 = 0.210 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,1} &= 0.210 \times 1.13 = 0.2373 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Element 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n,2} &= 0.15 \times 1.10 = 0.165 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,2} &= 0.165 \times 1.13 = 0.18645 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Element 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n,3} &= 1.80 \times 1.00 = 1.800 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,3} &= 1.800 \times 1.13 = 2.034 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Element 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n,4} &= 0.18 \times 0.95 = 0.171 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,4} &= 0.171 \times 1.13 = 0.19323 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Element 5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n,5} &= 0.22 \times 1.00 = 0.220 \text{ min} \\
T_{s,5} &= 0.220 \times 1.13 = 0.2486 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Totals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= 0.210 + 0.165 + 1.800 + 0.171 + 0.220 = 2.566 \text{ min} \\
T_s &= 0.2373 + 0.18645 + 2.034 + 0.19323 + 0.2486 = 2.89958 \text{ min/piece} \\
N &= \dfrac{8 \times 60}{2.89958} = \dfrac{480}{2.89958} \approx 165.5 \approx 165 \text{ pieces/shift}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $T_s = 2.90$ min/piece, Standard Output = **165 pieces/shift**.

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What are **Therbligs**? Explain the **18 therbligs** with their symbols and indicate which are **effective** and which are **ineffective**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the layout of an **Element Breakdown Sheet** and describe how it is used in **standard data preparation**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** Therbligs are the **18 basic, indivisible human motions** identified by Frank and Lillian Gilbreth (1921) for the microscopic analysis of work. The word *therblig* is *Gilbreth* reversed.

**The 18 Therbligs (list with symbol):**

| SN | Therblig | Symbol | Effective / Ineffective |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Search | Sh | Ineffective |
| 2 | Find | F | Ineffective |
| 3 | Select | St | Ineffective |
| 4 | Grasp | G | Effective |
| 5 | Hold | H | Ineffective |
| 6 | Transport Loaded | TL | Effective |
| 7 | Transport Empty | TE | Ineffective |
| 8 | Position | P | Effective |
| 9 | Pre-position | PP | Ineffective |
| 10 | Release | RL | Effective |
| 11 | Assemble | A | Effective |
| 12 | Disassemble | DA | Effective |
| 13 | Use | U | Effective |
| 14 | Inspect | I | Ineffective |
| 15 | Unavoidable Delay | UD | Ineffective |
| 16 | Avoidable Delay | AD | Ineffective |
| 17 | Plan | Pn | Ineffective |
| 18 | Rest | R | Ineffective |

**Effective Therbligs (7):** Grasp, Transport Loaded, Position, Assemble, Disassemble, Use, Release — these add value.

**Ineffective Therbligs (11):** All others — these are targets for **elimination or reduction** during methods improvement.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Layout of Element Breakdown Sheet:**

*(Draw the table from Section 4.4 above with columns: SN, Description, Type, $T_o$, $R\%$, $T_n$, $A_f$, $T_s$.)*

**Use in Standard Data Preparation:**

1. **Element-wise time collection** – Each element's time is recorded cycle after cycle to obtain reliable averages.
2. **Rating application** – Each element is rated separately because the operator's effort differs across elements (e.g., a heavy lift gets higher rating than a light tap).
3. **Building element standard** – Once enough data are accumulated, an *element standard time* is fixed for that specific element.
4. **Reuse across jobs** – Element standards for common operations (e.g., "Pick up bolt from bin") become part of the **Standard Data Bank**.
5. **Synthesis for new jobs** – When a new job is designed, its standard time is built up by combining pre-existing element standards.
6. **Productivity control** – Standard data enables quick estimation of output, cost, and incentive wage without fresh time study.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Conflating Average Time with Normal Time** – Average = mean of readings; Normal = Average × Rating. Showing $T_n = \frac{\sum T_o}{n}$ without rating → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert percentage rating to decimal** – Writing $0.18 \times 110$ instead of $0.18 \times 1.10$ → **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Adding Allowance instead of multiplying** – Writing $T_s = T_n + A_f$ → **−2 marks**. Correct is $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A_f)$.
> 4. **Mixing manual and machine elements in one row** – Examiners expect separation.
> 5. **Not showing unit** – Always write **min** or **pieces/shift** to the right of every numerical answer.
> 6. **Skipping the element classification step** – Many students jump directly to time calculation. KTU board awards 2–3 marks for **explicit classification** (repetitive/occasional, manual/machine).
> 7. **Not labelling the End Points** – At least 2 end points must be mentioned in the procedure question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Work Element** = smallest measurable subdivision of a job with clear end points.
- **8 Golden Rules**: clear end points, short, manual/machine separated, constant/variable separated, rating element-wise, foreign elements separated.
- **Element Types:** Repetitive vs Occasional; Manual vs Machine vs Mixed; Constant vs Variable; Regular vs Foreign.
- **Therbligs = 18 basic motions** (Gilbreth); 7 Effective + 11 Ineffective.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $T_n = T_o \times R$
  - $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A_f)$
  - $N = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{T_s}$
- **Element Breakdown Sheet** = standard 8-column format: SN | Description | Type | $T_o$ | $R\%$ | $T_n$ | $A_f$ | $T_s$.
- **Standard Data** is built by accumulating element-wise times from many studies.
- **End Point** = a definite, observable event marking the boundary between two elements.
- **Foreign Elements** must NOT be added to standard cycle time — they are treated separately.
- **Manual elements are rated; machine elements are not** (rating factor = 100% for pure machine time).
- **For KTU exams:** always convert rating % to decimal; always show units; always draw the breakdown sheet with headings.
- **Real-world link:** every industrial engineering technique — Time Study, PMTS (MTM, MOST), Work Sampling, Standard Data, Incentive Wage — depends on proper element breakdown.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Sample Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Determination of Sample Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Work System Design** and **Work Measurement** (a core competency under Industrial Engineering), **Determination of Sample Size** refers to the statistically rigorous procedure used to calculate the minimum number of observations (n) required from a population or process so that the resulting sample statistic (mean, proportion, or standard deviation) represents the true population parameter within a pre-specified margin of error, at a stated **confidence level**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCIET403 – Work System Design), this topic is principally applied to:
- **Time Study observations** (stopwatch time study of manual/operator cycles).
- **Work Sampling studies** (occurrence rate / activity sampling).
- **Performance Rating normalization**.
- **Acceptance Sampling Plans** (Quality Assurance modules).

The sample size **n** is a direct function of four master variables: the population's variability (σ), the desired **confidence level** (Z), the tolerable **error / precision** (E or d), and in some cases, the population size (N).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Sample size is the minimum count of independent, random observations required to estimate an unknown population parameter (μ or p) such that the sampling error does not exceed a stipulated limit, with a specified probability of assurance (confidence coefficient).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the head chef of a large restaurant kitchen, and you want to know the **average time** it takes your line cooks to prepare a "Special Biriyani Plate". You cannot possibly stand and time every single plate made in a day (that would be the **census / 100% study**). Instead, you randomly time **a few plates** and from those few, you want to confidently state the average time for *all* plates.

**Three real-world questions dictate how many plates you should time:**

1. **How variable is the cooking time?** (Standard Deviation, σ) — If your cooks are highly consistent (like a machine), you need few plates. If they are wildly inconsistent, you need many.
2. **How sure do you want to be?** (Confidence Level, Z) — Do you want to be 90% sure, or 99% sure? Higher certainty needs more plates.
3. **How narrow an error band can you tolerate?** (Precision, E) — Is it okay if your estimate is off by ±2 minutes, or do you need it precise within ±30 seconds? Tighter precision needs more plates.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of sample size as the **"statistical insurance premium"** you pay to guard against being wrong. Higher insurance (confidence) and tighter accuracy (lower E) increase the premium (larger n).

## 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (As Per KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Parameter | Standard Value (Industrial Use) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Z** | Standard Normal Variate | **1.96** (95% CL), **2.58** (99% CL), **2.00** (95.45% CL) |
| **σ / s** | Standard Deviation | Computed from pilot study |
| **E / e** | Absolute / Relative Error | Specified by analyst |
| **n** | Sample Size | Output of the formula |
| **α** | Significance Level | 0.05 (95%) or 0.01 (99%) |
| **N** | Population Size | Finite correction applied when n/N > 5% |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a KTU question on this topic is asked, immediately write the four variables (Z, σ, E, n) and state the relationship **n ∝ Z²·σ² / E²**. This alone fetches 2 marks in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Logic of Sampling

When we draw a random sample of size **n** from a population, the **sampling distribution of the mean** (X̄) has the following properties (Central Limit Theorem applied to work measurement):

- **Mean of X̄** = Population Mean (μ)
- **Standard Error of X̄** = σ / √n

This means: as **n increases**, the standard error **decreases** (the sample mean gets closer to the true mean). However, the decrease is proportional to **√n**, not n. So to **halve** the error, you must **quadruple** the sample size — a critical insight for industrial engineers balancing study cost versus statistical accuracy.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step by Step

The fundamental probability statement is:

$$P\left( \left| \bar{X} - \mu \right| \le E \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

This means: *"The probability that the sample mean X̄ deviates from the true mean μ by no more than E is equal to our confidence level (1 − α)."*

By the Central Limit Theorem, the standardized form is:

$$P\left( \left| Z \right| \le \frac{E}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

This leads to the **core sample size equation** (Case I – Mean, Infinite Population):

$$n = \left( \frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$

The **finite population correction** is applied when the sample is more than **5%** of the population (Case II):

$$n_{adj} = \frac{n}{1 + \frac{n-1}{N}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Scenario | Formula | Variables | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Mean — Infinite Population** | $n = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | Z, σ, E | Most common KTU form |
| 2 | **Mean — Relative Precision** | $n = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot s}{e \cdot \bar{X}}\right)^2$ | Z, s, e, X̄ | e = relative error (decimal) |
| 3 | **Mean — Finite Population** | $n_{adj} = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}$ | n₀, N | Used when n/N > 0.05 |
| 4 | **Proportion — Infinite** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$ | Z, p, E | For attribute / work sampling |
| 5 | **Proportion — No Prior p** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2}{4 E^2}$ | Z, E | Conservative (p = 0.5) |
| 6 | **Sample Size for Standard Deviation** | $n = 1 + \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E_s}\right)^2$ | Z, σ, Es | Estimates σ within Es |
| 7 | **Time Study (Niebel) — Initial Study** | $n' = \left(\dfrac{40 \sqrt{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}}{\sum X}\right)^2$ | Pilot n, ΣX, ΣX² | Used when σ is unknown initially |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for Work Sampling (proportion studies):** If the activity percentage is unknown, always use **p = 0.5**. This gives the **maximum** sample size and thus the most conservative (safest) estimate.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

In **Work System Design**, a wrongly determined sample size has two extremes of consequence:

- **Under-sampling** (n too small) → Inflated sampling error → Inaccurate standard times → **Inflated labor costs** or **underbid contracts** for the firm.
- **Over-sampling** (n too large) → Wasted engineering man-hours, delayed method standardization, and **inflated consulting costs**.

The KTU curriculum stresses **economy of study** — getting the *minimum* n that still satisfies the precision and confidence demands. This is a recurring application in:

1. **Stopwatch Time Study** (PCIET403 Module 3 core).
2. **Work Sampling** (Module 3 / Module 4 typical extension).
3. **Standard Data Development** (Module 4 / 5).
4. **Acceptance Sampling by Attributes** (Quality core).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Case I: Mean, Infinite Population

We begin with the sampling statement:

$$P\left( \left| \bar{X} - \mu \right| \le E \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Standardize using the substitution $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$:

$$P\left( -Z_{\alpha/2} \le \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \le Z_{\alpha/2} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Multiply every term inside the inequality by the standard error $\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$:

$$P\left( -Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le \bar{X} - \mu \le Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Set the **precision E** equal to the maximum deviation:

$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Now, **solve for n** explicitly:

$$E \cdot \sqrt{n} = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma$$

Square both sides to remove the square root:

$$E^2 \cdot n = Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \sigma^2$$

$$n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}$$

$$\boxed{n = \left( \frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2}$$

This is the **master equation** for KTU board examinations on this topic.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Case II: Finite Population Correction

When the population is small and **n/N > 0.05**, the standard error of the mean becomes:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N - n}{N}}$$

Substitute this refined standard error into the precision equation:

$$E = Z \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N - n}{N}}$$

Square both sides:

$$E^2 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N - n}{N}$$

Rearrange to isolate terms with n:

$$E^2 \cdot N \cdot n = Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot (N - n)$$

$$E^2 \cdot N \cdot n = Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot N - Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot n$$

Collect n terms on the left:

$$n \cdot (E^2 \cdot N + Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2) = Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot N$$

$$n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot N}{E^2 \cdot N + Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $E^2$:

$$n = \frac{\dfrac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot N}{E^2}}{N + \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}}$$

Recognizing that $n_0 = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}$:

$$\boxed{n_{adj} = \frac{n_0 \cdot N}{N + n_0 - 1} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad n_{adj} = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Case III: Proportion (Attribute / Work Sampling)

For binomial data (success/failure, occurrence/non-occurrence), the standard error uses **p(1−p)**:

$$E = Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

Square both sides:

$$E^2 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{n}$$

Solve for n:

$$\boxed{n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}}$$

When **p is unknown** (no pilot study), the maximum value of $p(1-p) = 0.25$ (at $p = 0.5$) is used as a conservative estimator:

$$\boxed{n = \frac{Z^2}{4 E^2}}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (For Analytical Clarity)

> **Problem:** A time study analyst takes a pilot of 10 observations of a manual assembly cycle. The pilot data gives ΣX = 80 sec and ΣX² = 660 sec². She needs 95% confidence and ±5% relative precision. Find the required sample size.

**Step 1 — Compute pilot mean and standard deviation:**

$$\bar{X}_{pilot} = \frac{\sum X}{n_{pilot}} = \frac{80}{10} = 8 \text{ sec}$$

$$\sigma_{pilot} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum X^2}{n_{pilot}} - \bar{X}_{pilot}^2} = \sqrt{\frac{660}{10} - 8^2} = \sqrt{66 - 64} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the parameters:**

- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence, two-tailed)
- $\sigma = 1.414$ sec
- $e = 0.05$ (5% relative precision)
- $\bar{X} = 8$ sec

**Step 3 — Apply the relative precision formula:**

$$n = \left( \frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{e \cdot \bar{X}} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 1.414}{0.05 \times 8} \right)^2$$

$$n = \left( \frac{2.7714}{0.4} \right)^2 = (6.9285)^2 \approx 48.0$$

**Step 4 — Round up (always round UP, never down):**

$$\boxed{n = 49 \text{ observations}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| **Condition** | **Formula to Use** | **Round-off Rule** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| σ known, infinite N, absolute error | $n = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | Always round UP |
| σ known, infinite N, relative error | $n = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma}{e \cdot \bar{X}}\right)^2$ | Always round UP |
| σ known, finite N | $n = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}$ | Always round UP |
| Attribute data, p known | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$ | Always round UP |
| Attribute data, p unknown | $n = \dfrac{Z^2}{4 E^2}$ | Always round UP |
| σ unknown (pilot only) | $n' = \left(\dfrac{40 \sqrt{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}}{\sum X}\right)^2$ | Niebel's shortcut (95% CL) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the **decision flow** an Industrial Engineer must follow when determining sample size, depending on data type, population size, and pilot information available.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Need to Determine Sample Size n] --> startB{What type of data?}
    startB -->|Variable/Continuous Time Data| pathC[Time Study or Measurement Study]
    startB -->|Attribute/Proportion Data| pathD[Work Sampling or Quality Inspection]

    pathC --> q1{Pilot study done or sigma known?}
    q1 -->|Yes, sigma known| calcA1[Use n = Z sigma divided by E squared]
    q1 -->|No, pilot done only| calcA2[Compute sigma from pilot data]
    calcA2 --> q2{Error specified as absolute or relative?}
    q2 -->|Absolute E in same units| calcA3[Use n = Z sigma divided by E squared]
    q2 -->|Relative e as percent| calcA4[Use n = Z sigma divided by e times Xbar squared]

    pathD --> q3{Prior estimate of proportion p available?}
    q3 -->|Yes, p known| calcD1[Use n = Z squared times p times 1-p divided by E squared]
    q3 -->|No, p unknown| calcD2[Conservative Use p equals 0.5]
    calcD2 --> calcD1

    calcA1 --> q4{Is sample more than 5 percent of population N?}
    calcA3 --> q4
    calcA4 --> q4
    calcD1 --> q4

    q4 -->|Yes, Finite Population| calcFPC[Apply FPC: n adjusted equals n0 divided by 1 plus n0 minus 1 over N]
    q4 -->|No, Infinite Population OK| noFPC[Use n as calculated]

    calcFPC --> roundStep
    noFPC --> roundStep
    calcA1 --> roundStep

    roundStep[Final Step: Round UP to next integer. That is the required sample size n.]
    roundStep --> endA[Output: Sample size n ready for field data collection]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Compilation — Inputs to Output Relationship Map

The diagram below shows the **four-metric dependency** of the sample size n on its governing statistical parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    z1[Confidence Level Z] -->|increases| n1[Sample Size n]
    s1[Standard Deviation sigma] -->|increases| n1
    e1[Precision Error E] -->|decreases| n1
    p1[Population Proportion p] -->|approaches 0.5| n1
    bigN[Population Size N] -->|finite correction| n2[Adjusted Sample Size n_adj]

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> outF[Final n for Work Study]

    style z1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style s1 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style e1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style p1 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style bigN fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style n1 fill:#fcd34d,color:#000
    style n2 fill:#fcd34d,color:#000
    style outF fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree Summary (Textual Reference)

> **Step 1:** Identify the data type → **Variable (time)** or **Attribute (proportion)**.
> **Step 2:** Identify whether **σ or p** is known (from prior data, standard data, or pilot study).
> **Step 3:** Identify the **error specification** → absolute E or relative e.
> **Step 4:** Identify the **population size** → infinite or finite.
> **Step 5:** Apply the appropriate formula from the matrix in Section 3.5.
> **Step 6:** **Round UP** the calculated n. This is non-negotiable in KTU valuation.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "Sample Size" in the context of Work Study. State the factors on which it depends. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Sample Size (n)** is the minimum number of observations required from a population to estimate a parameter (mean or proportion) within a specified **precision (E)** at a given **confidence level (1−α)**.
It depends on four factors:
1. **Confidence level (Z)** — higher confidence → larger n.
2. **Standard deviation (σ)** — greater variability → larger n.
3. **Allowable error (E)** — tighter precision → larger n.
4. **Population size (N)** — affects n when N is small (finite population correction).

### Q2. State the formula for sample size when the standard deviation is unknown and no prior estimate of proportion exists. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When the standard deviation σ is unknown and the population proportion p is unknown, the **conservative formula** is used:
$$n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$$
With no prior information on p, the maximum value of $p(1-p) = 0.25$ (at $p = 0.5$) is substituted, giving:
$$n = \frac{Z^2}{4 E^2}$$
For 95% confidence ($Z = 1.96$) and ±5% precision ($E = 0.05$):
$$n = \frac{(1.96)^2}{4 \times (0.05)^2} = \frac{3.8416}{0.01} \approx 385 \text{ observations}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** A time study analyst conducts a pilot study of 12 observations for a manual operation. The data yields ΣX = 144 sec and ΣX² = 1788 sec². Determine the sample size required for **95% confidence** and an **absolute precision of ±2 seconds**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the effect of changing the confidence level from 95% to 99% on the required sample size. Use numerical justification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Calculate pilot mean and standard deviation.**

$$\bar{X}_{pilot} = \frac{\sum X}{n_{pilot}} = \frac{144}{12} = 12 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\sigma_{pilot} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum X^2}{n_{pilot}} - \bar{X}_{pilot}^2} = \sqrt{\frac{1788}{12} - 12^2} = \sqrt{149 - 144} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 2: Identify parameters.**
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence)
- $\sigma = 2.236$ sec
- $E = 2$ sec (absolute precision)

**Step 3: Apply the sample size formula.**

$$n = \left( \frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 2.236}{2} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{4.3826}{2} \right)^2 = (2.1913)^2 \approx 4.80$$

**Step 4: Round up.**

$$\boxed{n = 5 \text{ observations}}$$

> **Award: 1 mark (formula), 2 marks (pilot statistics), 2 marks (substitution), 1 mark (final round-up), 1 mark (unit/justification).**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: State the general principle.**
Sample size is directly proportional to the **square** of Z:
$$n \propto Z^2$$

**Step 2: Recompute n for 99% confidence.**
For 99% confidence, $Z = 2.576$ (≈ 2.58). Using the same σ and E:

$$n_{99} = \left( \frac{2.576 \times 2.236}{2} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{5.8406}{2} \right)^2 = (2.9203)^2 \approx 8.53$$

$$\boxed{n_{99} = 9 \text{ observations}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{n_{99}}{n_{95}} = \left( \frac{2.576}{1.96} \right)^2 = (1.314)^2 \approx 1.73$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (with engineering interpretation).**
Increasing the confidence level from 95% to 99% increases the required sample size by approximately **73%** (from 5 to 9 observations). This is a substantial increase in study effort. **Engineers must weigh the marginal statistical gain in confidence against the cost of additional observations**, a classical economy-of-study trade-off emphasized in KTU 2024 Module 3.

> **Award: 2 marks (n ∝ Z²), 2 marks (recomputation), 1 mark (ratio), 2 marks (engineering interpretation).**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** A work sampling study is planned to estimate the proportion of time a machine is **idle**. Prior records indicate the machine is idle approximately **30%** of the time. Calculate the number of random observations required for **95% confidence** with an **absolute precision of ±4%**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the production manager insists on a **±2%** precision (instead of ±4%) at the same confidence level, how many additional observations are required? Comment on the feasibility. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence)
- $p = 0.30$ (idle proportion)
- $q = 1 - p = 0.70$
- $E = 0.04$ (4% absolute precision)

**Step 2: Apply the proportion formula.**

$$n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{E^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.04)^2}$$

$$n = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.21}{0.0016} = \frac{0.8067}{0.0016} \approx 504.2$$

**Step 3: Round up.**

$$\boxed{n = 505 \text{ observations}}$$

> **Award: 1 mark (formula selection), 2 marks (substitution), 2 marks (calculation), 1 mark (rounding), 1 mark (comment on conservatism).**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Recompute n with E = 0.02.**

$$n = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.02)^2} = \frac{0.8067}{0.0004} = 2016.75$$

**Step 2: Round up.**

$$\boxed{n = 2017 \text{ observations}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the additional observations needed.**

$$\Delta n = 2017 - 505 = 1512 \text{ additional observations}$$

**Step 4: Feasibility analysis.**
The precision was tightened by a factor of 2, but the sample size increased by a factor of **4** (since n ∝ 1/E²). This **quadratic relationship** means that even small improvements in precision demand disproportionately large increases in data collection effort. In practice, the manager must evaluate:
- The **cost of the extra 1512 observations** (in analyst man-hours).
- The **consequence of being wrong** by ±4% vs. ±2%.
- Whether **stratified sampling** or **focused observation windows** could reduce the required n.

A typical KTU-acceptable verdict: *"Tightening precision from ±4% to ±2% is rarely economically justified in work sampling; ±5% to ±10% is the industrial norm."*

> **Award: 2 marks (recomputation), 2 marks (difference), 1 mark (quadratic relationship), 2 marks (feasibility discussion).**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** — never write n = 4.8 and leave it. Always write n = 5. (−1 mark)
> 2. **Mixing up Z values** — Z = 1.96 is for 95% confidence (two-tailed). Z = 2.58 is for 99%. Confusing these gives wrong n. (−2 marks)
> 3. **Using absolute error formula when relative error is given** — read the question carefully. If precision is "5%" it is **relative**, so use $e \cdot \bar{X}$ in the denominator. (−2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting finite population correction** — when N is small and given, examiners expect the FPC step. (−1 to −2 marks)
> 5. **Using $p(1-p) = 0.25$ even when p is provided** — this is incorrect if a prior p is given. Use the actual p.
> 6. **Not stating units** — write "n = 5 observations" or "n = 505 observations" — not just "n = 5". (−0.5 mark)
> 7. **Skipping the pilot study computation** when ΣX and ΣX² are given — examiners expect σ derived from pilot. (−2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sample size n** = minimum number of observations needed to estimate a population parameter within a specified precision at a given confidence level.
- The **master equation** is: $n = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ for variable data (infinite population).
- For **proportion data**: $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$.
- For **finite population**: apply the correction $n_{adj} = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}$ when n/N > 5%.
- Sample size is **proportional to Z²** (square of confidence coefficient).
- Sample size is **proportional to σ²** (square of variability).
- Sample size is **inversely proportional to E²** (square of precision).
- Always **round UP** the calculated n to the next whole integer.
- For **unknown p** (no pilot), use the **conservative value p = 0.5**, which maximizes n.
- The relationship between precision and n is **quadratic**: halving E quadruples n.
- The relationship between confidence and n is also **quadratic**: small Z-changes cause large n-changes.
- The **Niebel shortcut formula** (40 · √(nΣX² − (ΣX)²) / ΣX)² is used when σ is unknown and a pilot of 5–10 readings is available.
- For **work sampling** (PCIET403 Module 3), the conservative formula $n = Z^2 / 4E^2$ is frequently the default starting point.
- Industrial norm for work sampling precision: **±5% to ±10%** (absolute).
- Industrial norm for time study precision: **±5% to ±10%** (relative).
- Always **state the assumptions**: random sampling, independence of observations, approximate normality of the sampling distribution.
- A **pilot study of 5–10 observations** is almost always recommended before committing to a full study in KTU board problems.
- The **cost-precision trade-off** is a recurring concept — examiners love questions asking "comment on feasibility" or "justify the sample size economically".

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Rating Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Rating Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Performance Rating** is a work measurement technique in which a qualified industrial engineer (the *rater*) evaluates the worker's demonstrated performance during a time study and expresses it as a percentage of standard performance. The **Performance Rating Factor (PRF)**, also called the **Rating (R)**, is then multiplied with the **Observed Time (OT)** to obtain the **Normal Time (NT)** for the task.

Mathematically, the foundational relation is:

$$\text{Normal Time (NT)} = \text{Observed Time (OT)} \times \text{Performance Rating Factor (R)}$$

The **Standard Performance** is universally defined as the pace at which a **qualified worker**, working under **specified conditions**, performs a task with **average skill and effort** without any physical or mental strain. On the conventional **100-Point Rating Scale**, this standard is anchored at the value **R = 100**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** Performance Rating is a *judgment-based* adjustment, not a measurement. The accuracy of work measurement output (Standard Time) is directly dependent on the accuracy of the rating, which depends on **rater skill, training, and consistency**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a 100-metre sprint. A runner finishes in 11 seconds. Is that "fast" or "slow"? You cannot tell unless you know what the **standard sprint** for that athlete should be. Now suppose the standard for an average trained sprinter is 10 seconds. Then this runner's pace is roughly **91%** of the standard.

**Performance Rating is the industrial engineer's "judging stopwatch" for pace.** Instead of timing an athlete, the engineer watches a worker perform a job and asks: *"How fast is this person working compared to a standard, qualified worker?"* If the answer is "90% as fast as standard," then the rating is **R = 90**. The observed time is then mathematically "leveled" upward to what a standard worker would have taken.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember:**
> - **Observed Time (OT)** = Actual clock time recorded during the study.
> - **Normal Time (NT)** = Time a *standard* worker would take at the observed pace. (OT × R)
> - **Standard Time (ST)** = Normal Time plus **Allowances** for personal, fatigue, and contingencies. (NT × (1+A))

## 1.3 Why Performance Rating Exists

In any workplace, two operators will never work at the *exact* same pace. Some work slower (novice), some faster (highly skilled), and some fluctuate (inconsistent). To establish a **fair, equitable, and consistent** production standard, we must "normalize" all observed times to a common reference — the standard pace. This is the only way:
- Wage incentives remain fair.
- Production planning uses realistic time estimates.
- Workforce capacity calculations are accurate.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Performance Rating

| Metric | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal Pace (BPM)** | **100 BPM** (footsteps/min for a walker) | Beats per minute |
| **Standard Rating (R)** | **100** | Dimensionless index |
| **Bedaux Scale (B)** | **60 BPM** = 100% | BPM |
| **Daywork Scale (B)** | **133 BPM** = 100% | BPM |
| **Basic Time Index** | **60 TMU = 1 second** | TMU (Methods-Time Measurement) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bedaux System (B):** 1 Point (B) = 0.6 minutes of working time including rest. A standard pace of 60 BPM corresponds to rating 100.
> **Daywork System:** 1 D = 1/480 hours. Standard pace of 133 BPM corresponds to rating 100.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition — The Performance Rating Pipeline

Performance Rating is not a single step. It is a four-stage judgment process where the rater must independently evaluate four sub-components of the worker's behavior:

1. **Skill (S):** The proficiency with which the worker follows the prescribed method. Ranges from *Super-Skilled* to *Poor*.
2. **Effort (E):** The degree of physical and mental exertion voluntarily applied. Ranges from *Excessive* to *Poor*.
3. **Conditions (C):** The workplace environment — lighting, temperature, noise, ventilation. Ranges from *Ideal* to *Very Bad*.
4. **Consistency (I):** The repeatability of the worker's pace within the study cycle. Ranges from *Perfect* to *Very Poor*.

These four factors are the foundation of the most widely taught system: the **Westinghouse Method**.

## 2.2 The Six Major Performance Rating Methods

### Method 1 — Speed Rating (The Traditional 100-Point Method)
- **Concept:** The rater assigns a single number (e.g., 85, 100, 115) representing the worker's overall speed.
- **Standard:** 100 = Normal pace.
- **Use:** Quick, simple, but highly prone to **rater bias**.

### Method 2 — Westinghouse Rating System (4-Factor Method)
- **Concept:** Decomposes rating into Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency, each given a numerical value (positive or negative). The **Rating = 100 + S + E + C + I**.
- **Use:** Most academically tested; preferred in KTU 2024 Scheme board exams.

### Method 3 — Objective Rating (Gilbreth Bedaux Method)
- **Concept:** A standardized motion picture of a standard pace is shown to the rater before the study. The rater visually compares the worker's motion to the reference.
- **Use:** Reduces bias, but requires training films and equipment.

### Method 4 — Synthetic Rating
- **Concept:** Standard times for elements are taken from **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** like Methods-Time Measurement (MTM) or Work Factor. The synthetic rating is computed as:

$$R_{synthetic} = \frac{\text{Standard Time from PMTS}}{\text{Observed Time}} \times 100$$

- **Use:** Eliminates subjective judgment entirely.

### Method 5 — Physiological Rating
- **Concept:** Worker effort is measured via **heart rate, oxygen consumption, or respiration rate**. Standard pulse (~97 BPM) corresponds to rating 100.
- **Use:** Research and very heavy work; rarely used in industry.

### Method 6 — Percentage Rating
- **Concept:** Like speed rating, but rater directly assigns a percentage (e.g., 87%) without anchoring to a strict scale.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Normal Time** | $NT = OT \times R \div 100$ | Convert observed time to standard pace |
| 2 | **Standard Time** | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | Add allowance factor (A as decimal) |
| 3 | **Westinghouse Total Rating** | $R = 100 + S + E + C + I$ | Sum of four factor sub-ratings |
| 4 | **Synthetic Rating** | $R_{syn} = (T_{std} / OT) \times 100$ | Rating from PMTS standards |
| 5 | **Bedaux Conversion** | $R_{Bedaux} = (B / 60) \times 100$ | Convert Bedaux points to % |
| 6 | **Daywork Conversion** | $R_{Daywork} = (B / 133) \times 100$ | Convert Daywork BPM to % |
| 7 | **Allowance Factor** | $A = A_{personal} + A_{fatigue} + A_{contingency}$ | Decoded relaxation factor |
| 8 | **Mean Performance Rating** | $\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_{i}}{n}$ | Average rating over n cycles |
| 9 | **Rating Accuracy Index** | $ARI = \sigma_R / \bar{R}$ | Measure rater consistency (lower is better) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** In board exams, the rating value `R` is often given as a number like `115` (meaning 115%). In some textbooks, it is given as a decimal `1.15`. Always check the convention used in the problem. If `R = 115` and the formula is `NT = OT × R`, the result is wrong by a factor of 100. The correct form is either `NT = OT × (R/100)` or `NT = OT × R` where `R = 1.15`.

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

Performance Rating is the *economic engine* of time study. In:
- **Wage Incentive Systems** (Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, Gantt): Rating directly determines the bonus paid.
- **Lean Manufacturing:** Standard time feeds into *takt time* and *line balancing*.
- **Ergonomics & Safety:** Highly inflated ratings (R > 130) indicate **unrealistic pacing**, which leads to musculoskeletal disorders.
- **Capacity Planning:** Bottleneck identification and worker assignment depend on accurate, normalized times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Trend:** Many KTU-aligned industries now prefer **PMTS (Predetermined Motion Time Systems)** like MTM-1, MTM-2, MOST, or BasicMOST, which make rating obsolete. However, KTU 2024 scheme still emphasizes classical rating for *work system design* courses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Observed Time to Standard Time (Complete Chain)

The work measurement pipeline is mathematical, with rating as the critical normalization step.

### Step 1: Record Observed Time (OT)
The rater uses a stopwatch to time `n` repetitions (cycles) of the task. Let the recorded times be $T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n$.

The **mean observed time** is:

$$\bar{T}_{obs} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

### Step 2: Apply Performance Rating to Obtain Normal Time
The rater evaluates the worker's pace and assigns rating $R$. The normal time accounts for the fact that the observed worker is not at standard pace:

$$NT = \bar{T}_{obs} \times \frac{R}{100}$$

### Step 3: Apply Allowances to Obtain Standard Time
A normal worker cannot sustain 100% effort for the entire shift. Allowances are added:

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A)$$

where $A = A_{personal} + A_{fatigue} + A_{contingency}$.

### Step 4: Full Chain Equation (Final Form)

$$ST = \bar{T}_{obs} \times \frac{R}{100} \times (1 + A)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** OT → NT scales the time to what a *standard* worker would have taken at the worker's pace. NT → ST adds rest allowances so the *standard worker* can be scheduled for an 8-hour shift without exhaustion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Westinghouse 4-Factor Rating (Detailed Breakdown)

The Westinghouse system assigns sub-ratings from standard tables. The total rating is the sum of the four sub-ratings, added to the base of 100.

$$R_{total} = 100 + R_{skill} + R_{effort} + R_{conditions} + R_{consistency}$$

### Sample Westinghouse Sub-Rating Tables (Excerpt for KTU Reference)

**Skill (S):**
| Classification | Degree | Rating Adjustment |
|---|---|---|
| Super-Skilled | A1 | $+0.15$ |
|  | A2 | $+0.13$ |
| Excellent | B1 | $+0.11$ |
|  | B2 | $+0.08$ |
| Good | C1 | $+0.06$ |
|  | C2 | $+0.03$ |
| Average | D | $0.00$ |
| Poor | E1 | $-0.05$ |
|  | E2 | $-0.10$ |

**Effort (E):**
| Classification | Degree | Rating Adjustment |
|---|---|---|
| Excessive | A1 | $+0.13$ |
|  | A2 | $+0.12$ |
| Excellent | B1 | $+0.10$ |
|  | B2 | $+0.08$ |
| Good | C1 | $+0.05$ |
|  | C2 | $+0.02$ |
| Average | D | $0.00$ |
| Poor | E1 | $-0.04$ |
|  | E2 | $-0.08$ |

**Conditions (C):** Ideal = $0.00$, Good = $+0.02$, Fair = $-0.03$, Poor = $-0.07$.
**Consistency (I):** Perfect = $+0.04$, Average = $0.00$, Poor = $-0.04$.

> **Conversion Logic:** The base of 100 represents "average on all four dimensions." Positive adjustments mean the worker exceeds the average on that dimension; negative means below average. The sum of all four gives the *relative performance index* of the worker.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A worker is observed for 5 cycles. Times: 1.20, 1.25, 1.18, 1.22, 1.15 minutes. The rater assigns Skill = +0.06, Effort = +0.05, Conditions = +0.02, Consistency = +0.04. Allowance factor = 15%. Calculate Standard Time.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate mean observed time.

$$\bar{T}_{obs} = \frac{1.20 + 1.25 + 1.18 + 1.22 + 1.15}{5} = \frac{6.00}{5} = 1.20 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2:** Compute Westinghouse rating.

$$R = 100 + 0.06 + 0.05 + 0.02 + 0.04 = 100.17$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Normal Time.

$$NT = 1.20 \times \frac{100.17}{100} = 1.20 \times 1.0017 = 1.20204 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4:** Apply 15% allowance.

$$ST = 1.20204 \times (1 + 0.15) = 1.20204 \times 1.15 = 1.38235 \text{ min}$$

**Result:** **Standard Time ≈ 1.38 minutes per cycle.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Synthetic Rating (PMTS Method)

When a Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS) like MTM gives a standard time $T_{std}$ for a task, but the actual worker took an observed time $T_{obs}$, the *implied* rating is:

$$R_{synthetic} = \frac{T_{std}}{T_{obs}} \times 100$$

**Example:** A worker took 2.50 minutes on a task. The MTM standard for the task is 2.00 minutes. Compute the synthetic rating.

**Solution:**

$$R_{synthetic} = \frac{2.00}{2.50} \times 100 = 80$$

**Interpretation:** The worker was working at **80% of standard pace**.

> **Conversion Logic:** If the worker took *longer* than the PMTS standard, the rating is *below* 100. If the worker took *less* time, the rating is *above* 100. This provides a check on subjective ratings.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Rating Methods

| Method | Basis | Bias Risk | Use Case | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed Rating** | Subjective pace judgment | Very High | Quick field estimates | Low |
| **Westinghouse** | 4-factor analysis | Moderate | Academic, structured study | **Very High** |
| **Objective Rating** | Visual comparison to film | Moderate-Low | Trained raters, cycle work | Medium |
| **Synthetic Rating** | PMTS calculation | **None** | Engineering offices, MTM users | **High** |
| **Physiological** | Heart rate, oxygen | Low (instrumental) | Research, heavy labor | Low |
| **Percentage Rating** | Direct % judgment | High | Informal | Low |

## 3.5 Common Rating Errors (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Error | Definition | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Halo Effect** | Rater's overall impression (good/bad worker) bleeds into rating | Rate element-by-element |
