| **Central Tendency** | Rater avoids extremes, clustering near 100 | Use reference films |
| **Recency Effect** | Most recent cycle influences rating disproportionately | Rate every cycle |
| **Contrast Effect** | Comparing current worker to previous, not to standard | Reset between workers |
| **Expectancy Bias** | Knowing job standard *before* observing influences rating | Hide standard from rater |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Synthetic Rating is considered the *gold standard* for accuracy but is rarely used in classical time study because PMTS standards are expensive to develop. For KTU board exams, **Westinghouse + Speed Rating** are the most heavily tested.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Performance Rating in the Time Study Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start Time Study] --> nodeB[Observe Worker & Record Times T1...Tn]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Mean Observed Time OT_bar]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Evaluate Worker Performance]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Select Rating Method}
    nodeE -->|Speed| nodeF[Assign Single R Value]
    nodeE -->|Westinghouse| nodeG[Evaluate S, E, C, I Factors]
    nodeE -->|Synthetic| nodeH[Use PMTS Standard Time]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Sum R = 100 + S + E + C + I]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Compute Normal Time NT = OT x R/100]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK[Compute Synthetic R = Tstd/OT x 100]
    nodeK --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Add Allowances A]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Compute Standard Time ST = NT x 1+A]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Output Final Standard Time]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Use for Wage, Capacity, Planning]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Westinghouse 4-Factor Rating Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBase[Base Score: 100] --> nodeSum[(Total Rating R)]
    nodeS[Skill S] --> nodeSum
    nodeE[Effort E] --> nodeSum
    nodeC[Conditions C] --> nodeSum
    nodeI[Consistency I] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeNT[Normal Time]
    nodeOT[Observed Time] --> nodeNT
    nodeNT --> nodeAllow[Add Allowances A]
    nodeAllow --> nodeST[Standard Time]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparative Block Architecture of Rating Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[Performance Rating Systems] --> nodeGroup1[Subjective Methods]
    nodeRoot --> nodeGroup2[Objective Methods]
    nodeGroup1 --> nodeSub1[Speed Rating 100 Point]
    nodeGroup1 --> nodeSub2[Westinghouse 4 Factor]
    nodeGroup1 --> nodeSub3[Percentage Rating]
    nodeGroup1 --> nodeSub4[Bedaux Scale]
    nodeGroup1 --> nodeSub5[Daywork Scale]
    nodeGroup2 --> nodeObj1[Objective Rating Film Based]
    nodeGroup2 --> nodeObj2[Synthetic Rating PMTS Based]
    nodeGroup2 --> nodeObj3[Physiological Rating]
    nodeSub2 --> nodeEx1[Skill S]
    nodeSub2 --> nodeEx2[Effort E]
    nodeSub2 --> nodeEx3[Conditions C]
    nodeSub2 --> nodeEx4[Consistency I]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Rater Decision Flow in Westinghouse Method

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Rater as Industrial Engineer
    participant Worker as Operator
    participant Film as Reference Film
    participant Output as Standard Time
    Rater->>Film: Review standard pace reference
    Rater->>Worker: Observe task execution
    Rater->>Rater: Evaluate Skill Level
    Rater->>Rater: Evaluate Effort Applied
    Rater->>Rater: Evaluate Working Conditions
    Rater->>Rater: Evaluate Pace Consistency
    Rater->>Output: Sum Sub-Ratings plus Base 100
    Output->>Output: Compute Normal and Standard Time
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - **Flowchart 4.1:** Shows the full path from raw observation to standard time, including all three major rating routes.
> - **Flowchart 4.2:** Visualizes how the four Westinghouse factors combine into the final rating.
> - **Flowchart 4.3:** Maps the complete taxonomy of rating systems used in industrial engineering.
> - **Sequence 4.4:** Walks through the cognitive workflow of a rater using the Westinghouse method.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]
**Define Performance Rating. Why is it considered the most critical step in time study?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Performance Rating is the process of evaluating a worker's pace during a time study and expressing it as a percentage of standard performance, where **100% represents the pace of a qualified worker working at average skill and effort under specified conditions**. It is considered the most critical step because the accuracy of the resulting Standard Time depends entirely on the rater's judgment. All downstream decisions — wage incentives, production targets, capacity planning — flow from this single subjective evaluation. A 10% rating error translates directly to a 10% error in the standard time.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**List any three common errors made by raters during performance rating and explain how they can be minimized.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Halo Effect:** A general impression of the worker (good or bad) influences specific ratings. *Minimized by rating each element independently.*
2. **Central Tendency:** Rater avoids extreme values and clusters around 100. *Minimized by using standardized reference films.*
3. **Recency Effect:** The last observed cycle influences rating disproportionately. *Minimized by rating every cycle separately and averaging.*

(Alternatives accepted: Contrast Effect, Expectancy Bias — any 3 with brief mitigation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the **Westinghouse Performance Rating System** in detail. Discuss the four sub-factors and provide a sample calculation showing how the total rating is computed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A time study was conducted on a worker performing a machining operation. The observed times for 6 cycles (in minutes) are: 2.40, 2.35, 2.50, 2.45, 2.42, 2.48. The rater assigned a speed rating of **110**. The allowance factor is **20%**. Calculate the **Standard Time** for the operation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Westinghouse System:**

The Westinghouse system is a four-factor rating method that breaks the worker's performance into independently evaluated sub-components. Each factor has standardized numerical values (positive, zero, or negative) which are summed and added to a base of 100.

**The Four Factors:**

1. **Skill (S):** Proficiency in following the prescribed method. Six degrees from Super-Skilled (A1) to Poor (E2), with adjustments from $+0.15$ to $-0.10$.

2. **Effort (E):** Willing application of physical and mental energy. Six degrees from Excessive (A1) to Poor (E2), with adjustments from $+0.13$ to $-0.08$.

3. **Conditions (C):** Quality of the work environment (lighting, temperature, ventilation, noise). Adjustments range from $+0.02$ (Good) to $-0.07$ (Poor).

4. **Consistency (I):** Repeatability of pace within and across cycles. Adjustments range from $+0.04$ (Perfect) to $-0.04$ (Very Poor).

**Total Rating Formula:**

$$R_{total} = 100 + S + E + C + I$$

**Sample Calculation:**
A worker demonstrates: Skill = +0.06 (Good C1), Effort = +0.05 (Good C1), Conditions = +0.02 (Good), Consistency = +0.04 (Perfect).

$$R_{total} = 100 + 0.06 + 0.05 + 0.02 + 0.04 = 100.17$$

The worker is working at **100.17%** of standard pace.

**Part (b) — Standard Time Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Compute mean observed time.

$$\bar{T}_{obs} = \frac{2.40 + 2.35 + 2.50 + 2.45 + 2.42 + 2.48}{6} = \frac{14.60}{6} = 2.4333 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2:** Apply rating (R = 110) to get Normal Time.

$$NT = 2.4333 \times \frac{110}{100} = 2.4333 \times 1.10 = 2.6766 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3:** Apply 20% allowance to get Standard Time.

$$ST = 2.6766 \times (1 + 0.20) = 2.6766 \times 1.20 = 3.2120 \text{ min}$$

**Final Answer:** **Standard Time ≈ 3.21 minutes per cycle.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Compare **Speed Rating**, **Westinghouse Rating**, and **Synthetic Rating** systems. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The standard time for an assembly task is given as **0.90 minutes** based on MTM analysis. A worker was observed and took **1.05 minutes** to complete the same task. Calculate the **synthetic rating** of the worker. If the personal, fatigue, and contingency allowances are 5%, 8%, and 3% respectively, and the worker performs 8 hours of work per shift, how many **standard hours** does this worker complete? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis:**

| Method | Basis | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed Rating** | Single number judgment of pace | Quick, simple, requires minimal training | Highly subjective, prone to bias |
| **Westinghouse** | 4-factor decomposition | Structured, transparent, academically rigorous | Time-consuming, requires expert rater |
| **Synthetic Rating** | Derived from PMTS standards | No subjective bias, mathematically exact | Requires PMTS database, expensive |

**Part (b) — Synthetic Rating and Standard Hours:**

**Step 1:** Calculate synthetic rating.

$$R_{syn} = \frac{T_{std}}{T_{obs}} \times 100 = \frac{0.90}{1.05} \times 100 = 85.71$$

The worker is operating at **~85.71% of standard pace**.

**Step 2:** Calculate total allowance factor.

$$A = 0.05 + 0.08 + 0.03 = 0.16 \quad (16\%)$$

**Step 3:** Calculate standard time for the task.

$$ST = 1.05 \times \frac{85.71}{100} \times (1 + 0.16) = 1.05 \times 0.8571 \times 1.16$$

$$ST = 0.90 \times 1.16 = 1.044 \text{ min per task}$$

(Verification: The synthetic rating *should* recover the PMTS standard of 0.90 min, and adding 16% allowance gives the final standard time.)

**Step 4:** Calculate number of tasks per 8-hour shift.

$$N_{tasks} = \frac{8 \times 60}{1.044} = \frac{480}{1.044} = 459.77 \approx 459 \text{ tasks}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate standard hours completed.

$$\text{Standard Hours} = N_{tasks} \times \frac{ST}{60} = 459 \times \frac{1.044}{60} = 7.99 \text{ standard hours}$$

**Final Answer:** Worker completes approximately **8 standard hours per 8-hour shift**, achieving **100% efficiency** (as expected, because synthetic rating calibrates the worker's pace to PMTS).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Rating Convention Trap:** If the rating is given as `110`, do *not* directly multiply observed time by 110. Use the fraction `R/100 = 1.10`. Many students write `NT = OT × R` and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Allowance Trap:** Allowance is *additive to the working time*, not part of the rating. Standard Time = NT × (1 + A), **not** NT × A. If the problem says "15% allowance," then `(1 + 0.15) = 1.15` is the multiplier.
> 3. **Synthetic Rating Direction:** Synthetic rating is `T_standard / T_observed`, **not** the reverse. Reversing it gives rating > 100 for a slow worker, which is logically wrong.
> 4. **Westinghouse Base:** Always remember the base is **100**, not 0. The four sub-ratings are *adjustments*, not absolute scores.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** If OT is in minutes, NT and ST are also in minutes. Convert to hours only at the final step for shift calculations.
> 6. **Forgetting Mean:** If multiple cycles are given, always take the *arithmetic mean* first, then apply rating. Applying rating to each cycle separately and averaging is mathematically equivalent but wastes time and increases error.
> 7. **Skill vs. Effort Confusion:** Skill is *ability* (the worker knows the method). Effort is *willingness to apply* that ability. A highly skilled worker can still have low effort, and vice versa.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Performance Rating Systems:**

- **Definition:** Performance Rating is the *adjustment* of observed time to account for worker pace, expressed as a percentage of standard.
- **Core Formula Chain:** $ST = OT \times (R/100) \times (1 + A)$.
- **Standard Anchor:** 100 = pace of a qualified, average worker under specified conditions.
- **Six Methods:** Speed, Westinghouse, Objective, Synthetic, Physiological, Percentage.
- **Westinghouse Formula:** $R = 100 + S + E + C + I$ (four independent sub-ratings).
- **Synthetic Rating Formula:** $R_{syn} = (T_{std} / T_{obs}) \times 100$.
- **Bedaux Scale:** 60 BPM = rating 100. **Daywork Scale:** 133 BPM = rating 100.
- **Five Rating Errors:** Halo Effect, Central Tendency, Recency Effect, Contrast Effect, Expectancy Bias.
- **Allowance Components:** Personal + Fatigue + Contingency (always additive).
- **TMU Unit:** 1 second = 60 TMU (in MTM-based systems).
- **Most Tested in KTU:** Westinghouse 4-factor + Standard Time calculation.
- **Rater Training:** Uses reference films, blind rating, and element-by-element scoring.
- **Modern Trend:** PMTS (MTM, MOST) eliminates rating subjectivity — but is rarely the KTU board focus.
- **Industrial Use:** Wage incentives, capacity planning, line balancing, ergonomics.
- **Fairness Rule:** Rating must be done by a *qualified, trained, and consistent* rater.
- **Cross-Check:** Use synthetic rating to validate subjective ratings whenever possible.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Performance Rating Systems**
> *(Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, PCIET403 - Work System Design)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Allowances and Estimation of Standard Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Allowances and Estimation of Standard Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Time** is defined as the amount of time an average, skilled worker, working at a **normal pace**, under **normal working conditions**, and given the **necessary allowances** (for personal needs, fatigue, and contingencies), is expected to take to perform a specified task by following a prescribed method.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, standard time is treated as the **fundamental output of work measurement**, and is used directly for:
- Production planning & control
- Wage incentive scheme design
- Cost estimation & budgeting
- Manpower and capacity planning

The governing relationship is the universally accepted KTU formula:

$$
\text{Standard Time (ST)} = \text{Normal Time (NT)} + \text{Allowances (A)}
$$

or equivalently, when the allowance is expressed as a percentage of normal time:

$$
ST = NT \times (1 + \text{Allowance Factor})
$$

where **Normal Time** is the time required by a *rated* worker (i.e., performance adjusted for skill and effort) operating at a normal pace.

$$
NT = \text{Observed Time (OT)} \times \text{Performance Rating (PR)}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marathon Runner's Pace" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Standard Time like the "expected finish time" on a marathon timing chip.**

Imagine timing a marathon runner:
- **Observed Time** = the raw stopwatch reading when the runner crosses the finish line (say 3 hours 30 min).
- **Performance Rating** = how fast this particular runner was going compared to a "normal" reference runner. A world-class runner might be rated **110** (10 % faster than normal), while a casual jogger might be rated **80** (20 % slower).
- **Normal Time** = the time the *reference* runner would have taken on the same course under the same conditions.
- **Allowances** = the extra time budgeted for water breaks, restroom stops, tying a shoelace — the unavoidable human realities that the stopwatch doesn't credit.
- **Standard Time** = the official, planned time the *organization* uses for scheduling, deadlines, and resource planning.

Just as a race organizer cannot schedule buses for runners based only on the world-record holder's time, a production engineer cannot plan a factory using only the fastest employee's pace. Standard time is the **fair, normalized, planned time** the whole system is built around.

## 1.3 Key Physical/Standard Metrics (KTU Convention)

- **Normal Pace Rating**: **100** (B-rated worker, average skill, average effort)
- **Typical Total Allowance Range**: **15 % – 25 %** of Normal Time
- **Performance Rating Range**: **60 (very slow) – 140 (very fast)** in Westinghouse system
- **Standard Day**: **8 hours = 480 minutes** of productive work after allowances

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For PCIET403, Module 3 expects students to (i) classify and compute the **five categories of allowances**, (ii) apply **rating factors** to convert observed times into normal times, and (iii) compute **standard time** for single-element and multi-element operations using **time study, standard data, and predetermined motion time systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Standard Categories of Allowances

Allowances compensate the worker for time lost during the day that does **not directly contribute to productive work**. The KTU syllabus mandates mastery of the following five categories:

### (i) Personal Allowance (PA)
- **Purpose:** Time for personal needs — drinking water, restroom visits, washing hands, brief social interactions.
- **Typical KTU Standard:** **5 %** of normal time for men, **7 %** for women.
- **Why it exists:** ILO and KTU/ISI conventions recognize these as physiological necessities that cannot be eliminated.

### (ii) Rest / Fatigue Allowance (RA)
- **Purpose:** Recovery from physical and mental fatigue.
- **Typical KTU Standard:** **5 % – 10 %** depending on:
  - Heavy manual work → upper end
  - Sedentary light work → lower end
- **KTU-recommended sub-classification (based on Westinghouse):**
  - **Basic Fatigue Allowance:** 4 % (always added)
  - **Variable Fatigue Allowance:** 0 % – 9 % added based on working posture, light intensity, noise, monotony, etc.

### (iii) Contingency Allowance (CA)
- **Purpose:** For unpredictable delays like tool breakage, minor power fluctuation, brief supervisor consultation.
- **Typical KTU Standard:** **2 % – 5 %** of normal time.
- **Distinguishing feature:** Covers *random, low-probability* events not covered by personal or rest allowances.

### (iv) Special Allowance (SA)
- **Purpose:** For **exceptional or non-standard working conditions** such as:
  - Extreme heat, dust, fumes
  - Use of personal protective equipment (PPE) slowing movement
  - Awkward posture (lying, reaching overhead)
- **Typical KTU Standard:** Variable; computed case-by-case or as per company policy.

### (v) Process / Interference Allowance
- **Purpose:** When a worker on a machine-loaded cycle is *free* but cannot begin the next job because of upstream/downstream machine imbalance.
- **Typical KTU Standard:** Calculated from synchronization analysis of machine cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Allowance (A)** is the **arithmetic sum** of all applicable allowances. KTU examiners expect students to compute and justify each component individually rather than simply using a flat percentage.

## 2.2 Methods of Computing Allowances (KTU 2024 Framework)

There are **three recognized methods** in the KTU PCIET403 syllabus:

### Method 1 — Conventional / Arbitrary Percentage Method
- Fixed company-policy percentages (e.g., 5 % + 5 % + 5 % = **15 %** total).
- Simple, fast, but lacks scientific justification.
- Used widely in small-scale industries.

### Method 2 — Workman's Pace Method
- Based on **observed frequency** of delays recorded during a time-study stopwatch run.
- Formula:

$$
A\ \% = \frac{\text{Total Delay Time}}{\text{Productive Time}} \times 100
$$

### Method 3 — Statistical Method (Westinghouse / ILCI)
- Delays are recorded over **many cycles** (typically 20–30) and tabulated.
- Average delay time + standard deviation is used to set the allowance.
- Considered the **most scientific** and is the KTU-preferred method for examiner questions.

## 2.3 Methods of Estimating Standard Time (KTU Module 3 Coverage)

### (A) Direct Time Study (Stopwatch Method)
The classical Gilbreth approach. Steps:
1. Select the job, worker, and method.
2. Record observed times over multiple cycles.
3. Apply performance rating to convert to **normal time**.
4. Add allowances to obtain **standard time**.

### (B) Standard Data System
- Time values for **basic elements** are established once and stored.
- New standard times are assembled by **synthesizing** element standard times.
- Highly efficient for repetitive job families.

### (C) Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)
- Uses **synthesized micro-motion times** (e.g., MTM, Work Factor, Basic Motion Timestudy).
- No stopwatch needed; the method is broken into Therbligs or basic motions and timed against TMU tables.
- **Advantage:** Fair, non-controversial rating since rating is *baked into* the tables.

### (D) Work Sampling
- Statistical sampling of operator activity over many random observations.
- Used to set allowances and verify standard times on a plant-wide basis.

### (E) Historical / Analytical Estimation
- Standard times inferred from past production records, operator logs, or standard data systems.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | KTU-Standard Values & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Time | $NT$ | minutes, seconds | $NT = OT \times \dfrac{PR}{100}$ |
| 2 | Performance Rating | $PR$ | dimensionless, **100 = normal** | Skill + Effort + Conditions + Consistency (Westinghouse) |
| 3 | Standard Time | $ST$ | minutes, seconds | $ST = NT + A$ |
| 4 | Allowance (percentage form) | $A\ \%$ | **15 – 25 %** typical | $A\ \% = PA + RA + CA + SA$ |
| 5 | Allowance (absolute time) | $A_t$ | minutes | $A_t = NT \times \dfrac{A\ \%}{100}$ |
| 6 | Compact Standard Time Formula | $ST$ | minutes | $ST = OT \times \dfrac{PR}{100} \times (1 + A\ \%)$ |
| 7 | Standard Daily Output | $N$ | pieces/day | $N = \dfrac{\text{Available Working Time per Day}}{ST}$ |
| 8 | Number of Cycles Studied (statistical) | $n$ | integer | $n = \left(\dfrac{Z \sigma}{E \bar{x}}\right)^2$ |
| 9 | Allowances via Workman's Pace | $A\ \%$ | percent | $A\ \% = \dfrac{\text{Delay Time}}{\text{Productive Time}} \times 100$ |
| 10 | Westinghouse Rating Adjustment | $R_f$ | rating multiplier | $R_f = 1 + (\text{Skill} + \text{Effort} + \text{Conditions} + \text{Consistency})/100$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **Compact Standard Time Formula** (#6 in the table) — it is the single most-tested equation in Module 3 numerical problems. Almost every Part B (14-mark) question reduces to plugging values into this expression after working out each component.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Standard time is the **backbone of industrial engineering practice** in Kerala's manufacturing belt (Cochin, Palakkad, Coimbatore cluster). It is used to:
- **Quote delivery dates** in make-to-order shops (e.g., Kerala Electrical & Allied Engineering Co.)
- **Compute piece-rate wages** under the Indian Factory Act 1948 (Section 59) incentive schemes.
- **Balance assembly lines** in automotive ancillaries.
- **Plan ER&D manpower** in Technopark / Infopark IT companies for process-engineering teams.
- **Benchmark Industry 4.0** before/after automation — the *reduction* in standard time is a direct measure of productivity gain.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Standard Time Equation

We begin from the **first principles** of time study.

### Step 1 — Define the Observed Time (OT)
The raw stopwatch reading for a single element/cycle performed by the worker actually studied:

$$
OT = T_{\text{stopwatch}}
$$

### Step 2 — Adjust for the Worker's Performance (Normal Time)
A worker faster or slower than the "B-rated normal worker" must have their time normalized. Performance Rating (PR) is the percentage of normal speed, so:

$$
\text{Normal Time per cycle} = \text{Actual Time} \times \frac{\text{PR}}{100}
$$

$$
NT = OT \times \frac{PR}{100}
$$

*Why divide by 100?* Because PR is conventionally expressed as a percentage (100 = normal). A worker rated **110** is 10 % faster than normal, so they take only $\frac{100}{110} = 90.9\ \%$ of the normal worker's time. Multiplying by PR/100 with PR=110 yields $OT \times 1.10$ — wait, this requires careful sign convention: KTU convention is **higher PR = faster**, and the formula above is:

$$
NT = OT \times \frac{PR}{100}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign-convention pitfall (KTU Examiner trap):** Some textbooks define PR as a *speed index* where PR > 100 means slower and PR < 100 means faster (the *reversed* convention). Always verify which convention your KTU course handout uses. The **2024 Scheme PCIET403 workbook follows the standard Gilbreth convention: PR > 100 ⇒ faster**.

### Step 3 — Add Allowances
The worker cannot operate 100 % of the working day on production. Allowance time must be appended:

$$
ST = NT + A_t
$$

where

$$
A_t = NT \times \frac{A\ \%}{100}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine the Two Expressions

$$
ST = NT + NT \times \frac{A\ \%}{100} = NT \left(1 + \frac{A\ \%}{100}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute NT from Step 2

$$
ST = \left(OT \times \frac{PR}{100}\right) \times \left(1 + \frac{A\ \%}{100}\right)
$$

This is the **Master Compact Formula** that KTU examiners test in nearly every Part B numerical.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Given:** Observed time for one cycle = **1.20 min**, Performance rating = **110**, Allowances = Personal 5 % + Rest 10 % + Contingency 3 % = **18 %**.

**Step 6a:** Compute Normal Time.

$$
NT = 1.20 \times \frac{110}{100} = 1.20 \times 1.10 = 1.32 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 6b:** Compute Allowance time.

$$
A_t = 1.32 \times \frac{18}{100} = 1.32 \times 0.18 = 0.2376 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 6c:** Compute Standard Time.

$$
ST = 1.32 + 0.2376 = 1.5576 \text{ min} \approx 1.56 \text{ min}
$$

**Verification using the compact formula:**

$$
ST = 1.20 \times 1.10 \times (1 + 0.18) = 1.32 \times 1.18 = 1.5576 \text{ min}
$$

Both paths agree, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Methods (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Criterion | Time Study | Standard Data | PMTS (MTM/WF) | Work Sampling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tools needed** | Stopwatch, study board | Reference tables | TMU motion tables | Random observation log |
| **Rating required?** | Yes (subjective) | No (uses pre-rated data) | No (inherent rating) | No |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (depends on rater) | High for similar jobs | Very high | Statistical (95 % CL) |
| **Setup cost** | Low | Medium | High (training) | Medium |
| **Best for** | New jobs, one-off studies | Repetitive job families | Ergonomic design | Setting allowances |
| **KTU recommendation** | Module 3 core | Module 3 reference | Module 3 elective | Module 3 statistical |
| **Examiner question weight** | **Very High (8–10 marks)** | Medium (4 marks) | Medium (4 marks) | Low–Medium (2–3 marks) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters in KTU exams:** Questions like *"Compare and contrast time study and PMTS"* (often 7 marks) test exactly the rows above. Memorize the columns and you secure full marks.

---

## 3.3 Extended Derivation — Number of Cycles Required in a Time Study

For a statistically valid time study, the **number of observations $n$** required to achieve a confidence level of $\pm E$ is given by:

$$
n = \left( \frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E \cdot \bar{x}} \right)^2
$$

Where:
- $Z$ = standard normal deviate (for **95 % confidence, $Z = 1.96$**)
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation of observed cycle times
- $\bar{x}$ = average observed time
- $E$ = permissible error as a fraction of the mean (e.g., $\pm 5\ \% \Rightarrow E = 0.05$)

### Numerical Example
Given $\bar{x} = 1.20$ min, $\sigma = 0.10$ min, $E = 0.05$, confidence level 95 %:

**Step 1:** Identify $Z$.

$$
Z = 1.96 \quad (\text{for 95 \% confidence})
$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator.

$$
Z \cdot \sigma = 1.96 \times 0.10 = 0.196
$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator.

$$
E \cdot \bar{x} = 0.05 \times 1.20 = 0.060
$$

**Step 4:** Square the ratio.

$$
n = \left( \frac{0.196}{0.060} \right)^2 = (3.2667)^2 = 10.67
$$

**Step 5:** Round up to the next whole number.

$$
n = 11 \text{ cycles}
$$

This is the **minimum** number of stopwatch readings required for a statistically reliable time study with 95 % confidence.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Standard Time Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end data flow** from a stopwatch reading to the final standard time used on the shop floor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Stopwatch Observation OT] --> B[Average Over n Cycles]
    B --> C[OT_avg]
    C --> D{Performance Rating PR Applied?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Normal Time NT = OT_avg x PR/100]
    D -- No --> F[Direct PMTS or Standard Data Lookup]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Compute Allowance A_t = NT x Allowance Percent/100]
    G --> H[Standard Time ST = NT + A_t]
    H --> I{Standard Day Available}
    I --> J[Daily Output N = 480 min / ST]
    J --> K[Production Planning and Costing]
    H --> L[Incentive Wage Calculation]
    H --> M[Line Balancing Input]
```

## 4.2 Westinghouse Rating System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observed Worker Behaviour] --> B[Skill Rating]
    A --> C[Effort Rating]
    A --> D[Conditions Rating]
    A --> E[Consistency Rating]
    B --> F[Sum of Rating Adjustments]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Performance Rating Multiplier R_f]
    G --> H[NT = OT x R_f]
    H --> I[ST = NT + Allowances]
```

## 4.3 Allowance Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Allowance] --> B[Personal Allowance]
    A --> C[Rest and Fatigue Allowance]
    A --> D[Contingency Allowance]
    A --> E[Special Allowance]
    A --> F[Process Allowance]
    C --> G[Basic Fatigue 4 percent]
    C --> H[Variable Fatigue 0 to 9 percent]
    E --> I[Heat Dust Fumes PPE]
    E --> J[Awkward Posture]
    E --> K[Exceptional Conditions]
```

## 4.4 Cycle of Time-Study Methodology (Functional Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Job and Method] --> B[Select Worker and Equipment]
    B --> C[Divide Job into Elements]
    C --> D[Record Observed Times n Cycles]
    D --> E[Compute OT_avg and sigma]
    E --> F{Number of Cycles Sufficient?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Performance Rating]
    G --> H[Compute Normal Time]
    H --> I[Determine Allowances]
    I --> J[Compute Standard Time]
    J --> K[Document and Publish ST]
    K --> L{Periodic Review Needed?}
    L -- Yes --> A
    L -- No --> M[ST Used in Planning and Incentives]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box is a **data processing stage**, and each arrow is a **data dependency**. The KTU examiner frequently asks students to label the inputs/outputs of each block — being able to read this flow correctly is worth 2–3 marks on a typical 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define standard time. List any four factors on which standard time depends.**

**Model Answer:**
> Standard time is the time required by an average skilled worker, working at a normal pace under normal working conditions, with due allowance for personal needs, fatigue, and contingencies, to complete a specified task by a prescribed method.
>
> The four key factors are:
> 1. **Observed time** measured by stopwatch.
> 2. **Performance rating** of the operator.
> 3. **Allowances** for personal, rest, and contingency needs.
> 4. **Method and equipment** used to perform the job (a change in method changes standard time).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Normal Time and Standard Time.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Normal Time | Standard Time |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Time for a *rated, normal worker* | Normal time + allowances |
| Includes allowances? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Formula | $NT = OT \times PR/100$ | $ST = NT + A_t$ |
| Use | Work measurement analysis | Production planning, costing, incentives |
| Magnitude | Smaller | Larger (since $A_t > 0$) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Either A or B)

---

### **Question B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** During a time study on a drilling operation, the following readings (in seconds) were taken for the element "drill and retract": 18, 20, 19, 21, 17, 22, 18, 19, 20, 21. The performance rating was found to be **110**. The allowances are: Personal **5 %**, Rest **10 %**, Contingency **3 %**. Calculate the **standard time** for the element.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Westinghouse system of performance rating** with a neat sketch/table.

---

#### **Solution to Q (a)**

**Step 1 — Sum the observed times.**

$$
\sum OT = 18 + 20 + 19 + 21 + 17 + 22 + 18 + 19 + 20 + 21 = 195 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute average observed time.**

$$
OT_{avg} = \frac{195}{10} = 19.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply performance rating.**

$$
NT = 19.5 \times \frac{110}{100} = 19.5 \times 1.10 = 21.45 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total allowance percentage.**

$$
A\ \% = 5 + 10 + 3 = 18\ \%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute allowance time.**

$$
A_t = 21.45 \times \frac{18}{100} = 21.45 \times 0.18 = 3.861 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute standard time.**

$$
ST = NT + A_t = 21.45 + 3.861 = 25.311 \text{ s}
$$

**Verification via compact formula:**

$$
ST = 19.5 \times 1.10 \times (1 + 0.18) = 21.45 \times 1.18 = 25.311 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** Standard time for the element ≈ **25.31 seconds**.

---

#### **Solution to Q (b) — Westinghouse System**

The **Westinghouse system** rates a worker's pace by adding four independent adjustment factors, each evaluated on a tabular scale.

**The four factors are:**

| Factor | What it measures | Typical range |
|---|---|---|
| **Skill** | Proficiency at the task | −22 % to +15 % |
| **Effort** | Willingness to work | −20 % to +13 % |
| **Conditions** | Lighting, heat, ventilation, noise | −20 % to +10 % |
| **Consistency** | Repeatability of cycle times | −12 % to +5 % |

**Procedure:**
1. Observe the worker performing several cycles.
2. Assign each of the four factors a numerical value (with sign) from the Westinghouse tables based on observed behaviour.
3. Compute the **rating multiplier $R_f$**:

$$
R_f = 1 + \frac{S + E + C + I}{100}
$$

where $S$ = Skill, $E$ = Effort, $C$ = Conditions, $I$ = Consistency adjustments.

4. Compute Normal Time:

$$
NT = OT_{avg} \times R_f
$$

5. Add allowances to get Standard Time.

**Example:** If Skill = +6, Effort = +5, Conditions = 0, Consistency = +3:

$$
R_f = 1 + \frac{6 + 5 + 0 + 3}{100} = 1 + 0.14 = 1.14
$$

$$
NT = OT_{avg} \times 1.14
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose 2 marks by **forgetting to convert the percentage sum into a decimal** (writing $+14$ instead of $+0.14$ when computing $R_f$). Always express the rating adjustments as **decimals before applying**.

---

### **Question B — Option B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for conducting a stopwatch time study**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A worker's observed times (in min) for 8 cycles of an operation are: 1.8, 2.0, 1.9, 2.1, 1.7, 2.2, 1.8, 2.0. The operator is rated at **90** (i.e., 10 % slower than normal). Total allowances are **20 %**. Calculate the **standard time per cycle** and the **number of standard pieces per 8-hour shift**.

---

#### **Solution to Q (a) — Time Study Procedure**

**Step 1 — Prepare for the study**
- Define the **job**, select a **qualified worker** performing the task under **normal conditions**, and fix the **method** to be studied.

**Step 2 — Divide the job into elements**
- Break the operation into **small, easily identifiable** elements (grasp, position, drill, retract, etc.) so each can be timed accurately.

**Step 3 — Choose the timing equipment**
- Use a stopwatch (decimal-minute or decimal-hour preferred) and a **time-study observation sheet**.

**Step 4 — Record observed times**
- Using the **continuous timing** or **snap-back** method, record times for each element over **20–30 cycles** (more for short, repetitive elements).

**Step 5 — Rate the performance**
- Simultaneously or after, rate the operator's pace (skill, effort, conditions, consistency) and convert to **Normal Time**.

**Step 6 — Determine the number of cycles**
- Compute $n$ using the statistical formula (Section 3.3); if $n$ is greater than cycles already taken, observe more.

**Step 7 — Add allowances**
- Determine and apply personal, rest, fatigue, and contingency allowances based on the working conditions.

**Step 8 — Compute Standard Time**
- Use $ST = NT \times (1 + A\%)$ for each element and **sum** across elements for the operation standard time.

**Step 9 — Document and publish**
- Prepare the **Standard Time Card** listing method, equipment, element times, ratings, allowances, and final standard time.

---

#### **Solution to Q (b) — Numerical**

**Step 1 — Sum of observed times.**

$$
\sum OT = 1.8 + 2.0 + 1.9 + 2.1 + 1.7 + 2.2 + 1.8 + 2.0 = 17.5 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Average observed time.**

$$
OT_{avg} = \frac{17.5}{8} = 2.1875 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply performance rating (PR = 90).**

$$
NT = 2.1875 \times \frac{90}{100} = 2.1875 \times 0.90 = 1.96875 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute allowance time (A = 20 %).**

$$
A_t = 1.96875 \times 0.20 = 0.39375 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Standard time per cycle.**

$$
ST = NT + A_t = 1.96875 + 0.39375 = 2.3625 \text{ min} \approx 2.36 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute available working time per 8-hour shift.**
Assuming the worker is present 8 hours = **480 min**. (In some KTU problems, allowances are *already inside* ST, so available time = 480 min directly. We'll use this convention.)

**Step 7 — Standard pieces per shift.**

$$
N = \frac{480}{ST} = \frac{480}{2.3625} = 203.17 \approx 203 \text{ pieces}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Standard time per cycle = 2.36 min**
- **Standard pieces per 8-hour shift ≈ 203 pieces**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Three common mistakes cost easy marks here:
> 1. **Wrong rating direction** — PR = 90 means the operator is *slower*, so NT *decreases* (multiply by 0.90), not increases. Forgetting this gives ST ≈ 2.6 min and is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Available time error** — Students sometimes subtract the *full* allowance from 480 min *and* also add it inside ST. Choose one convention consistently.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — Always convert all times to the same unit (minutes or seconds) before computing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Standard Time = Normal Time + Allowances** is the master equation.
- [x] **Normal Time = Observed Time × (Performance Rating / 100)**.
- [x] **Compact form:** $ST = OT \times \dfrac{PR}{100} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A\%}{100}\right)$.
- [x] **Five categories of allowance:** Personal, Rest/Fatigue, Contingency, Special, Process.
- [x] **Typical total allowance range:** **15 % – 25 %** (Personal 5 %, Rest 5–10 %, Contingency 2–5 %, Special case-dependent).
- [x] **PR > 100 ⇒ faster worker** (Gilbreth/KTU convention).
- [x] **Westinghouse rating** uses four factors: **Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency**.
- [x] **PMTS systems** (MTM, Work Factor) are *self-rating* — no stopwatch or subjective rating needed.
- [x] **Number of cycles** required: $n = (Z\sigma/E\bar{x})^2$; at **95 % confidence, Z = 1.96**.
- [x] **Standard pieces per day:** $N = \dfrac{\text{Available Working Time per Day}}{ST}$.
- [x] Always **convert units consistently** before applying formulas.
- [x] Always **show all derivation steps** — KTU examiners award partial credit generously when work is shown, but zero if only the final answer is written.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark Part B question on this topic, follow the **three-step grid**: (1) Compute OT_avg; (2) Compute NT using PR; (3) Compute ST using total allowance %. Memorize the **compact formula** and the **five allowance categories** — these two alone cover ~70 % of the marks in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of Incentive Schemes with Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Development of Incentive Schemes with Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Development of Incentive Schemes with Examples

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incentive Scheme:** A formally structured **wage-payment plan** that links a portion of the worker's earnings directly to the **quantity** or **quality of output produced beyond a standard**, while ensuring a guaranteed base wage for the time worked. In the context of **Work System Design (PCIET403)**, an incentive scheme is the financial mechanism that translates the **Standard Time** established via methods-time measurement (MTM), work sampling, or time study into a **motivational, equitable, and productivity-enhancing compensation policy**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, incentive schemes are studied under **Module 3: Work Measurement & Compensation Design**, where the student is expected to:
- Distinguish between **Time Wage** and **Piece Wage** systems.
- Derive earnings equations for **Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, Emerson, Taylor, Gantt, and 100% Premium Plans**.
- Apply the schemes to numerical problems and recommend the most suitable plan for a given industrial scenario.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **delivery boy** in a food delivery app:
- **Base Pay (Time Wage):** He gets a fixed ₹200 for an 8-hour shift, regardless of how many orders he delivers.
- **Piece Wage:** He gets ₹20 *per order delivered*. More effort = more money.
- **Incentive Scheme:** He gets ₹200 guaranteed, *plus* ₹5 extra for every order delivered *over* a target of 40. The harder he works beyond standard, the more he earns — but he never earns *less* than the base.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** An incentive scheme is the **"carrot"** that pays workers for being **faster, better, or more efficient** than the standard, while the **base wage** acts as the **"floor"** that protects them from earning too little.

### 🎯 Purpose of Incentive Schemes in Work System Design

| Objective | Engineering / Managerial Logic |
|---|---|
| **Increase Productivity** | Workers are motivated to exceed standard times. |
| **Fair Wage Distribution** | Pay is proportional to effort, not just hours. |
| **Cost Control** | Output per labor-hour rises, reducing unit cost. |
| **Quality Maintenance** | Good plans (e.g., Bedaux) link bonus to **standard quality**. |
| **Worker Welfare** | Higher earnings without forcing longer hours. |
| **Industrial Harmony** | Reduces conflict between labor and management. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Standard Time (Ts):** **100%** efficiency benchmark (in minutes or hours).
> - **Actual Time (Ta):** Time actually taken by the worker.
> - **Efficiency Index (E):** $E = \dfrac{T_s}{T_a} \times 100\%$
> - **High Day = 100% Bonus Day** in many plans.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧩 Pre-Requisite Concepts (Build-Up Logic)

Before deriving the incentive plans, three foundational building blocks must be crystal clear.

### 2.1 Time Wage System (No Incentive)
The worker is paid a fixed rate **per unit of time** (per hour or per day), irrespective of output.

$$\text{Earnings} = R \times T_a$$

where $R$ = hourly rate (₹/hr) and $T_a$ = actual hours worked. This gives **security** but **no motivation**.

### 2.2 Pure Piece Wage System (100% Incentive)
The worker is paid a fixed rate **per piece produced**. Earnings are independent of time.

$$\text{Earnings} = P \times N$$

where $P$ = piece rate (₹/piece) and $N$ = number of pieces produced. The rate is set such that producing the standard quantity in standard time yields the same wage as time wage: $P = \dfrac{R \times T_s}{N_s}$ (where $N_s$ is standard output).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Incentive Schemes Exist:** Pure piece wage is risky (workers may rush and damage quality) and time wage is lazy (no effort). Incentive schemes are the **balanced hybrid**.

### 2.3 Classification of Incentive Schemes

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Individual – Straight** | Linear pay-per-piece | Piece Wage |
| **Individual – Differential** | Two or more rates based on efficiency | Taylor, Emerson, Gantt |
| **Group Incentive** | Bonus shared among workers | Scanlon, Rucker |
| **Premium Plans** | Share of time-savings between worker & management | Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux |

---

## 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Variables Used in ALL Formulas:**
> - $T_s$ = Standard Time (hours) — for producing **1 unit** or for the **task**
> - $T_a$ = Actual Time taken (hours)
> - $R$ = Basic Time Wage Rate (₹/hour)
> - $T_s \cdot R$ = Guaranteed Base Wage (₹)
> - Time Saved = $T_s - T_a$ (only positive; otherwise no bonus)
> - Efficiency $E = \dfrac{T_s}{T_a} \times 100\%$

| # | Scheme | Earnings Formula | Bonus / Premium Formula | Worker Share of Time Saved |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Halsey Premium Plan** | $E = R \cdot T_a + \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s - T_a)$ | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s - T_a)$ | **50%** |
| 2 | **Rowan Premium Plan** | $E = R \cdot T_a + R (T_s - T_a) \cdot \dfrac{T_a}{T_s}$ | $P = R (T_s - T_a) \cdot \dfrac{T_a}{T_s}$ | $\dfrac{T_a}{T_s}$ (i.e., *time ratio*) |
| 3 | **Bedaux Point System (B = 60)** | $E = R \cdot T_a + \dfrac{3}{4} R (T_s - T_a)$ | $P = \dfrac{3}{4} R (T_s - T_a)$ | **75%** (worker gets $\frac{3}{4}$ of time saved in points) |
| 4 | **100% Premium Plan (Gilbreth / Halsey's variant)** | $E = R \cdot T_a + R (T_s - T_a)$ | $P = R (T_s - T_a)$ | **100%** |
| 5 | **Emerson Efficiency Plan** | $E = R \cdot T_a + \text{Bonus per hour} \times T_a$ | Bonus = (Efficiency − 66.67%) × $R \cdot T_a$ × (slope) | Variable; **starts at 66.67%** |
| 6 | **Taylor's Differential Piece Rate** | $E = \begin{cases} 0.8 \cdot P_s \cdot N & \text{if } E < 100\% \\ 1.2 \cdot P_s \cdot N & \text{if } E \geq 100\% \end{cases}$ | Differential rate | **High pay only for high efficiency** |
| 7 | **Gantt Task & Bonus** | $E = R \cdot T_a + \text{Bonus} \cdot T_a$ | Bonus = 20% to 50% of base if $T_s$ met; minimum 20% bonus for failing | **20%–50%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule of Thumb for KTU Problems:** Always compute **Time Saved** first: $S = T_s - T_a$. If $S < 0$, **no bonus** is paid; the worker only receives the base wage $R \cdot T_a$.

### 2.4 Why Each Plan Was Designed — The "Engineering Story"

- **Halsey (1890):** First to introduce the **50–50 sharing** of time saved. Conservative, hence widely adopted.
- **Rowan (1898):** Bonus is proportional to **time taken relative to standard**. The "rate of bonus decreases" as the worker gets faster, capping earnings — a self-balancing plan.
- **Bedaux (1916):** Uses **B-points (B = 60 seconds = 1 minute of standard effort)**. Worker gets 75% of points saved; supervisor gets 25%. **Most scientifically designed.**
- **Emerson (1890):** Treats efficiency **below 66.67%** as unsatisfactory — worker is paid only time wage; between **66.67%–100%**, a graduated bonus is paid; **above 100%**, an additional bonus is added.
- **Taylor (1895):** **Punitive** for low efficiency (low piece rate) and **rewarding** for high efficiency (high piece rate). Built to **eliminate soldiering**.
- **Gantt (1901):** Worker who meets standard gets a **bonus of 20%–50%** of the basic wage; those who fail still get a **20% bonus** to encourage them.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 3.1 Halsey Premium Plan — Full Derivation

**Hypothesis:** When a worker saves time, both the worker and the employer should **share the gain equally** (50–50).

**Step 1:** Define the base wage the worker is guaranteed (time wage component):

$$\text{Base Wage} = R \cdot T_a$$

**Step 2:** Time saved is the difference between standard and actual time:

$$\text{Time Saved} = T_s - T_a$$

**Step 3:** The **value** of time saved (in money) is the rate × time saved:

$$\text{Value of Time Saved} = R \cdot (T_s - T_a)$$

**Step 4:** By the 50–50 rule, the worker's **premium (bonus)** is half the value of time saved:

$$\text{Bonus}_{\text{Halsey}} = \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s - T_a)$$

**Step 5:** Total earnings combine base wage + bonus:

$$\text{Total Earnings} = R \cdot T_a + \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s - T_a)$$

**Step 6:** Simplification (KTU often asks this expanded form):

$$\text{Total Earnings} = \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s + T_a)$$

---

## 🧮 3.2 Rowan Premium Plan — Full Derivation

**Hypothesis:** The bonus should be proportional to the **ratio of actual time to standard time**, so the bonus approaches zero as the worker becomes infinitely fast (preventing "speed-up accidents").

**Step 1:** Same base wage:

$$\text{Base Wage} = R \cdot T_a$$

**Step 2:** Time saved is again $T_s - T_a$.

**Step 3:** But the bonus is **not 50%**; it is the *value of time saved multiplied by the time ratio*:

$$\text{Bonus}_{\text{Rowan}} = R \cdot (T_s - T_a) \cdot \dfrac{T_a}{T_s}$$

**Step 4:** Total earnings:

$$\text{Total Earnings} = R \cdot T_a + R (T_s - T_a) \cdot \dfrac{T_a}{T_s}$$

**Step 5:** Expanding:

$$\text{Total Earnings} = R \cdot T_a + R \cdot T_a - R \cdot \dfrac{T_a^2}{T_s}$$

$$\text{Total Earnings} = 2 R T_a - R \cdot \dfrac{T_a^2}{T_s}$$

---

## 🧮 3.3 Bedaux Point System — Full Derivation

**Hypothesis:** Time is measured in **Bedaux Points (B)**, where **1 B = 0.01 hour = 36 seconds** (some texts use 1 B = 1 minute; KTU convention: **1 B = 1 minute of standard effort, 60 B = 1 hour**). The worker's bonus is **75% of points saved**; the foreman's bonus is 25% (incentive for supervision).

**Step 1:** Standard points to be earned:

$$\text{Standard Points} = \dfrac{T_s \text{ (in minutes)}}{1} = 60 \cdot T_s \text{ (in hours)}$$

**Step 2:** Actual points earned:

$$\text{Actual Points} = 60 \cdot T_a$$

**Step 3:** Points saved:

$$\text{Points Saved} = 60 (T_s - T_a)$$

**Step 4:** Worker's share (75%):

$$\text{Worker's Bonus} = \dfrac{3}{4} \times R \times (T_s - T_a) \text{ in hours}$$

**Step 5:** Total earnings:

$$\text{Total Earnings} = R \cdot T_a + \dfrac{3}{4} R (T_s - T_a)$$

---

## 🧮 3.4 Emerson Efficiency Plan — Full Derivation

**Hypothesis:** The plan rewards **any efficiency ≥ 66.67%** with a graduated bonus, and **efficiency ≥ 100%** with an extra high bonus.

**Step 1:** Efficiency:

$$E = \dfrac{T_s}{T_a} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2:** Three zones:

| Zone | Efficiency | Bonus |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** | $E < 66.67\%$ | **No bonus** (only time wage $R T_a$) |
| **Medium** | $66.67\% \leq E < 100\%$ | Bonus = $\dfrac{1}{20} \cdot (E - 66.67) \cdot R \cdot T_a$ |
| **High** | $E \geq 100\%$ | Bonus = $\dfrac{1}{10} \cdot (E - 100) \cdot R \cdot T_a + 20\% \text{ of base}$ |

**Step 3:** The graduated structure ensures a **smooth, continuous reward function** (a key advantage over the discontinuous Taylor plan).

---

## 🧮 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

> **Problem:** A worker has a standard time $T_s = 10$ hours, hourly rate $R = ₹50$/hr, and actual time $T_a = 8$ hours. Compute earnings under Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, and 100% Premium Plans.

**Step 1: Time Saved**

$$S = T_s - T_a = 10 - 8 = 2 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Halsey**

$$E_H = R T_a + \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s - T_a) = 50(8) + 0.5 \times 50 \times 2 = 400 + 50 = ₹450$$

**Step 3: Rowan**

$$E_R = R T_a + R (T_s - T_a) \cdot \dfrac{T_a}{T_s} = 50(8) + 50(2) \cdot \dfrac{8}{10} = 400 + 80 = ₹480$$

**Step 4: Bedaux (75%)**

$$E_B = R T_a + \dfrac{3}{4} R (T_s - T_a) = 400 + 0.75 \times 50 \times 2 = 400 + 75 = ₹475$$

**Step 5: 100% Premium**

$$E_{100} = R T_a + R (T_s - T_a) = 400 + 50 \times 2 = 400 + 100 = ₹500$$

**Step 6: Ranking by Total Earnings**

$$\text{Time Wage} < \text{Halsey} < \text{Bedaux} < \text{Rowan} < \text{100\% Premium}$$

**Efficiency Check:** $E = \dfrac{10}{8} \times 100\% = 125\%$ — high performer, earns more under Rowan and 100% plans.

---

## 🧮 3.6 Comparison Matrix for KTU Theory Questions

| Criterion | Halsey | Rowan | Bedaux | Emerson | Taylor | Gantt |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Worker Share of Savings** | 50% | $\frac{T_a}{T_s}$ | 75% | Variable (graduated) | 0% (below std) / 120% (above) | 20–50% |
| **Guaranteed Minimum** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (no base) | Yes (with 20% consolation) |
| **Eases Speed-up** | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent | Poor | Moderate |
| **Quality Control** | Low | Low | **High (B-points)** | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Best Suited For** | Skilled repetitive work | Variable tasks | Mass production, multi-task | Learning-curve phases | Hard-driving management | Training, skill-building |
| **Disadvantage** | Slow earner & fast earner paid closer | Complex formula | Complex measurement | Many zones, complex | Punitive, causes resentment | No incentive for over-standardization |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔁 4.1 Wage-System Decision Tree (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Worker Produces Output in Time Ta] --> B{Is Ta less than or equal to Ts?}
    B -- No Ta exceeds Ts --> C[Worker is SLOWER than Standard]
    C --> D[Pay = R times Ta only]
    C --> E[No Bonus Paid in Premium Plans]
    B -- Yes Ta less than or equal to Ts --> F[Worker is FASTER than Standard]
    F --> G[Time Saved S = Ts minus Ta]
    G --> H{Which Incentive Plan is Active?}
    H --> I1[Halsey: Bonus = half R times S]
    H --> I2[Rowan: Bonus = R times S times Ta divided by Ts]
    H --> I3[Bedaux: Bonus = three quarters R times S]
    H --> I4[Emerson: Bonus based on Efficiency Zone]
    H --> I5[Taylor: Differential Piece Rate]
    H --> I6[Gantt: Fixed percentage Bonus]
    I1 --> J[Total Earnings = Base Wage plus Bonus]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    I6 --> J
    J --> K[Compare with Standard to compute Efficiency Index]
```

## 🔁 4.2 Efficiency Zones in Emerson's Plan

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Below 66.67 percent Efficiency] --> Z2[66.67 percent to 100 percent Efficiency]
    Z2 --> Z3[Above 100 percent Efficiency]
    Z1 --> R1[Pay: Only Time Wage R times Ta]
    R1 --> B1[No Bonus at All]
    Z2 --> R2[Pay: Time Wage plus Graduated Bonus]
    R2 --> B2[Bonus = 1 divided by 20 times E minus 66.67 times R times Ta]
    Z3 --> R3[Pay: Time Wage plus High Bonus plus 20 percent of Base]
    R3 --> B3[Bonus = 1 divided by 10 times E minus 100 times R times Ta plus 20 percent R times Ta]
```

## 🔁 4.3 Taylor's Differential Piece-Rate Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Worker produces N units] --> V{Compare Efficiency with 100 percent}
    V -- Efficiency below 100 percent --> L[Pay at LOW Piece Rate: 0.8 times Ps times N]
    V -- Efficiency at or above 100 percent --> H[Pay at HIGH Piece Rate: 1.2 times Ps times N]
    L --> M[Sharp Penalty for Poor Performance]
    H --> N[Generous Reward for High Performance]
    M --> O[Goal: Eliminate Soldiering and Loafing]
    N --> O
```

## 🔁 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Work-Study Incentive Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Method Study: Design Best Work Method] --> B[Work Measurement: Establish Standard Time Ts]
    B --> C[Time Study and Rating]
    C --> D[Set Basic Time Wage Rate R]
    D --> E[Choose Incentive Plan: Halsey or Rowan or Bedaux or Emerson]
    E --> F[Worker Executes Task in Actual Time Ta]
    F --> G{Compute Time Saved S = Ts minus Ta}
    G -- S greater than 0 --> H[Compute Bonus per Active Plan]
    G -- S less than or equal to 0 --> I[Pay only Base Wage R times Ta]
    H --> J[Total Earnings Paid to Worker]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Efficiency E = Ts divided by Ta times 100]
    K --> L[Feedback to Method Study for Continuous Improvement]
    L --> A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Differentiate between Time Wage System and Piece Wage System. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Time Wage | Piece Wage |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of Payment** | Per unit of time (hour/day) | Per unit of output (piece) |
| **Risk** | Borne by employer | Borne by worker |
| **Quality** | Worker is careful; quality is high | Worker may rush; quality may drop |
| **Earnings** | Fixed, irrespective of output | Variable, depends on output |
| **Best For** | Quality-critical jobs (e.g., watch assembly) | Repetitive, measurable jobs (e.g., bricklaying) |

**Advantage — Time Wage:** Guarantees stable income; suitable where output cannot be measured easily. **Advantage — Piece Wage:** Strong incentive; output rises substantially.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is a "Premium Bonus" in the context of incentive wage payment? Name two premium plans.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Premium Bonus** is the **extra payment** given to a worker, over and above the basic time wage, for **saving time** (i.e., completing a job in less than the standard time). It represents the worker's share of the **economic gain** from improved efficiency.

**Two Premium Plans:**
1. **Halsey Premium Plan** (50% sharing of time saved)
2. **Rowan Premium Plan** (proportional sharing based on time ratio)

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **any ONE** full question (a + b). Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]*

**Q.A (a)** Define the **Halsey Premium Plan** and the **Rowan Premium Plan**. Derive the earnings formula for each. Under what conditions do both plans yield the **same earnings**? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Halsey Premium Plan Definition:** A wage-payment plan in which the worker is paid the **basic time wage** for actual hours worked, plus a **premium equal to 50%** of the value of time saved.

**Rowan Premium Plan Definition:** A wage-payment plan in which the worker is paid the **basic time wage** for actual hours worked, plus a **premium equal to the time saved multiplied by the ratio of actual time to standard time** ($T_a / T_s$).

**Halsey Earnings Derivation:**

$$\text{Base Wage} = R \cdot T_a$$

$$\text{Time Saved} = T_s - T_a$$

$$\text{Premium} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot R \cdot (T_s - T_a)$$

$$\text{Total Earnings}_H = R \cdot T_a + \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s - T_a) = \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s + T_a)$$

**Rowan Earnings Derivation:**

$$\text{Total Earnings}_R = R \cdot T_a + R (T_s - T_a) \cdot \dfrac{T_a}{T_s}$$

$$\text{Total Earnings}_R = R \cdot T_a + \dfrac{R T_a (T_s - T_a)}{T_s}$$

**Condition for Equivalence:** Setting $E_H = E_R$:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s + T_a) = R T_a + \dfrac{R T_a (T_s - T_a)}{T_s}$$

Dividing throughout by $R$ and rearranging:

$$\dfrac{1}{2}(T_s + T_a) = T_a + \dfrac{T_a(T_s - T_a)}{T_s}$$

After algebraic simplification, the condition $E_H = E_R$ holds when:

$$T_a = T_s \quad \text{or equivalently when efficiency} = 100\%$$

i.e., when the worker completes the task **exactly in standard time**, both plans give identical earnings.

---

**Q.A (b)** A worker's **standard time** for a job is **12 hours**, the **basic hourly rate** is **₹40**, and the worker completes the job in **9 hours**.
**Compute the total earnings under:**
(i) Time Wage, (ii) Halsey Plan, (iii) Rowan Plan, (iv) Bedaux Plan, (v) 100% Premium Plan.
**Also calculate the Efficiency Index.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$T_s = 12$ hr, $T_a = 9$ hr, $R = ₹40$/hr, $S = 12 - 9 = 3$ hr.

**Efficiency Index:**

$$E = \dfrac{T_s}{T_a} \times 100\% = \dfrac{12}{9} \times 100\% = 133.33\%$$

**(i) Time Wage:**

$$E_{TW} = R \cdot T_a = 40 \times 9 = ₹360$$

**(ii) Halsey Plan:**

$$E_H = \dfrac{1}{2} R (T_s + T_a) = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 40 \times (12 + 9) = 0.5 \times 40 \times 21 = ₹420$$

**(iii) Rowan Plan:**

$$E_R = R T_a + R (T_s - T_a) \cdot \dfrac{T_a}{T_s} = 40(9) + 40(3) \cdot \dfrac{9}{12} = 360 + 90 = ₹450$$

**(iv) Bedaux Plan (75%):**

$$E_B = R T_a + \dfrac{3}{4} R (T_s - T_a) = 360 + 0.75 \times 40 \times 3 = 360 + 90 = ₹450$$

**(v) 100% Premium Plan:**

$$E_{100} = R T_a + R (T_s - T_a) = 360 + 40 \times 3 = 360 + 120 = ₹480$$

**Summary Table:**

| Plan | Earnings (₹) |
|---|---|
| Time Wage | 360 |
| Halsey | 420 |
| Rowan | 450 |
| Bedaux | 450 |
| 100% Premium | 480 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **Emerson Efficiency Plan** in detail. How does it differ from the Halsey Plan? Compute the **earnings** of a worker under Emerson's plan if standard time = 8 hours, actual time = 6 hours, and rate = ₹50/hr. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Emerson Efficiency Plan — Explanation:**
The Emerson plan is a **graduated bonus plan** that recognizes effort in **three distinct efficiency zones**:

- **Zone 1 (Below 66.67% efficiency):** Worker is paid only the time wage. No bonus — this is a "warning zone" indicating unsatisfactory performance.
- **Zone 2 (66.67% to 100% efficiency):** Bonus is paid as a fraction of the difference between actual efficiency and 66.67%, with a **slope of 1/20**.
- **Zone 3 (Above 100% efficiency):** An additional bonus with a **steeper slope of 1/10** plus a guaranteed 20% bonus for meeting the standard.

**Bonus Formula:**

$$\text{Bonus} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } E < 66.67\% \\ \dfrac{1}{20}(E - 66.67) \cdot R \cdot T_a & \text{if } 66.67\% \leq E < 100\% \\ \dfrac{1}{10}(E - 100) \cdot R \cdot T_a + 0.20 \cdot R \cdot T_a & \text{if } E \geq 100\% \end{cases}$$

**Difference from Halsey:**

| Criterion | Halsey | Emerson |
|---|---|---|
| Bonus payment | Starts at any saving | Starts only at 66.67% |
| Worker below standard | Still gets 50% bonus if he saves | Gets no bonus |
| Fairness | Ignores low efficiency | Penalizes low efficiency |

**Numerical Computation:**

$E = \dfrac{8}{6} \times 100\% = 133.33\%$

Since $E \geq 100\%$, the worker falls in **Zone 3**:

$$\text{Bonus} = \dfrac{1}{10}(133.33 - 100) \times 50 \times 6 + 0.20 \times 50 \times 6$$

$$\text{Bonus} = 0.1 \times 33.33 \times 300 + 0.20 \times 300$$

$$\text{Bonus} = 1000 + 60 = ₹1060$$

$$\text{Total Earnings} = R T_a + \text{Bonus} = 300 + 1060 = ₹1360$$

---

**Q.B (b)** A worker produces **40 units** in a 40-hour week. Standard output for 40 hours is **30 units**. Basic time wage is ₹30/hr. Compute the worker's earnings under **(i) Taylor's Differential Piece-Rate System** (low rate = 80% of standard, high rate = 120% of standard) and **(ii) Gantt Task & Bonus Plan** (Bonus = 25% of base wage for meeting standard). (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 40$ units, $N_s = 30$ units, $R = ₹30$/hr, $T = 40$ hr.

**Base Weekly Wage:**

$$W = R \cdot T = 30 \times 40 = ₹1200$$

**Standard Piece Rate (Ps):** Worker producing $N_s = 30$ units in 40 hours should earn $W = ₹1200$:

$$P_s = \dfrac{W}{N_s} = \dfrac{1200}{30} = ₹40 \text{ per unit}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$E = \dfrac{N}{N_s} \times 100\% = \dfrac{40}{30} \times 100\% = 133.33\%$$

Since $E \geq 100\%$, the worker qualifies for the **high piece rate** under Taylor.

**(i) Taylor's Differential Piece-Rate System:**

$$\text{Earnings} = 1.2 \times P_s \times N = 1.2 \times 40 \times 40 = ₹1920$$

**Bonus over base wage:** $1920 - 1200 = ₹720$

**(ii) Gantt Task & Bonus Plan (Bonus = 25% of base):**

$$\text{Base Wage} = ₹1200$$

$$\text{Bonus} = 0.25 \times 1200 = ₹300$$

$$\text{Total Earnings} = 1200 + 300 = ₹1500$$

**Comparison:**

| Plan | Earnings (₹) |
|---|---|
| Time Wage | 1200 |
| Gantt (25% bonus) | 1500 |
| Taylor (high rate) | 1920 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of Time Saved:** If $T_a > T_s$, there is **NO bonus** under Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux. Many students wrongly plug in a negative bonus — this is incorrect. Worker is paid only base wage.
> 2. **Rowan Formula Trap:** Students often write Bonus = $R (T_s - T_a) \cdot \frac{T_s}{T_a}$. The correct ratio is $\frac{T_a}{T_s}$, **not** its reciprocal. This single error costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Emerson Zone Confusion:** For efficiency between 66.67% and 100%, the bonus is calculated using the **1/20 slope**, not the 1/10 slope. Mixing these is the most common error.
> 4. **Taylor's Differential Rate:** The piece rate $P_s$ must be calculated from the **standard wage and standard output**, not the actual output.
> 5. **Unit Consistency:** Always express $T_s$ and $T_a$ in the **same unit** (hours or minutes) before applying the formula. Mixing hours and minutes is a 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Gantt's "Consolation Bonus":** Workers who *fail* the standard but perform reasonably well still receive a **20% minimum bonus** under Gantt. Forgetting this loses marks in descriptive answers.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam!):**

- ✅ **Time Wage** = $R \cdot T_a$ | **Piece Wage** = $P \cdot N$ — Two extremes of compensation.
- ✅ **Halsey** → 50% sharing → Formula simplifies to $\frac{1}{2} R (T_s + T_a)$.
- ✅ **Rowan** → Bonus proportional to $\frac{T_a}{T_s}$ → Self-limiting, prevents speed-up accidents.
- ✅ **Bedaux** → 75% sharing → Uses **B-points (1 B = 1 min standard effort)**; supervisor gets 25% → Best for **quality-controlled** mass production.
- ✅ **Emerson** → **Three zones** (below 66.67%, 66.67–100%, above 100%) → Graduated bonus with slopes **1/20 and 1/10**.
- ✅ **Taylor's Differential** → **80% piece rate** if $E < 100\%$, **120% piece rate** if $E \geq 100\%$ → Punitive-residual system.
- ✅ **Gantt** → **20%–50% bonus** for meeting standard; **20% consolation bonus** for failing but trying.
- ✅ **100% Premium** → Worker gets the **full value** of time saved (highest possible incentive).
- ✅ **Efficiency Index** is the **master diagnostic** in every problem: $E = \frac{T_s}{T_a} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **No Time Saved → No Bonus** (in all premium plans). Base wage is always guaranteed.
- ✅ When $T_a = T_s$ (efficiency = 100%), **Halsey and Rowan yield identical earnings**.
- ✅ Rowan earnings > Halsey earnings when efficiency < 100%, and the reverse when efficiency > 100%.
- ✅ Rowan plan protects against **unrealistic speed-up** because bonus → 0 as $T_a$ → 0.
- ✅ **Bedaux is the most engineer-friendly** plan (links to standard time, includes supervision bonus, ensures quality).
- ✅ **Taylor is the harshest** plan — no guaranteed minimum wage, sharp penalty for low efficiency.
- ✅ KTU frequently asks **"Which plan is best for a given scenario?"** — answer with: **Halsey for stable industries, Rowan for learning-curve tasks, Bedaux for mass production, Emerson for training new workers, Gantt for skill development, Taylor for hard-driving management environments.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Sampling** is a statistical technique of work measurement in which a large number of instantaneous, random observations are made over a period of time on a worker, machine, or process to determine the proportion of time spent in various defined categories of activity (working, idle, delayed, etc.). It is based on the **Law of Large Numbers** and the **Theory of Probability**.

The technique was first introduced by **L.H.C. Tippett (1935)** in the British cotton industry, and later formalized and popularized by the United Nations and industrial engineering practitioners. Work sampling is also known by several synonymous terms:

- **Activity Sampling** (when applied to human workers)
- **Ratio Delay Study** (older British terminology)
- **Snap-Reading Method** (older American terminology)
- **Statistical Sampling** (generalized industrial term)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Waiting Room"

Imagine you are a hospital administrator who wants to know: *"What percentage of time does Dr. Smith actually spend with patients versus doing paperwork?"* You cannot watch Dr. Smith continuously for 6 hours — that would be exhausting and expensive.

So instead, you position yourself at the door and make **quick glances (snap observations)** at random instants — say 200 times throughout the day. Each glance classifies Dr. Smith as either "**WITH PATIENT**" or "**DOING PAPERWORK**" or "**IDLE**." At the end of the day, you simply count:

- 120 glances → With Patient → 60%
- 60 glances → Paperwork → 30%
- 20 glances → Idle → 10%

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, if you took enough random snapshots, the **percentage of time Dr. Smith was in each state equals the percentage of glances that caught him in that state.** That, in essence, is **Work Sampling** — measuring *how time is spent* by sampling *moments in time*.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Constants

The following are the standard statistical parameters universally used in work sampling analysis:

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Statistical Parameters for Work Sampling**
> - **p** = Proportion of time spent in the activity being studied (sample proportion)
> - **q** = Proportion of time NOT spent in the activity = **(1 − p)**
> - **N** = Total number of random observations taken
> - **X** = Number of observations falling in the activity category of interest
> - **σ (Sigma)** = Standard deviation of the sampling distribution
> - **E** = Absolute error (precision) of the estimate
> - **S** = Relative error (accuracy) of the estimate, in **%**
> - **Z** = Standard normal variate (for KTU problems, typically **Z = 2** for 95.45% confidence)
> - **L** = Allowable error limit (in absolute terms of proportion)

## 1.4 Why Use Work Sampling? — Engineering Justification

> [!TIP]
> **Work Sampling is preferred over continuous time study (stopwatch method) when:**
> 1. The **cycle time is long or non-repetitive** (e.g., administrative work, maintenance, hospital rounds).
> 2. The **objective is to determine delay allowances or machine utilization** rather than standard time.
> 3. **Many workers/machines** must be studied simultaneously.
> 4. **Cost of continuous observation is prohibitive** and statistical accuracy is sufficient.
> 5. The work involves **mental effort or unpredictable activity** that cannot be measured by stopwatch easily.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Statistical Pillars

Work sampling rests on three foundational pillars of probability theory:

**Pillar 1: Binomial Distribution of Observations**
Each random observation is a Bernoulli trial — it either belongs to the category of interest ("success") or it does not ("failure"). For *N* independent random observations, the count *X* of "successes" follows a **Binomial Distribution**:

$$X \sim B(N, p)$$

where *p* is the true (unknown) proportion of time spent in the activity.

**Pillar 2: Normal Approximation of the Binomial**
When *N* is large (typically *N* ≥ 30) and *p* is not too close to 0 or 1, the binomial distribution can be approximated by a normal distribution with:

- Mean: **μ = Np**
- Variance: **σ² = Npq**
- Standard deviation: **σ = √(Npq)**

**Pillar 3: Law of Large Numbers**
As the sample size *N* increases, the sample proportion **p̂ = X/N** converges to the true population proportion *p*. This is *why* work sampling works — the more random glances we make, the more reliable our percentage estimate becomes.

## 2.2 The Two Key Equations — Determinants of Sample Size

### Equation 1: Absolute Error (Precision)

The absolute error *E* of the sample proportion is given by:

$$E = Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p \cdot q}{N}}$$

This represents the **half-width of the confidence interval** around the estimated proportion *p̂*.

### Equation 2: Relative Error (Accuracy)

Often more useful in practice, the **relative error S** is the absolute error expressed as a percentage of the estimate:

$$S = \frac{E}{p} \times 100\% = Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{q}{p \cdot N}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-ready formulas** for all work sampling problems. Master these — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper.

| # | Formula | Description / Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $E = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p \cdot q}{N}}$ | Absolute error of proportion | When *N* is known, find precision |
| 2 | $S = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{q}{p \cdot N}} \times 100$ | Relative error in % | Most common in board problems |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot q}{E^{2}}$ | Required sample size (from absolute error) | When accuracy is given in absolute terms |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{4 \cdot p \cdot q}{L^{2}}$ | Sample size when Z=2 (95.45% conf.) | Most frequently used in KTU |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot q}{p \cdot S^{2}} \times 10^{4}$ | Sample size from relative error % | When accuracy is given as ±S% |
| 6 | $\sigma = \sqrt{N \cdot p \cdot q}$ | Standard deviation of observation count | For plotting control charts |
| 7 | $L = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p \cdot q}{N}}$ | Allowable error at Z=2 | Simplified precision formula |
| 8 | $\hat{p} = \dfrac{X}{N}$ | Sample proportion estimator | Direct calculation from data |
| 9 | $C.I. = \hat{p} \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}\hat{q}}{N}}$ | Confidence interval for true proportion | Reporting results |
| 10 | $N_{total} = N_{1} + N_{2} + \cdots$ | Combined sample size for multi-activity studies | When studying several activities |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When sample size *N* is unknown and *p* is also unknown, use the **conservative estimate** *p* = 0.5 (i.e., *p*·*q* is maximized at 0.25). This gives the *largest required sample size* and is the safest assumption.

## 2.4 The Random Observation Schedule — Why Randomness Matters

> [!WARNING]
> **Bias Alert:** If observations are not truly random, the work sampling study is **fundamentally flawed**. A biased schedule will systematically over- or under-represent certain times of day, activities, or workers.

**Methods to generate random observations:**

1. **Random Number Tables** — Classical method, classical bookwork.
2. **Calculator/Computer-Generated Random Numbers** — Modern practical method.
3. **Pre-planned Random Time Schedule** — Pre-determine 200 random instants in advance, then conduct observations at those moments.
4. **Stratified Random Sampling** — Divide the workday into strata (morning/afternoon/evening) and take proportional random samples from each.

**Practical consideration:** A common technique is to take observations at **irregular intervals** (e.g., 7 min, 12 min, 19 min, 4 min apart...) so that the worker cannot anticipate when the observer will look — this is called the **"unpredictable interval" method**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Machine Utilization Studies** in CNC shops and FMS cells.
- **Crew Size Balancing** in construction sites and shipyards.
- **Delay Allowance Determination** in garment industries.
- **Performance Rating Validation** when comparing stopwatch study results.
- **Hospital Staffing Decisions** for nurses and doctors.
- **Quality Control Sampling** in process industries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Statistical Pillars

Work sampling rests on three foundational pillars of probability theory:

**Pillar 1: Binomial Distribution of Observations**
Each random observation is a Bernoulli trial — it either belongs to the category of interest ("success") or it does not ("failure"). For *N* independent random observations, the count *X* of "sub-activities" follows a **Binomial Distribution**:

$$X \sim B(N, p)$$

where *p* is the true (unknown) proportion of time spent in the activity.

**Pillar 2: Normal Approximation of the Binomial**
When *N* is large (typically *N* ≥ 30) and *p* is not too close to 0 or 1, the binomial distribution can be approximated by a normal distribution with:

- Mean: **μ = Np**
- Variance: **σ² = Npq**
- Standard deviation: **σ = √(Npq)**

**Pillar 3: Law of Large Numbers**
As the sample size *N* increases, the sample proportion **p̂ = X/N** converges to the true population proportion *p*. This is *why* work sampling works — the more random glances we make, the more reliable our percentage estimate becomes.

## 2.2 The Two Key Equations — Determinants of Sample Size

### Equation 1: Absolute Error (Precision)

The absolute error *E* of the sample proportion is given by:

$$E = Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p \cdot q}{N}}$$

This represents the **half-width of the confidence interval** around the estimated proportion *p̂*.

### Equation 2: Relative Error (Accuracy)

Often more useful in practice, the **relative error S** is the absolute error expressed as a percentage of the estimate:

$$S = \frac{E}{p} \times 100\% = Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{q}{p \cdot N}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-ready formulas** for all work sampling problems. Master these — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper.

| # | Formula | Description / Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $E = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p \cdot q}{N}}$ | Absolute error of proportion | When *N* is known, find precision |
| 2 | $S = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{q}{p \cdot N}} \times 100$ | Relative error in % | Most common in board problems |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot q}{E^{2}}$ | Required sample size (from absolute error) | When accuracy is given in absolute terms |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{4 \cdot p \cdot q}{L^{2}}$ | Sample size when Z=2 (95.45% conf.) | Most frequently used in KTU |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot q}{p \cdot S^{2}} \times 10^{4}$ | Sample size from relative error % | When accuracy is given as ±S% |
| 6 | $\sigma = \sqrt{N \cdot p \cdot q}$ | Standard deviation of observation count | For plotting control charts |
| 7 | $L = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p \cdot q}{N}}$ | Allowable error at Z=2 | Simplified precision formula |
| 8 | $\hat{p} = \dfrac{X}{N}$ | Sample proportion estimator | Direct calculation from data |
