| 9 | $C.I. = \hat{p} \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}\hat{q}}{N}}$ | Confidence interval for true proportion | Reporting results |
| 10 | $N_{total} = N_{1} + N_{2} + \cdots$ | Combined sample size for multi-activity studies | When studying several activities |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When sample size *N* is unknown and *p* is also unknown, use the **conservative estimate** *p* = 0.5 (i.e., *p*·*q* is maximized at 0.25). This gives the *largest required sample size* and is the safest assumption.

## 2.4 The Random Observation Schedule — Why Randomness Matters

> [!WARNING]
> **Bias Alert:** If observations are not truly random, the work sampling study is **fundamentally flawed**. A biased schedule will systematically over- or under-represent certain times of day, activities, or workers.

**Methods to generate random observations:**

1. **Random Number Tables** — Classical method, classical bookwork.
2. **Calculator/Computer-Generated Random Numbers** — Modern practical method.
3. **Pre-planned Random Time Schedule** — Pre-determine 200 random instants in advance, then conduct observations at those moments.
4. **Stratified Random Sampling** — Divide the workday into strata (morning/afternoon/evening) and take proportional random samples from each.

**Practical consideration:** A common technique is to take observations at **irregular intervals** (e.g., 7 min, 12 min, 19 min, 4 min apart...) so that the worker cannot anticipate when the observer will look — this is called the **"unpredictable interval" method**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Machine Utilization Studies** in CNC shops and FMS cells.
- **Crew Size Balancing** in construction sites and shipyards.
- **Delay Allowance Determination** in garment industries.
- **Performance Rating Validation** when comparing stopwatch study results.
- **Hospital Staffing Decisions** for nurses and doctors.
- **Quality Control Sampling** in process industries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sample-Size Formula from First Principles

The **core derivation** that KTU examiners love to test begins with the standard error of a proportion in a binomial distribution.

### Step 1 — Start with the Binomial Variance

For *N* Bernoulli trials with success probability *p*, the variance of the count of successes is:

$$\sigma_{X}^{2} = N \cdot p \cdot q$$

### Step 2 — Convert to the Variance of the Proportion

Since the sample proportion is $\hat{p} = X/N$, its variance is:

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_{X}^{2}}{N^{2}} = \frac{N \cdot p \cdot q}{N^{2}} = \frac{p \cdot q}{N}$$

### Step 3 — Standard Error of the Proportion

Taking the square root:

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p \cdot q}{N}}$$

### Step 4 — Express the Allowable Error

By the central limit theorem, the 95.45% confidence interval for *p̂* is *p̂* ± 2σ*p̂*. The **allowable error E** is therefore:

$$E = 2 \cdot \sigma_{\hat{p}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p \cdot q}{N}}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for N

Squaring both sides:

$$E^{2} = 4 \cdot \frac{p \cdot q}{N}$$

Rearranging:

$$N = \frac{4 \cdot p \cdot q}{E^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently derived formula in KTU exams** for work sampling. The same logic applied to the relative error *S* (where *S* = *E*/*p* × 100) gives:
> 
> $$N = \frac{4 \cdot q}{p \cdot S^{2}} \times 10^{4}$$
> 
> after multiplying by 10000 to convert the percentage to a fraction.

## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU-Style Problem (Full Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Model QP Pattern):**
> A work sampling study was conducted on a lathe operator to determine the proportion of time spent on actual machining. Out of **400** random observations, the operator was found to be machining in **120** observations.
> 
> Calculate:
> (i) The estimated proportion *p̂* of time spent on machining.
> (ii) The standard error of the proportion.
> (iii) The 95.45% confidence interval for the true proportion.
> (iv) The relative accuracy of the study.
> 
> *(Assume Z = 2 for 95.45% confidence.)*

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- Total observations: **N = 400**
- Observations in "machining" category: **X = 120**
- Confidence level: **95.45% → Z = 2**

**Step 1: Compute the sample proportion**

$$\hat{p} = \frac{X}{N} = \frac{120}{400} = 0.30$$

Therefore:
$$q = 1 - p = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70$$

**Step 2: Compute the standard error of the proportion**

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p \cdot q}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.30 \times 0.70}{400}}$$

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.21}{400}} = \sqrt{0.000525} = 0.02291$$

**Step 3: Compute the absolute error (E) and confidence interval**

$$E = Z \cdot \sigma_{\hat{p}} = 2 \times 0.02291 = 0.04582$$

The 95.45% confidence interval for the true proportion is:

$$\hat{p} \pm E = 0.30 \pm 0.04582$$

$$C.I. = (0.2542, \, 0.3458) \text{ or } (25.42\%, \, 34.58\%)$$

**Step 4: Compute the relative accuracy (S)**

$$S = \frac{E}{p} \times 100\% = \frac{0.04582}{0.30} \times 100\% = 15.27\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The estimate says the operator machines **30% of the time**, with a margin of error of about **±4.58 percentage points** at 95.45% confidence. The relative accuracy is **±15.27%** — this is the *error as a fraction of the estimate*. If ±10% accuracy were required, we would need to increase *N* substantially.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sample Size vs. Accuracy Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> The following table illustrates how the required sample size *N* changes with the desired relative accuracy *S* for a fixed *p* = 0.30 (so *q* = 0.70) at Z = 2.

| Required Relative Accuracy (S %) | Formula Used | Sample Size N (rounded up) |
|----------------------------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| ±1% | $N = \dfrac{4q}{p \cdot S^{2}} \times 10^{4}$ | $N = \dfrac{4 \times 0.70}{0.30 \times 1^{2}} \times 10^{4} = 93{,}333$ |
| ±5% | Same | $N = \dfrac{4 \times 0.70}{0.30 \times 25} \times 10^{4} = 3{,}733$ |
| ±10% | Same | $N = \dfrac{4 \times 0.70}{0.30 \times 100} \times 10^{4} = 933$ |
| ±15% | Same | $N = \dfrac{4 \times 0.70}{0.30 \times 225} \times 10^{4} = 415$ |
| ±20% | Same | $N = \dfrac{4 \times 0.70}{0.30 \times 400} \times 10^{4} = 233$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** The *p*·*q* product is **maximized at *p* = 0.5**, where *p·q* = 0.25. If you have no prior information about *p*, ALWAYS use *p* = 0.5 to get the *largest* (safest) sample size.

## 3.4 Multi-Activity Studies — Combining Sample Sizes

When a study classifies observations into **k** different activity categories simultaneously, the sample size is determined by the **category that requires the largest N**. This is because each observation is recorded into *all k* categories (e.g., a worker can be "machining" AND "standing" simultaneously — multi-class recording).

**Procedure:**

1. Compute *Nᵢ* for each activity *i* using $N_i = \dfrac{4 p_i q_i}{E_i^{2}}$.
2. Take **N = max(N₁, N₂, ..., Nₖ)** as the required sample size.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The activity with the *smallest* proportion *p* generally requires the *largest* N. This is because for small *p*, *q* ≈ 1, and the relative error grows. **Always pilot-study first** to get a rough estimate of each *pᵢ*, then compute the final N.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Work Sampling Procedure — Step-by-Step Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Objectives of Study] --> B[Step 2: Identify Activity Categories]
    B --> C[Step 3: Pilot Study - Rough Estimate of p]
    C --> D[Step 4: Decide Desired Accuracy S and Confidence Z]
    D --> E[Step 5: Calculate Required Sample Size N]
    E --> F[Step 6: Design Random Observation Schedule]
    F --> G[Step 7: Take N Random Observations]
    G --> H[Step 8: Tally Observations by Category]
    H --> I[Step 9: Compute Sample Proportions p-hat]
    I --> J[Step 10: Calculate Confidence Intervals]
    J --> K[Step 11: Verify Achieved Accuracy]
    K --> L{Accuracy Met?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Step 12: Draw Conclusions and Report]
    L -- No --> N[Take Additional Observations]
    N --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Start at node A and follow the flow downward. The decision diamond at node L is the **validation gate** — if the achieved accuracy is not met, additional observations must be taken and the analysis re-run.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Work Sampling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        P1[Define Activities] --> P2[Set Confidence Level Z]
        P2 --> P3[Set Desired Accuracy S]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATION BLOCK]
        Q1[Apply Formula N = 4q / pS² × 10⁴] --> Q2[Compute Sample Size N]
        Q2 --> Q3[Generate Random Times via RNG]
    end

    subgraph OBSERVE[OBSERVATION BLOCK]
        R1[Observer Visits Site] --> R2[Record Activity at Random Instant]
        R2 --> R3[Tally Counts per Category]
    end

    subgraph ANALYZE[ANALYSIS BLOCK]
        S1[Compute p-hat = X / N] --> S2[Compute Standard Error sigma]
        S2 --> S3[Build Confidence Interval p-hat ± 2sigma]
        S3 --> S4[Verify Achieved Accuracy]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        T1[Activity Percentages] --> T2[Standard Times and Allowances]
        T2 --> T3[Management Decision Report]
    end

    INPUT --> COMPUTE
    COMPUTE --> OBSERVE
    OBSERVE --> ANALYZE
    ANALYZE --> OUTPUT
```

## 4.3 The Accuracy vs. Sample Size Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[For p = 0.5, q = 0.5] --> B[S = ±1% : N = 10000]
    A --> C[S = ±5% : N = 400]
    A --> D[S = ±10% : N = 100]
    A --> E[S = ±20% : N = 25]

    B --> F[Very High Accuracy - Costly]
    C --> G[High Accuracy - Typical Research]
    D --> H[Moderate Accuracy - Routine Study]
    E --> I[Low Accuracy - Pilot Study Only]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip:** The required sample size *N* is **inversely proportional to the square of the desired accuracy S**. This means: doubling the accuracy (halving S) requires **four times** as many observations. This is why extremely high accuracy is prohibitively expensive in practice — KTU problems often test this trade-off concept.

## 4.4 Activity Classification — Block Diagram for Multi-Activity Recording

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single Random Observation Instant] --> B{Worker Activity}

    B --> C1[Category 1: Productive Work]
    B --> C2[Category 2: Idle / Waiting]
    B --> C3[Category 3: Personal Time]
    B --> C4[Category 4: Delay / Breakdown]
    B --> C5[Category 5: Getting Ready]

    C1 --> D[Tally Count X1]
    C2 --> E[Tally Count X2]
    C3 --> F[Tally Count X3]
    C4 --> G[Tally Count X4]
    C5 --> H[Tally Count X5]

    D --> I[Compute p1 = X1 / N]
    E --> J[Compute p2 = X2 / N]
    F --> K[Compute p3 = X3 / N]
    G --> L[Compute p4 = X4 / N]
    H --> M[Compute p5 = X5 / N]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** The percentages across all categories should sum to 100% (or 1.0). If they do not, you have a recording error. Always perform this sanity check at the end of any work sampling study.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
> 
> **Define Work Sampling. List any FOUR advantages of Work Sampling over continuous stopwatch time study.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Work Sampling is a statistical technique used to determine the proportion of time spent by workers/machines on various activities by taking a large number of **instantaneous, random observations** and applying probability theory to estimate the true time proportions.

**Any FOUR Advantages (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Economical:** Less observer time is required compared to continuous time study — cost savings of 50–80% are typical.
2. **Suitable for non-repetitive work:** Can be applied to long-cycle, irregular, or mental work where stopwatch study is impractical.
3. **Multiple subjects studied simultaneously:** A single observer can record data on several workers/machines at the same observation instant.
4. **Less stressful for the worker:** Worker is observed only momentarily, reducing the "Hawthorne effect" (worker changing behavior when continuously watched).
5. **Provides delay allowances directly:** The proportion of delay time emerges naturally as one of the activity categories.
6. **No timing instrument fatigue:** Observer does not need to operate a stopwatch continuously.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**
> 
> **State the Law of Large Numbers. Explain its relevance to Work Sampling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement of the Law (1.5 Marks):**
The **Law of Large Numbers** states that as the number of independent random observations *N* increases, the **sample proportion p̂** converges to the **true population proportion p** with probability tending to 1.

**Relevance to Work Sampling (1.5 Marks):**
In work sampling, each random observation is a Bernoulli trial representing the worker's state at a single instant. By taking a *sufficiently large* number of random observations, the percentage of observations in each activity category becomes a **reliable estimate of the true time proportion** spent in that activity. This is what makes the method statistically valid — without this law, work sampling would have no theoretical foundation.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Work Sampling study. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** In a work sampling study of an assembly worker, **600** random observations were taken. The worker was found to be **assembling** in **180** observations. Calculate: **(i)** the sample proportion, **(ii)** the standard error at 95% confidence (Z = 1.96), **(iii)** the 95% confidence interval, and **(iv)** the relative accuracy of the study. **(7 Marks)**

#### (a) Model Answer — Procedure for Work Sampling Study (7 Marks)

1. **Define the Objective (1 Mark):**
   Clearly state why the study is being conducted — e.g., to determine machine utilization, to set delay allowance, or to measure standard time for a long-cycle activity.

2. **Identify and Define Activity Categories (1 Mark):**
   List mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE) categories such as: Productive Work, Idle, Personal, Delay, Setup. Each observation must be classifiable into exactly one category.

3. **Conduct a Pilot Study (1 Mark):**
   Take 50–100 preliminary observations to get a rough estimate of each proportion *pᵢ*. This helps determine the final sample size and reveals operational issues.

4. **Determine Required Sample Size N (1 Mark):**
   For each category, compute $N_i = \dfrac{4 p_i q_i}{E_i^{2}}$ or $N_i = \dfrac{4 q_i}{p_i S_i^{2}} \times 10^{4}$, then take **N = max(Nᵢ)**.

5. **Design the Random Observation Schedule (1 Mark):**
   Use a random number table or RNG to generate N random observation times distributed over the study period. Use **unpredictable intervals** to prevent the worker from anticipating the observation.

6. **Take and Record Observations (1 Mark):**
   Conduct the N observations exactly at the scheduled random instants. Each observation is recorded as a tally mark under the appropriate activity category. The observer must not interrupt the work.

7. **Analyze Data and Report (1 Mark):**
   Compute sample proportions, standard errors, confidence intervals, and relative accuracy. Compare achieved accuracy with the target. If inadequate, take additional observations. Prepare the final report with management recommendations.

#### (b) Model Answer — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- N = 600 observations
- X = 180 observations in "assembling"
- Z = 1.96 (for 95% confidence)

**Step (i) Sample proportion (1.5 Marks):**

$$\hat{p} = \frac{X}{N} = \frac{180}{600} = 0.30$$

$$q = 1 - p = 0.70$$

**Step (ii) Standard error at 95% confidence (2 Marks):**

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p \cdot q}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.30 \times 0.70}{600}}$$

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.21}{600}} = \sqrt{0.00035} = 0.01871$$

**Step (iii) 95% Confidence interval (2 Marks):**

$$E = Z \cdot \sigma_{\hat{p}} = 1.96 \times 0.01871 = 0.03668$$

$$\hat{p} \pm E = 0.30 \pm 0.0367$$

$$C.I. = (0.2633, \, 0.3367) \text{ or } (26.33\%, \, 33.67\%)$$

**Step (iv) Relative accuracy (1.5 Marks):**

$$S = \frac{E}{p} \times 100\% = \frac{0.03668}{0.30} \times 100\% = 12.23\%$$

**Conclusion (Optional):** The worker spends **30% of the time** on assembly with **±12.23%** relative accuracy at 95% confidence.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]**
> 
> **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the types of Work Sampling studies. Discuss the **3σ control limits** concept used to verify the consistency of data during a work sampling study. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A foreman wants to estimate the proportion of time a machine is **idle** with an accuracy of **±5%** at 95.45% confidence. A pilot study of 100 observations showed the machine idle in **30** observations. Calculate: **(i)** the pilot estimate of *p*, **(ii)** the required number of observations for the final study, and **(iii)** the number of additional observations needed beyond the pilot. **(7 Marks)**

#### (a) Model Answer — Types of Work Sampling and Control Limits (7 Marks)

**Types of Work Sampling Studies (4 Marks):**

1. **Activity Sampling (Men Study):** Determines the proportion of time workers spend in various defined activities. Used to balance workloads, establish rest allowances, and measure productive time.

2. **Machine/Equipment Utilization Study:** Determines the percentage of time a machine is running, idle, under repair, or being set up. Used to justify equipment purchases and improve capacity planning.

3. **Time/Location Study:** Examines the proportion of time spent in different locations or on different operations. Used in workflow analysis and layout planning.

4. **Performance Sampling:** A variation where the observer also rates the worker's performance on a scale (e.g., standard, slow, fast) at each random instant. Combines activity data with performance rating.

5. **Method Study Sampling:** Studies the proportion of time spent using different methods or procedures. Used to evaluate standardization efforts.

**3σ Control Limits Concept (3 Marks):**

In an ongoing work sampling study, the cumulative count of "activity" observations can be plotted on a control chart to detect **non-random trends** (e.g., a worker slowing down because they know they are being observed). The **3-sigma control limits** are:

- **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** $UCL = N \cdot p + 3\sqrt{N \cdot p \cdot q}$
- **Center Line (CL):** $CL = N \cdot p$
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** $LCL = N \cdot p - 3\sqrt{N \cdot p \cdot q}$

If the cumulative count falls **outside** the UCL or LCL, the study is considered to be **out of statistical control**, and the cause must be investigated (often, the worker has changed behavior).

#### (b) Model Answer — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Desired relative accuracy: S = ±5%
- Confidence: 95.45% → Z = 2
- Pilot study: N₀ = 100 observations
- Pilot "idle" count: X₀ = 30

**Step (i) Pilot estimate of p (2 Marks):**

$$\hat{p} = \frac{X_0}{N_0} = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30$$

$$q = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70$$

**Step (ii) Required sample size for final study (3 Marks):**

Using the relative accuracy formula:

$$N = \frac{4 \cdot q}{p \cdot S^2} \times 10^4$$

$$N = \frac{4 \times 0.70}{0.30 \times (5)^2} \times 10^4$$

$$N = \frac{2.80}{0.30 \times 25} \times 10^4 = \frac{2.80}{7.50} \times 10^4$$

$$N = 0.37333 \times 10^4 = 3733.33$$

Since *N* must be an integer, round **up**:

$$N = 3734 \text{ observations}$$

**Step (iii) Additional observations needed (2 Marks):**

$$N_{additional} = N - N_0 = 3734 - 100 = 3634 \text{ additional observations}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always round UP the sample size.** If you compute N = 933.33, the answer is **934**, not 933. Under-rounding reduces the achieved accuracy below the target. *(−1 mark if missed)*
> 2. **Use *p* = 0.5 if no pilot data is available.** Many students forget this and lose a mark for not addressing the case of an unknown *p*. *(−1 mark if missed)*
> 3. **State units in confidence interval answers.** Write the CI as (0.263, 0.337) **or** (26.3%, 33.7%), not just the decimal numbers.
> 4. **Distinguish absolute error E from relative error S.** KTU questions often specify one and students substitute the other — read the question twice!
> 5. **In multi-activity problems, take N = max(Nᵢ).** Students sometimes sum or average the *Nᵢ* values, which is mathematically incorrect.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Work Sampling (Module 4)**

**Core Concept (Must Know):**
- [ ] Work Sampling is a **statistical, observation-based** work measurement technique based on the **Law of Large Numbers** and **binomial probability**.
- [ ] Pioneered by **L.H.C. Tippett (1935)**; also called **Activity Sampling, Ratio Delay Study, or Snap-Reading Method**.
- [ ] Used when cycle times are long, work is non-repetitive, or many subjects must be studied simultaneously.

**Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
- [ ] $\hat{p} = X / N$ — sample proportion.
- [ ] $E = 2\sqrt{pq/N}$ — absolute error (at Z = 2).
- [ ] $S = (E/p) \times 100$ — relative error in %.
- [ ] $N = 4pq/L^{2}$ — sample size from absolute error.
- [ ] $N = (4q / pS^{2}) \times 10^{4}$ — sample size from relative error %.

**Statistical Constants (Must Remember):**
- [ ] Z = 2 corresponds to **95.45% confidence**.
- [ ] Z = 1.96 corresponds to **95% confidence**.
- [ ] Z = 3 corresponds to **99.73% confidence**.
- [ ] *p·q* is maximized at *p* = 0.5, where *p·q* = **0.25** (use this for conservative N).

**Procedure Steps (In Order):**
1. Define objectives → 2. Identify activity categories → 3. Pilot study → 4. Decide accuracy & confidence → 5. Calculate N → 6. Random schedule → 7. Take observations → 8. Tally → 9. Compute p̂ → 10. Confidence interval → 11. Verify accuracy → 12. Report.

**Important Don'ts:**
- [ ] Don't take observations at regular intervals (introduces bias).
- [ ] Don't forget to round **N up** to the next integer.
- [ ] Don't use *p* = 0.5 if a pilot estimate is already available.
- [ ] Don't confuse **absolute** error (E) with **relative** error (S).

**Engineering Applications (for viva/short answers):**
- [ ] Machine utilization, delay allowance, crew size, hospital staffing, performance rating validation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Sample Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Sample Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Determination of Sample Size** is the statistical procedure of calculating the minimum number of observations, measurements, or trials required from a population to estimate a parameter (such as a process mean, proportion, or variance) with a pre-specified level of **confidence** and a permissible **margin of error** (precision).

In the context of **Work System Design** (PCIET403), this concept is foundational for:
- **Time Studies** (determining the number of cycles to observe per element)
- **Work Sampling Studies** (determining the number of random observations)
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** (determining subgroup sizes)
- **Process Capability Analysis** (Cpk, Ppk)
- **Acceptance Sampling Plans** (single, double, multiple sampling)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes both *theoretical derivations* of sample size formulas and their *practical industrial application*. Students must master the distinction between *known population standard deviation* (Z-distribution) and *unknown population standard deviation* (t-distribution) cases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tea Tasting Problem"**
>
> Imagine you are the Quality Head of a tea factory producing 10,000 tea bags per day. You cannot taste-test every single bag (that would be impractical and wasteful). So you randomly pull a few bags and taste them to *infer* the quality of the entire day's production. The question is: **how many bags do you need to taste?**
>
> - If you taste only **2 bags**, your estimate of average quality will be wildly imprecise — you might get lucky or unlucky.
> - If you taste **200 bags**, your estimate will be very precise but you have wasted time and resources.
> - The *optimum number* depends on three things:
>   1. **How confident** you want to be (95%? 99%?)
>   2. **How much error** you can tolerate (±0.5 grams? ±2 grams?)
>   3. **How variable** the population is (homogeneous tea vs. mixed quality)
>
> This trade-off — confidence, error, and variability — is the *heart* of sample size determination.

## 1.3 Key Standard Statistical Parameters

The following standardized values are **bolded** because they are universally required in KTU examinations:

- **Confidence Level (1 − α):** The probability that the true population parameter lies within the calculated confidence interval. Standard values: **90% (Z = 1.645)**, **95% (Z = 1.96)**, **99% (Z = 2.576)**.
- **Significance Level (α):** Probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis (Type I error). **α = 0.05** (most common), **α = 0.01**.
- **Margin of Error (E):** Half-width of the confidence interval; the maximum acceptable estimation error.
- **Z<sub>α/2</sub>:** Critical value from the standard normal distribution corresponding to a two-tailed test at significance level α.
- **t<sub>α/2, n−1</sub>:** Critical value from Student's t-distribution with *n − 1* degrees of freedom.
- **Effect Size (δ or Δ):** The smallest meaningful difference you wish to detect between groups or from a target value.
- **Statistical Power (1 − β):** Probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis (avoiding Type II error). **Standard power = 0.80 (β = 0.20)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Framework

The determination of sample size rests upon the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, which states that the sampling distribution of the sample mean approaches a normal distribution as *n* increases, regardless of the population's underlying distribution (provided *n ≥ 30*).

### 2.1.1 Underlying Logic Steps

1. **Define the Parameter of Interest:** Identify whether we are estimating a *mean* (μ) or a *proportion* (p). This dictates which formula family to use.

2. **Specify the Confidence Level (1 − α):** Choose how certain we want to be. Higher confidence → larger sample size.

3. **Quantify the Acceptable Error (E):** Decide the maximum tolerable deviation between the sample statistic and the true parameter. Smaller error → larger sample size.

4. **Estimate Variability (σ or p):** Obtain a *prior* estimate of population standard deviation (σ) or proportion (p) from pilot studies, historical data, or literature.

5. **Apply the Appropriate Formula:** Use the derived equation linking *n*, *Z*, *σ*, and *E* (or their t/p counterparts).

6. **Apply Finite Population Correction (FPC):** If the sample size exceeds 5% of the population (n/N > 0.05), reduce *n* using the FPC factor.

7. **Round Up to Integer:** Always round the computed *n* **upward** to the next whole number to preserve the desired precision.

8. **Validate with Sensitivity Analysis:** Re-check the formula by plugging the final *n* back and confirming that the resulting *E* is within the acceptable range.

### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind the Formulas

The confidence interval for a population mean (σ known) is:

$$\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

For this interval to have a half-width no greater than *E*, we require:

$$Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq E$$

Squaring both sides and solving for *n* yields the canonical formula. The same logic — bounding the standard error — generates the proportion formula.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Scenario | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Mean, σ known** | $n = \left( \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^{2}$ | Uses Z-distribution; population SD known |
| 2 | **Mean, σ unknown** | $n = \left( \dfrac{t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot s}{E} \right)^{2}$ | **Iterative** procedure; start with Z, refine using t |
| 3 | **Proportion, p unknown** | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | When *p* is unknown, use **p = 0.5** (most conservative) |
| 4 | **Proportion, p known** | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | Plug in known historical proportion |
| 5 | **Two Independent Means (equal n)** | $n_{\text{per group}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma^{2} \cdot (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}}{(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})^{2}}$ | For comparing two treatments; assumes equal variance |
| 6 | **Two Independent Proportions** | $n_{\text{per group}} = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot [p_{1}(1-p_{1}) + p_{2}(1-p_{2})]}{(p_{1} - p_{2})^{2}}$ | Hypothesis-testing context |
| 7 | **Finite Population Correction** | $n_{\text{adj}} = \dfrac{n}{1 + \dfrac{n-1}{N}}$ | Apply when *n/N > 0.05* |
| 8 | **Work Sampling Formula (Barnes)** | $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{L^{2}}$ | *L* = absolute precision (in percentage points) |
| 9 | **Time Study Sample Size** | $n = \left( \dfrac{Z \cdot s}{A \cdot \bar{x}} \right)^{2}$ | *A* = allowable error as % of mean |
| 10 | **Sample Size for Variance (χ²)** | $n = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma^{4}}{E^{2}} \cdot (Z_{\alpha/2})^{2} + 1$ (approx.) | For estimating population variance with precision E |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In Question 3 (proportion with unknown *p*), if no prior estimate is available, KTU examiners expect students to use **p = 0.5**, which maximizes *p(1−p) = 0.25* and yields the most conservative (largest) sample size.

## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Applications

- **Manufacturing:** Determining the number of units to inspect in an acceptance sampling plan (ANSI/ASQ Z1.4).
- **Ergonomics / Work Physiology:** Calculating the number of workers to measure for a study on lifting capacity.
- **Quality Engineering:** Determining subgroup size in X-bar and R control charts (typically n = 4 or 5).
- **Service Industry:** Work sampling studies to determine the proportion of time a server spends on different activities in a restaurant.
- **Healthcare Systems Engineering:** Estimating average patient wait time with a pre-specified margin of error.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sample Size for Mean (σ Known)

**Starting Point:** The (1 − α) × 100% confidence interval for the population mean μ, when σ is known, is:

$$\bar{x} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;\leq\; \mu \;\leq\; \bar{x} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Margin of Error:**
The half-width of the interval is defined as the margin of error *E*:

$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Set E Equal to the Desired Precision:**
We require the half-width to be at most *E*:

$$Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = E$$

**Step 3 — Isolate √n:**
Divide both sides by *E*, then multiply by √n:

$$\sqrt{n} = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Square Both Sides:**

$$n = \left( \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^{2} = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{2}}{E^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Round Up:** Take the ceiling of *n* to ensure the desired precision is met or exceeded.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sample Size for Proportion

**Starting Point:** The standard error of a sample proportion is:

$$SE(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

**Step 1 — Margin of Error for a Proportion:**

$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Square Both Sides:**

$$E^{2} = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \frac{p(1-p)}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for n:**

$$n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — When p is Unknown:** Substitute *p = 0.5* to obtain the *worst-case* (maximum) sample size, because *p(1 − p)* is maximized at *p = 0.5*:

$$n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot 0.25}{E^{2}} = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2}}{4 E^{2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sample Size for Two Independent Means (Hypothesis Testing)

**Scenario:** Testing H<sub>0</sub>: μ<sub>1</sub> = μ<sub>2</sub> versus H<sub>1</sub>: μ<sub>1</sub> ≠ μ<sub>2</sub>, with equal sample sizes *n* per group and known common variance σ².

**Step 1 — Test Statistic:**

$$Z = \frac{(\bar{X}_{1} - \bar{X}_{2}) - 0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{n} + \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{n}}} = \frac{\bar{X}_{1} - \bar{X}_{2}}{\sigma \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{n}}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Effect Size:** The true difference we wish to detect is *δ = μ<sub>1</sub> − μ<sub>2</sub>*.

**Step 3 — Type I Error Constraint:** Reject H<sub>0</sub> when *Z > Z<sub>α/2</sub>*. Under H<sub>1</sub>:

$$P\left(Z > Z_{\alpha/2} \mid \mu_{1} - \mu_{2} = \delta\right) = 1 - \beta \quad \text{(power)}$$

**Step 4 — Standardize the Effect:**

$$\frac{\delta - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma \sqrt{2/n}}{\sigma \sqrt{2/n}} = -Z_{\beta}$$

**Step 5 — Solve Algebraically:**

$$\frac{\delta}{\sigma \sqrt{2/n}} = Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}$$

**Step 6 — Square and Rearrange:**

$$\frac{\delta^{2} \cdot n}{2 \sigma^{2}} = (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Final Formula:**

$$n = \frac{2 \sigma^{2} (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}}{(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})^{2}} = \frac{2 \sigma^{2} (Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}}{\delta^{2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:**
> A Work System Design consultant is conducting a time study on a CNC machining operation. From a pilot study of 10 cycles, the sample standard deviation was *s = 4.2 seconds*. The consultant wants to estimate the true mean cycle time within *E = 1.0 second* with *95% confidence*. Determine the required sample size. If the daily production is *N = 200 units*, apply the Finite Population Correction.

**Step 1 — Set Up Parameters:**

$$\alpha = 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96, \quad s = 4.2 \text{ s}, \quad E = 1.0 \text{ s}, \quad N = 200$$

**Step 2 — First Approximation (use Z):**

$$n_{0} = \left( \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot s}{E} \right)^{2} = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 4.2}{1.0} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$1.96 \times 4.2 = 8.232$$

**Step 4 — Square the Result:**

$$n_{0} = (8.232)^{2} = 67.766$$

**Step 5 — Round Up:**

$$n_{0} = 68 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 6 — Iteration with t-Distribution:**
Since σ is unknown, refine using *t<sub>0.025, 67</sub>* ≈ **1.996** (from t-tables).

$$n_{1} = \left( \frac{1.996 \times 4.2}{1.0} \right)^{2} = (8.383)^{2} = 70.28 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{1} = 71$$

**Step 7 — Second Iteration:** Use *t<sub>0.025, 70</sub>* ≈ **1.994**:

$$n_{2} = \left( \frac{1.994 \times 4.2}{1.0} \right)^{2} = (8.375)^{2} = 70.14 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{2} = 71$$

**Step 8 — Convergence:** The iteration has converged at **n = 71 cycles**.

**Step 9 — Apply FPC:** Check if *n/N > 0.05*:

$$\frac{71}{200} = 0.355 > 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FPC is required.}$$

**Step 10 — Compute Adjusted n:**

$$n_{\text{adj}} = \frac{n}{1 + \dfrac{n-1}{N}} = \frac{71}{1 + \dfrac{70}{200}} = \frac{71}{1 + 0.35} = \frac{71}{1.35}$$

**Step 11 — Final Calculation:**

$$n_{\text{adj}} = 52.59 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{n_{\text{adj}} = 53 \text{ cycles}}$$

**Interpretation:** The consultant must observe **53 complete CNC cycles** to estimate the true mean cycle time within ±1 second at the 95% confidence level, after accounting for the finite population of 200 daily units.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example for Proportion (Work Sampling)

**Problem Statement:**
> A work-sampling study is being conducted at a hospital to determine the proportion of time nurses spend on direct patient care. A pilot study estimated *p̂ = 0.40*. The analyst requires a **95% confidence level** and an **absolute error L = 0.05 (5 percentage points)**. Compute the required number of random observations.

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters:**

$$Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96, \quad \hat{p} = 0.40, \quad L = 0.05$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Work Sampling Formula:**

$$n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{L^{2}} = \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot 0.40 \cdot 0.60}{(0.05)^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerator:**

$$(1.96)^{2} = 3.8416 \quad ; \quad 3.8416 \times 0.24 = 0.9220$$

**Step 4 — Compute Denominator:**

$$(0.05)^{2} = 0.0025$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$n = \frac{0.9220}{0.0025} = 368.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{n = 369 \text{ observations}}$$

**Interpretation:** The analyst must collect **369 randomly timed observations** of nurses to estimate the proportion of time spent on direct care within ±5% at 95% confidence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Sample Size Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define Research Objective"] --> B["Parameter of Interest?"]
    B -- Mean --> C{"Sigma Known?"}
    B -- Proportion --> D{"p Known?"}
    B -- Difference --> E["Two-Sample Test"]

    C -- Yes --> F["Use Z Formula: n equals Z alpha/2 squared times sigma squared over E squared"]
    C -- No --> G["Iterative: Start with Z, refine using t distribution"]

    D -- Yes --> H["Use p-hat in formula: n equals Z squared times p times 1 minus p over E squared"]
    D -- No --> I["Use p equals 0.5 for conservative estimate"]

    E --> J["Apply two-sample formula with Z alpha/2 and Z beta"]

    F --> K{"n over N greater than 0.05?"}
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K -- Yes --> L["Apply Finite Population Correction"]
    K -- No --> M["Skip FPC Step"]

    L --> N["Round n upward to next integer"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["Validate: Recompute E and confirm precision"]
    O --> P["Final Sample Size n"]
```

```mermaid
    B --> F["Higher confidence means larger n"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the term 'margin of error' in the context of sample size determination. How does it influence the required sample size?**

**Model Answer:**
The **margin of error (E)** is the maximum acceptable difference between the sample statistic (e.g., x̄ or p̂) and the true population parameter (μ or p), at a specified confidence level. It represents half the width of the confidence interval.

**Influence on sample size:** The sample size *n* is **inversely proportional to the square of E**. Therefore:
- Halving the margin of error (e.g., from ±4% to ±2%) **quadruples** the required sample size.
- Doubling the margin of error **quarters** the required sample size.

**Formula linkage:** For estimating a mean, $n \propto 1/E^{2}$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the formula for determining sample size when estimating a population proportion, and explain why *p = 0.5* is used when no prior estimate is available.**

**Model Answer:**

$$n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$$

**Why *p = 0.5*:** The product *p(1 − p)* is a concave-down quadratic function of *p*, attaining its **maximum value of 0.25 at p = 0.5**. Using *p = 0.5* therefore maximizes the numerator, producing the **most conservative (largest)** sample size. This guarantees that the desired precision is achieved regardless of the true (unknown) proportion. Any other value of *p* would yield a smaller *n* and risk under-sampling.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the formula for determining the sample size *n* required to estimate a population mean μ within a margin of error *E*, when the population standard deviation σ is known. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm wants to estimate the average daily output of a production line. Historical data indicate that the standard deviation of daily output is **σ = 12 units**. The firm requires the estimate to be within **±3 units** of the true mean with **99% confidence**. Calculate the required sample size. If the population consists of **N = 90 days** of records, apply the Finite Population Correction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. The population is either normal, or the sample size is large enough (n ≥ 30) for the CLT to apply.
2. The population standard deviation σ is known.
3. Observations are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.).
4. Sampling is simple random sampling (SRS).

**Derivation:**

The (1 − α) × 100% confidence interval for μ is:

$$\bar{X} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

The margin of error is:

$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\sqrt{n} = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\boxed{n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{2}}{E^{2}}}$$

Round *n* up to the next integer to ensure precision is met.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters:**

$$Z_{\alpha/2} = 2.576 \quad (\text{for } 99\% \text{ confidence}), \quad \sigma = 12, \quad E = 3, \quad N = 90$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{2} = (2.576)^{2} \cdot (12)^{2} = 6.6358 \times 144 = 955.55$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Denominator:**

$$E^{2} = (3)^{2} = 9$$

**Step 4 — Initial Sample Size:**

$$n_{0} = \frac{955.55}{9} = 106.17 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{0} = 107$$

**Step 5 — Check FPC Condition:**

$$\frac{n_{0}}{N} = \frac{107}{90} = 1.189 > 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FPC required.}$$

**Step 6 — Apply FPC:**

$$n_{\text{adj}} = \frac{n_{0}}{1 + \dfrac{n_{0} - 1}{N}} = \frac{107}{1 + \dfrac{106}{90}} = \frac{107}{1 + 1.1778} = \frac{107}{2.1778}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer:**

$$n_{\text{adj}} = 49.13 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{n_{\text{adj}} = 50 \text{ days}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the iterative procedure used to determine sample size when the population standard deviation σ is **unknown**. Why is the Z-based formula not directly applicable in this case? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A work-sampling study at an automotive assembly plant aims to estimate the proportion of time that workers spend in *value-added activities*. A pilot study of 100 observations gave p̂ = 0.30. The analyst wants the estimate to be within **±4%** with **95% confidence**. Determine the number of random observations required. If the study will run for 5 days with 200 observations per day possible, can it be completed in the planned duration? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Why Z is not directly applicable:**
When σ is unknown, the test/interval statistic follows Student's **t-distribution** with *n − 1* degrees of freedom, not the standard normal Z-distribution. Since *n* itself is the unknown we are trying to find, we cannot directly look up *t<sub>α/2, n−1</sub>* from a table.

**Iterative Procedure:**

**Iteration 1:** Start by using *Z<sub>α/2</sub>* as a conservative approximation:

$$n_{1} = \left( \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot s}{E} \right)^{2}$$

**Iteration 2:** Round *n<sub>1</sub>* up; look up *t<sub>α/2, n<sub>1</sub>−1</sub>* from the t-table; recompute:

$$n_{2} = \left( \frac{t_{\alpha/2,\,n_{1}-1} \cdot s}{E} \right)^{2}$$

**Iteration 3:** Repeat until *n<sub>k+1</sub> = n<sub>k</sub>*. Convergence typically occurs within 2–3 iterations.

**Rationale:** The t-critical value *decreases* as degrees of freedom increase, approaching *Z<sub>α/2</sub>*. Thus the iterative process converges to a stable, slightly larger *n* than the Z-based estimate, properly accounting for the additional uncertainty of estimating σ from the sample.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters:**

$$Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96, \quad \hat{p} = 0.30, \quad E = 0.04$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Proportion Formula:**

$$n = \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot 0.30 \cdot 0.70}{(0.04)^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerator:**

$$(1.96)^{2} = 3.8416 \quad ; \quad 3.8416 \times 0.21 = 0.8067$$

**Step 4 — Compute Denominator:**

$$(0.04)^{2} = 0.0016$$

**Step 5 — Final Sample Size:**

$$n = \frac{0.8067}{0.0016} = 504.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{n = 505 \text{ observations}}$$

**Step 6 — Feasibility Check:**

$$\text{Total observations in 5 days} = 5 \times 200 = 1000 \text{ observations}$$

Since 1000 ≥ 505, the study **can be completed** within the planned 5-day duration with surplus capacity for pilot reruns or contingencies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP:** Always use the **ceiling** function. Writing *n = 106.17* without rounding loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Missing the FPC step:** When *n/N > 0.05*, omitting the Finite Population Correction is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Z and t:** When σ is unknown, using Z throughout (without iterating to t) is a common error; you lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Wrong Z-value:** Using *Z = 1.96* for 99% confidence (should be *Z = 2.576*) costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always list the CLT assumption, SRS, and independence explicitly to earn full marks on derivation questions.
> 6. **P = 0.5 vs. p̂ = 0.5:** When the problem gives a pilot *p̂*, use **that value**; only use 0.5 if no prior information is given.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sample size is governed by four drivers:** confidence level (1−α), margin of error (E), population variability (σ or p), and population size (N).
- **Doubling the precision (halving E) quadruples *n*** because *n* is inversely proportional to *E²*.
- **Increasing confidence from 95% to 99%** increases *n* by a factor of *(2.576/1.96)² ≈ 1.73*.
- **For means:** Use **Z** if σ is known; use **iterative t** if σ is unknown.
- **For proportions:** Substitute **p = 0.5** when no prior estimate exists (most conservative).
- **FPC rule:** Apply only when *n/N > 0.05*; otherwise, the correction is negligible (<5%).
- **Always round UP:** The ceiling of *n* is the minimum integer that satisfies the precision requirement.
- **Two-sample formulas** require both **Z<sub>α/2</sub>** (Type I) and **Z<sub>β</sub>** (Type II / power). Standard power = 0.80 ⇒ *Z<sub>β</sub> = 0.84*.
- **Work sampling formula** is a direct application of the proportion formula, with *L* = absolute precision in decimal form.
- **Validation step:** Always recompute the resulting margin of error after rounding *n* to confirm it meets the specification.
- **KTU favorite trick question:** "What happens to *n* if confidence increases but error also increases?" — analyze each effect separately and net them out.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conducting Work Sampling Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conducting Work Sampling Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Work Sampling** is a statistical technique used in work measurement and method study to determine the **proportion of time** spent by workers, machines, or processes on various activities by taking a large number of **instantaneous, random, and discontinuous observations** over a period of time. It is governed by the laws of probability and the theory of sampling, allowing engineers to estimate activity ratios with a quantifiable level of **statistical confidence** without resorting to continuous stopwatch time studies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Reference:** *"Work sampling is a technique of taking a large number of instantaneous observations of a group of machines, processes, or workers, the results of which are used to determine the proportion of time spent in various activities."* — Based on Niebel & Freivalds, *Motion and Time Study*.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Used in the Study

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Observation** | A single instantaneous snapshot of what the worker/machine is doing at a randomly chosen moment. |
| **Random Observation** | An observation taken at a time chosen without bias — every instant has an equal chance of selection. |
| **Activity Category** | A predefined class of work (e.g., productive work, idle, delay, setup). |
| **Sample Size (n)** | Total number of observations required to achieve a desired level of accuracy and confidence. |
| **Confidence Level (1 − α)** | Probability that the true activity proportion lies within the stated error band; commonly **95%** or **99%**. |
| **Precision (Accuracy, E)** | Allowable error in the estimate, expressed as an absolute value (±E) or relative percentage. |
| **Activity Ratio / Proportion (p)** | Fraction of total observations that fall into a particular activity category. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Camera-Snapshot Method"

Imagine you want to know what a **busy chef** in a hotel kitchen does throughout an 8-hour shift. You cannot stand behind him with a stopwatch all day (that is *continuous time study*). Instead, you snap a **photograph** of him at **100 perfectly random instants** spread across the day. Out of 100 snapshots:

- 40 show him cutting vegetables → **40% Productive Work**
- 25 show him at the stove → **25% Cooking**
- 20 show him idle / talking → **20% Idle**
- 15 show him washing dishes → **15% Cleanup**

Because the snapshot instants were **truly random**, the **Law of Large Numbers** guarantees that these percentages very closely approximate the **true fraction of total time** he actually spent on each activity. Work sampling does precisely this for industrial workers and machines — at much larger scale (often **600 to 2000 observations** per study) and with **mathematically proven precision**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Work Sampling?** Unlike stopwatch time study, work sampling does NOT require the observer to be present continuously. It minimizes **observer interference**, reduces **worker awareness** (Hawthorne effect), and is **far less expensive** for long-duration studies spanning days or weeks.

## 1.4 When is Work Sampling Most Useful?

Work sampling is the preferred technique in the following situations, per KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Long-cycle operations** — where one job runs for hours or days (e.g., crane operation, foundry work).
2. **Group activities** — when multiple workers/machines are studied simultaneously.
3. **Delays and idle-time studies** — quantifying waiting, breakdowns, and non-productive periods.
4. **Machine utilization analysis** — determining % utilization of CNC, presses, etc.
5. **Workload determination** — establishing standard times indirectly for non-repetitive tasks.
6. **Performance rating justification** — supplementing conventional time studies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Phases of a Work Sampling Study

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures the conduct of a work sampling study into the following six sequential phases. Each phase has a specific purpose, statistical underpinning, and practical engineering output.

### Phase 1 — Define the Objectives and Activity Categories
- Clearly state **why** the study is being conducted (e.g., reduce idle time, justify manpower).
- Break the work into **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE) categories**.
- Typical categories in a manufacturing setup: **Productive work, Support work, Idle, Personal, Delay**.

### Phase 2 — Design the Observation Sheet
- Pre-print the **activity categories** and leave spaces for tally marks.
- Include study ID, observer name, date, shift, machine/worker ID.
- Sheet design must allow **rapid recording** so that one observation takes **< 10 seconds**.

### Phase 3 — Determine the Required Number of Observations (Sample Size)
- This is the **statistical heart** of work sampling.
- The sample size `n` is computed using the binomial proportion formula, derived in Section 3.

### Phase 4 — Plan the Observation Schedule (Randomization)
- Decide **when** and **how often** to observe over the total study period.
- Use a **random-number table** or computer-generated random times to schedule observations.
- Observations should be **spread across all shifts, days, and operators** to avoid bias.

### Phase 5 — Take the Observations and Record Data
- At each scheduled random instant, glance at the operator/machine and **classify** the activity.
- A single observation = **one tally mark** in one activity column.
- **Discipline**: do not classify based on what happened a moment ago — only what is observed at the *instant*.

### Phase 6 — Tabulate, Analyze and Report Results
- Compute proportions `p` for each activity.
- Compute **confidence intervals** to express precision.
- Compare against standards; recommend improvements.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these five formulas — they account for ~70% of numerical marks in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Size Formula** (absolute accuracy) | $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | $n$ = required observations; $Z$ = standard normal deviate for confidence; $p$ = estimated proportion; $E$ = allowable absolute error | Compute number of random observations needed for the study |
| 2 | **Sample Size Formula** (relative accuracy) | $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot (1-p)}{p \cdot S^{2}}$ | $S$ = allowable relative error, $S = E/p$ | Used when error is expressed as a percentage of $p$ |
| 3 | **Standard Error of Proportion** | $\sigma_{p} = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | $\sigma_{p}$ = standard error of the activity ratio | Quantifies the typical deviation of sample proportion from true value |
| 4 | **Confidence Interval (two-sided)** | $p \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | Interval within which the true proportion lies with confidence $1-\alpha$ | Reporting the precision of activity ratio estimate |
| 5 | **Total Study Length (days)** | $T = \dfrac{n}{r \cdot d \cdot m}$ | $T$ = number of days; $r$ = observations per day; $d$ = workers/machines studied; $m$ = observers | Convert sample size into calendar duration |

### 2.3 Standard Z-Values Used in Work Sampling (Memorize)

| Confidence Level $(1-\alpha)$ | Z-Value |
|---|---|
| **90%** | **1.645** |
| **95%** | **1.96** |
| **99%** | **2.576** |
| **99.9%** | **3.291** |

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions and Practical Approximations

1. **Unknown proportion (preliminary run)**: When $p$ is not known in advance, use $p = 0.5$, which **maximizes** the variance $p(1-p) = 0.25$. This gives the **most conservative (safe) sample size**.
2. **Pre-study pilot**: KTU recommends taking **100–200 preliminary observations** to estimate $p$, then re-computing $n$ using that estimate to reduce total observation effort.
3. **Minimum sample size rule of thumb**: Always $n \geq 384$ for a 95% confidence with ±5% precision when $p = 0.5$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Production System** uses work sampling to quantify *muda* (waste) by classifying operator movements into value-adding and non-value-adding categories.
- **Hospital administration** uses work sampling to determine how nurses allocate time across patient care, documentation, and administrative work.
- **Mining industry** uses it for equipment utilization studies (truck, shovel, drill utilization).
- **IT service desks** use it to study call-handling patterns and downtime distribution.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sample-Size Formula

The sample-size formula in work sampling is derived from the **binomial distribution** under large-sample (normal) approximation.

### Step 1 — Start with the Binomial Distribution

When we take $n$ independent random observations, each observation either falls in the activity of interest (probability $p$) or not (probability $1-p$). The number of "hits" follows the binomial distribution:

$$X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$$

with **mean** $\mu = np$ and **variance** $\sigma^{2} = np(1-p)$.

### Step 2 — Define the Sample Proportion

The sample proportion is $\hat{p} = X/n$. Its variance is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{p}) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{X}{n}\right) = \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{n^{2}} = \frac{np(1-p)}{n^{2}} = \frac{p(1-p)}{n}$$

Hence the **standard error** of the sample proportion is:

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Central Limit Theorem

For large $n$ (which is essential in work sampling — typically $n > 100$), $\hat{p}$ is approximately normally distributed:

$$\hat{p} \sim N\left(p, \frac{p(1-p)}{n}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Express the Confidence Interval

A two-sided confidence interval with confidence level $(1 - \alpha)$ is:

$$\hat{p} \pm Z_{1-\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

where $Z_{1-\alpha/2}$ is the standard normal critical value.

### Step 5 — Define the Precision (Allowable Error)

The half-width of the confidence interval is the **allowable error** $E$:

$$E = Z_{1-\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the Sample Size $n$

Squaring both sides:

$$E^{2} = Z^{2} \cdot \frac{p(1-p)}{n}$$

Rearranging to isolate $n$:

$$\boxed{n = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}}$$

> This is the **fundamental sample-size equation** for work sampling, derivable in any KTU examination.

### Step 3.2 — Worked Example 1 (Standard KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A factory manager wants to conduct a work sampling study to determine the percentage of time a CNC machine is idle. Preliminary observations indicate that the machine is idle approximately **40%** of the time. The manager wants the result to be **within ±4%** of the true value with **95% confidence**. Determine:
(a) The number of observations required.
(b) The standard error of the proportion.
(c) The 95% confidence interval.

**Given Data:**
- Estimated proportion: $p = 0.40$
- Allowable error: $E = 0.04$
- Confidence level: 95% → $Z = 1.96$

**Solution:**

**(a) Sample Size $n$:**

$$n = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$$

$$n = \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot (0.40)(0.60)}{(0.04)^{2}}$$

Compute the numerator step-by-step:
- $(1.96)^{2} = 3.8416$
- $p(1-p) = 0.40 \times 0.60 = 0.24$
- Numerator $= 3.8416 \times 0.24 = 0.921984$

Compute the denominator:
- $(0.04)^{2} = 0.0016$

Therefore:
$$n = \frac{0.921984}{0.0016} = 576.24$$

Since $n$ must be a whole number and we round **up** to be safe:

$$\boxed{n = 577 \text{ observations}}$$

**(b) Standard Error $\sigma_{\hat{p}}$:**

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.24}{577}}$$

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{0.0004159} = 0.02040$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\hat{p}} \approx 2.04\%}$$

**(c) 95% Confidence Interval:**

$$p \pm Z \cdot \sigma_{\hat{p}} = 0.40 \pm 1.96 \times 0.0204$$

$$= 0.40 \pm 0.0400 = (0.3600, 0.4400)$$

$$\boxed{\text{95% CI: } 36\% \text{ to } 44\%}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true proportion of idle time lies between **36% and 44%**, with our point estimate being 40%.

### Step 3.3 — Worked Example 2 (Using Relative Accuracy)

**Problem:** A supervisor wants to estimate the proportion of productive time of a worker with ±5% **relative accuracy** at 95% confidence. A pilot study of 100 observations shows productive work in 45 cases.

**Given:**
- $\hat{p} = 45/100 = 0.45$
- Relative accuracy $S = 0.05$
- $Z = 1.96$

**Solution:**

$$n = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot (1-p)}{p \cdot S^{2}}$$

$$n = \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot (1 - 0.45)}{0.45 \cdot (0.05)^{2}}$$

- Numerator: $(1.96)^{2} \times 0.55 = 3.8416 \times 0.55 = 2.1129$
- Denominator: $0.45 \times 0.0025 = 0.001125$

$$n = \frac{2.1129}{0.001125} = 1878.13$$

$$\boxed{n = 1879 \text{ observations}}$$

### Step 3.4 — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | $p$ | $E$ | $Z$ | Required $n$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unknown $p$, conservative | 0.50 | 0.05 | 1.96 | 384 | Pilot study recommended |
| Idle time, moderate accuracy | 0.40 | 0.04 | 1.96 | 577 | Used in Example 1 |
| Productive time, tight precision | 0.85 | 0.02 | 1.96 | 1225 | High precision demands more data |
| Rare activity (delay ≈ 5%) | 0.05 | 0.01 | 2.576 | 3151 | Very large sample needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Rare activities (small $p$) require **dramatically more** observations. Notice how a 5% activity needs **~5× more observations** than a 40% activity at the same precision.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Sequential Procedure for Conducting Work Sampling Study

The following block diagram maps the **complete six-phase procedural architecture** as specified in the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Phase 1 Define Objectives and Activity Categories"] --> B["Phase 2 Design Observation Sheet and Tally Format"]
    B --> C["Phase 3 Estimate Preliminary Proportion p via Pilot of 100 to 200 Observations"]
    C --> D{"Is p Already Known from Prior Data"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Phase 4 Compute Required Sample Size n Using Z p and E"]
    D -- No --> F["Phase 4a Use Conservative p equals 0.50 for Maximum Variance"]
    F --> E
    E --> G["Phase 5 Plan Random Observation Schedule using Random Number Tables"]
    G --> H["Phase 6 Conduct Random Observations Across Shifts and Days"]
    H --> I["Phase 7 Tabulate Tallies into Activity Frequencies"]
    I --> J["Phase 8 Compute Sample Proportions p hat for each Activity"]
    J --> K["Phase 9 Construct Confidence Intervals and Check Precision"]
    K --> L{"Is Precision Within E"}
    L -- No --> M["Phase 10 Take Additional Observations and Recompute"]
    M --> J
    L -- Yes --> N["Phase 11 Prepare Final Report with Recommendations"]
    N --> O["Output Standard Activity Ratios and Improvement Plan"]
```

```mermaid
    Input1["Input Worker Machine ID"] --> C1["Central Classifier Engine"]
    Input2["Input Random Time Generator"] --> C1
    Input3["Input Activity Category Codes"] --> C1
    C1 --> Output1["Output Tallied Observation Record"]
    Output1 --> Engine["Statistical Engine"]
    Engine --> Formula1["Compute p hat per Category"]
    Engine --> Formula2["Compute Standard Error sigma"]
    Engine --> Formula3["Compute Confidence Interval"]
    Formula1 --> Report["Final Analytical Report"]
    Formula2 --> Report
    Formula3 --> Report
    Report --> Decision["Decision Idle Time Below Threshold"]
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    Observing --> Classify: Snapshot at Random Instant
    Classify --> Productive: Operator Actively Working on Job

    Classify --> Personal: Break Tea Lunch
    Classify --> Delay: Machine Breakdown Quality Issue

```

```mermaid
    B --> C["Confidence Interval p hat plus or minus E"]

    D --> F["Engineering Decision Within Allowable Error"]
```
