# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define work sampling. List any two advantages of work sampling over continuous stopwatch time study.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Work sampling is a statistical technique in which a large number of instantaneous, random, and discontinuous observations are taken over a period of time to determine the **proportion of time** spent by workers or machines on various activities. (2 marks)

Two advantages over stopwatch time study:
1. **Reduced observer interference** — the worker is observed only at random instants, not continuously, minimizing the Hawthorne effect. (0.5 marks)
2. **Lower cost for long-duration studies** — multiple workers/machines can be studied simultaneously by one observer, making it economical for studies spanning days or weeks. (0.5 marks)

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is meant by "confidence level" and "precision" in a work sampling study? How are they related mathematically?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Confidence level** $(1-\alpha)$ is the probability that the confidence interval constructed from the sample contains the true activity proportion; common values are 90%, 95%, and 99%. (1.5 marks)

**Precision** (or accuracy, denoted $E$) is the allowable error in the estimate — i.e., the half-width of the confidence interval around the sample proportion $\hat{p}$. (1 mark)

**Mathematical relation:** Both are linked through the sample-size formula $n = Z^{2}p(1-p)/E^{2}$. A higher confidence level (larger $Z$) or tighter precision (smaller $E$) demands a **larger sample size**. (0.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 4 Internal Choice)

### Question A — Full 14-Mark Question

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCIET403 Module 4]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting a work sampling study in an industrial setup. List the six phases and briefly describe each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A foreman wishes to determine the proportion of time a machine is being productively operated. A preliminary study of 200 observations shows the machine productive in 120 observations. The foreman desires the final estimate to be within **±3%** of the true value with **98% confidence**. Calculate:
   (i) The required number of observations.
   (ii) The standard error of the proportion.
   (iii) The 98% confidence interval for the proportion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Six Phases of Work Sampling Study (7 marks):**

1. **Define objectives and classify activities** (1.5 marks): Clearly specify the purpose of the study and break the work into mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE) activity categories such as productive, idle, support, personal, and delay. A pilot walk-through helps refine categories.

2. **Design the observation sheet** (1 mark): Pre-print activity categories with spaces for tally marks. Include essential metadata — date, shift, observer name, study ID, machine/worker ID — to enable rapid recording within seconds per observation.

3. **Determine sample size using statistical formula** (1.5 marks): Use the formula $n = Z^{2} p (1-p) / E^{2}$. For unknown $p$, set $p = 0.5$ in the pilot phase. Choose confidence level (typically 95%) and precision (typically ±4% to ±5%).

4. **Plan the random observation schedule** (1 mark): Use a random number table or computer-generated random times to schedule observations across all shifts, days, and operators. This eliminates observer bias.

5. **Collect observations** (1 mark): At each random instant, classify the activity and place a tally mark. Do NOT observe continuously; observations must be instantaneous and independent.

6. **Tabulate, analyze and report** (1 mark): Compute sample proportions $\hat{p}$ for each activity, calculate standard errors, construct confidence intervals, and present results with engineering recommendations.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Preliminary observations $n_0 = 200$, hits $= 120$ → estimated $\hat{p} = 120/200 = 0.60$
- Allowable error $E = 0.03$
- Confidence level $= 98\%$ → From standard normal tables, $Z = 2.326$

**(i) Required sample size (3 marks):**

$$n = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$$

$$n = \frac{(2.326)^{2} \cdot (0.60)(0.40)}{(0.03)^{2}}$$

Step-by-step computation:
- $Z^{2} = (2.326)^{2} = 5.4103$
- $p(1-p) = 0.60 \times 0.40 = 0.24$
- Numerator $= 5.4103 \times 0.24 = 1.2985$
- Denominator $= (0.03)^{2} = 0.0009$

$$n = \frac{1.2985}{0.0009} = 1442.78$$

Rounding up:

$$\boxed{n = 1443 \text{ observations}}$$

**(ii) Standard error (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.24}{1443}}$$

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{0.0001663} = 0.01289$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\hat{p}} \approx 1.29\%}$$

**(iii) 98% Confidence interval (2 marks):**

$$\hat{p} \pm Z \cdot \sigma_{\hat{p}} = 0.60 \pm 2.326 \times 0.01289$$

$$= 0.60 \pm 0.0300 = (0.570, 0.630)$$

$$\boxed{\text{98% CI: } 57.0\% \text{ to } 63.0\%}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The foreman can be 98% confident that the machine's true productive proportion lies between **57% and 63%**, with a point estimate of 60%.

---

### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCIET403 Module 4]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **statistical theory** underlying work sampling. Explain how the binomial distribution and Central Limit Theorem are used to derive the sample-size formula. State clearly the role of $Z$, $p$, $1-p$, and $E$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a work sampling study of a foundry, an observer takes **500 random observations** of a group of workers. The results are: **Productive = 275, Support = 100, Idle = 75, Delay = 50.** For each activity category:
   (i) Compute the proportion and percentage.
   (ii) Compute the standard error.
   (iii) Construct the 95% confidence interval.
   (iv) Interpret the result for the most critical category (delay). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Statistical Theory (7 marks):**

Work sampling rests on the **binomial probability model**. Each random observation is a **Bernoulli trial** — either the activity of interest occurs (success, probability $p$) or it does not (failure, probability $1-p$). For $n$ such independent trials, the number of successes $X$ follows the binomial distribution: $X \sim B(n, p)$ with mean $np$ and variance $np(1-p)$. (1.5 marks)

The sample proportion $\hat{p} = X/n$ is therefore a random variable with mean $p$ and variance $p(1-p)/n$. As $n$ grows large (a requirement in work sampling), the **Central Limit Theorem** ensures that $\hat{p}$ is approximately normally distributed: $\hat{p} \sim N(p, p(1-p)/n)$. (1.5 marks)

The **confidence interval** is built as $\hat{p} \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$. (1 mark)

Setting the half-width equal to the allowable error $E$:

$$Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} = E \implies n = \frac{Z^{2} p(1-p)}{E^{2}} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Role of variables:**
- $Z$: standard normal deviate corresponding to chosen confidence level (e.g., 1.96 for 95%) — higher confidence → larger $Z$ → larger $n$.
- $p(1-p)$: variance term, maximized at $p = 0.5$ giving 0.25 — when $p$ is unknown, conservatively set $p = 0.5$.
- $E$: precision requirement — smaller $E$ (tighter) → $n$ grows as $1/E^{2}$. (1.5 marks)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — 1.75 marks per category):**

**Given:** $n = 500$, $Z = 1.96$ for 95% confidence.

| Category | Count | $\hat{p}$ | $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}$ | 95% CI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Productive | 275 | 0.550 | $\sqrt{0.2475/500} = 0.0223$ | $0.550 \pm 0.0436 = (0.506, 0.594)$ |
| Support | 100 | 0.200 | $\sqrt{0.16/500} = 0.0179$ | $0.200 \pm 0.0350 = (0.165, 0.235)$ |
| Idle | 75 | 0.150 | $\sqrt{0.1275/500} = 0.01598$ | $0.150 \pm 0.0313 = (0.119, 0.181)$ |
| Delay | 50 | 0.100 | $\sqrt{0.09/500} = 0.01342$ | $0.100 \pm 0.0263 = (0.074, 0.126)$ |

**Detailed step for Delay category (most critical):**
- $\hat{p} = 50/500 = 0.10$
- $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{0.10 \times 0.90 / 500} = \sqrt{0.00018} = 0.01342$
- Margin $= 1.96 \times 0.01342 = 0.0263$
- Lower bound $= 0.10 - 0.0263 = 0.0737 \approx 0.074$
- Upper bound $= 0.10 + 0.0263 = 0.1263 \approx 0.126$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that delay time lies between **7.4% and 12.6%** of total time. Since this is significantly non-zero, the foundry should investigate causes (machine breakdowns, material shortages, mould defects) and target reduction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP the sample size** — KTU expects ceiling of $n$, e.g., 576.24 → 577. Writing 576 loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong Z-value** — students often confuse one-tailed and two-tailed. For two-sided confidence interval, use $Z_{1-\alpha/2}$ (e.g., $Z = 1.96$ for 95%).
> 3. **Using $p = 0.5$ always** — this is conservative but wastes observations. After pilot study, use the actual estimated $p$.
> 4. **Ignoring units** — report proportions as decimals but state interpretation as percentages.
> 5. **Confusing precision and accuracy** — precision (allowable error) refers to the width of the interval, not the center.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Work sampling = statistical estimation of activity proportions** via instantaneous random observations — NOT continuous timing.
- [x] **Three pillars:** randomness of observation instants, statistical sample size, mutually exclusive activity categories.
- [x] **Master formula:** $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \, p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$
- [x] **Z-values to memorize:** 90% → 1.645, 95% → 1.96, 99% → 2.576.
- [x] **Use $p = 0.5$** when no prior estimate exists — this gives the safest (largest) sample size.
- [x] **Standard error:** $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$.
- [x] **Confidence interval:** $\hat{p} \pm Z \cdot \sigma_{\hat{p}}$.
- [x] **Always round $n$ up** to the next whole integer — you cannot take 576.4 observations.
- [x] **Rare activities** (small $p$) need **prohibitively large** $n$ — consider stratified sampling or longer pilot studies.
- [x] **Six-phase procedure:** Define → Design → Determine → Plan → Observe → Analyze & Report.
- [x] **Advantages:** lower cost, less observer interference, suitable for long cycles and group studies.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** not suitable for very short repetitive cycles (use stopwatch), provides only proportions (not exact times for elements).
- [x] **Two applications in KTU exams:** (1) Sample size calculation, (2) Confidence interval construction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation Procedures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Calculation Procedures** in Work System Design refer to the systematic, mathematical, and statistical methodologies employed to convert raw observational data from work measurement techniques (such as time study, work sampling, and predetermined motion time systems) into standardized, actionable production metrics. These procedures form the quantitative backbone of industrial engineering, enabling the determination of **Standard Time (ST)**, **Normal Time (NT)**, **Basic Time (BT)**, and the establishment of **statistical confidence in work measurement data**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, calculation procedures encompass the end-to-end analytical pipeline:
1. Data acquisition from the shop floor
2. Performance rating of the operator
3. Application of relaxation and contingency allowances
4. Statistical validation (sample size, confidence intervals, control limits)
5. Derivation of the final standard time for cost estimation, capacity planning, and incentive wage design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Calculation procedures are *not* just arithmetic — they are governed by **statistical rigor**. Any standard time derived without a confidence interval is considered *incomplete* under the revised NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Shop Timer" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a campus coffee shop and want to determine the *standard time* a barista should take to make a cappuccino.

- You **observe** 5 different baristas making cappuccinos. The times you record are the **Observed Times** (raw data).
- However, Barista A is exceptionally fast (a *110-rated* performer), and Barista B is slow and tired that day (a *85-rated* performer). You cannot just average these and call it the standard.
- So you apply a **Performance Rating Factor** — like a "normalization lens" — to bring everyone to a *100% normal pace*. This gives you the **Normal Time**.
- But humans are not machines. They need to rest, drink water, stretch, and use the restroom. You add **Allowances** (e.g., 15% relaxation) on top of the normal time. This final figure is the **Standard Time** — the time a *qualified, motivated worker* should take under *normal working conditions*.
- To be *scientifically confident*, you also calculate a **Confidence Interval** (e.g., "with 95% confidence, the true mean lies between 4.2 and 4.8 minutes") using statistical procedures.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Idea:** Calculation procedures turn *messy human data* into *defensible engineering standards* through normalization (rating) and statistical validation (confidence intervals).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard Performance Rating = 100 (BS 100)** — the universally accepted "normal working pace" of a qualified worker.
- **Rest Allowances:** typically **5% to 15%** of normal time, depending on working conditions.
- **Personal Allowances:** typically **5% to 12%** for fatigue and personal needs.
- **Z-score for 95% Confidence Level:** **Z = 1.96** (a critical value used in nearly all KTU statistical work-sampling problems).
- **Confidence Level commonly used in industry:** **95%** (occasionally 90% or 99%).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Calculation Pipeline

The calculation procedure in Work System Design follows a **strict sequential pipeline**. Skipping any stage invalidates the standard time.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Observed Time, OT)
The stopwatch records the actual time taken by the operator to complete one cycle of the operation. For statistical validity, multiple cycles (typically **n ≥ 30** for KTU exam problems) must be recorded.

### Stage 2 — Averaging (Basic Time, BT)
The arithmetic mean of all observed times is computed:

$$BT = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} OT_i}{n}$$

This is the *unadjusted* average time before any human-performance correction.

### Stage 3 — Performance Rating (Normal Time, NT)
The observer (a trained time-study analyst) assigns a **Performance Rating Factor (PRF)** based on the worker's pace compared to the standard 100% rating. This normalizes the data:

$$NT = BT \times \frac{PRF}{100}$$

The PRF is determined using methods such as **Westinghouse System** (skill, effort, conditions, consistency) or **Synthetic Rating**.

### Stage 4 — Allowance Application (Standard Time, ST)
Allowances account for *unproductive but necessary* time (rest, personal needs, contingencies). They are added as a multiplier:

$$ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$$

where **AF** is the *Allowance Factor* expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.15 for 15%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** Some texts (especially older British Standards) use the **reciprocal form** for contingency allowances. Always verify whether the problem states the allowance *as a fraction of total time* or *as a fraction of normal time*. The two formulas are **NOT** mathematically equivalent.

### Stage 5 — Statistical Validation
For work sampling and time study with limited samples, the **confidence interval** must be calculated:

$$CI = \bar{X} \pm Z \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

where $\bar{X}$ is the sample mean, $\sigma$ is the standard deviation, $n$ is the sample size, and $Z$ is the standard normal variate for the chosen confidence level.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in over 80% of KTU ESE and internal examination problems on this module.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Time | $BT = \frac{\sum OT_i}{n}$ | OT in minutes/seconds, n = cycles | Time study data reduction |
| 2 | Normal Time | $NT = BT \times \frac{PRF}{100}$ | PRF dimensionless (typically 60–135) | Performance normalization |
| 3 | Standard Time | $ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$ | AF = decimal (e.g., 0.15) | Final standard for production planning |
| 4 | Standard Time (Alt) | $ST = \frac{NT}{1 - AF}$ | When AF is *of total time* | Older BS nomenclature |
| 5 | Sample Size (Work Sampling) | $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$ | p = activity proportion, E = acceptable error | Planning a work-sampling study |
| 6 | Confidence Interval | $CI = \bar{X} \pm Z \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Z = 1.96 (95%), $\sigma$ = std dev | Validating measured standard time |
| 7 | Work Sampling Estimate | $P = \frac{X}{N}$ | X = observations of activity, N = total random observations | Estimating % time spent on activity |
| 8 | Westinghouse Rating | $R = S \times E \times C \times (1 + CS)$ | Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency | Computing composite rating factor |
| 9 | Allowance Factor Sum | $AF = RA + PA + CA$ | Rest, Personal, Contingency | Summing all allowance categories |
| 10 | Control Chart Limits | $UCL / LCL = \bar{p} \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p}$ = average proportion | Detecting abnormal work patterns |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industries:** Calculation procedures drive **line balancing**, **capacity planning**, and **lean production targets**.
- **Wage Incentive Systems:** Standard time forms the basis of **Halsey, Rowan, and Bedaux incentive plans**.
- **Healthcare & Service Sectors:** Work sampling is used to determine **nurse-to-patient ratios** and **bank teller utilization**.
- **Ergonomics & Safety:** Allowances calculated for **heat, noise, and posture** (e.g., **Niebel's standard allowance table**) are integrated into the formula to ensure physiologically feasible standards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard Time Formula from First Principles

We start from the fundamental principle that the **total work content (TW)** of a job consists of productive work plus necessary unproductive allowance.

**Step 1:** Define the total content of the job.

$$TW = NT + \text{Allowance Time}$$

**Step 2:** Express Allowance Time as a fraction of Normal Time (the standard industrial convention).

$$\text{Allowance Time} = NT \times AF$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the total content equation.

$$TW = NT + NT \times AF$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $NT$.

$$TW = NT \times (1 + AF)$$

**Step 5:** The Standard Time is defined as the *total work content* under normal working conditions.

$$\boxed{ST = NT \times (1 + AF)}$$

**Step 6 (Verification with units):** If NT is in minutes and AF is dimensionless, then ST is in minutes. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Work Sampling Sample Size Formula

This derivation shows *why* the famous $n = \frac{Z^2 p (1-p)}{E^2}$ formula exists.

**Step 1:** The standard error of a proportion in random sampling is given by the binomial standard deviation.

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

**Step 2:** The allowable error (E) is defined as a multiple (Z) of the standard error — this gives the confidence interval half-width.

$$E = Z \times SE = Z \times \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides to remove the square root.

$$E^2 = Z^2 \times \frac{p(1-p)}{n}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for n (the required number of observations).

$$n \times E^2 = Z^2 \times p(1-p)$$

**Step 5:** Final rearrangement.

$$\boxed{n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The required sample size $n$ is directly proportional to $Z^2$ (higher confidence → more samples) and inversely proportional to $E^2$ (tighter error tolerance → more samples, *quadratically*).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Standard

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A time study analyst records the following observed times (in minutes) for a manual assembly operation over 10 cycles: 1.20, 1.25, 1.18, 1.30, 1.22, 1.28, 1.21, 1.24, 1.19, 1.23. The worker's performance was rated at **110%**, and the total allowance factor is **20%**. Calculate the **Standard Time** per piece. If the company wants a **95% confidence interval with a margin of error of ±0.05 minutes**, how many additional cycles must be observed? (Given $Z = 1.96$, $\sigma = 0.0386$ min).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the Basic Time (BT).**

$$BT = \frac{\sum OT_i}{n} = \frac{1.20 + 1.25 + 1.18 + 1.30 + 1.22 + 1.28 + 1.21 + 1.24 + 1.19 + 1.23}{10}$$

$$BT = \frac{12.30}{10} = 1.230 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Normal Time (NT).**

$$NT = BT \times \frac{PRF}{100} = 1.230 \times \frac{110}{100}$$

$$NT = 1.230 \times 1.10 = 1.353 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Standard Time (ST).**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + AF) = 1.353 \times (1 + 0.20)$$

$$ST = 1.353 \times 1.20 = 1.6236 \text{ minutes per piece}$$

**Step 4: Determine the additional sample size for the desired confidence interval.**

Using the formula $E = Z \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$, solve for n:

$$n = \left(\frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 0.0386}{0.05}\right)^2$$

$$n = \left(\frac{0.07566}{0.05}\right)^2 = (1.5131)^2 \approx 2.29$$

Rounding up to the next integer (statistical convention):

$$n = 3 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 5: Subtract the already-observed cycles to find *additional* cycles needed.**

$$\text{Additional cycles} = 3 - 10 = -7 \text{ (i.e., 0 additional cycles)}$$

Since the existing sample size (10) *exceeds* the required size (3), **no additional observations are needed**. The current standard time of **1.6236 min/piece** is already statistically valid at 95% confidence with ±0.05 min margin.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Rating Methods

| Method | Input Parameters | Output | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Westinghouse System** | Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency | Composite Rating (e.g., +5%, +10%) | Detailed, formal time studies |
| **Synthetic Rating** | Speed of 4 reference motions | Rating factor | Quick, on-the-floor estimation |
| **Objective Rating (100%+)** | Pace only | Numeric rating (60–135) | ILO/Modern standardized time study |
| **Subjective Rating** | Analyst intuition | Rough estimate | Initial scoping studies only |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Calculation Procedure Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start: Raw Stopwatch Observations] --> node2[Stage 1: Record OT for n cycles]
    node2 --> node3{Outliers present?}
    node3 --> node1[Yes: Apply Chauvenets or 3-sigma rule, re-record] 
    node3 --> node4[No: Proceed to averaging]
    node4 --> node5[Stage 2: Calculate BT = mean of OT]
    node5 --> node6[Stage 3: Apply Performance Rating]
    node6 --> node7[Compute NT = BT x PRF / 100]
    node7 --> node8[Stage 4: Add Allowances]
    node8 --> node9[Compute ST = NT x 1 + AF]
    node9 --> node10[Stage 5: Statistical Validation]
    node10 --> node11[Compute Confidence Interval]
    node11 --> node12{CI acceptable?}
    node12 --> node13[No: Increase sample size n and restart] 
    node12 --> node14[Yes: Final ST certified]
    node14 --> node15[End: Publish Standard Time for Planning]
```

## 4.2 Work Sampling Statistical Framework (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Define Activity being measured] --> nodeB[Estimate p from pilot study]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Choose confidence level and error]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute n = Z squared p 1-p over E squared]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Schedule N random observations]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Conduct study: count X occurrences]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute P-hat = X / N]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute p-hat plus/minus Z sigma]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Decision: Standard Met or Re-sample]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The work-sampling block architecture shows the *iterative statistical core* — every block feeds into the next, and the final block loops back if statistical sufficiency is not met. This is why work sampling is considered a *statistical* rather than *deterministic* measurement technique.

## 4.3 Allowance Categorization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX[Total Allowance Factor AF] --> nodeY[Personal Allowance PA]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Rest Allowance RA]
    nodeX --> nodeW[Contingency Allowance CA]
    nodeY --> nodeY1[Drinking water]
    nodeY --> nodeY2[Toilet breaks]
    nodeY --> nodeY3[Personal conversation]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ1[Fatigue from posture]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ2[Heat and humidity recovery]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ3[Visual and mental effort]
    nodeW --> nodeW1[Machine downtime]
    nodeW --> nodeW2[Tool unavailability]
    nodeW --> nodeW3[Supervisor instructions]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *design* an allowance structure in an exam, always classify allowances into these **three canonical categories** (Personal, Rest, Contingency). Examiners award marks specifically for this taxonomy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]

**Q1.** Define **Basic Time (BT)**, **Normal Time (NT)**, and **Standard Time (ST)**. State the mathematical relationship between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Basic Time (BT):** The arithmetic mean of the observed cycle times recorded by the time-study analyst, before any rating or allowance adjustment. $BT = \frac{\sum OT_i}{n}$ **(1 mark)**
- **Normal Time (NT):** The time required by a *qualified worker* working at a *normal pace* (rating = 100%) to perform the operation. It is obtained by multiplying the BT by the Performance Rating Factor. $NT = BT \times \frac{PRF}{100}$ **(1 mark)**
- **Standard Time (ST):** The total time a worker should be allowed to complete one unit of work, including *productive time* plus *allowances* for personal needs, rest, and contingencies. $ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$ **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]

**Q2.** In a work-sampling study, why is the required sample size **n = 2700** when $Z = 2$, $p = 0.5$, and $E = 0.02$? Justify the choice of p = 0.5.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Substituting the values: $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.5 \times 0.5}{(0.02)^2} = \frac{1}{0.0004} = 2500$. *(Note: 2700 is achieved if Z = 2.08, but the standard KTU answer for Z=2 is 2500; either value accepted with correct substitution shown.)* **(1.5 marks)**
- The value **p = 0.5** is chosen because the product $p(1-p)$ is **maximized** at this point (it equals 0.25, the maximum of the parabola $p - p^2$). Using $p = 0.5$ gives the **most conservative (largest) sample size**, which is statistically safe when the true proportion is unknown. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *reciprocal* allowance formula with the standard formula. If the problem says "allowance is 15% **of total time**," use $ST = \frac{NT}{1-0.15}$. If it says "15% **of normal time**," use $ST = NT \times 1.15$. Misreading this costs **2–3 marks** per question.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Pattern]

**Q(a) [7 marks].** A time study was conducted on a drilling operation. The observed times for 8 cycles (in seconds) are: 42, 45, 44, 41, 46, 43, 45, 42. The worker's performance was rated at **115%**. Compute the **Normal Time** and the **Standard Time** if a **20% allowance** is provided.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute the Basic Time.**

$$BT = \frac{42 + 45 + 44 + 41 + 46 + 43 + 45 + 42}{8} = \frac{348}{8} = 43.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Normal Time.**

$$NT = BT \times \frac{PRF}{100} = 43.5 \times \frac{115}{100} = 50.025 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Standard Time.**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + AF) = 50.025 \times (1 + 0.20) = 50.025 \times 1.20 = 60.03 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answer:** NT = **50.03 s**, ST = **60.03 s** per piece. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

**Q(b) [7 marks].** A work-sampling study is planned to determine the proportion of time a machine is idle. A pilot study indicated $p = 0.30$. The analyst wants a **95% confidence level** and a **maximum error of ±0.04**. Calculate the **number of observations** required. If 1500 observations are taken and the machine was found idle in 480 of them, calculate the **95% confidence interval** for the true proportion. $(Z_{0.05} = 1.96)$.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate required sample size n.**

$$n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.04)^2}$$

$$n = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.21}{0.0016} = \frac{0.8067}{0.0016} \approx 504.2$$

Rounding up: $n = 505$ observations. **(3 marks)**

**Step 2: Calculate the sample proportion from the actual study.**

$$\hat{p} = \frac{X}{N} = \frac{480}{1500} = 0.32$$

**Step 3: Calculate the standard error of the proportion.**

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.32 \times 0.68}{1500}} = \sqrt{0.0001451} = 0.01205$$

**Step 4: Calculate the 95% confidence interval.**

$$CI = \hat{p} \pm Z \cdot SE = 0.32 \pm 1.96 \times 0.01205$$

$$CI = 0.32 \pm 0.0236 = (0.2964, 0.3436)$$

**Final Answer:** Required n = **505**; 95% CI for true idle proportion = **(29.64%, 34.36%)**. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Pattern]

**Q(a) [7 marks].** A worker produces 120 pieces in an 8-hour shift. The company has a **personal allowance of 10%** and a **fatigue allowance of 15%** and a **contingency allowance of 5%**. If the worker is rated at **90%**, determine the **Standard Time per piece** and the **expected daily output** at the standard rate.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute the Basic Time per piece.**

$$BT = \frac{8 \times 60 \text{ minutes}}{120 \text{ pieces}} = \frac{480}{120} = 4.0 \text{ minutes per piece}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Normal Time per piece.**

$$NT = BT \times \frac{PRF}{100} = 4.0 \times \frac{90}{100} = 3.6 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Sum the allowance factors.**

$$AF = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.05 = 0.30 \text{ (i.e., 30%)}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Standard Time per piece.**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + AF) = 3.6 \times 1.30 = 4.68 \text{ minutes per piece}$$

**Step 5: Compute expected output at standard rate.**

$$\text{Daily Output} = \frac{8 \times 60}{4.68} = \frac{480}{4.68} \approx 102.56 \approx 102 \text{ pieces per shift}$$

**Final Answer:** ST = **4.68 min/piece**; Expected standard output = **102 pieces/day**. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

**Q(b) [7 marks].** Explain the **Westinghouse System of Performance Rating** with its four factors. A worker's skill is rated **B2 (Excellent +)**, effort is **C1 (Average)**, conditions are **D (Fair)**, and consistency is **E (Excellent)**. Determine the **composite rating factor** and the **Normal Time** if the observed time is **0.75 minutes**.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Westinghouse System Explanation.**

The Westinghouse System rates a worker's performance using four independent factors **(1 mark)**:

1. **Skill** — Proficiency in following procedures. Ratings: A (Super Skilled) to F (Poor).
2. **Effort** — Willingness to work. Ratings: A (Excessive) to F (Very Low).
3. **Conditions** — Physical environment (heat, light, noise). Ratings: A (Ideal) to D (Fair/Poor).
4. **Consistency** — Repeatability of cycle times. Ratings: A (Perfect) to E (Poor).

**Step 2: Assign Westinghouse numerical values from the standard table.**

| Factor | Rating | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Skill | B2 (Excellent +) | **+0.08** |
| Effort | C1 (Average) | **+0.00** |
| Conditions | D (Fair) | **−0.03** |
| Consistency | E (Excellent) | **+0.02** |

**Step 3: Sum the factors to get the composite rating adjustment.**

$$RA = 0.08 + 0.00 + (-0.03) + 0.02 = +0.07$$

**Step 4: Compute the Performance Rating Factor.**

$$PRF = 100 + 7 = 107$$

**Step 5: Compute the Normal Time.**

$$NT = BT \times \frac{PRF}{100} = 0.75 \times \frac{107}{100} = 0.8025 \text{ minutes}$$

**Final Answer:** PRF = **107%**; NT = **0.8025 minutes** (≈ 48.15 seconds). **(Full 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers in Calculation Procedure Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always write "minutes" or "pieces/day" — a bare number loses 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Rounding down instead of rounding up** the sample size n. Statistical convention requires ceiling (e.g., 504.2 → 505).
> 3. **Using the wrong Z-value.** KTU problems almost always use $Z = 1.96$ for 95% confidence, *not* $Z = 2$ (unless explicitly stated). The 2 is a rougher approximation.
> 4. **Confusing $p$ (true population proportion) with $\hat{p}$ (sample proportion).** Use $p$ in the *planning* formula and $\hat{p}$ in the *confidence interval* formula.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the rating.** Many students compute ST directly from BT, skipping the NT stage. This is an automatic 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Three-tier time framework:** OT (raw) → BT (averaged) → NT (rated) → ST (with allowances). Memorize this chain.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$ is the **single most tested equation** in this module.
- ✅ **Work sampling size formula:** $n = \frac{Z^2 p (1-p)}{E^2}$. Default values to remember: $Z = 1.96$ (95%), $p = 0.5$ (worst case).
- ✅ **Confidence Interval formula:** $CI = \bar{X} \pm Z \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- ✅ **Westinghouse System** uses four factors: **Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency**.
- ✅ **Three allowance categories:** Personal (PA), Rest/Fatigue (RA), Contingency (CA) — always classify allowances into these.
- ✅ **Standard rating is 100** (the "BS 100" normal pace).
- ✅ **Always round sample sizes UP** to the next integer.
- ✅ **Units matter:** Write them in the final answer (minutes, seconds, pieces/day).
- ✅ **KTU favourite trap:** Difference between "allowance as a fraction of normal time" vs. "allowance as a fraction of total time" — leads to two different formulas.
- ✅ **Statistical sufficiency:** If the required $n$ is less than the observed $n$, no additional observations are required.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MTM and MOST in Work Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** MTM and MOST in Work Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)?

A **Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS)** is a work measurement technique in which standard time values for basic human motions have been pre-established through extensive laboratory research and motion-film analysis. These published time values are then used to build up the time required for any manual operation by combining the appropriate basic-motion times.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 4 – Work Measurement)**
> A Predetermined Motion Time System is a standardized catalogue of motion-time values (measured in **TMU – Time Measurement Units**) that converts a worker's basic body movements (reach, move, grasp, position, release) into standard times *without stopwatch time study*. The two most commercially significant systems in industrial engineering practice are **Methods-Time Measurement (MTM)** and **Maynard Operation Sequence Technique (MOST)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Alphabet of Human Motion"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of human work motions the way a child learns language. Before forming sentences (a job), you must master letters (Reach, Move, Grasp, etc.). PMTS gives every "letter" a fixed time value, just as every alphabet letter has a fixed pronunciation. By stringing the letters into a sentence — using the proper grammar (sequencing rules) — you can "read" the time for any operation.

### 1.3 The Time Measurement Unit (TMU)

Every PMTS (MTM, MOST, Work Factor, Basic Motion Timestudy) is built on a common micro-time unit so that motion times from one system are convertible to another.

$$1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ hour} = 0.0006 \text{ minute} = 0.036 \text{ second}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this conversion ladder (examiners love it):**
> - **1 hour = 100,000 TMU**
> - **1 minute = 1,666.67 TMU**
> - **1 second = 27.78 TMU**

### 1.4 MTM – Methods-Time Measurement

**Methods-Time Measurement (MTM)** is the oldest, most widely used PMTS. It was developed during 1940–1948 by a team led by **H. C. Zippin, G. C. Embry, H. G. Muffly and H. W. Kinkade** at the United States Naval Supply Research and Development Facility, based on extensive film studies of industrial operations.

MTM decomposes every manual task into a combination of **eight basic motions (Therbligs of the MTM kind)**:

| S. No. | Basic MTM Motion | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reach | R | Move hand empty to a location |
| 2 | Move | M | Move hand with object to a location |
| 3 | Turn | T | Rotate hand/wrist about its axis |
| 4 | Grasp | G | Gain control of an object |
| 5 | Position | P | Orient, align or mate an object |
| 6 | Release | R-L | Release control of an object |
| 7 | Disengage | D | Break contact between two objects |
| 8 | Apply Pressure | A-P | Apply additional muscular force |

### 1.5 MOST – Maynard Operation Sequence Technique

**MOST** was developed by **H. B. Maynard (USA) in 1964** as a faster, simpler alternative to MTM. It is also a PMTS but uses three standardized activity sequences that cover *all* manual work:

1. **General Move Sequence (G-Move)** – to move an object through space
2. **Controlled Move Sequence (M-Move)** – to move and place an object with some control
3. **Tool Usage Sequence (A-Move / T-Move)** – for tool-using operations

MOST exists in three levels of detail:

| MOST Level | Index Unit (TMU per index value) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **MaxiMOST** | **100 TMU per index** | Long-cycle, project planning |
| **MiniMOST** | **1 TMU per index** | Short-cycle, scientific work measurement |
| **BasicMOST** | **10 TMU per index** | Standard industrial analysis (most taught) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept of MTM

MTM works on a closed, deterministic philosophy: *the time required to perform a basic motion depends only on the type and distance of the motion, NOT on the worker's skill or the workplace's motivation*. Hence MTM-derived times are called **Normal Times** that already include the **rating factor of 100%** (i.e., for a qualified worker working at standard pace).

### 2.1.1 The MTM Logic Pipeline (How to apply MTM)

1. **Method Description** – Observe and record the worker's method, ideally using a "right-hand / left-hand" process chart.
2. **Motion Analysis** – Break the job into its constituent basic motions (R, M, T, G, P, R-L, D, A-P).
3. **Distance / Case Selection** – For each motion, measure the distance moved and select the appropriate **case** (A, B, C, D, E depending on object type, target, and distance).
4. **TMU Lookup** – Read off the TMU value from the published MTM tables.
5. **Summation** – Add the TMU values for the whole job to obtain the **Basic MTM Time** = **Normal Time** (since rating = 1).
6. **Allowance Application** – Convert Normal Time to **Standard Time** by adding **PFD, personal, contingency and other allowances**.

### 2.1.2 MTM Case Selection (the "why" behind each case)

| Motion | Case Distinguishing Criterion |
|---|---|
| **Reach (R)** | Distance to the object AND whether the target is fixed/vague/jumbled |
| **Move (M)** | Distance moved AND type of target (fixed/vague/jumbled) |
| **Turn (T)** | Weight being turned (case A: 0–2.5 lb, B: 2.5–7.5 lb, C: 7.5–22.5 lb) |
| **Grasp (G)** | Object size, shape, ease of grasp (1A, 1B, 2, 3, 4, 5) |
| **Position (P)** | Symmetry, ease of fit, surface contact (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) |
| **Release (R-L)** | Normal release (A) vs. release with re-grasping (B) |
| **Disengage (D)** | Effort required (A: easy, B: medium, C: difficult) |
| **Apply Pressure (A-P)** | Only one case (AP-1) – pressing a button or inserting a key |

> [!NOTE]
> **MTM-1 Standard TMU Values (commonly examined)**
>
> | Motion | Case A | Case B | Case C | Case D | Case E |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | **R** (Reach) | 5.1 | 7.7 | 9.1 | 12.0 | 16.2 |
> | **M** (Move) | 5.6 | 8.2 | 10.0 | 13.0 | 17.0 |
> | **T** (Turn) | 5.6 | 10.4 | 17.4 | — | — |
> | **G** | G1A=2.0 / G1B=3.5 / G2=7.3 / G3=9.1 / G4=12.9 / G5=18.2 | | | | |
> | **P** | P1=5.6 / P2=16.2 / P3=25.7 / P4=38.4 / P5=49.0 / P6=76.0 | | | | |
> | **R-L** | 2.0 (A) | 7.7 (B) | — | — | — |
> | **D** | 4.0 | 7.5 | 18.7 | — | — |
> | **A-P** | 10.6 (single case) | | | | |

### 2.2 Operational Concept of MOST

MOST collapses MTM's eight motions into three logical sequences, each built from a fixed list of "index words" whose value (in TMU) is known a-priori.

### 2.2.1 The General Move Sequence (G-Move) – `ABG – ABP – MXD`

The activity **"Get the tool – Place the tool – Move & Process"** is universal to most manual work. MOST formalises it as:

$$\underbrace{A\,B\,G}_{\text{Get the object}} \;\;–\;\; \underbrace{A\,B\,P}_{\text{Place the object}} \;\;–\;\; \underbrace{M\,X}_{\text{Move & Process}}$$

| Index | Meaning | Possible Values (BasicMOST) |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | **Action distance** (hand/object travel, in inches) | A0, A1, A2, A3, A6, A10, A18, A30 |
| **B** | **Body motion** (bend, sit, stand, walk) | B0, B1, B6, B16, B30 |
| **G** | **Gain control** (type of grasp) | G0, G1, G3, G6, G12 |
| **P** | **Placement** (loose, locate, align, mate) | P0, P1, P2, P3, P5, P11, P18 |
| **M** | **Move object** (move after placement) | M1, M2, M3, M4, M5, M6, M8, M12, M16 |
| **X** | **Process / auxiliary time** (e.g., screw, read, align) | X1, X2, X3, X4, X5, X6, X10, X16, X20, X25, X30, X40, X50, X60, X80 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Rule:** Each index value in **BasicMOST = 10 TMU**. Therefore:
>
> $$\text{Total Time (TMU)} = (\Sigma \text{indices}) \times 10 \text{ TMU/index}$$
>
> For **MiniMOST**, multiply by 1 TMU/index; for **MaxiMOST**, multiply by 100 TMU/index.

### 2.2.2 The Controlled Move Sequence (M-Move) – `ABG – ABP – MXD – I`

The M-Move is used when a moving object is constrained by external forces (e.g., guiding a part into a fixture) or when the worker's eyes must follow the motion. It adds an extra "I" (index for **constraints** such as cranking, sighting, fastening).

### 2.2.3 The Tool-Use Sequence (A-Move / T-Move) – `ABG – ABP – AD`

Used when a tool is fastened, used, and returned. Example: tightening a screw, operating a hand drill.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TMU Definition | 1 TMU = 0.00001 hr = 0.0006 min = 0.036 s | — |
| 2 | Convert TMU → seconds | Seconds = TMU × 0.036 | seconds |
| 3 | Convert seconds → TMU | TMU = Seconds ÷ 0.036 = Seconds × 27.78 | TMU |
| 4 | Convert minutes → TMU | TMU = Minutes × 1,666.67 | TMU |
| 5 | MTM Normal Time | NT (TMU) = Σ(Motion TMU values) | TMU |
| 6 | MTM Standard Time | ST = NT × (1 + Allowance Fraction) | minutes |
| 7 | MOST Basic Time | T = (Σ index values) × 10 | TMU |
| 8 | MOST Mini Time | T = (Σ index values) × 1 | TMU |
| 9 | MOST Maxi Time | T = (Σ index values) × 100 | TMU |
| 10 | Standard Time | ST = NT × (1 + A) where A = allowance fraction | time |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

* **Method Comparison without Time Study:** PMTS lets industrial engineers compare two proposed methods *off-line* on paper.
* **Pre-Setting Standards for New Jobs:** Where stopwatch study is impossible (e.g., long cycle, one-off, dangerous tasks), PMTS provides defensible times.
* **Workplace Design & Ergonomics:** PMTS identifies wasteful motion (e.g., excessive reach, unnecessary turns) and guides layout.
* **Negotiated-Rate Setting:** Used as a basis for incentive wage systems because the rating factor is fixed at 100%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Conversion Derivations (Board Favourite)

### 3.1.1 Derivation of 1 hour = 100,000 TMU

By the formal KTU/MTM-1 definition:

$$1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ hour}$$

Multiplying both sides by 100,000:

$$100{,}000 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \times 100{,}000 = 1 \text{ hour}$$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of 1 minute = 1,666.67 TMU

$$1 \text{ hour} = 60 \text{ minutes} = 100{,}000 \text{ TMU}$$

Therefore:

$$1 \text{ minute} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ TMU}}{60} = 1{,}666.67 \text{ TMU}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of 1 second = 27.78 TMU

$$1 \text{ minute} = 60 \text{ seconds} = 1{,}666.67 \text{ TMU}$$

Therefore:

$$1 \text{ second} = \frac{1{,}666.67}{60} = 27.78 \text{ TMU}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The inverse (most-used in KTU problems):**
> $$\text{TMU} = \text{Seconds} \times 27.78$$

## 3.2 Worked Example – MTM Analysis of "Pick up a pencil and insert it into a holder"

### Step 1: Method Decomposition
| # | Sub-action | Basic Motion(s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Move hand to pencil | R (Reach) |
| 2 | Grasp the pencil | G (Grasp) |
| 3 | Move hand with pencil to holder | M (Move) |
| 4 | Position pencil into hole | P (Position) |
| 5 | Release the pencil | R-L (Release) |

### Step 2: Case Selection and TMU Lookup
| Step | Motion | Case Selected | Reason | TMU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | R | R-B | Reach a clearly located object at moderate distance | 7.7 |
| 2 | G | G-1A | Pencil is cylindrical & easy to grasp | 2.0 |
| 3 | M | M-B | Move to fixed target at moderate distance | 8.2 |
| 4 | P | P-2 | Loose, easy positioning into an open hole | 16.2 |
| 5 | R-L | R-L-A | Normal release | 2.0 |

### Step 3: Summation (Exhaustive, no skipping)

$$\text{Normal Time} = 7.7 + 2.0 + 8.2 + 16.2 + 2.0 = 36.1 \text{ TMU}$$

### Step 4: Convert to seconds

$$\text{Normal Time (s)} = 36.1 \times 0.036 = 1.2996 \text{ s} \approx 1.30 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 5: Apply allowance (assume 15% relaxation + 5% contingency = 20%)

$$\text{Standard Time} = 1.30 \times (1 + 0.20) = 1.56 \text{ seconds per cycle}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example – MOST Analysis of the Same Operation

Using **BasicMOST** for the same job:

| Sub-action | Index | Value | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reach to pencil | A1 | 1 | Action distance ≈ 1 inch |
| Bend slightly | B1 | 1 | Minimal body motion |
| Grasp pencil | G1 | 1 | Cylindrical, easy grasp |
| Place into holder | P3 | 3 | Locate (precise) |
| Move pencil in | M2 | 2 | Short move |
| Process (insert) | X3 | 3 | Aligning into a tight hole |

$$\Sigma \text{ indices} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 + 2 + 3 = 11$$

$$\text{Total Time} = 11 \times 10 \text{ TMU} = 110 \text{ TMU}$$

Convert to seconds:

$$\text{Time (s)} = 110 \times 0.036 = 3.96 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The MOST estimate (3.96 s) is higher than the MTM estimate (1.30 s) because MOST is designed to be a *conservative* (i.e., generous to the worker) estimate. Engineers then apply a "performance factor" if needed. In practice, both methods give comparable results once a company's experience curve is applied.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – When to Use MTM vs MOST

| Criterion | MTM | MOST |
|---|---|---|
| **Detail level** | Very high (8 basic motions, multiple cases) | Moderate (3 sequences, 6 index letters) |
| **Time required for analysis** | Long (3–5× MORE) | Short |
| **Skill required** | Highly trained analyst | Trained technician |
| **Accuracy of result** | Highest | Very good (after adjustment) |
| **Number of TMU tables** | Many | Few |
| **Best for** | Short-cycle, repetitive work, scientific studies | General industrial use, method improvements |
| **Cost of implementation** | High | Moderate |
| **Patent / Training** | International MTM Association | H. B. Maynard & Co. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MTM-Based Work Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observe Operation] --> B[Record Right-Hand and Left-Hand Process Chart]
    B --> C[Decompose into Basic MTM Motions]
    C --> D{Identify Motion Type}
    D --> D1[R Reach]
    D --> D2[M Move]
    D --> D3[T Turn]
    D --> D4[G Grasp]
    D --> D5[P Position]
    D --> D6[RL Release]
    D --> D7[D Disengage]
    D --> D8[AP Apply Pressure]
    D1 --> E[Measure Distance and Case]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    D8 --> E
    E --> F[Lookup TMU from MTM Table]
    F --> G[Sum All TMU Values]
    G --> H[NT equals Sum TMU Normal Time]
    H --> I[Add Personal Fatigue Delay Allowance]
    I --> J[Standard Time Output]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a MOST-Based Work Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Observe Operation] --> L[Identify Type of Sequence Needed]
    L --> L1[G-Move General Move]
    L --> L2[M-Move Controlled Move]
    L --> L3[A-Move Tool Use]
    L1 --> M[Assign Index Values A B G A B P M X]
    L2 --> M2[Assign Index Values A B G A B P M X I]
    L3 --> M3[Assign Index Values A B G A B P A D]
    M --> N[Sum All Index Values]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    N --> O[Choose MOST Level]
    O --> O1[BasicMOST Multiply by 10 TMU]
    O --> O2[MiniMOST Multiply by 1 TMU]
    O --> O3[MaxiMOST Multiply by 100 TMU]
    O1 --> P[Convert TMU to Minutes or Seconds]
    O2 --> P
    O3 --> P
    P --> Q[Add Allowances]
    Q --> R[Final Standard Time]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow: MTM vs MOST

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[New Job Needs Standard Time] --> T{Is stopwatch study possible?}
    T -->|Yes, and cycle short| U[Use Stopwatch Time Study]
    T -->|No, or new method| V{Is high accuracy critical?}
    V -->|Yes, scientific| W[Apply MTM]
    V -->|No, general industrial| X[Apply MOST]
    W --> Y[Train MTM Analyst, use MTM Tables]
    X --> Z[Trained Technician, use MOST Index Tables]
    Y --> AA[Output MTM Standard Time]
    Z --> AB[Output MOST Standard Time]
    AA --> AC[Method Comparison and Improvement]
    AB --> AC
```

## 4.4 TMU Conversion Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Hours] -- x 100000 --> TMU
    TMU -- divide 100000 --> H1
    M1[Minutes] -- x 1666.67 --> TMU
    TMU -- divide 1666.67 --> M1
    S1[Seconds] -- x 27.78 --> TMU
    TMU -- divide 27.78 --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term TMU. State its relationship with hour, minute and second.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
TMU stands for **Time Measurement Unit**, the fundamental time unit used in Predetermined Motion Time Systems such as MTM and MOST.
- **1 TMU = 0.00001 hour = 0.0006 minute = 0.036 second**
- Equivalently: **1 hour = 100,000 TMU**, **1 minute = 1666.67 TMU**, **1 second = 27.78 TMU**
- It is used to express the time for human micro-motions, and therefore the smallest unit of work measurement in industrial engineering. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the eight basic motions of MTM-1 and state the purpose of the "Position (P)" motion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The eight basic MTM-1 motions are: **R – Reach, M – Move, T – Turn, G – Grasp, P – Position, R-L – Release, D – Disengage, A-P – Apply Pressure.** The **Position (P)** motion is used to align, orient, or insert an object so that it mates with another object; it is the most time-consuming motion because the time depends on the symmetry and tightness of fit (Cases P-1 to P-6). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** What is a Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS)? Compare MTM and MOST on any **seven** points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker performs the operation "Pick up a bolt from a tray, screw it into a fixture, and release". The motion sequence is:
- Reach to bolt (Case B) → 7.7 TMU
- Grasp bolt (G-3) → 9.1 TMU
- Move bolt to fixture (Case C) → 10.0 TMU
- Position bolt into hole (P-2) → 16.2 TMU
- Turn the bolt (T-B) → 10.4 TMU
- Release (R-L-A) → 2.0 TMU

Compute the (i) Normal Time in TMU and seconds, (ii) Standard Time if personal, fatigue and contingency allowance totals 18%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) PMTS – Definition & MTM vs MOST (7 Marks):**

A **Predetermined Motion Time System** is a work measurement technique in which standard time values for basic human motions are pre-determined and tabulated from laboratory motion-film analysis. The total time for any manual task is obtained by combining the appropriate motion-time values. **(1 mark)**

Comparison Table: **(6 marks – 1 mark per point)**

| # | Parameter | MTM | MOST |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Year developed | 1948 | 1964 |
| 2 | Origin | US Naval Supply Center | H. B. Maynard, USA |
| 3 | Number of basic motions / sequences | 8 basic motions | 3 activity sequences |
| 4 | Time unit | TMU | TMU |
| 5 | Time required for analysis | High | Low (about 1/3 of MTM) |
| 6 | Skill level required | Highly trained analyst | Trained technician |
| 7 | Level of detail | Very high | Moderate |
| 8 | Accuracy | Highest | Slightly conservative |

**(b) MTM Calculation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Normal Time in TMU:**

$$NT = 7.7 + 9.1 + 10.0 + 16.2 + 10.4 + 2.0$$

Step-by-step addition:

$$7.7 + 9.1 = 16.8 \text{ TMU}$$
$$16.8 + 10.0 = 26.8 \text{ TMU}$$
$$26.8 + 16.2 = 43.0 \text{ TMU}$$
$$43.0 + 10.4 = 53.4 \text{ TMU}$$
$$53.4 + 2.0 = 55.4 \text{ TMU}$$

**Normal Time = 55.4 TMU = 55.4 × 0.036 = 1.9944 s ≈ 1.99 s** **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Standard Time with 18% allowance:**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 1.9944 \times 1.18$$

$$ST = 1.9944 \times 1.18$$

$$= 2.353 \text{ seconds per cycle}$$

**Standard Time ≈ 2.353 s** **(4 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice – **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the three activity sequences used in **BasicMOST** with the help of examples. List the index letters used in each sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a MOST analysis of an assembly operation, the index values are: **A3, B1, G1, A2, B0, P3, M4, X6**. Compute the BasicMOST time in (i) TMU, (ii) seconds, (iii) minutes. If 60 assemblies are produced per hour, what is the production time per unit in seconds? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three Activity Sequences of BasicMOST (7 Marks):**

1. **G-Move (General Move) – `ABG – ABP – MX`** **(2 marks)**
   Used when an object is moved *freely* through space with no constraint. Example: picking up a bolt from a bin and placing it on a table.
   Index letters: **A, B, G, P, M, X**.

2. **M-Move (Controlled Move) – `ABG – ABP – MXD – I`** **(2 marks)**
   Used when the moved object is *constrained* (e.g., guided, sighted, fastened). Example: guiding a screwdriver into a screw head. Adds the index letter **I** (for constraint – cranking, sighting, fastening, etc.).

3. **A-Move (Tool Use Sequence) – `ABG – ABP – AD`** **(3 marks)**
   Used for tool-using tasks like cranking, pumping, or triggering a tool. Example: taking a wrench, applying torque, and returning it. Index letters: **A, B, G, P, A, D**.

**(b) MOST Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given index values: **A3, B1, G1, A2, B0, P3, M4, X6**

**(i) Sum of indices:**

$$\Sigma = 3 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 0 + 3 + 4 + 6 = 20 \text{ indices}$$

For **BasicMOST**, each index = 10 TMU.

$$T = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ TMU}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Convert to seconds:**

$$T = 200 \times 0.036 = 7.2 \text{ s}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Convert to minutes:**

$$T = 7.2 \div 60 = 0.12 \text{ minutes}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Production time check:**

If 60 assemblies are produced per hour, time available per assembly = 3600 s ÷ 60 = 60 s per unit. The MOST-derived cycle time of 7.2 s is well within the available time, indicating that the operation is not the bottleneck. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert TMU to seconds/minutes:** Always state the final answer in the unit asked in the question. TMU by itself is rarely the final answer.
> 2. **Mixing up the MTM "R" and "M" motions:** R = reach (empty hand), M = move (with object). Confusing these two gives wrong times and a full step deduction.
> 3. **MOST index multiplier mistake:** BasicMOST = × 10, MiniMOST = × 1, MaxiMOST = × 100. Examiner allocates 1 mark for the correct multiplier alone.
> 4. **Skipping the "reason for case selection":** KTU now awards marks for explaining *why* a particular case (A, B, C, D) is chosen. Do not just write the TMU value.
> 5. **Forgetting the allowance step:** Normal Time → Standard Time requires the allowance multiplier. Many students lose 2 marks for skipping this.
> 6. **Decimal point errors in TMU-to-seconds conversion:** Always use 0.036 (NOT 0.0360) and 27.78 (NOT 27.8) for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap – Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **TMU definition** = 0.00001 hour = 0.036 second. **Memorize: 1 s = 27.78 TMU.**
- **MTM-1 has 8 basic motions:** Reach, Move, Turn, Grasp, Position, Release, Disengage, Apply Pressure.
- **MTM case letters:** A (easy/short) → E (difficult/long) for R and M; weight-based A, B, C for Turn.
- **Position (P) is the most expensive motion** in MTM; values range from 5.6 TMU (P-1) to 76 TMU (P-6).
- **MOST has 3 sequences:** G-Move (general), M-Move (controlled), A-Move (tool use).
- **MOST index letters:** A, B, G, P, M, X (and I for M-Move, D for A-Move).
- **MOST levels and multipliers:** BasicMOST × 10 TMU, MiniMOST × 1 TMU, MaxiMOST × 100 TMU.
- **MTM gives Normal Time directly** because rating = 1; **Standard Time = Normal Time × (1 + Allowances)**.
- **PMTS advantages:** No stopwatch, no worker rating, no learning-curve bias, can be used pre-implementation.
- **PMTS disadvantages:** Time-consuming, requires trained analyst, may be conservative for very short motions (< 2 s).
- **MTM vs MOST – exam key:** MTM = more accurate, more time; MOST = faster, slightly conservative.
- **Conversion ladder to memorize:**
  - 1 hour = 100,000 TMU
  - 1 minute = 1,666.67 TMU
  - 1 second = 27.78 TMU
- **Always show summation step-by-step** in MTM/MOST problems; examiners deduct marks for "one-line" answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Motion Study: Therbligs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Micro Motion Study: Therbligs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Micro Motion Study — Formal Definition

**Micro Motion Study** is a branch of **Methods Engineering** in which the fundamental motions of a worker's hands, body parts, and limbs are recorded, analyzed, and classified into their smallest indivisible units by using specialized high-speed cinematography (motion pictures at speeds of 50 to 1,000 frames per second) and a stopwatch-based frame-by-frame micro-clock. It was pioneered by **Frank B. Gilbreth** (1868–1924), who, along with his wife **Lillian M. Gilbreth**, reduced all human work into 18 basic elemental units called **Therbligs** (a phonetic reversal of the name **"Gilbreth"**, with letters rearranged).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Micro motion study is the analysis of work using a motion picture camera, micro-clock, and a "simultaneous motion" technique, in which the activity of each body member (left hand, right hand, eyes, legs) is recorded against an absolute time scale so that minute elements of motion can be identified, classified as therbligs, and analyzed for elimination, combination, or simplification.*

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "Movie Director in Slow Motion"

Imagine you are watching a **slow-motion replay** of Sachin Tendulkar's cover drive — you cannot see the exact joint movement at 30 frames per second. But if you record it with a **high-speed camera at 1,000 fps** and play it back frame-by-frame, you can see that the entire shot is composed of micro-events: *back-foot pivot, wrist load, bat-swing arc, follow-through, recovery stance.* Similarly, **Micro Motion Study** decomposes a worker's task into its smallest temporal building blocks using high-speed imaging, and **Therbligs** are the standard vocabulary for naming each micro-event. Without a common vocabulary, two engineers cannot communicate about which motion is wasteful; with therbligs, every motion gets a name, a symbol, and a category — making the analysis objective and repeatable.

## 1.3 Key Equipment of Micro Motion Study

| Equipment | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Motion Picture Camera** | Records operator's work at high speed (50–1,000 fps) |
| **Micro-clock** (1/2000 sec dial) | Provides absolute time scale superimposed on film |
| **Film Analyzer / Projector** | Plays film frame-by-frame for stop-action study |
| **Simultaneous Motion Cycle Chart** | Charts both hands' therbligs side-by-side on time axis |
| **Cyclegraph & Chronocyclegraph** | Light-trail records showing path and duration of motion |

## 1.4 Origin of the Term "Therblig"

> [!NOTE]
> The word **THERBLIG** = **GILBRETH** with letters rearranged, **read backwards**, with an added **"T"** for typing convenience. It represents **any one of the 18 basic elemental motions** into which all manual work can be subdivided.

**Standardized by:** The American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) in 1932.
**Father of Motion Study:** Frank B. Gilbreth — *"Motion is the essence of all work; perfect motion is perfect work."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 18 Basic Therbligs — Complete Classification

Therbligs are classified into **three functional categories** based on whether they actually advance the work, merely assist the work, or waste effort:

### Category A — EFFECTIVE Therbligs (Directly accomplish work / advance the object)
| S/N | Therblig | Symbol | Color Code | Description |
| :-- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Transport Empty (Reach) | TE | Green | Empty hand reaches for object |
| 2 | Grasp | G | Red ochre | Closing fingers on object |
| 3 | Transport Loaded (Move) | TL | Green | Hand moves loaded with object |
| 4 | Position | P | Blue | Orienting/aligning object accurately |
| 5 | Pre-Position | PP | Sky blue | Partial positioning for next operation |
| 6 | Use | U | Purple | Using the tool/object for intended purpose |
| 7 | Assemble | A | Orange | Joining two parts together |
| 8 | Disassemble | DA | Orange | Separating two parts |

### Category B — HOLDING (Effectively idle but necessary)
| S/N | Therblig | Symbol | Color Code | Description |
| :-- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 9 | Hold | H | Gold | Holding object stationary while work occurs |

### Category C — INEFFECTIVE Therbligs (Do NOT accomplish the work)
| S/N | Therblig | Symbol | Color Code | Description |
| :-- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 10 | Release Load | RL | Carmine red | Letting go of object |
| 11 | Inspect | I | Lemon yellow | Comparing object with standard |
| 12 | Search | SH | Black | Eye/hand tries to locate object |
| 13 | Find | F | Black | Eye/hand locates object |
| 14 | Select | ST | Lavender | Choosing among several objects |
| 15 | Plan | PL | Brown | Pausing to determine next action |
| 16 | Rest for Overcoming Fatigue | R | Orange | Worker pauses to recover energy |
| 17 | Unavoidable Delay | UD | Yellow | Delay caused by process/machine |
| 18 | Avoidable Delay | AD | Yellow | Delay caused by worker (loss) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook — "TEA, H, RLISFPSRUA":** *TEA = Transport Empty/loaded, Assemble; H = Hold; RLISFPSRUA = Release, Inspect, Search, Find, Plan, Select, Rest, Unavoidable, Avoidable (with Use embedded in Effective).*

## 2.2 Strategic Grouping — Another Useful Classification

| Functional Group | Therbligs Included | Engineering Goal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Productive** | Transport Loaded, Assemble, Use, Disassemble | Cannot be eliminated; optimize only |
| **Preparatory** | Transport Empty, Grasp, Position, Pre-position | Reduce distance/simplify |
| **Non-Productive** | Hold, Inspect, Search, Select, Plan, Rest, Delays | **Eliminate** wherever possible |

## 2.3 The Six Fundamental Categories of Therbligs (Practical View)

1. **Productive (P)** — TL, A, U, DA — *Work accomplished*
2. **Supporting Preparatory (SP)** — TE, G, P, PP — *Setup for productive*
3. **Supporting Retaining (SR)** — H — *Holding in place*
4. **Supporting Disposing (SD)** — RL — *Releasing object*
5. **Inspecting (I)** — I — *Verifying quality*
6. **Non-Effective (NE)** — SH, F, ST, PL, R, UD, AD — *Waste to be removed*

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Critical Equations for Motion & Time Analysis

| S/N | Formula / Equation | Purpose / Interpretation |
| :-- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T_s = T_o \times R \times (1 + A)$ | Standard Time = Observed × Rating × Allowance Factor |
| 2 | $T_n = T_o \times R$ | Normal Time = Observed Time × Performance Rating |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_{eff}}{\sum t_{cycle}} \times 100$ | Therblig Effectiveness Ratio (in %) |
| 4 | $\eta_{prod} = \dfrac{\sum t_{prod}}{\sum t_{cycle}} \times 100$ | Productive Time Ratio |
| 5 | $\Delta T = T_{old} - T_{new}$ | Time Saved per Cycle |
| 6 | $S = \Delta T \times N \times W \times (1 + O)$ | Annual Savings = Time Saved × Units × Wage × Overhead |
| 7 | $N_{opt} = \dfrac{T_{eff}}{t_{std}} \times N_{req}$ | New Worker Requirement after Improvement |

Where the variables are defined as:

- $T_s$ = Standard time per piece (minutes)
- $T_o$ = Average observed time per cycle
- $R$ = Performance rating factor (e.g., **1.10 for fast worker**, **0.95 for slow worker**)
- $A$ = Allowance fraction (e.g., **0.15 = 15% personal + fatigue + delay**)
- $N$ = Number of cycles/units per year
- $W$ = Wage rate per minute (₹/min)
- $O$ = Overhead factor (e.g., 0.20 = 20%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Problems:** Allowances are typically **15% to 25%** of normal time. Always express effectiveness ratio as a **percentage**, not a fraction.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Industry

- **Automotive Assembly:** Nissan, Toyota, and Tata Motors use therblig analysis to design glove-box, dashboard, and door-assembly stations — eliminating up to **30% non-productive motions**.
- **CNC Machining Cells:** Therblig helps distinguish between machine-controlled "Unavoidable Delays" (UD) and operator-controlled "Avoidable Delays" (AD).
- **Ergonomics & Safety:** "Hold" therbligs flagged as long-duration trigger **Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI)** interventions.
- **Hospital Surgery Suites:** Therblig coding of surgeon's motions reduces operating theater time by 12–18% (Karolininska Institute study).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cycle Time Decomposition from a Simo-Chart

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
The two-handed process chart of an operator assembling a ball-point pen on a fixture yielded the following therblig times (in seconds) for **one complete cycle**:

| Therblig | Left Hand (s) | Right Hand (s) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Transport Empty (TE) | 0.20 | 0.18 |
| Grasp (G) | 0.08 | 0.06 |
| Transport Loaded (TL) | 0.30 | 0.28 |
| Position (P) | 0.15 | 0.12 |
| Assemble (A) | 0.25 | 0.22 |
| Use (U) | 0.40 | 0.35 |
| Hold (H) | 0.10 | 0.08 |
| Release Load (RL) | 0.06 | 0.05 |
| Inspect (I) | 0.12 | 0.10 |
| Search (SH) | 0.18 | 0.00 |
| Avoidable Delay (AD) | 0.00 | 0.15 |
| Rest (R) | 0.10 | 0.10 |

**Step 1 — Compute the Cycle Time for each hand**

For the **Left Hand**, summing all therblig durations:

$$T_{LH} = 0.20 + 0.08 + 0.30 + 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.10 + 0.06 + 0.12 + 0.18 + 0.00 + 0.10$$

$$\boxed{T_{LH} = 1.94 \text{ seconds}}$$

For the **Right Hand**, similarly:

$$T_{RH} = 0.18 + 0.06 + 0.28 + 0.12 + 0.22 + 0.35 + 0.08 + 0.05 + 0.10 + 0.00 + 0.15 + 0.10$$

$$\boxed{T_{RH} = 1.69 \text{ seconds}}$$

The **cycle time** is dictated by the **slower hand**:

$$T_{cycle} = \max(T_{LH},\, T_{RH}) = 1.94 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Classify each therblig as Effective (E), Hold (H), or Ineffective (IN)**

| Therblig | Left (s) | Right (s) | Category |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| TE | 0.20 | 0.18 | E (Preparatory) |
| G | 0.08 | 0.06 | E (Preparatory) |
| TL | 0.30 | 0.28 | **E (Productive)** |
| P | 0.15 | 0.12 | E (Preparatory) |
| A | 0.25 | 0.22 | **E (Productive)** |
| U | 0.40 | 0.35 | **E (Productive)** |
| H | 0.10 | 0.08 | **H (Hold)** |
| RL | 0.06 | 0.05 | E (Disposing) |
| I | 0.12 | 0.10 | IN |
| SH | 0.18 | 0.00 | IN |
| AD | 0.00 | 0.15 | IN |
| R | 0.10 | 0.10 | IN |

**Step 3 — Compute Total Productive Time (both hands combined)**

$$T_{prod} = (TL + A + U)_{LH} + (TL + A + U)_{RH}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{prod} &= (0.30 + 0.25 + 0.40) + (0.28 + 0.22 + 0.35) \\
&= 0.95 + 0.85 \\
&= 1.80 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T_{prod} = 1.80 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Therblig Effectiveness Ratio $\eta$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{T_{prod}}{2 \times T_{cycle}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.80}{2 \times 1.94} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.80}{3.88} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 46.39\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only **46.39%** of the operator's available motion time produces real value. The remaining **53.61%** is preparatory, holding, inspecting, or delay — this is the **improvement potential** that micro-motion study targets.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Standard Time & Annual Savings Calculation

**Given (continued from above):**
After method improvement, the new cycle time becomes **1.50 s/cycle**, all non-productive therbligs eliminated except for an unavoidable Inspect of **0.06 s/hand**. Operator's rating is **110%** (fast worker). Allowance is **20%**.

**Step 1 — Compute New Normal Time**

$$T_n = T_o \times R$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_n &= 1.50 \times 1.10 \\
&= 1.65 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Add Allowance to get Standard Time**

$$T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_s &= 1.65 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
&= 1.65 \times 1.20 \\
&= 1.98 \text{ s/piece}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T_s = 1.98 \text{ s per pen}}$$

**Step 3 — Time Saved per Cycle**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{old} - T_{new} \\
&= 1.94 - 1.50 \\
&= 0.44 \text{ s/cycle}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Savings (Assume N = 5,00,000 pens/year, Wage = ₹0.50/min, Overhead = 30%)**

Convert cycle time saving to minutes:

$$\Delta T_{min} = \frac{0.44}{60} = 7.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ min/cycle}$$

Annual savings:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \Delta T_{min} \times N \times W \times (1 + O) \\
&= 7.333 \times 10^{-3} \times 5,00,000 \times 0.50 \times 1.30 \\
&= 2383.33 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx \text{₹} 2,383/\text{year}}$$

## 3.3 The 10 Principles of Motion Economy (Gilbreth's Laws Applied to Therbligs)

These are derived from Frank Gilbreth's empirical observations and are **directly testable** in any therblig chart:

| # | Principle | Therblig-Level Implication |
| :-- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Both hands should begin & end motions **simultaneously** | Avoid idle Hold on one hand while other works |
| 2 | Both hands should not be **idle simultaneously** | Eliminate avoidable delays |
| 3 | Motions of arms should be **symmetrical & simultaneous** | Mirror left-right therblig patterns |
| 4 | Hand motions should be **confined to lowest classification** | Use fingers > wrist > arm > body |
| 5 | Momentum should be **used to assist worker** | Curve trajectories, avoid abrupt direction changes |
| 6 | Smooth continuous curved motions > straight zig-zag | Eliminate sharp Change-Direction therbligs |
| 7 | **Ballistic motions** (fast) are easier than controlled | Reduce positional corrections |
| 8 | **Rhythm** is essential — uniform work pattern | Eliminate Rest through paced workflow |
| 9 | Work should be arranged to permit **automatic eye control** | Eliminate Search therbligs via color-coding |
| 10 | Workplace & tools should be designed per **anatomical limits** | Reduce Transport Empty distance |

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — What to Do with Each Therblig

| Therblig Found in Excess | Engineering Action |
| :--- | :--- |
| Repeated **Search (SH)** | Use color/feel-coding, fixture locator, shadow-board |
| Frequent **Position (P)** | Pre-position jigs, orienting hopper feeders |
| Long **Hold (H)** | Use mechanical clamp, magnetic holder, vacuum fixture |
| Excessive **Transport Empty (TE)** | Re-layout workplace, foot-pedal triggers |
| **Plan (PL)** pauses | Provide jigs, written instructions, SOPs at point of use |
| **Avoidable Delay (AD)** | Investigate root cause: boredom, instruction unclear, fatigue |
| Frequent **Rest (R)** | Improve ergonomics, anti-fatigue mats, job rotation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
