# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Standing Workbench Height from Anthropometric Data

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
We are asked to design a **standing workbench for precision electronic assembly**. From the anthropometric database of Indian industrial workers (representative regional survey), the following **P5 elbow height** for Indian male workers is given as:

$$H_{elbow, P5} = 980 \text{ mm}$$

For precision work, the work surface should be raised to bring the work close to the eye line; the ergonomic recommendation is to add **+50 mm to +100 mm** above the P5 elbow height.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the governing ergonomic principle.**

For precision work, the operator must be able to see the work *clearly* without hunching. Raising the work surface by an offset $\Delta$ above the elbow height tilts the sight line *downward by an angle* $\theta$, making the work visible while the neck remains neutral.

**Step 2 — Express the workbench height as a sum.**

$$H_{bench} = H_{elbow, P5} + \Delta$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the recommended offset for precision work.**

For precision work, $\Delta$ is taken in the range $50 \le \Delta \le 100$ mm.

**Step 4 — Compute the lower bound of $H_{bench}$.**

$$H_{bench,\,min} = 980 + 50 = 1030 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the upper bound of $H_{bench}$.**

$$H_{bench,\,max} = 980 + 100 = 1080 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — State the final design recommendation.**

$$\boxed{H_{bench} \in [1030,\; 1080] \text{ mm}}$$

A design value of **1050 mm** is the typical mid-range recommendation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Normal Reach Envelope Radius

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
Given the **shoulder-to-fingertip length** of an Indian male industrial worker (P50) is **740 mm**, derive the *normal reach* radius $R_n$ to be used as the boundary for the most-frequently-used controls and tools on a standing workstation.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the empirical reach ratio.**

Empirical ergonomics studies show that the **normal horizontal reach** is approximately **55 %** of the shoulder-to-fingertip length. This ratio accounts for the natural arc of the arm without shoulder or trunk extension.

$$R_n = k \times L_{s-f}$$

where $k \approx 0.55$ and $L_{s-f}$ is the shoulder-to-fingertip length.

**Step 2 — Substitute the value.**

$$R_n = 0.55 \times 740$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical result.**

$$R_n = 407 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Round to engineering tolerance.**

$$\boxed{R_n \approx 400 \text{ to } 500 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

Any control, tool, or material bin used **more than once per minute** should sit within this 400–500 mm radius from the operator's shoulder pivot. Items used less frequently may sit in the *maximum reach* zone, defined next.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Reach Envelope Radius

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup
Continuing from the same anthropometric data ($L_{s-f} = 740$ mm for P50), find the *maximum reach* radius $R_{max}$ for occasional-use items.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the empirical ratio for maximum reach.**

$$R_{max} = k_{max} \times L_{s-f}, \quad k_{max} \approx 0.85$$

The factor **0.85** corresponds to a forward arm extension that includes shoulder rotation but no torso lean.

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$R_{max} = 0.85 \times 740$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$R_{max} = 629 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Round.**

$$\boxed{R_{max} \approx 600 \text{ to } 700 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

Items outside the $R_n$ but inside $R_{max}$ are reachable with *shoulder rotation only*; items beyond $R_{max}$ require *torso lean* or *stepping* and must be repositioned or placed at the workstation boundary.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Wrist Torque During Tool Use

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup
An operator uses a **screwdriver of length $L = 220$ mm** to apply a **rotational force** to a fastener. The operator's hand applies a force $F$ perpendicular to the handle at the end. If the **maximum voluntary wrist torque** is $\tau_{max} = 25$ N·m, find the **maximum force** the operator can apply at the handle end before wrist strain occurs.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the torque equation for a force perpendicular to a lever.**

$$\tau = F \times d$$

where $d$ is the perpendicular distance from the wrist pivot (axis of rotation) to the line of action of the force. For a screwdriver gripped at its end and rotated about the wrist axis, $d \approx L$, the length of the screwdriver.

**Step 2 — Solve for $F$.**

$$F = \dfrac{\tau}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\tau = 25$ N·m and $d = 0.220$ m.**

$$F = \dfrac{25}{0.220}$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$F = 113.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Round and interpret.**

$$\boxed{F_{max} \approx 114 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering insight.**

If the screw requires more than 114 N at the screwdriver end, the operator *cannot* generate the torque by wrist action alone. The designer must then provide:
  - a **longer handle** (increasing $d$),
  - a **ratchet** (repositioning the wrist),
  - a **mechanical advantage** (gear, breaker bar), or
  - a **powered screwdriver** (eliminating manual force).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Workbench Height vs Task Type

The following derivation-based matrix summarises the **recommended standing workbench height** for the P5 Indian male worker ($H_{elbow, P5} = 980$ mm) across task types.

| Task Type | Recommended Offset $\Delta$ | Lower Bound $H_{bench}$ (mm) | Upper Bound $H_{bench}$ (mm) | Design Value (mm) |
|-----------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|
| Heavy manual work (lifting, hammering) | $-100$ to $0$ | 880 | 980 | 930 |
| General assembly | $0$ to $+50$ | 980 | 1030 | 1000 |
| Precision assembly (electronics) | $+50$ to $+100$ | 1030 | 1080 | 1050 |
| Inspection (microscope-aided) | $+100$ to $+150$ | 1080 | 1130 | 1100 |
| Light bench inspection (no microscope) | $+50$ to $+75$ | 1030 | 1055 | 1040 |

> [!NOTE]
> The offset $\Delta$ is the **single most exam-relevant parameter** in Module 4. Memorise the *sign convention*:
> - $\Delta < 0$ for **heavy** work (work below elbow).
> - $\Delta \approx 0$ for **general** work (work at elbow).
> - $\Delta > 0$ for **precision / inspection** work (work above elbow, close to eye line).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Grip Diameter vs Grip Type

| Grip Type | Recommended Diameter (mm) | Typical Tool Examples |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Precision pinch | 8 – 15 | Tweezers, small screwdriver for electronics, scalpel |
| Precision wrap | 15 – 25 | Pencil, small soldering iron |
| Medium power | 30 – 40 | Screwdriver for general assembly, pliers |
| Heavy power | 40 – 50 | Hammer handle, heavy wrench |
| Two-handled | 30 – 50 per handle | Bolt cutter, large pliers |

**Why this matters for tool design:** A grip that is *too thin* forces the operator to over-squeeze, producing hand fatigue within minutes. A grip that is *too thick* reduces the contact area and the operator cannot generate full force. The **30–50 mm** range is the empirical sweet spot for power grips.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Workplace Reach Zones (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** of the reach zones around an operator. It maps the *spatial* reach regions to the *frequency-of-use* categories of items placed within them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Operator Shoulder Pivot] --> B1[Zone 1 - Normal Reach - 400 to 500 mm]
    A0 --> B2[Zone 2 - Maximum Reach - 600 to 700 mm]
    A0 --> B3[Zone 3 - Extended Reach - Beyond 700 mm with Lean]

    B1 --> C1[Frequently used tools and controls]
    B1 --> C2[Primary material bin]
    B1 --> C3[Visual display and gauges]

    B2 --> C4[Occasionally used tools]
    B2 --> C5[Secondary work instructions]

    B3 --> C6[Rare use items only]
    B3 --> C7[Safety equipment standby]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The operator's shoulder pivot is the *origin*. Items placed in **Zone 1** are reachable without any arm or shoulder movement — these should be the **most frequently used** items. Items in **Zone 3** require *trunk lean or stepping* and should be reserved for *infrequent* or *safety* items. The frequency-of-use design rule is the single most frequently asked concept in this module.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Tool Design Decision Flow (Selection Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start - New Tool Requirement] --> S1{Is the task a single high-force application?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Use powered tool or mechanical advantage]
    S1 -- No --> S3{Is the task repeated more than 5 times per minute?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Use light weight tool mass less than 1 kg]
    S3 -- No --> S5{Is the task precision critical to within 1 mm?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Use precision grip tool diameter 8 to 25 mm]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Use general purpose tool grip diameter 30 to 50 mm]
    S2 --> S8[Check vibration isolation and noise]
    S4 --> S9[Check for repetitive strain injury risk]
    S6 --> S10[Check visibility and sight line]
    S7 --> S11[Finalise handle shape and grip material]
    S8 --> S12[Tool Selection Complete]
    S9 --> S12
    S10 --> S12
    S11 --> S12
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every tool design question in the KTU 2024 paper traces through this decision tree. Force, frequency, and precision are the **three primary filters**. After selecting the tool class, ergonomic refinements (vibration, RSI, visibility) are layered on.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Workbench Height Selection by Task Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Determine Task Type] --> T1{Task is Heavy Manual?}
    T1 -- Yes --> T2[Set bench at or below 5th percentile elbow height]
    T1 -- No --> T3{Task is General Assembly?}
    T3 -- Yes --> T4[Set bench at 5th percentile elbow height]
    T3 -- No --> T5{Task is Precision Assembly?}
    T5 -- Yes --> T6[Set bench 50 to 100 mm above 5th percentile elbow]
    T5 -- No --> T7{Task is Microscopic Inspection?}
    T7 -- Yes --> T8[Set bench 100 to 150 mm above 5th percentile elbow]
    T7 -- No --> T9[Default to seated workstation design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace any task type through the diamond decision nodes. The final block is the **height specification**. The "5th percentile elbow height" is the *anchor* in every branch — this is the most important anthropometric anchor in the module.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Workplace Layout — Plan View Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Workplace Plan View Layout] --> P1[Center - Primary Work Zone]
    P0 --> P2[Left Side - Incoming Material Bin]
    P0 --> P3[Right Side - Outgoing Finished Goods Bin]
    P0 --> P4[Top Edge - Visual Display and Instructions]
    P0 --> P5[Bottom Edge - Foot Space and Pedal Area]

    P1 --> P1A[Fixture or Jig at Center]
    P1 --> P1B[Tool Tray within 500 mm Reach]
    P1 --> P1C[Lighting Source from Top]

    P2 --> P2A[P1 to P5 Items - Frequently Used]
    P2 --> P2B[P5 to P15 Items - Occasionally Used]

    P3 --> P3A[Output Tilted Toward Operator for Easy Reach]

    P4 --> P4A[Standard Operating Procedure Card]
    P4 --> P4B[Andon Light Status]

    P5 --> P5A[Minimum 600 mm Depth]
    P5 --> P5B[Minimum 500 mm Width]
    P5 --> P5C[Foot Pedal placed 100 to 150 mm from front edge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The plan-view workplace layout is the most direct KTU 2024 question. **Material flow** enters from the left, work happens at the centre, finished goods exit from the right. The top edge is for *information*; the bottom edge is for *legs and feet*. This left-to-right flow is the dominant workplace-design pattern in lean assembly cells.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Tool Grip Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[Tool Grip Cross Section] --> G1[Grip Outer Surface - Non-slip and slightly compressible]
    G0 --> G2[Handle Core - Rigid and transmits force]
    G0 --> G3[Internal Cavity Optional - Weight reduction or balance insertion]
    G0 --> G4[End Cap - Prevents hand slip and protects tool]

    G1 --> G1A[Surface texture 0.5 to 1.5 mm ridges]
    G1 --> G1B[Material Shore A hardness 30 to 60]

    G2 --> G2A[Material aluminium or reinforced polymer]
    G2 --> G2B[Diameter 30 to 50 mm for power grip]

    G3 --> G3A[Weight 200 to 400 grams typical]

    G4 --> G4A[Rounded contour no sharp edge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A well-designed tool handle is a **layered structure**: an *outer grip layer* (comfort + friction), a *rigid core* (force transmission), an optional *internal cavity* (mass tuning), and an *end cap* (safety). The diameter and material properties in the inner blocks are the **KTU-exam-expected numerical values**.
