# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Workplace" as applied in Work System Design. List any four factors that influence workplace design.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Workplace** is the physically bounded spatial envelope in which an operator, machine, and tooling interact to perform a defined task. It includes the work surface, fixtures, tools, controls, displays, material bins, and environmental conditioning arranged around the operator.

**Four factors influencing workplace design (½ mark each):**

1. **Anthropometry** of the operator population (body dimensions, reach, strength).
2. **Task characteristics** — frequency, force, precision, duration.
3. **Process requirements** — feeding, fixturing, cycle time, quality.
4. **Environmental conditions** — lighting, noise, temperature, vibration.

*(Optional 4th factor: Safety and ergonomic regulations; or 5S / lean layout philosophy.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain any two principles of motion economy that govern the placement of tools in a workplace.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Principle of Simultaneous Motion:** Both hands should begin and end their motions at the same time, working in symmetric and balanced patterns. (1½ marks)

2. **Principle of Minimum Reach:** Frequently used tools and materials should be placed within the *normal reach envelope* (≈ 500 mm from the shoulder pivot). Infrequently used items may be at the *maximum reach* zone. (1½ marks)

*(Equally valid alternative: Principle of Fixed Work Location, or Principle of Gravity Feed, or Principle of Eye Focus.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not** state a single workbench height without specifying the **task type** (heavy / general / precision / inspection). Each task maps to a different $\Delta$ offset.
> 2. **Do not** forget to mention **which percentile** (P5, P50, P95) you are using and **why**. Examiners will deduct up to 2 marks for "blind" use of anthropometric numbers.
> 3. **Do not** confuse *normal reach* (0.55 × arm length) with *maximum reach* (0.85 × arm length). This is a 2–3 mark deduction.
> 4. **Always show the units** in the final numerical answer. A "1050" without "mm" is considered incomplete.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Define **anthropometry** and explain the difference between **static and dynamic anthropometry**. Why is the 5th–95th percentile range used in workplace design? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **standing precision electronic assembly workstation**, the P5 elbow height of the operator population is **980 mm**. Determine the recommended workbench height range. List any three additional design considerations for this workstation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Definition and Percentile Logic (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** *Anthropometry* is the empirical study and measurement of human body dimensions — bone lengths, segment lengths, joint pivots, reach distances, grip spans, and joint ranges of motion — across a defined population. It forms the **empirical foundation** of ergonomic workplace and tool design.

**Static vs Dynamic Anthropometry (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Anthropometry | Dynamic Anthropometry |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Definition | Measurements of the body *at rest* in fixed postures | Measurements *during motion* and functional reach |
| Examples | Standing height, sitting height, arm length, shoulder width | Forward reach while seated, trunk rotation, eye rotation |
| Design use | Clearance dimensions, fixed barriers, doorway width | Working reach to controls, comfortable motion arcs |
| Measurement method | Anthropometer, stadiometer (fixed callipers) | Motion capture, functional reach tests |

The two are *complementary*: static dimensions define *envelopes* (where the body fits), while dynamic dimensions define *interactions* (how the body works).

**5th–95th Percentile Rule (2 marks):** The design must accommodate the operator population *between* the 5th and 95th percentiles. This range covers **90 % of the population** while keeping design extremes within reasonable cost. A P5 dimension (e.g., the lowest elbow height) sets the *minimum workbench height*; a P95 dimension (e.g., the tallest standing height) sets the *maximum clearance*. Designing below P5 or above P95 would either *exclude* a tail of the population or *waste material* with no inclusive benefit.

##### Part (b) — Workbench Height Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the governing formula (1 mark):**

$$H_{bench} = H_{elbow, P5} + \Delta$$

**Step 2 — Identify the offset for precision electronic assembly (1 mark):** Precision work requires the surface to be **50 mm to 100 mm above** the P5 elbow height, so $50 \le \Delta \le 100$ mm.

**Step 3 — Substitute the lower bound (1 mark):**

$$H_{bench,\,min} = 980 + 50 = 1030 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the upper bound (1 mark):**

$$H_{bench,\,max} = 980 + 100 = 1080 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — State the final design value (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{H_{bench} \in [1030,\; 1080] \text{ mm, design value 1050 mm}}$$

**Three additional design considerations (2 marks, 2/3 each):**

1. **Lighting:** Precision electronic work requires **≥ 500 lux** of uniform, glare-free illumination, preferably with a task lamp focused on the work zone.
2. **ESD Protection:** The workbench must include an **anti-static mat** and grounding wrist strap, since electronic components are sensitive to electrostatic discharge.
3. **Seating or Sit–Stand Stool:** A height-adjustable stool (or sit–stand platform) allows postural variation and reduces fatigue for operators working at the bench for > 2 hours continuously.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Define a **tool** in the context of work system design. List and briefly explain **any four ergonomic variables** that govern tool design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An operator applies a **force $F$ perpendicular to a screwdriver handle of length 250 mm** to tighten a screw. The maximum voluntary wrist torque is **20 N·m**. Determine the **maximum sustainable force** at the handle. If the screw requires a torque of **8 N·m**, find the **minimum handle length** the operator can use without exceeding the wrist-torque limit when applying the same force. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Tool Definition and Ergonomic Variables (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** A **tool** in work system design is any *manually held, hand-guided, or hand-powered implement* that extends, amplifies, or refines the human hand's natural capability to apply force, cut, hold, shape, join, or measure. Tools bridge the gap between the operator's hand and the workpiece, and their design directly influences operator fatigue, accuracy, and safety.

**Four ergonomic variables (5 marks, 1.25 each):**

1. **Grip diameter** — Should be **30–50 mm** for power grip and **8–25 mm** for precision grip. A grip that is too small forces over-squeezing; too large reduces contact area and force.
2. **Tool mass** — Should generally be **less than 2.5 kg** for continuous overhead or extended use. Heavy tools accelerate fatigue and risk musculoskeletal injury.
3. **Handle length** — Must exceed the breadth of the closed hand (≥ 100 mm for a power grip) to allow a full wrap and maximum force transfer.
4. **Centre-of-mass alignment** — The mass should lie along the grip axis (or slightly forward of the grip) so that no parasitic wrist torque is generated when the tool is held horizontal.

*(Valid alternative variables: grip surface texture, vibration isolation, trigger force, handle shape/angle, balance point.)*

##### Part (b) — Screwdriver Force and Length Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the torque equation (1 mark):**

$$\tau = F \times d$$

where $d$ is the perpendicular distance from the wrist pivot to the line of action of $F$. For a screwdriver, $d$ is taken as the handle length.

**Step 2 — Solve for $F$ in the first case (1 mark):**

$$F = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\tau_{max} = 20$ N·m and $d = 0.250$ m (1 mark):**

$$F = \dfrac{20}{0.250} = 80 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{max,\,sustained} = 80 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4 — Note the result (1 mark):** The operator can apply a sustained force of **80 N** at the end of a 250 mm screwdriver before reaching the wrist-torque limit.

**Step 5 — Set up the second case (1 mark):** For the same force $F = 80$ N, and required torque $\tau_{req} = 8$ N·m, the minimum handle length is:

$$d_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{req}}{F} = \dfrac{8}{80} = 0.100 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Final answer and check (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{d_{min} = 100 \text{ mm}}$$

*Cross-check:* With $d = 100$ mm and $F = 80$ N, torque is $80 \times 0.1 = 8$ N·m, which exactly matches the required torque. Since $8 \text{ N·m} \le \tau_{max} = 20$ N·m, the operator remains within the safe limit. ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Workplace and Tools (Module 4)**

- **Workplace definition** — the spatial envelope where the operator interfaces with machines, tools, and materials.
- **Workplace components** — work surface, fixtures, material bins, controls, displays, environmental conditioning, safety devices.
- **Anthropometry** — empirical science of body measurements; the **P5–P95 range** accommodates 90 % of the population.
- **Static anthropometry** — body at rest (clearance, fixed dimensions). **Dynamic anthropometry** — body in motion (reach, control placement).
- **Workbench height formula** — $H_{bench} = H_{elbow, P5} + \Delta$, where $\Delta$ is **negative for heavy work**, **zero for general work**, and **positive for precision/inspection work**.
- **Standard workbench heights** — Standing heavy: **880–980 mm**; Standing general: **980–1030 mm**; Standing precision: **1030–1080 mm**; Seated: **700–760 mm**.
- **Reach zones** — Normal reach ≈ **0.55 × arm length** (≈ 400–500 mm); Maximum reach ≈ **0.85 × arm length** (≈ 600–700 mm).
- **Foot clearance** — minimum **600 mm depth × 500 mm width** under seated workstations.
- **Grip force** — Precision pinch: **20–60 N**; Power grip: **100–400 N**.
- **Tool ergonomic variables** — grip diameter (8–50 mm), mass (< 2.5 kg), handle length (≥ 100 mm), centre-of-mass alignment, surface texture, vibration.
- **Wrist torque** — $\tau = F \times d$; maximum voluntary wrist torque ≈ **20–25 N·m** for sustained work.
- **Principles of motion economy** — simultaneous hands, normal reach, fixed locations, gravity feed, eye focus.
- **Environmental standards** — illuminance ≥ **500 lux** for precision; noise ≤ **85 dB(A)** for 8 h exposure.
- **Workstation types** — standing, seated, sit–stand, high-stool; selection based on task type, force, duration, and precision.
- **5S connection** — workplace design and tool selection directly support *Seiri* (sort), *Seiton* (set in order), *Seiso* (shine), *Seiketsu* (standardise), *Shitsuke* (sustain).

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Workplace and Tools | PCIET403 — Work System Design | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Worker
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Worker

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WORKER (in Work System Design Context):**
> A *worker* is the human element within a work system who converts inputs (raw materials, energy, information) into outputs (products, services) through a combination of physical effort, mental cognition, sensory perception, and skill-based decisions. In Work System Design (WSD), the worker is treated as a **biological, psychological, and sociological subsystem** whose capabilities, limitations, and motivations must be matched to the demands of the task and the workplace environment to optimize productivity, safety, and well-being.

In the framework of KTU's PCIET403 (Work System Design), the *worker* is studied as the central entity interacting with **machines, materials, methods, and the environment**. The discipline evaluates the worker using three primary lenses:

1. **Physical (Anthropometric) Lens** – body dimensions, strength, endurance, range of motion.
2. **Cognitive (Psychological) Lens** – perception, memory, decision-making, reaction time.
3. **Behavioral (Socio-psychological) Lens** – motivation, satisfaction, fatigue, learning curve.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Orchestra Musician:**
> Think of a worker as a **musician in an orchestra**. The musician (worker) has natural strengths (dexterity, pitch) and limitations (lung capacity, finger stretch). The musical score (the *method*) sets the rhythm and notes. The instrument (the *machine*) is shaped to suit the musician's hand size and posture. The conductor (the *supervisor/work designer*) adjusts tempo, rest periods, and seating to keep the musician comfortable and productive. If the instrument is too heavy, the chair too high, or the score too complex — performance drops and fatigue rises. **Work System Design is exactly this orchestration** — designing the score, instrument, and stage to fit the musician.

## Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Human Metrics Used in Worker Analysis:**
> - **Standard Rest Allowance:** typically **10%–20%** of cycle time.
> - **Standard Human Output (Normal Operator):** a worker who achieves **100 on the standard rating scale** with no personal, fatigue, or contingency (PFD) allowances applied.
> - **Reaction Time (Simple):** approximately **0.25 seconds**.
> - **Maximum Heart Rate (Working Limit):** approximately **200 − age** beats per minute (Astrand formula).
> - **Standard Lifting Limit (NIOSH):** Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) of **23 kg** under ideal conditions at **0 kg horizontal distance, 25 cm lift origin, 175 cm destination**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Worker as a Subsystem — Inputs, Process, Outputs

### Step 1 — Inputs to the Worker
The worker receives multiple streams from the workplace:
- **Material/Energy Inputs** — raw stock, power tool energy, information feeds.
- **Sensory Inputs** — visual cues (gauges, screens), auditory signals (alarms, voice), tactile feedback.
- **Instructional Inputs** — SOPs, SOP sheets, digital work instructions.

### Step 2 — Internal Processing
The human body processes these through:
- **Perception** → **Cognition** → **Decision** → **Motor Response**
This is the classic **Information-Processing Model of Human Performance**.

### Step 3 — Outputs from the Worker
- **Physical Output** — assembled parts, lifted loads, typed characters.
- **Feedback Output** — status reports, exception flags.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step
Work design fails when the *processing step* is overloaded. A worker cannot lift 50 kg if the muscle system cannot generate that force, regardless of motivation. Similarly, a worker cannot process 15 simultaneous signals if **Miller's Law (7 ± 2 items in working memory)** is exceeded.

## 2.2 Worker Characteristics — Taxonomy

### A. Physical Characteristics
- **Anthropometry** — body dimensions; basis for workplace design (chair height, reach envelope).
- **Strength** — static (isometric) and dynamic (isotonic) capacities.
- **Endurance** — capacity to sustain effort over a shift (typically **8 hours**).
- **Speed of Movement** — governed by **Fitts' Law**.

### B. Sensory Characteristics
- **Visual Acuity** — typically declines after age **40 (presbyopia)**.
- **Auditory Range** — most sensitive at **2000–5000 Hz**.
- **Tactile Sensitivity** — fingertips: ~10 μm gap detection.

### C. Mental/Behavioral Characteristics
- **Motivation** (Maslow, Herzberg, Vroom theories apply).
- **Learning Curve Effect** — time reduces with repetition.
- **Fatigue & Boredom** — degrade output without rest allowances.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FORMULA CHEAT SHEET — WORKER ANALYSIS**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Boundary / Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Time | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | $A$ = Allowance fraction | minutes (min) |
| Normal Time | $NT = OT \times R$ | $R$ = Rating on 100 scale | minutes |
| Performance Rating | $R = \dfrac{\text{Observed Speed}}{\text{Normal Speed}} \times 100$ | $100$ = standard worker | % (points) |
| PFD Allowance | $A = A_p + A_f + A_c$ | $A_p$=personal, $A_f$=fatigue, $A_c$=contingency | decimal |
| NIOSH RWL | $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | $LC = 23$ kg | kg |
| Fitts' Law (Movement Time) | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$ | $a,b$ = empirical constants | seconds (s) |
| Learning Curve | $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{b}$ | $b = \dfrac{\log(\text{learning \%})}{\log 2}$ | time units |
| Heart Rate Limit | $HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age}$ | U.S. Army / Astrand models | beats/min |
| Energy Expenditure Limit | $E \leq 5$ kcal/min (sustained) | for 8-hour shift | kcal/min |
| Heat Stress Limit (WBGT) | $WBGT \leq 30^\circ C$ (heavy work) | ISO 7243 | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **RWL** is used by **NIOSH** to prevent lifting injuries in warehouses and hospitals.
> - **Fitts' Law** is used to design **computer touchscreens, HMIs, and surgical robot controls**.
> - **Learning Curve** is used to **forecast production capacity** in semiconductor and aircraft assembly.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Standard Time from Worker Performance Data

### Problem Setup
A KTU examiner-style problem: *A worker is observed to take 45 seconds to complete an operation. The rating observer judges the worker's speed as 120% of normal. The PFD allowance is 18%. Calculate the Standard Time.*

### Given
- Observed Time $OT = 45$ seconds $= 0.75$ min.
- Performance Rating $R = 120\%$.
- Allowance Factor $A = 18\% = 0.18$.

### Step 1 — Convert Observed Time to Minutes
This is necessary because allowances and ratings are conventionally expressed in **minutes per cycle**.

$$
OT_{min} = \dfrac{45}{60} = 0.75 \text{ min}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* There are 60 seconds in one minute. Dividing the seconds by 60 gives the equivalent value in minutes.

### Step 2 — Compute Normal Time (NT)
The Normal Time is the time a **standard-rated worker (R = 100)** would require at the same conditions.

$$
NT = OT_{min} \times \dfrac{R}{100}
$$

Substituting:

$$
NT = 0.75 \times \dfrac{120}{100}
$$

$$
NT = 0.75 \times 1.20
$$

$$
NT = 0.90 \text{ min}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The observed worker was 20% faster than the standard. To bring the time to the standard reference, we multiply by the ratio $R/100$.

### Step 3 — Compute Standard Time (ST)
Standard Time adds PFD allowances to cover personal needs, fatigue, and contingencies.

$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A)
$$

Substituting:

$$
ST = 0.90 \times (1 + 0.18)
$$

$$
ST = 0.90 \times 1.18
$$

$$
ST = 1.062 \text{ min}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* An 18% allowance means for every 100 units of normal work, the system allows 18 extra units to cover breaks and fatigue. So total standard time is $100\% + 18\% = 118\%$ of normal time.

### Final Answer
$$
\boxed{ST = 1.062 \text{ min per cycle} \;\; (\approx 63.72 \text{ sec})}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)

### Problem Setup
A worker must lift a 15 kg box from a horizontal distance of 30 cm, with a vertical lift origin of 50 cm above the floor and a destination of 150 cm. The object is 25 cm wide and has no handles. The worker twists moderately. Calculate the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** and the **Lifting Index (LI)**.

### Given
- Load Constant $LC = 23$ kg.
- Horizontal Multiplier $HM = \dfrac{25}{H}$ where $H$ is horizontal distance in cm.
- Vertical Multiplier $VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$.
- Distance Multiplier $DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{4.5}{D}$.
- Asymmetry Multiplier $AM = 1 - 0.0032 A$.
- Frequency Multiplier $FM$ (assume low frequency = 1.00).
- Coupling Multiplier $CM$ (poor coupling = 0.90).

### Step 1 — Compute Each Multiplier

**Horizontal Multiplier (HM):**
Given $H = 30$ cm.

$$
HM = \dfrac{25}{30} = 0.833
$$

*Conversion Logic:* NIOSH prescribes 25 cm as the ideal horizontal distance. The multiplier is inversely proportional to the actual distance — greater distance reduces safe capacity.

**Vertical Multiplier (VM):**
Average vertical location $V = \dfrac{50 + 150}{2} = 100$ cm.

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 100 - 75 \vert
$$

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 25 = 1 - 0.075 = 0.925
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The ideal vertical lift region is around 75 cm. Deviation from 75 cm reduces the multiplier linearly.

**Distance Multiplier (DM):**
Vertical travel $D = 150 - 50 = 100$ cm.

$$
DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{4.5}{100} = 0.82 + 0.045 = 0.865
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Larger vertical travel requires more energy and stability; the multiplier slightly above 0.82 reflects minimum travel penalty.

**Asymmetry Multiplier (AM):**
Assume asymmetry angle $A = 45^\circ$.

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 45 = 1 - 0.144 = 0.856
$$

**Frequency Multiplier (FM):**
Low frequency, short duration → $FM = 1.00$.

**Coupling Multiplier (CM):**
No handles, poor grip → $CM = 0.90$.

### Step 2 — Compute RWL

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

$$
RWL = 23 \times 0.833 \times 0.925 \times 0.865 \times 0.856 \times 1.00 \times 0.90
$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$
23 \times 0.833 = 19.159
$$

$$
19.159 \times 0.925 = 17.722
$$

$$
17.722 \times 0.865 = 15.330
$$

$$
15.330 \times 0.856 = 13.123
$$

$$
13.123 \times 1.00 = 13.123
$$

$$
13.123 \times 0.90 = 11.811 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Lifting Index (LI)

$$
LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{RWL}} = \dfrac{15}{11.811} = 1.270
$$

### Interpretation
$LI > 1.0$ indicates the lift **exceeds the recommended limit**, exposing the worker to elevated risk of musculoskeletal injury. A redesign is recommended.

$$
\boxed{RWL \approx 11.81 \text{ kg}, \quad LI \approx 1.27 \; (\text{Risk Exists})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Fitts' Law Movement Time

### Problem Setup
A quality inspector must move a hand between two targets on a touchscreen. Distance $D = 20$ cm, target width $W = 4$ cm. Empirical constants $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.20$ s/bit. Compute Movement Time $MT$.

### Step 1 — Compute Index of Difficulty (ID)

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right) = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{20}{4}+1\right) = \log_2(6)
$$

$$
\log_2(6) = \dfrac{\ln 6}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{1.7918}{0.6931} = 2.585 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute MT

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 0.05 + 0.20 \cdot 2.585
$$

$$
MT = 0.05 + 0.517 = 0.567 \text{ seconds}
$$

### Interpretation
The worker requires **~0.57 seconds** for this movement — useful for HMI design where total task time must fit a cycle.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worker Design Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **PRINCIPLE → APPLICATION → METRIC** (Rapid KTU Reference)

| Design Principle | Application Context | Verification Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Match task to worker strength | Manual material handling | NIOSH LI $<$ 1.0 |
| Match task to worker reach | Workstation layout | Anthropometric 5th–95th percentile fit |
| Reduce information load | Control panel design | Miller's 7 ± 2 chunks |
| Match pace to human limits | Assembly line balancing | Cycle time ≥ 1.1 × NT |
| Provide rest allowances | Shift scheduling | Allowance ≥ 12% for moderate work |
| Use Learning Curve | Capacity planning | $T_n = T_1 n^b$ |
| Manage fatigue | Repetitive tasks | Heart rate ≤ 110 bpm sustained |
| Improve motivation | Wage/incentive systems | Herzberg hygiene factors |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Worker as a Subsystem — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inputs Material Energy Information] --> B[Worker Human Subsystem]
    C[Sensory Stimuli Light Sound Vibration] --> B
    D[Instructions SOPs Digital Cues] --> B
    B --> E[Physical Output Products Actions]
    B --> F[Feedback Status Reports]
    B --> G[Fatigue Recovery Loop]
    G --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Worker** block consumes three streams: materials, sensory stimuli, and instructions.
- It produces two streams: physical output and feedback.
- A **fatigue-recovery loop** modulates future capacity.

## 4.2 Information Processing Model Inside the Worker

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stimulus Reception Sensory Organs] --> P2[Perception Pattern Recognition]
    P2 --> P3[Short Term Memory 7 plus minus 2]
    P3 --> P4[Decision Making Cognition]
    P4 --> P5[Motor Response Muscles]
    P5 --> P6[Feedback Loop to Perception]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Sequential flow through perception → memory → decision → action.
- Feedback closes the loop for **closed-loop skill** (e.g., driving, surgery).

## 4.3 Worker Design Trade-Off Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Demand] --> B{Human Capable}
    B -- Yes --> C[Direct Worker Assignment]
    B -- No --> D[Modify Task Subdivide]
    D --> E[Use Machine Aid Mechatronics]
    E --> F[Redesign Workstation]
    F --> G[Re-evaluate Human Capable]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Iterative redesign loop. If the worker cannot handle the demand, the system designer must modify the task, not the worker.

## 4.4 Compensation Equation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Standard Worker R equals 100] --> B[Observed Time OT]
    B --> C[Normal Time NT]
    C --> D[Allowance A]
    D --> E[Standard Time ST]
    A -.multiplied by.-> C
    C -.multiplied by.-> E
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define the term "Worker" in the context of Work System Design. List any four physical characteristics of a worker that influence work design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A worker is the human element of a work system who transforms inputs into outputs using physical, cognitive, and sensory capabilities, and whose performance is shaped by ergonomic, psychological, and environmental factors.
- **Four Physical Characteristics (1 mark, any four):**
  1. Anthropometric dimensions (body size).
  2. Muscular strength (static and dynamic).
  3. Cardiovascular endurance.
  4. Range of motion of joints.
  5. Sensory acuity (vision, hearing, touch).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**What is "Normal Time"? How is it related to Observed Time and Performance Rating?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Normal Time (1 mark):** The time a standard-rated worker (R = 100) would take to perform a task at a normal pace.
- **Relation (2 marks):** Normal Time is computed as
$$
NT = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100}
$$
where $OT$ is the observed time and $R$ is the rating on a 100-point scale. If $R > 100$, the worker is faster than standard; if $R < 100$, slower.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 4):** Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (each 14 marks, sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **Physical, Sensory, and Mental/Behavioral characteristics** of a worker in Work System Design. Describe how each characteristic influences **workstation design**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A worker is observed to complete a job in **48 seconds**. The **rating** is **110%**. The **personal allowance** is **8%**, **fatigue allowance** is **10%**, and **contingency allowance** is **3%**. Compute the **Standard Time** per cycle. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Characteristics Influencing Workstation Design:**

**1. Physical Characteristics (2 marks):**
- **Anthropometry:** Body dimensions dictate reach zones, seat height, and clearance. Workstations are designed for the **5th–95th percentile** of the worker population.
- **Strength:** Static and dynamic lifting capacities limit manual handling tasks; NIOSH RWL must not be exceeded.
- **Endurance:** Determines acceptable shift length and rest frequency.
- **Influence:** Operators' chairs, tool heights, and conveyor positions are sized to fit the human body to reduce strain.

**2. Sensory Characteristics (2 marks):**
- **Vision:** Most important in assembly. Adequate lighting (**300–500 lux** for general, **1000+ lux** for fine work) is essential.
- **Hearing:** Critical for alarm recognition; noisy environments mask signals and induce fatigue.
- **Touch:** Important for assembly verification and tool handling.
- **Influence:** Display panels must place critical gauges within **±15° of the normal line of sight** to minimize neck rotation.

**3. Mental/Behavioral Characteristics (3 marks):**
- **Perception & Memory:** Information displays should obey **Miller's Law (7 ± 2 chunks)** to avoid overload.
- **Decision-Making:** Reaction time (~0.25 s for simple stimulus) sets minimum cycle limits.
- **Motivation (Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory):** Hygiene factors (salary, working conditions) and motivators (recognition, growth) shape productivity.
- **Learning Curve:** Workers improve with practice; $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^b$.
- **Influence:** Job rotation, training programs, and incentive schemes all stem from these mental/behavioral traits.

**Part (b) — Standard Time Calculation (Full 7-Mark Solution):**

**Given:**
- Observed Time $OT = 48$ sec $= 0.80$ min.
- Rating $R = 110\%$.
- Total Allowance $A = A_p + A_f + A_c = 0.08 + 0.10 + 0.03 = 0.21$ (i.e., 21%).

**Step 1 — Compute Normal Time:**

$$
NT = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100} = 0.80 \times 1.10 = 0.88 \text{ min}
$$

*Logic:* The worker was 10% faster than the standard, so we scale the time up by 1.10 to normalize it.

**Step 2 — Compute Standard Time:**

$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 0.88 \times 1.21 = 1.0648 \text{ min}
$$

*Logic:* A 21% allowance means the worker needs 121% of normal time to cover breaks, fatigue, and contingencies.

**Step 3 — Express in seconds for clarity:**

$$
ST = 1.0648 \times 60 = 63.89 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{ST = 1.0648 \text{ min} \;\;(\approx 63.89 \text{ sec per cycle})}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **Information Processing Model** of a worker. How does it help in designing **control panels and displays**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A lifting task has the following parameters: Horizontal distance **25 cm**, Vertical origin **40 cm**, Vertical destination **160 cm**, Asymmetry angle **30°**, Object weight **10 kg**, Frequency low, Coupling fair ($CM = 0.95$). Calculate the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** and **Lifting Index (LI)**. Is the task safe? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Information Processing Model (7 Marks):**

The **Information Processing Model** treats the worker as a communication channel with discrete stages:

1. **Stimulus Reception (Sensory Input):** Information enters via eyes, ears, skin. *(1 mark)*
2. **Perception:** The brain interprets raw stimuli into meaningful patterns. *(1 mark)*
3. **Short-Term Memory:** Holds **7 ± 2 chunks** of information (Miller's Law) for active decision-making. *(1 mark)*
4. **Long-Term Memory:** Stores rules, procedures, and motor patterns. *(1 mark)*
5. **Decision-Making:** Compares inputs to stored rules and selects a response. *(1 mark)*
6. **Motor Response:** Muscles execute the chosen action; takes **Fitts' Law** time $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$. *(1 mark)*
7. **Feedback:** Outcome returns to perception, closing the loop for closed-skill tasks. *(1 mark)*

**Application to Control Panel Design:**
- Group related gauges by **perceptual chunking** (≤7 per panel).
- Place critical displays in the **primary visual cone** (±15° from straight-ahead).
- Use **Fitts' Law** to size and space push-buttons; bigger, closer buttons → faster response.
- Provide **redundant auditory + visual signals** for alarms to support multi-sensory perception.
- Use **consistent coding** (color, shape) to leverage long-term memory.

**Part (b) — NIOSH Lifting Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $LC = 23$ kg.
- $H = 25$ cm → $HM = 25/25 = 1.00$.
- $V = (40 + 160)/2 = 100$ cm → $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 100 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.075 = 0.925$.
- $D = 160 - 40 = 120$ cm → $DM = 0.82 + 4.5/120 = 0.82 + 0.0375 = 0.8575$.
- $A = 30°$ → $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904$.
- $FM = 1.00$ (low frequency).
- $CM = 0.95$ (fair coupling).

**Step 1 — RWL Calculation:**

$$
RWL = 23 \times 1.00 \times 0.925 \times 0.8575 \times 0.904 \times 1.00 \times 0.95
$$

$$
23 \times 1.00 = 23
$$

$$
23 \times 0.925 = 21.275
$$

$$
21.275 \times 0.8575 = 18.244
$$

$$
18.244 \times 0.904 = 16.493
$$

$$
16.493 \times 1.00 = 16.493
$$

$$
16.493 \times 0.95 = 15.668 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Lifting Index:**

$$
LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Weight}}{\text{RWL}} = \dfrac{10}{15.668} = 0.638
$$

**Interpretation:**
Since $LI = 0.638 < 1.0$, the lift is **within the safe recommended limit**, and most workers can perform it without elevated injury risk.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{RWL \approx 15.67 \text{ kg}, \quad LI \approx 0.64 \; (\text{Task is SAFE})}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> - **Forgetting to convert seconds to minutes** before applying rating and allowances. Always work in **minutes** for standard time problems.
> - **Confusing NIOSH multipliers**: Horizontal multiplier uses $\vert H \vert$ from the body, NOT from the object. Vertical distance $D$ is the **travel**, not the destination.
> - **Wrong formula for Fitts' Law**: ID uses $\log_2(D/W + 1)$, NOT $\log(D/W)$.
> - **Skipping units** in NIOSH and Standard Time derivations — examiners penalize this **1 mark per major step**.
> - **Ignoring feedback loops** when describing the Information Processing Model — it must explicitly include the **feedback arrow**.
> - **Forgetting allowance components**: $A = A_p + A_f + A_c$ — students often use only one component.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — WORKER (Module 4)**
>
> - **Worker = human subsystem** with physical, sensory, and mental/behavioral traits.
> - **Standard Time formula:** $ST = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times (1 + A)$ — memorize in this exact form.
> - **Allowances always stated as decimals** (e.g., 15% = 0.15).
> - **NIOSH RWL = 7 multipliers** — horizontal, vertical, distance, asymmetry, frequency, coupling (Load Constant = 23 kg).
> - **Lifting Index** LI $>$ 1.0 → redesign needed; LI $\leq$ 1.0 → acceptable.
> - **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — used in HMI, touchscreen, button design.
> - **Information Processing Model:** Stimulus → Perception → Memory → Decision → Motor → Feedback.
> - **Miller's Law:** $7 \pm 2$ chunks in working memory — controls information overload.
> - **Heart rate limit:** $HR_{max} \approx 220 - \text{age}$.
> - **Learning curve:** $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^b$, where $b = \log(\text{learning ratio})/\log 2$.
> - **Workstation design** must serve the **5th–95th percentile** anthropometric range.
> - **Motivation theories:** Maslow (hierarchy), Herzberg (two-factor), Vroom (expectancy) — all relevant to worker performance.
> - **Always include feedback loop** when drawing the worker as a system.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of These Principles in Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET403 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications of These Principles in Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Applications of Design Principles** refer to the systematic translation of the fundamental rules of **Motion Economy, Work Design, Ergonomics, and Anthropometry** into the physical and procedural configuration of workstations, tools, equipment, controls, displays, and the broader work environment. The objective is to minimize human effort, fatigue, and error while maximizing productivity, safety, comfort, and product quality. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, the word "**These Principles**" in Module 4 refers to the consolidated set of design rules covered in earlier modules — namely **Therblig classification & motion economy (Gilbreth's 22 principles)**, **work simplification rules**, **ergonomic principles** (posture, force, frequency, duration), and **anthropometric design boundaries** (5th–95th percentile accommodation). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of designing a work system like **tailoring a suit for a worker**. Just as a tailor measures shoulders, arm length, and posture to cut fabric, an industrial engineer measures human body dimensions, reach, strength, and cognitive limits to "cut" the workstation. The **principles** are the tailor's drafting rules; the **applications** are the final stitched garment. A perfect garment (well-designed workstation) lets the wearer (operator) move freely, feel comfortable, and perform with elegance — without ever noticing the constraints. ## 1.3 Key Universal Design Parameters (KTU Constants & Standards) > [!NOTE] > **Critical Anthropometric & Physiological Constants used throughout KTU 2024 Module 4:** > - **5th–95th percentile accommodation range** — Standard design boundary (covers 90% of working population). > - **Normal reach (front sweep)** — approximately **450 mm** from shoulder rotation point. > - **Maximum reach (full arm extension)** — approximately **720 mm**. > - **Optimum work surface height (standing)** — **50 mm below elbow height** (≈ 1000–1050 mm for average adult). > - **Optimum work surface height (seated)** — **50–100 mm above elbow height** (≈ 720–760 mm). > - **Maximum sustained force (one hand)** — approximately **100 N (≈ 10 kgf)** without fatigue. > - **Recommended work-surface width** — at least **750 mm** to allow forearm support. > - **Standard heart-rate for sustained physical work** — **< 110 bpm** (continuous limit); **< 90 bpm** for prolonged light work. ## 1.4 Why These Applications Matter in KTU Examinations Module 4 is **high-weightage** because it is the **integration module** — almost every KTU Part B (14-mark) question from this module tests whether the student can map a principle to a real design situation (e.g., "design a sewing workstation" or "redesign a hand tool"). Mastery of applications is therefore the single most scoring area. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Application (Mapping "Principles → Design") The "principles" covered in earlier modules cluster into **three application pillars** that Module 4 demands students apply in design problems: | # | Principle Cluster | What It Governs | Typical Design Output | |---|------------------|-----------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Motion Economy Principles** (22 Gilbreth rules) | Path, effort, simultaneity of human motion | Tool shape, control layout, sequence of operations | | 2 | **Ergonomic / Human Factors Principles** | Posture, force, frequency, duration | Workstation height, seat, display/control design | | 3 | **Anthropometric Principles** | Body dimension accommodation | Reach envelopes, clearance dimensions, adjustability | ## 2.2 Application of Motion Economy Principles (Therblig Framework) Gilbreth's principles are grouped by the KTU syllabus into **4 families** for application: **A. Principles Related to the USE OF THE HUMAN BODY** - Work should be performed with **both hands simultaneously** (symmetry). - Both arms should move in **symmetrical, opposite, and simultaneous** directions. - The **rest period** should be used to overcome fatigue — distributed rests, not block rests. - **Hand and body motions** should be confined to the lowest classification (Class 1: finger; Class 2: wrist; Class 3: elbow; Class 4: shoulder; Class 5: trunk). - **Momentum** should be used to assist the worker (gravity drop, slide delivery), never hindered. - **Continuous curved motions** are preferred over straight-line motions with sudden direction changes. - **Rhythmic, automatic motions** are preferred over jerky, hesitating motions. **B. Principles Related to the ARRANGEMENT OF THE WORKPLACE** - Fixed locations for tools and materials (definite location). - Tools and materials should be placed in a **predetermined position** (pre-positioning). - **Gravity feed / gravity drop** for materials to the point of use. - **Drop delivery / chute delivery** to the next station. **C. Principles Related to the DESIGN OF TOOLS & EQUIPMENT** - **Combine tools** to eliminate unnecessary motions (jigs, multi-spindle heads). - **Pre-position** tools for instant grasp. - **Finish** handles, handles for proper grip, color/symbol coding. **D. Principles Related to the TIME ECONOMY** - The **number of motions** should be reduced to a minimum. - The **length of each motion** should be as short as possible. - The **difficulty of motion** should be reduced (Class-1 over Class-5). > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Examination Trick:** When asked to *redesign* a workstation, always state which **Therblig** is being eliminated (e.g., *Search, Select, Transport Empty, Position*) and which **motion class** is being reduced (e.g., *Class 3 elbow → Class 1 finger*). Examiners award marks for this explicit mapping. ## 2.3 Application of Ergonomic Principles in Workstation Design | Ergonomic Principle | Design Application | Quantified Parameter | |---------------------|--------------------|----------------------| | Maintain neutral posture | Adjustable seat height & backrest | Seat pan tilt **0°–10°** | | Reduce sustained force | Counterbalanced tools, jigs | Hold force < **10 % of MVC** | | Minimize repetitive motion | Job rotation, power assists | Cycle time > **30 s** for high-frequency tasks | | Keep load close to body | Lift aids, self-propelled trolleys | Horizontal distance < **250 mm** | | Provide adequate vision | Task lighting **300–500 lux** | Contrast ratio > **0.5** | | Avoid static loading | Sit–stand alternation | Standing duration < **30 min continuous** | | Match controls to expectation | Directional consistency | Lever up = ON / forward = ON | | Provide feedback | Status lights, audible cues | Reaction time < **0.5 s** | ## 2.4 Application of Anthropometric Principles (5th–95th Percentile Design) The **percentile rule** is the KTU-favoured design formula: $$P_{design} = P_{50} \pm k \cdot \sigma$$ where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the relevant body dimension, $P_{50}$ is the median, and $k$ is selected based on the percentage of population to be accommodated: - **90 % accommodation** → $k \approx 1.645$ → design for **5th to 95th percentile**. - **95 % accommodation** → $k \approx 1.96$ → design for **2.5th to 97.5th percentile**. For **clearance** (e.g., door, access opening) → use **95th-percentile male** dimension. For **reach** (e.g., control height) → use **5th-percentile female** dimension. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Code Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL: Pipes are not used inside table cells — vertical bars are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the KTU-rendered markdown.** | # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Application Context | Typical Value / Unit | |---|----------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------| | 1 | $P_{design} = P_{50} \pm k \cdot \sigma$ | Percentile design | Anthropometric workstation | $k = 1.645$ (90 %) | | 2 | $H_{bench,stand} = H_{elbow} - 50$ mm | Standing work height | Manual bench work | 1000–1050 mm | | 3 | $H_{bench,seated} = H_{elbow} + (50 \text{ to } 100)$ mm | Seated work height | Precision seated work | 720–760 mm | | 4 | $R_{normal} = 450$ mm (approx) | Normal reach | Primary control zone | 450 mm | | 5 | $R_{max} = 720$ mm (approx) | Maximum reach | Secondary control zone | 720 mm | | 6 | $F_{sustained,max} \leq 100$ N | Max safe holding force | One-hand grip tools | 100 N | | 7 | $NMT = T_s \cdot (1 + A)$ | Normal Motion Time | Method-time measurement | TMU (1 TMU = 0.00001 h) | | 8 | $t_{rest} = T_{std} \cdot A_{fatigue}$ | Rest allowance | Sustained work | 5–25 % of $T_{std}$ | | 9 | $VO_2 \leq 1.0\ \text{L/min}$ | Aerobic work limit | Sustained physical work | $\approx$ 5 kcal/min | | 10 | $HR_{recovery} \leq 110$ bpm | Cardiac recovery limit | Endurance tasks | 110 bpm | | 11 | $L_{lift,NIOSH} \leq 23$ kg | Recommended Weight Limit | Manual lifting (ideal) | 23 kg | | 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{useful}}{P_{total}} \times 100\ \%$ | Workstation efficiency | Multi-station line | 80–95 % | | 13 | $N_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{t_a \cdot R}{t_b}}$ | Optimal number of stations | Assembly line balancing | dimensionless | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing:** Designing jigs, fixtures, and assembly benches for automotive, electronics, garment industries. - **Healthcare:** Operating-theatre layout, instrument-tray arrangement (GYN/ortho ergonomics). - **Aviation:** Cockpit display & control reach envelopes, seat geometry (EASA CS-25, FAR Part 25). - **Warehousing:** Pallet height, conveyor working height, tote-pick station geometry. - **Software/UI:** Mapping physical ergonomic principles to digital interface design (Fitts's Law, Hick's Law). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Work-Surface Height (Standing) **Step 1 — Define the reference anthropometric variable.** Let $H_{e}$ = elbow height (vertical distance from floor to the underside of the bent elbow, measured with the upper arm hanging naturally). **Step 2 — Apply the ergonomic principle.** A downward reach of **≈ 50 mm** from the elbow produces the most natural posture for light assembly work. The work surface must therefore sit at: $$H_{bench} = H_{e} - d$$ where $d$ is the vertical drop below the elbow. **Step 3 — Plug the KTU-recommended value.** For light precision assembly, $d = 50$ mm; for heavy work, $d$ increases to 100–150 mm to allow mechanical advantage. $$H_{bench} = H_{e} - 50\ \text{mm}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute Indian population anthropometric data.** For an average Indian male adult, $H_{e} \approx 1050$ mm. Hence: $$H_{bench} = 1050\ \text{mm} - 50\ \text{mm} = 1000\ \text{mm}$$ **Step 5 — Apply the 5th–95th percentile correction.** If design must accommodate 90 % of operators, the surface must be **adjustable** between: $$H_{bench,min} = P_{5,e} - 50\ \text{mm} \approx 950\ \text{mm}$$ $$H_{bench,max} = P_{95,e} - 50\ \text{mm} \approx 1100\ \text{mm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The standing work surface must be **adjustable from 950 mm to 1100 mm** to accommodate 90 % of the working population. A fixed-height design at 1000 mm is acceptable only for a homogeneous workforce whose elbow heights fall within a ±50 mm range. ## 3.2 Derivation: Normal-Motion-Time (NMT) for a Redesigned Operation **Step 1 — Original method (MOST analysis).** Let the original operation consist of $n$ basic-motion elements with index $T_{s,i}$ in TMU. $$NMT_{orig} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{s,i}\quad [\text{TMU}]$$ **Step 2 — Apply fatigue allowance $A$.** The standard time is: $$T_{std} = NMT_{orig} \cdot (1 + A)$$ For a moderately fatiguing operation, $A = 0.15$ (15 % rest allowance). **Step 3 — Redesign using motion-economy principles.** By replacing *Transport Empty* (T-E) by *Drop Delivery* and combining two *Position* (P) elements into a *Pre-position* jig, we save 3 elements (each 10 TMU): $$\Delta NMT = 3 \times 10\ \text{TMU} = 30\ \text{TMU}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the new standard time.** $$NMT_{new} = NMT_{orig} - 30\ \text{TMU}$$ $$T_{std,new} = (NMT_{orig} - 30) \cdot (1 + 0.15)\ \text{TMU}$$ **Step 5 — Convert TMU to seconds.** Recall: $1\ \text{TMU} = 0.00001\ \text{h} = 0.036\ \text{s}$. $$T_{std,new}\,[\text{s}] = 0.036 \cdot (NMT_{orig} - 30) \cdot 1.15$$ **Numerical example:** Suppose $NMT_{orig} = 200$ TMU. $$T_{std,new} = 0.036 \times (200 - 30) \times 1.15 = 7.0158\ \text{s}$$ **Productivity gain:** $$\% \text{ gain} = \frac{T_{std,old} - T_{std,new}}{T_{std,old}} \times 100 = \frac{(200 \times 1.15) - (170 \times 1.15)}{200 \times 1.15} \times 100 = 15\ \%$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Optimal Number of Stations in a Simple Assembly Line (Heuristic) **Step 1 — Define parameters.** Let $T_{a}$ = total assembly time, $R$ = required production rate (units/day), $t_{b}$ = bottleneck time. **Step 2 — Lower bound on number of stations.** $$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{a}}{C_{t}} \right\rceil$$ where $C_{t}$ is the desired cycle time and $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the ceiling. **Step 3 — Empirical optimum (Muther / KTU heuristic).** $$N_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{t_{a} \cdot R}{t_{b}}}$$ **Step 4 — Workstation efficiency.** $$\eta = \frac{T_{a}}{N_{opt} \cdot C_{t}} \times 100\ \%$$ **Step 5 — Balance delay.** $$BD = 1 - \eta$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Principle → Application → Design Output | Principle (Class) | Sub-Principle | Application Scenario | Concrete Design Output | KTU Expected Justification | |-------------------|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------------| | **Motion Economy (A)** | Both hands start/finish together | Hand-soldering PCB | Solder + iron stand, twin fixtures | Removes *Wait* Therblig | | **Motion Economy (A)** | Use lowest motion class | Stapling operation | Foot-pedal actuator | Class 5 trunk → Class 1 foot | | **Motion Economy (B)** | Pre-position tools | Assembly bench | Tool shadow-board | Removes *Search* (10 TMU) | | **Motion Economy (B)** | Gravity feed | Hopper feed for bolts | Inclined chute | Replaces *Transport* | | **Motion Economy (C)** | Combine tools | Sheet-metal marking | Multi-tip scriber | Replaces 2 *Transport* | | **Motion Economy (D)** | Reduce number of motions | Keyboard typing | Touch-typing posture | Class 5 → Class 1 | | **Ergonomic** | Neutral posture | Sewing workstation | Adjustable chair (lumbar support) | Reduces back MSD | | **Ergonomic** | Reduce force | Bolt tightening | Torque-controlled power tool | F ≤ 100 N | | **Ergonomic** | Adequate vision | Inspection table | Task lamp 500 lux + magnifier | Contrast ≥ 0.5 | | **Anthropometric** | 5th–95th %ile reach | Control panel | Primary zone ≤ 450 mm radius | 90 % reach coverage | | **Anthropometric** | Clearance | Maintenance opening | ≥ 95th %ile shoulder breadth | 510 mm typical | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Anthropometric Console Layout A control console is being designed for 5th–95th percentile seated Indian operators. Given: - 5th-percentile female **shoulder-to-grip reach** = **580 mm**. - 95th-percentile male **shoulder-to-grip reach** = **780 mm**. **Step 1 — Locate the "primary control zone."** Use 5th-percentile reach as the **maximum distance** for frequently used controls (so 95 % of operators can reach them). $$R_{primary} = 580\ \text{mm}$$ **Step 2 — Locate the "secondary control zone."** Use 95th-percentile reach as the **maximum distance** for infrequently used controls. $$R_{secondary} = 780\ \text{mm}$$ **Step 3 — Map the zones on the console.** $$\text{Primary (frequent)} \quad \vert \quad \text{Secondary (occasional)} \quad \vert \quad \text{Tertiary (emergency-only)}$$ $$\quad\;\;\leq 580\ \text{mm}\;\;\quad \leq 780\ \text{mm}\quad \quad\quad\;\;\;> 780\ \text{mm}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always cite the **population segment** (5th %ile female / 95th %ile male) when justifying a reach distance. Generic statements like "operators can reach" earn zero marks. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map: From Principles to Design Outputs ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Motion Economy Principles
22 Gilbreth Rules] P2[Ergonomic Principles
Posture Force Frequency Duration] P3[Anthropometric Principles
5th to 95th Percentile] P1 --> A1[Workplace Layout Design] P1 --> A2[Tool and Equipment Design] P1 --> A3[Method Simplification] P2 --> B1[Workstation Geometry] P2 --> B2[Controls and Displays] P2 --> B3[Manual Material Handling] P3 --> C1[Reach Envelope Definition] P3 --> C2[Clearance Dimensioning] P3 --> C3[Adjustability Specification] A1 --> OUT1[Reduced Therbligs and TMU] A2 --> OUT1 A3 --> OUT1 B1 --> OUT2[Improved Posture and Comfort] B2 --> OUT2 B3 --> OUT2 C1 --> OUT3[Population Coverage 90 percent] C2 --> OUT3 C3 --> OUT3 OUT1 --> KPI[Higher Productivity
Lower Fatigue
Lower Error Rate] OUT2 --> KPI OUT3 --> KPI ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Design Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Define the Operation
and Operator Population] --> S2[Step 2: Collect Principles
Motion Economy and Ergonomics and Anthropometry] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Generate Alternative
Workstation Layouts] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Score Each Layout
Therblig Count and TMU and RULA Score] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Select Optimal Design
and Document Specification] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Pilot Test
and Iterate] S1 -. uses .-> DB1[(Anthropometric Database
5th to 95th percentile)] S2 -. uses .-> DB2[(Motion-Economy
22 Rule Checklist)] S3 -. uses .-> DB3[(Ergonomic Guideline
NIOSH and IS 1786)] S4 -. uses .-> DB4[(MTM and MOST
Time Database)] ``` ## 4.3 Workstation Reach Zones (Schematic Description) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual layout (described in words since precise CAD schematics are out of Mermaid scope):** > > Imagine the operator seated at the **origin (0,0)** — the **shoulder joint** viewed from above. > > - **Zone A — Primary (frequent) reach:** a forward semi-circle of radius **≈ 450 mm**. > - **Zone B — Secondary (occasional) reach:** an annular ring from 450 mm to **≈ 720 mm**. > - **Zone C — Emergency-only:** beyond 720 mm — labelled and protected by a guard. > > **Application:** Critical / high-frequency controls go in Zone A; monitoring displays in Zone B; **Emergency-Stop (E-Stop) mushroom button** in Zone C so it is reached deliberately, not by accident. ## 4.4 Application Decision Tree (Which Principle to Apply First) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is the task
repetitive
cycle less than 30 s?} Q1 -- YES --> R1[Apply Motion Economy
Reduce Therbligs first] Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is the task
physically heavy
force more than 100 N?} Q2 -- YES --> R2[Apply Ergonomic
Mechanise or assist] Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Does the layout
fit 90 percent
of operators?} Q3 -- NO --> R3[Apply Anthropometric
Add adjustability] Q3 -- YES --> R4[Document Final Design] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** State **any six** Gilbreth's principles of motion economy that relate to the **arrangement of the workplace**. **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):** 1. A definite and fixed place for every tool and material. 2. Tools and materials should be pre-positioned for instant grasp. 3. Gravity-feed and gravity-drop delivery wherever possible. 4. Drop-delivery / chute-delivery to the next workstation. 5. Materials and tools located within the normal working area (primary reach zone). 6. Locate tools in the order of use to eliminate searching. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Differentiate between the **5th-percentile** and **95th-percentile** design approaches. State one industrial example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | 5th-Percentile Design | 95th-Percentile Design | |--------|----------------------|------------------------| | **Population accommodated** | Smallest 5 % of the workforce (e.g., 5th-percentile **female**) | Largest 5 % of the workforce (e.g., 95th-percentile **male**) | | **Used for** | **Reach distances** — controls must be reachable by small operators | **Clearances** — doorways, openings must pass large operators | | **Example 1** | Height of the most-used pushbutton on a console | Width of a maintenance access hatch | | **Example 2** | Distance of a circuit breaker handle from shoulder | Height of an overhead crane walkway hand-rail | | **Formula basis** | $P_{5} = P_{50} - 1.645\,\sigma$ | $P_{95} = P_{50} + 1.645\,\sigma$ | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Never** describe a "redesign" without explicitly mapping it to a **Therblig** being eliminated or a **motion class** being reduced. You will lose 2–3 marks. > - **Always** mention the **population percentile** (5th / 95th) when justifying a clearance or reach dimension. > - **Avoid** generic statements like "the workstation should be ergonomic" — give the **quantified parameter** (e.g., "seat pan tilt 0°–10°" or "task lighting 500 lux"). > - For questions worth 7+7, ensure **both** sub-parts are answered with equal depth; examiners frequently cut marks on the second sub-part. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 marks]** A manual assembly of a small electronic component currently requires the operator to: - Reach with the **right hand** to a tray placed **850 mm** to the right. - Pick up the component (**Therblig = Select + Grasp**). - Walk **5 steps** to the assembly bench. - Position the component with **both hands** at a workbench whose surface is **1200 mm** above the floor. Apply the **principles of motion economy and ergonomics** to **redesign** this workstation. Justify **each** modification by naming the **Therblig eliminated / motion class reduced**, and provide a **quantified design specification** for the new layout. **(b) [7 marks]** A control panel for a CNC machine is to be designed for seated operators. Using anthropometric data (5th-percentile seated female shoulder-grip reach = **580 mm**; 95th-percentile seated male = **780 mm**), determine: - (i) the **radius of the primary control zone**, - (ii) the **radius of the secondary control zone**, - (iii) where the **Emergency-Stop button** should be placed, with justification. - (iv) Calculate the **workstation efficiency** if the line has 6 stations with a bottleneck time of 90 s and a total assembly time of 480 s. #### **Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]** | # | Existing Problem | Principle Applied | Redesign Modification | Therblig / Class Affected | Quantified Specification | |---|------------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Tray 850 mm away — far reach | **Pre-position** + **Shorten distance** | Mount tray on a **swivel arm at 400 mm** from shoulder | Eliminates *Transport Empty* (1 TMU/step × 5 = 5 TMU saved) | Reach ≤ 450 mm (normal) | | 2 | Operator walks 5 steps | **Locate all materials within primary zone** | Place tray on the **bench itself** | Eliminates *Walk* (≈ 17 TMU per step × 5) | Steps = 0 | | 3 | One-handed operation | **Symmetry — both hands used** | Twin-feed tray + dual fixture | Enables *Use* by both hands | Cycle halved for two-handed steps | | 4 | Bench at 1200 mm | **Optimum work-surface height** | Lower to **1000 mm** (elbow – 50 mm) | Reduces class-4 shoulder motion to class-2 wrist | 1000 mm | | 5 | No footrest / no anti-fatigue mat | **Reduce static fatigue** | Add **sit-stand stool + mat** | Reduces *Hold* effort | HR < 110 bpm | | 6 | Component not pre-oriented | **Pre-position tools/materials** | Add **vibratory bowl feeder** | Removes *Position* (20 TMU) | Bowl at 45° incline | **Standard time saving (quantified):** Original: ~5 + 85 + 20 = 110 TMU affected. Redesign savings: ~85 TMU. At 0.036 s/TMU and 1.15 allowance: Saving ≈ **85 × 0.036 × 1.15 = 3.5 s/cycle** (≈ 15–20 % productivity gain). #### **Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]** **(i) Primary control zone radius** Frequently used controls must be reachable by the smallest operator (5th-percentile female): $$R_{primary} = 580\ \text{mm}$$ **(ii) Secondary control zone radius** Occasionally used controls can be located at the 95th-percentile reach: $$R_{secondary} = 780\ \text{mm}$$ **(iii) Emergency-Stop placement** E-Stop should be placed **beyond 780 mm** (or in a deliberate guarded position) so that **accidental activation is impossible**, but the operator can still reach it with a deliberate lunge/lean. Justification: this satisfies ISO 13850 — *"Emergency-stop devices shall be located where they can be reached readily"* while preventing inadvertent activation during normal cycle. **(iv) Workstation efficiency** $$\eta = \frac{T_{a}}{N \cdot C_{t}} \times 100 = \frac{480}{6 \times 90} \times 100 = \frac{480}{540} \times 100 = 88.89\ \%$$ **Balance Delay** $= 100 - 88.89 = 11.11\ \%$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a **schematic reach-envelope diagram (describe in words)**, explain how the **5th–95th percentile anthropometric principle** is applied to: - (i) a **sewing workstation** (seated, light precision work). - (ii) a **hand-tool** for repetitive bolt-tightening. - State the **recommended numerical values** for work-surface height, seat pan tilt, and maximum one-hand grip force for each case. **(b) [7 marks]** A manual material-handling task requires an operator to lift a **20 kg** box from floor to a **750 mm** high pallet, **30 times per hour**. - (i) Compute the **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** using the simplified NIOSH equation: $$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$ Take all multipliers = **1.0** except: - Horizontal distance **HM = 0.71** (object 25 cm from body) - Vertical location **VM = 0.97** (origin at 750 mm) - Lifting **Index LI = Load / RWL** - (ii) Comment on the **lifting risk** and recommend **two** ergonomic interventions. #### **Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]** **(i) Sewing workstation (seated, precision):** - **Work-surface height:** Light precision work → surface **50–100 mm above elbow**, i.e., **720–760 mm** for an average adult (elbow ≈ 680 mm seated). - **Seat pan:** Adjustable height, **tilt 0°–10°** forward, padded; lumbar support at **150–200 mm** above seat pan. - **Reach envelope (described):** Draw a top-view **arc** with origin at the **shoulder joint**: - Inner arc: **0–450 mm** → **primary zone** (fabric, needle, bobbin). - Outer arc: **450–720 mm** → **secondary zone** (thread spool, scissor, trim bin). - Pedal at foot: **250–300 mm** in front, knee angle **90°–110°**. - **Maximum grip force:** Holding fabric = **< 10 N** (negligible), but the wrist should be straight to avoid carpal-tunnel MSD. **(ii) Hand-tool for bolt-tightening:** - **Grip diameter:** **30–50 mm** for cylindrical power grasp (5th–95th %ile hand breadth). - **Handle length:** **100–125 mm** to fit 95th %ile palm. - **Trigger force:** **< 20 N** for index-finger activation. - **Wrist posture:** Tool designed so wrist deviation is **< 15°** from neutral. - **Maximum one-hand grip force sustained:** **< 100 N** (10 kgf) — if torque required exceeds this, use a **torque-controlled power tool**. #### **Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]** **(i) RWL calculation:** $$LC = 23\ \text{kg (load constant for ideal lift)}$$ $$RWL = 23 \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$ $$RWL = 23 \times 0.71 \times 0.97 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0$$ $$RWL = 23 \times 0.6887 = 15.84\ \text{kg}$$ **Lifting Index:** $$LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL} = \frac{20}{15.84} = 1.26$$ **(ii) Risk comment and interventions:** - **LI > 1.0** indicates **increased risk** of lower-back injury for a substantial portion of the workforce. **LI between 1.0 and 1.5** is "moderate" risk; **LI > 1.5** is "high". - **Intervention 1 (Engineering):** Introduce a **mechanical lift-assist** (scissor lift table) to raise the pallet automatically to elbow height, reducing VM penalty to **1.0** → new RWL = 23 × 0.71 × 1.0 = **16.33 kg**, still close — but combined with **Intervention 2**, the LI drops below 1.0. - **Intervention 2 (Work-organisation):** **Job rotation** + reduce frequency from 30 lifts/hr to **12 lifts/hr** (cycle 5 min), and reduce box weight to **15 kg** → new LI = 15/16.33 = **0.92 < 1.0** → acceptable. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION POINTS — read this 30 minutes before the KTU exam.** - [ ] The 22 motion-economy principles are grouped into **4 families**: Use of Body / Workplace / Tools & Equipment / Time Economy. - [ ] **Therbligs eliminated by redesign** → Search, Select, Transport Empty, Position, Assemble — each worth **≈ 10 TMU**; the more eliminated, the greater the savings. - [ ] **Motion classes** (lowest to highest cost): Finger → Wrist → Elbow → Shoulder → Trunk. Always design to keep motion at the **lowest class**. - [ ] **Work-surface height (standing)** = elbow height − 50 mm; **(seated, light)** = elbow height + 50–100 mm. - [ ] **Normal reach = 450 mm**, **Maximum reach = 720 mm** — used to define **primary** and **secondary** control zones. - [ ] **Anthropometric rule of thumb**: reach → use **5th-percentile**; clearance → use **95th-percentile**. - [ ] **5th–95th percentile** corresponds to $k = 1.645\,\sigma$ (90 % population coverage). - [ ] **Maximum sustained one-hand force ≈ 100 N (≈ 10 kgf)** beyond which fatigue, MSD, and error rates rise sharply. - [ ] **E-Stop button** must be placed **beyond 720 mm** (deliberate activation, ISO 13850). - [ ] **NIOSH Lifting Index LI = Load / RWL**; **LI ≤ 1.0** = acceptable, **1.0 < LI ≤ 1.5** = moderate risk, **LI > 1.5** = high risk. - [ ] **Standard time** $T_{std} = NMT \times (1 + A)$, where $A$ is the **fatigue/rest allowance** (typically 5–25 %). - [ ] **Workstation efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{T_{a}}{N \cdot C_{t}} \times 100\ \%$ and **balance delay** $BD = 1 - \eta$. - [ ] Always pair the **principle cited** with a **quantified design parameter** in your answer — this is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one in KTU 2024. - [ ] **Two-application rule:** For every redesign, justify by **(i) Therblig/class eliminated** and **(ii) quantified ergonomic improvement** (height, force, frequency, posture). - [ ] Module 4 integrates Modules 1–3 — be ready for **"Design a … workstation for …"** type 14-mark questions, where the answer must cover **layout, tools, anthropometry, and time** in one coherent flow. ---
